title: PRIMARY AND SECONDARY DEFECTS OF THE THYMUS
summary: The thymus is the primary lymphoid organ essential for T-cell development and generation of a diverse T-cell receptor repertoire capable of recognizing pathogens while maintaining immune tolerance through negative selection. Primary defects, including genetic mutations in transcription factors such as TBX1, HOXA3, PAX1, and PAX9, or loss of AIRE function, result in thymus aplasia or impaired central tolerance, leading to severe immunodeficiency and autoimmunity. Secondary thymic dysfunction arises from multiple insults including chemotherapy, radiation, infections, malnutrition, and elevated glucocorticoids, all of which trigger thymic atrophy and consequently reduce naïve T-cell output and TCR diversity. These secondary defects increase vulnerability to infections, malignancies, and autoimmune diseases, effects particularly pronounced with age-related thymic decline. Thymic epithelial-mesenchymal crosstalk mediated by bone morphogenetic proteins, fibroblast growth factors, and hedgehog proteins, along with thymocyte-stromal interactions involving RANK, CD40, and lymphotoxin signaling, are critical for proper thymic architecture and T-cell selection. Therapeutic interventions including soluble factor administration and thymic tissue transplantation show promise in restoring thymic function and immune competence in patients with primary and secondary thymic disorders.

title: lncRNA TTTY14 participates in the progression of repeated implantation failure by regulating the miR-6088/SEMA5A axis
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study identifies a regulatory axis involving the long non-coding RNA TTTY14, microRNA miR-6088, and the semaphorin protein SEMA5A that contributes to repeated implantation failure (RIF). Through weighted gene coexpression network analysis, the authors found that TTTY14 functions as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) to regulate SEMA5A expression by modulating miR-6088 levels. SEMA5A and the zinc finger protein ZNF555 were significantly upregulated in RIF patient samples compared to controls, suggesting a pathogenic role in implantation dysfunction. The dysregulated genes in this pathway, along with PPIEL and miR-6088, are proposed as novel biomarkers for RIF diagnosis and prognosis. This TTTY14-miR-6088-SEMA5A regulatory network may impair endometrial function by disrupting plasma membrane organization, thus providing mechanistic insights into the molecular etiology of implantation failure.

title: ABCC4 suppresses glioblastoma progression and recurrence by restraining cGMP-PKG signalling
summary: ABCC4 functions as a tumor suppressor in glioblastoma by actively restraining cGMP-PKG signaling through efflux of cyclic nucleotides, and loss of ABCC4 expression permits elevated intracellular cGMP levels that activate PKG signaling and promote malignancy. Low ABCC4 expression is significantly associated with poor prognosis, including increased recurrence and reduced overall survival in glioblastoma patients. Aspirin-loaded hydrogels represent a novel therapeutic strategy that upregulates ABCC4 expression to suppress glioblastoma progression while enhancing the efficacy of standard-of-care treatments. This work reveals that cyclic nucleotide signaling, particularly the cGMP-PKG axis, is a critical mediator of glioblastoma aggressiveness and recurrence, positioning ABCC4 restoration as a viable translational approach to improve patient outcomes.

title: Identification of new markers of angiogenic sprouting using transcriptomics: New role for RND3
summary:
# Summary

This study used transcriptomics to identify novel markers of angiogenic sprouting and establish a new functional role for RND3 in endothelial cell invasion. Endothelial cells undergoing angiogenic sprouting exhibit rapid transcriptional reprogramming, with 39 candidate genes upregulated within six hours, including key regulators SNAI1, PTGS2, and JUNB that promote endothelial-mesenchymal transition and invasion responses. Functional validation demonstrated that both SNAI1 and JUNB are required for optimal endothelial cell invasion, as their knockdown significantly reduced invasion density and distance. RND3 (RhoE), previously uncharacterized in angiogenesis, emerged as a critical regulator of filopodia extension and tip cell phenotype; RND3 knockdown reduced invasion distance and filopodia length, findings confirmed in vivo using Rnd3 heterozygous mice. Nearly half of the upregulated genes function in immune system processes, implicating immune signaling pathways alongside signal transduction and morphogenesis in coordinating the angiogenic response. These findings identify RND3 and associated transcriptional networks as potential therapeutic targets for conditions involving pathological angiogenesis, including endometriosis.

title: Differential expression of follicular fluid exosomal microRNA in women with diminished ovarian reserve
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study identified 592 differentially expressed microRNAs (miRNAs) in follicular fluid exosomes from women with diminished ovarian reserve (DOR), with 213 miRNAs upregulated and 379 downregulated compared to controls. Follicular fluid exosomal miRNAs regulate granulosa cell function, a critical process for ovarian reserve and reproductive outcomes. The target genes of these differentially expressed miRNAs were predominantly enriched in cancer-related pathways, including the PI3K/AKT signaling cascade and actin cytoskeleton signaling, suggesting shared molecular mechanisms between DOR pathogenesis and oncogenic transformation. These findings implicate follicular fluid exosomal miRNAs as key biomarkers and potential contributors to DOR development. The altered miRNA profiles may reflect compromised ovarian microenvironments that impair granulosa cell function and oocyte quality in affected women.

title: Mining phase separation-related diagnostic biomarkers for endometriosis through WGCNA and multiple machine learning techniques: a retrospective and nomogram study
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study employed weighted gene coexpression network analysis and machine learning algorithms to identify phase separation-related biomarkers for endometriosis diagnosis. Nine differentially expressed genes were identified as significantly associated with endometriosis pathogenesis, with five feature genes—FOS, CFD, CCNA1, CA4, and CST1—selected as optimal diagnostic biomarkers through machine learning approaches. These feature genes are implicated in multiple pathological pathways including mismatch repair, cell cycle regulation, complement and coagulation cascades, and IL-17 inflammatory signaling. Gene set enrichment analysis linked the dysregulated genes to DNA replication, chemokine signaling, and immune dysfunction pathways. The study also revealed notable alterations in immune cell populations, including CD4 T cells, CD8 T cells, dendritic cells, and macrophages in endometriosis tissue compared to normal endometrium. A diagnostic nomogram was developed using the five feature genes to provide clinically applicable endometriosis risk assessment based on phase separation biology.

title: Meta-analysis of endometrial transcriptome data reveals novel molecular targets for recurrent implantation failure
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This meta-analysis identified a 49-gene meta-signature associated with recurrent implantation failure (RIF), comprising 38 upregulated and 11 downregulated genes in the endometrium of affected patients. Key implantation-related genes including GADD45A, IGF2, and LIF, along with lesser-studied fertility-associated genes such as OPRK1, PSIP1, SMCHD1, and SOD2, were identified as dysregulated in RIF. The dysregulated genes are predominantly involved in critical endometrial processes including cell differentiation, communication, and adhesion, with many functioning through inflammatory signaling pathways including MAPK and PI3K-Akt cascades. miRNA-mediated regulation of these 49 dysregulated mRNAs was investigated as a potential mechanism underlying RIF pathology. These findings suggest that the identified meta-signature genes could serve as novel diagnostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets to improve patient stratification and enable personalized treatment strategies for recurrent implantation failure.

title: Super-enhancers: drivers of cells’ identities and cells’ debacles
summary: Super-enhancers are clusters of densely occupied enhancer regions that function as major drivers of gene expression, particularly for genes that establish and maintain cellular identity. These regulatory elements orchestrate the expression of identity-defining genes through concentrated recruitment of transcriptional activators, creating stronger and more sustained gene expression compared to typical enhancers. Super-enhancers play critical roles in directing cell type development and specification across diverse biological contexts. Beyond their normal physiological functions, super-enhancers are significantly associated with both noncancerous and cancerous disease states, suggesting that dysregulation of these regulatory regions contributes to pathological cellular identities and disease development. This dual role—supporting both normal cellular identity establishment and disease-associated cellular transformation—positions super-enhancers as key targets for understanding fundamental mechanisms underlying both normal development and disease pathogenesis, including disorders relevant to endometriosis and related conditions.

title: DLX genes and proteins in mammalian forebrain development
summary: The Dlx gene family encodes homeobox transcription factors that play critical roles in mammalian forebrain development, particularly in controlling the differentiation and development of GABAergic neurons. DLX proteins function as key regulators within gene regulatory networks by interacting with general transcriptional regulators and co-binding with other transcription factors at enhancer regions to coordinate gene expression. These homeodomain transcription factors integrate genetic and biochemical signals to govern the specification and maturation of inhibitory GABAergic neuronal populations essential for proper forebrain circuitry formation. The ability of DLX proteins to orchestrate complex transcriptional networks through protein-protein interactions and combinatorial binding demonstrates their central importance as developmental regulators. Understanding these Dlx-mediated molecular mechanisms provides insights into transcriptional control mechanisms that may be dysregulated in developmental and pathological conditions affecting neural tissues.

title: MiR-135a-5p regulates window of implantation by suppressing pinopodes development and decidualization of endometrial stromal cells
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that miR-135a-5p plays a critical regulatory role in endometrial receptivity and the window of implantation by suppressing key developmental processes. During the normal window of implantation, miR-135a-5p is significantly downregulated, and elevated expression of this microRNA impairs both pinopode development and endometrial stromal cell decidualization. The inhibitory effects of miR-135a-5p on decidualization occur through its downregulation of two essential targets, HOXA10 and BMPR2, which are normally required for successful endometrial remodeling. The upstream regulator CEBPD controls miR-135a-5p expression, providing a mechanistic link in the regulatory cascade. Collectively, these findings reveal that dysregulation of the miR-135a-5p/HOXA10/BMPR2 axis compromises endometrial receptivity and may contribute to implantation failure and abnormal pregnancy outcomes, highlighting this pathway as a potential therapeutic target for reproductive dysfunction.

title: Reverse Repurposing: Potential Utility of Cancer Drugs in Non-Malignant Illnesses
summary: This article demonstrates the principle of reverse repurposing, wherein cancer drugs targeting dysregulated kinase pathways are repurposed to treat non-malignant diseases sharing identical molecular alterations. Deep infiltrating endometriosis involves somatic mutations in PIK3CA and KRAS genes, which activate the PI3K/AKT/mTOR and MEK signaling pathways respectively—the same pathways dysregulated in multiple cancers. MEK inhibitors and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway inhibitors show theoretical and experimental benefit in reducing endometriosis symptoms by suppressing these aberrant kinase cascades. The article illustrates this reverse repurposing strategy across multiple non-malignant conditions, including neurofibromatosis types 1 and 2, tuberous sclerosis, and various hereditary overgrowth syndromes, all effectively treated with FDA-approved cancer kinase inhibitors targeting their specific molecular drivers. This approach highlights how understanding shared molecular pathology between cancer and benign disease enables the rational repositioning of oncologic therapeutics, potentially accelerating treatment development for endometriosis and other conditions with actionable kinase pathway mutations.

title: Transcriptomic profiles associated with experimental placebo effects in chronic pain
summary:
# Transcriptomic Profiles Associated with Experimental Placebo Effects in Chronic Pain

This study identified transcriptomic signatures underlying placebo analgesia by analyzing gene expression profiles in chronic pain patients experiencing placebo-induced pain reduction. Six genes—CCDC85B, FBXL15, HAGH, PI3, SELENOM, and TNFRSF4—were significantly upregulated in placebo responders, with SELENOM playing a particularly important role in dopaminergic synaptic function and cognitive processes relevant to placebo response. Pathway analysis revealed that placebo effects were associated with multiple biological processes including RNA metabolism, SLIT/ROBO signaling (involved in neural axon guidance and angiogenesis), ribosome biogenesis, and cellular stress response. The study integrated genetic findings by highlighting previously identified polymorphisms in OPRM1 (opioid pathway), COMT (cognition and emotion), and FAAH (endocannabinoid pathway) as contributors to placebo responsiveness. The results suggest that placebo effects involve coordinated changes across neurological, metabolic, and immune-regulatory processes, with descending neural pathways inhibiting nociceptive signaling through dopaminergic, opioid, serotonergic, and endocannabinoid mechanisms. These findings demonstrate a substantial genetic and transcriptomic basis for individual differences in placebo analgesia in chronic pain conditions.

title: Going Beyond Childhood and Gender-Based Violence: Epigenetic Modifications and Inheritance
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how childhood and gender-based violence trigger long-term health consequences through epigenetic mechanisms that can be transmitted across generations. Traumatic violence exposure causes psychological distress, post-traumatic stress disorder, and increased morbidity and mortality, with epigenetic modifications emerging as a key mechanistic link. Specific epigenetic changes—including DNA methylation of genes like NR3C1, FKBP5, BDNF, and the serotonin transporter gene 5HTT—dysregulate the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and stress response systems, contributing to various psychiatric and somatic disorders including PTSD, depression, and impulsive behaviors. The article highlights dose-dependent relationships where abuse intensity and frequency correlate with methylation severity, while emotional support can mitigate these epigenetic modifications. Remarkably, childhood victimization and HPA axis dysfunction cause epigenetic age acceleration, suggesting that early life trauma leaves lasting molecular signatures. These findings underscore how environmental trauma becomes biologically embedded through heritable epigenetic changes, potentially explaining the intergenerational transmission of trauma's detrimental health effects.

title: Integrative bioinformatics analysis to identify ferroptosis-related genes in non-obstructive azoospermia
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This bioinformatics study identified ferroptosis-related genes (FRGs) as key players in non-obstructive azoospermia pathogenesis, uncovering 35 differentially expressed FRGs involved in ferroptotic pathways. Among these, HMOX1 and GPX4 emerged as pivotal genes, with GPX4 showing prominent expression in spermatids and sperm, suggesting critical roles in male fertility. The analysis revealed that azoospermia is associated with decreased infiltration of activated dendritic cells and altered immune responses, with SAT1 and HMGCR genes correlating with immune cell infiltration patterns. Eight hub genes demonstrated robust diagnostic potential with AUC values exceeding 0.75, positioning them as promising biomarkers for azoospermia detection and classification. These findings establish ferroptosis as a previously underappreciated mechanism in male infertility and provide a molecular foundation for developing diagnostic tools and potential therapeutic interventions targeting ferroptotic pathways in non-obstructive azoospermia.

title: Exploring the correlation between homocysteine, red blood cell folate and MTHFRC677T genotypes with female infertility
summary: This study investigated the metabolic basis of female infertility by examining associations between homocysteine levels, red blood cell folate status, and MTHFR C677T genetic variants. Elevated serum homocysteine emerged as a key marker strongly associated with infertility, with women carrying the MTHFR C677T TT genotype exhibiting particularly high homocysteine levels compared to women of normal reproductive age. The MTHFR C677T TT genotype was identified as a genetic risk factor conferring increased infertility susceptibility in childbearing-aged women, likely through impaired methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase enzyme function. Red blood cell folate levels showed a moderate association with infertility status, suggesting that folate metabolism dysregulation may contribute to reproductive dysfunction. These findings establish a mechanistic link between impaired one-carbon metabolism, marked by elevated homocysteine and reduced folate availability, and female infertility, with specific MTHFR genotypes predisposing to metabolic perturbations. The results suggest that homocysteine and folate status, particularly in the context of MTHFR genetic polymorphisms, may serve as biomarkers for identifying women at risk of infertility.

title: Integrative Multiomics in the Lung Reveals a Protective Role of Asporin in Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies asporin (ASPN) as a protective hub gene upregulated in pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) lungs and plasma across multiple patient cohorts. Asporin exerts its protective effects by directly binding to and inhibiting TGF-β1 signaling, thereby suppressing smooth muscle cell proliferation and promoting apoptosis in pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells. Mechanistically, hypoxia induces ASPN expression through STAT5A signaling, while genetic variants in rs271681 that reduce asporin protein levels are associated with increased pulmonary arterial wall density and disease severity. Functional studies confirm that ASPN knockdown exacerbates PAH pathology in animal models, whereas recombinant asporin administration attenuates disease, and plasma asporin levels positively correlate with favorable hemodynamic parameters including cardiac output and right ventricular function. These findings establish asporin as a compensatory protective mechanism in PAH and suggest it may serve as both a biomarker and therapeutic target for this devastating vascular disease.

title: Female reproductive tract microbiota varies with MHC profile
journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences
summary: This study demonstrates that major histocompatibility complex (MHC) genes significantly shape the composition and diversity of the female reproductive tract microbiota, with women carrying greater MHC diversity exhibiting correspondingly higher microbiome diversity characterized by bacteria associated with vaginal health. Women with similar MHC genotypes showed similar microbiome compositions, indicating that MHC genetic makeup is a key determinant of bacterial assemblage in the reproductive tract. The research further revealed that follicular fluid microbiota composition closely mirrors that of the vaginal microbiota, suggesting bacterial transmission or establishment throughout the reproductive tract. These findings underscore the fundamental importance of host genetic factors in shaping women's reproductive microbiota and open new avenues for investigating whether microbiota composition mediates the relationship between MHC variation and reproductive success. This work establishes an important link between immunogenetic variation and microbial ecology in the female reproductive system, with potential implications for understanding reproductive outcomes and conditions such as infertility.

title: Evaluation of the diagnostic utility of immune microenvironment-related biomarkers in endometriosis using multidimensional transcriptomic data
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study identified immune microenvironment-related biomarkers for endometriosis by integrating multidimensional transcriptomic data, revealing that endometriosis is associated with abnormal expression of microRNAs (DEMIs) and messenger RNAs (DEMs) alongside dysregulation of nine immune cell populations. Through network analysis, the researchers identified 176 candidate genes that are miRNA targets, associated with aberrant immune cell abundance, and abnormally expressed in endometriosis. Further refinement narrowed these to eleven crucial genes regulated by eight specific miRNAs, with eight of these genes demonstrating superior diagnostic efficiency for endometriosis. These immune-associated gene regulatory networks underscore the central role of disrupted immune microenvironment in endometriosis pathogenesis, particularly given that approximately fifty percent of women with endometriosis experience infertility. The identified miRNA-gene regulatory interactions offer promising diagnostic biomarkers that could improve clinical detection of endometriosis while advancing understanding of its immune-mediated etiology.

title: ARID1A mutation associated with recurrence and shorter progression-free survival in atypical meningiomas
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: This study identifies ARID1A mutations as a significant prognostic marker in atypical meningiomas, demonstrating that these mutations are substantially enriched in recurrent tumors compared to primary tumors. ARID1A, a chromatin remodeling gene, shows a marked association with increased recurrence risk, conferring approximately a 7.26-fold elevation in hazard of recurrence when mutated in primary atypical meningiomas. The presence of ARID1A mutations in primary tumors strongly predicts poor prognosis and shorter progression-free survival, suggesting that ARID1A status could serve as a valuable molecular marker for identifying high-risk patients. The findings underscore the critical role of chromatin remodeling dysfunction in meningioma biology and tumor aggressiveness. These results may inform clinical decision-making regarding surveillance intensity and treatment strategies for patients with ARID1A-mutated atypical meningiomas.

title: Explore the alterations of downstream molecular pathways caused by ARID1A mutation/knockout in human endometrial cancer cells
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: ARID1A mutations represent early driving events in endometrial carcinogenesis, and knockout of this chromatin remodeling gene in endometrial cancer cells promotes malignant transformation through multiple interconnected pathways. ARID1A deficiency suppresses apoptosis by reducing critical pro-apoptotic factors including p21, caspase 7, and caspase 9, while simultaneously accelerating cell cycle progression from G0/G1 to S phase and enhancing overall cell proliferation. At the molecular level, ARID1A loss activates the phosphoinositide 3-kinase/protein kinase B (PI3K/Akt) signaling cascade and reduces key genomic stability markers such as mutL homologue 1 and progesterone receptor B, resulting in high-level microsatellite instability. These findings establish ARID1A as a central tumor suppressor in endometrial cancer whose inactivation drives malignant phenotypes through coordinated effects on cell survival, proliferation, and genomic integrity, positioning ARID1A as a promising therapeutic target for this malignancy.

title: Integrated bioinformatics and validation reveal SOX12 as potential biomarker in colon adenocarcinoma based on an immune infiltration-related ceRNA network
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: This study identifies SOX12 and H19 as key hub regulators with significant prognostic value in colon adenocarcinoma through a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) network analysis integrated with tumor-infiltrating immune cell profiling. Functional validation demonstrates that SOX12 knockdown suppresses proliferation, migration, and invasion in colon cancer cells, establishing it as an oncogenic driver. The elevated expression of both SOX12 and H19 was confirmed across multiple colorectal cancer cell lines, supporting their role as biomarkers. The research reveals a statistically significant relationship between these hub genes and tumor-infiltrating immune cells, suggesting that the ceRNA regulatory network mechanistically links cancer progression with immune infiltration patterns. By constructing ceRNA networks and utilizing CIBERSORT analysis to assess immune cell composition, the authors developed risk stratification models that substantiate SOX12 and H19 as potential biomarkers for colon adenocarcinoma diagnosis and prognosis. These findings highlight how integrating bioinformatics approaches with functional validation can illuminate molecular mechanisms driving both tumor development and immune microenvironment dynamics in colorectal cancer.

title: Clinical significance of determining the hypermethylation of the RUNX3 gene promoter and its cohypermethylation with the BRCA1 gene for patients with breast cancer
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: This study demonstrates that hypermethylation of the RUNX3 gene promoter is significantly associated with breast cancer, occurring in 71.6% of breast cancer tissue samples compared to 35.13% of control samples. The research reveals a strong cohypermethylation pattern between RUNX3 and BRCA1 genes in breast cancer tissues, with this combined methylation event markedly elevated compared to non-malignant controls. RUNX3 gene promoter hypermethylation is substantially higher in breast cancer patients than in control populations, indicating its clinical relevance as a potential biomarker. The coordinated hypermethylation of both RUNX3 and BRCA1 gene promoter regions appears to be a distinctive feature of breast cancer pathogenesis. These findings suggest that simultaneous assessment of RUNX3 and BRCA1 methylation status may provide enhanced diagnostic or prognostic value for identifying breast cancer patients, as the epigenetic silencing of these tumor-related genes through promoter hypermethylation represents an important molecular mechanism in breast cancer development.

title: Identification and validation of immune and diagnostic biomarkers for interstitial cystitis/painful bladder syndrome by integrating bioinformatics and machine-learning
summary:
# Summary

This study integrated bioinformatics and machine-learning approaches to identify immune and diagnostic biomarkers for interstitial cystitis/painful bladder syndrome (IC/BPS), revealing PLAC8, S100A8, and PPBP as three pivotal genes significantly upregulated in IC/BPS patients. PLAC8 emerged as a promising diagnostic biomarker that modulates immune responses and promotes urothelial cell proliferation while paradoxically inhibiting the AKT/mTOR/PI3K signaling pathway. IC/BPS pathology is characterized by extensive immune cell infiltration, with elevated levels of 23 distinct immune cell types including neutrophils, T cells, B cells, macrophages, and mast cells, alongside elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines such as CXCL-10. Pathway analysis identified key involvement of IL-17, AGE-RAGE, TGF-beta signaling, along with cell cycle and chemokine signaling pathways, while gene ontology revealed enrichment in extracellular matrix organization and collagen fibril organization, supporting the classification of IC/BPS as a chronic inflammatory disease driven by immune dysfunction. The findings suggest that PLAC8 expression is inducible by inflammatory stimuli like LPS and ATP, positioning it as both a biomarker for disease diagnosis and a potential therapeutic target in IC/BPS management.

title: Loss of ARID1A expression is associated with systemic inflammation markers and has important prognostic significance in gastric cancer
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: Loss of ARID1A expression, a tumor suppressor gene, is significantly associated with poor prognosis in gastric cancer through multiple interconnected pathways. ARID1A loss correlates with deficient mismatch repair (dMMR) status and elevated PD-L1 expression, molecular features linked to immunotherapy sensitivity. Low ARID1A expression is accompanied by aberrant systemic inflammatory markers and abnormal E-cadherin levels, indicating enhanced epithelial-mesenchymal transition and immune dysregulation. ARID1A protein expression emerged as an independent prognostic factor for gastric cancer survival in multivariate analysis, alongside established clinicopathological variables. Additionally, ARID1A loss associates with elevated platelet-lymphocyte ratio and decreased albumin levels, systemic inflammatory indicators with prognostic significance. These findings suggest ARID1A loss drives a pro-inflammatory, immunologically active phenotype in gastric cancer that warrants consideration in treatment selection and outcome prediction.

title: Single-cell sequencing uncovers the mechanistic role of DAPK1 in glioma and its diagnostic and prognostic implications
summary:
# Summary

This single-cell sequencing study identifies DAPK1 (Death-associated protein kinase 1) as a critical regulator of glioma progression and prognosis through its multifaceted control of apoptosis, cell cycle regulation, cell motility, and adhesion. DAPK1 functions as a tumor suppressor by phosphorylating key cell cycle regulators (p53, p21, Cdc25) and apoptotic mediators (Bcl-2 family members) while simultaneously modulating oncogenic signaling pathways including Wnt/β-catenin, NF-κB, and MAPK cascades. The study reveals that dysregulated DAPK1 expression correlates with poor prognosis in glioblastoma and associates with invasive tumor behavior, contrasting with its reduced expression in multiple other cancers (lung, breast, colon, gastric). Additionally, DAPK1 expression in tumor-associated macrophages influences the immunosuppressive microenvironment, as these macrophages promote tumor cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and metastasis through cytokine and growth factor release. These findings establish DAPK1 as a promising biomarker for glioma prognosis and a potential therapeutic target to enhance anti-tumor immunity and suppress glioma progression.

title: Plasma miRNAs in polycystic ovary syndrome drive endometrial cancer progression: insights into molecular pathways and therapeutic targets
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies dysregulated plasma exosomal microRNAs (miRNAs) in polycystic ovary syndrome as drivers of endometrial cancer progression. Twelve miRNAs including miR-483, miR-206, and miR-142 are upregulated in PCOS patients, while miR-331, miR-424, and let-7f are downregulated, with the latter group acting as protective factors associated with improved overall survival in endometrial cancer. The upregulated miRNAs form functional networks targeting genes involved in extracellular matrix organization and TGF-β signaling (SFRP4, APOD, CCL21, TWIST2, PAMR1, ECM1, CD248, WNT2), which paradoxically correlate with longer survival and promote apoptosis. Conversely, miR-330 elevation drives expression of mitotic and DNA replication genes (AURKA, NCAPG, YARS, RRM2, AUNIP, MTFR2) associated with shorter survival and nuclear division processes. These findings suggest that circulating miRNA signatures in PCOS represent a critical molecular link between metabolic dysfunction and endometrial malignancy, with distinct miRNA-mediated pathways controlling cancer progression through opposing effects on cell survival and proliferation.

title: Establishment of a prognostic signature and immune infiltration characteristics for uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma based on a disulfidptosis/ferroptosis-associated signature
summary: This study established a prognostic signature for uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma (UCEC) based on disulfidptosis and ferroptosis-related genes, identifying five key differentially expressed genes (CDKN2A, FZD7, LCN2, ACTN4, and MYH10) with high prognostic value. Disulfidptosis and ferroptosis are interconnected programmed cell death pathways sharing the common regulator SLC7A11, which inhibits ferroptosis when expressed but triggers disulfidptosis when overexpressed under glucose deprivation; conversely, SLC7A11 downregulation induces ferroptosis in endometrial cancer cells. The constructed risk model successfully stratified patients into high- and low-risk groups, with high-risk patients exhibiting significantly lower overall survival, more severe tumor stages, and higher expression of ACTN4 and LCN2. Additionally, high tumor mutational burden (TMB) associated with lower risk scores correlated with better survival outcomes, while pathway analysis revealed glycosphingolipid biosynthesis and extracellular interactions as key risk factors. The identified hsa-mir-4758 microRNA regulates multiple signature genes, providing additional therapeutic targets for endometrial cancer management.

title: Development and Validation of Prognostic Characteristics Associated With Chromatin Remodeling‐Related Genes in Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

Chromatin remodeling-related genes (CRRGs) regulate epigenetic processes and play pivotal roles in ovarian cancer development through alterations in chromatin structure and gene expression. The study identified seven prognostic CRRGs—ARID1B, ATRX, CHRAC1, HDAC1, INO80, MBD2, and SS18—whose expression patterns significantly stratify ovarian cancer patients into risk groups. High expression of ARID1B, HDAC1, and INO80 associates with poor prognosis and increased mortality, while elevated ATRX, CHRAC1, MBD2, and SS18 expression correlates with favorable outcomes and potential immunotherapy responsiveness. High-risk patients exhibit activation of cancer-promoting pathways including spindle mitosis and hedgehog signaling alongside suppression of oxidative phosphorylation and interferon responses, accompanied by elevated Type 2 helper cells and CD56dim natural killer cells. The derived risk score emerged as an independent predictor of overall survival, establishing chromatin remodeling genes as biomarkers for prognosis and potential therapeutic intervention targets in ovarian cancer.

title: Transcriptomic signatures of severe acute mountain sickness during rapid ascent to 4,300 m
summary:
# Summary

This transcriptomic study identified distinct molecular signatures distinguishing individuals susceptible to acute mountain sickness (AMS) during rapid ascent to 4,300 m. In AMS-susceptible individuals at high altitude, hemoglobin-related genes (HBA1, HBA2, HBB) and phosphodiesterase 5A (PDE5A) were significantly elevated, enhancing oxygen affinity for hemoglobin and activating G-protein coupled receptor signaling pathways. Concurrently, AMS-susceptible individuals exhibited dysregulated immune responses characterized by downregulation of protective genes (HLA-DQB1, NFκB2, STAT2, FOLR3, DRAXIN) and upregulation of IL1R2, which negatively regulates inflammatory signaling. The cAMP response element-binding protein (CREB) pathway and hypoxia-inducible factor signaling (HIF2α, ENGL1) emerged as critical mediators of hypoxic adaptation and AMS susceptibility. At sea level, AMS-susceptible individuals showed suppressed immune and neuroinflammatory pathways, but these switched to activation upon high-altitude exposure alongside enhanced macrophage activation and phagosome formation. These findings suggest that AMS susceptibility reflects an aberrant transcriptional response to hypoxia involving exaggerated hemoglobin compensation, dysregulated immune signaling, and altered inflammatory pathway activation.

title: Impaired Molecular Mechanisms Contributing to Chronic Pain in Patients with Hidradenitis Suppurativa: Exploring Potential Biomarkers and Therapeutic Targets
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies impaired epigenetic and molecular mechanisms underlying chronic pain in hidradenitis suppurativa (HS), a chronic inflammatory skin condition involving immune dysfunction and scar tissue formation. DNA methylation patterns are significantly altered in HS patients, with 253 differentially methylated CpG sites identified across genes regulating pain sensitization, including ABCC2, SLC39A8, and SLC39A9, as well as cytokines like TGFB1 and IL4. Multiple gene networks contribute to pain pathophysiology through distinct mechanisms: the endorphin system (BDNF, OPRM1), serotonin signaling (HTR receptors, TPH2), ion channel regulation (CACNA1C, SCN1A), wound healing (FGF2, PDGFC), and hormonal regulation via estrogen receptor beta (ESR2), which influences both inflammation and pain perception. BDNF emerges as a proposed biomarker of nociception in chronic pain, while dysregulated autophagy (involving ULK4) contributes to chronic inflammation and pain persistence. The study suggests that vitamin B12 supplementation and targeting PRKCA-mediated pain modulation through ion channel regulation represent potential therapeutic approaches for managing HS-associated pain.

title: Genome-wide profiling of circulating microRNAs in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis and their relation to spinal deformity severity, and disease pathophysiology
summary:
# Summary

This genome-wide profiling study identified a panel of six circulating microRNAs—miR-1-3p, miR-19a-3p, miR-19b-3p, miR-133b, miR-143-3p, and miR-148b-3p—that are upregulated in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) and correlate with severe spinal deformity. These miRNAs regulate key genes involved in skeletal development and muscle function, including fibronectin 1 (FN1), NOTCH2, DUSP6, and MAPK7, which are implicated in AIS pathogenesis through disruption of MAPK signaling and musculoskeletal homeostasis. The study revealed sex-specific patterns in miRNA dysregulation, with miR-18a-3p elevated in female progressors and miR-103a-3p and miR-107 downregulated in male non-progressors, indicating distinct disease progression mechanisms between sexes. Additionally, miR-551b-3p and miR-4665-5p predicted moderate spinal deformity progression specifically in males, suggesting these biomarkers could enable personalized risk stratification and improved clinical management of AIS.

title: Gene expression changes in blastocyst hatching affect embryo implantation success in mice
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Blastocyst hatching is essential for embryo implantation success and involves dynamic transcriptional regulation coordinated by steroid hormones, growth factors, and cytokines, with ion transporters and proteases facilitating zona pellucida hydrolysis. During hatching, immune-related genes including Ptgs1, Lyz2, Il-α, Cfb, and C3 are upregulated while Cd36 is downregulated, promoting embryo-uterine crosstalk through localization of C3 and IL-1β on the trophectoderm surface. Blastocysts hatching at favorable sites (A and B sites) demonstrate distinct immune gene expression profiles controlled by transcription factors TCF24 and DLX3, with elevated expression of Lyz2, Ptgs1, Il-1β, Cfb, and C3 positively correlating with higher birth rates. In contrast, blastocysts from poor fertility sites (C-site and non-hatching) show dysregulated immune gene expression with reduced Lyz2 and IL-1β levels, indicating impaired maternal-fetal interactions. Gene ontology analysis reveals that blastocyst hatching involves coordinated regulation of cell differentiation, proliferation, and apoptotic processes, suggesting that transcriptional changes during preimplantation development fundamentally determine implantation competence and reproductive success.

title: Validation of loci and genes associated with fertility in Holstein cows using gene-set enrichment analysis-SNP and genotype-by-sequencing
summary:
# Summary

This genomic validation study identified key molecular pathways and genes associated with reproductive fertility in Holstein cattle using gene-set enrichment analysis. Actin cytoskeleton organization genes, particularly twinfilin actin binding protein 1 (TWF1), RAC1, scinderin, and villin 1, emerged as the most frequently implicated genes across 58-59 enriched gene sets, directly influencing both conception rate to first breeding and breeding number requirements. Multiple interconnected pathways controlling cellular architecture—including actin polymerization, protein complex assembly, mitotic centrosome organization, and cilium morphogenesis—showed strong enrichment signatures for conception success, indicating that proper cellular structural maintenance is essential for reproductive competence. Additionally, cell cycle regulation, RNA splicing, and gap junction signaling pathways demonstrated significant associations with conception outcomes. These findings validate that bovine fertility depends critically on coordinated actin dynamics and cellular assembly processes, suggesting that disruptions in cytoskeletal organization and protein complex biogenesis may compromise reproductive success.

title: Systematic review on the DNA methylation role in endometriosis: current evidence and perspectives
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review establishes DNA methylation as a critical epigenetic mechanism in endometriosis pathogenesis, with genetic and epigenetic factors each contributing approximately 50% to disease etiology. DNA methylation, which typically induces silent chromatin when localized to regulatory regions, appears integral to endometriosis onset and progression, with oxidative stress contributing to disease advancement through epigenetic-mediated pathways. Key genes including HOXA10 and PGR show altered methylation patterns in endometriotic tissues, with HOXA10 exhibiting hypermethylation in eutopic endometrium, while multiple genes involved in cell proliferation, PI3K-Akt signaling, Wnt signaling, MAPK pathways, cell adhesion, apoptosis, immunity, and neurogenesis display polyepigenetic alterations. The steroid hormone receptors ESR1 and ESR2, which are epigenetically regulated, along with genes like COX-2 and TWIST1 showing hypomethylation patterns, contribute to the complex molecular landscape of endometrial dysfunction characterized by altered angiogenesis, proliferation, and immune responses. The authors advocate for identifying DNA methylation biomarkers to address the significant diagnostic delay currently affecting up to 10-15% of women of reproductive age, which would enable better understanding of disease mechanisms and development of targeted therapeutic interventions.

title: Prognostic implications of ERLncRNAs in ccRCC: a novel risk score model and its association with tumor mutation burden and immune microenvironment
summary:
# Summary

This study develops a novel prognostic risk score model based on efferocytosis-related long noncoding RNAs (ERLncRNAs) to predict outcomes in clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC). Seven key ERLncRNAs (LINC01615, RUNX3-AS1, FOXD2-AS1, AC002070.1, LINC02747, LINC00944, and AC092296.1) were identified and validated as independent prognostic indicators that stratify patients into high-risk and low-risk groups with significantly different survival rates. Efferocytosis—the phagocytic clearance of apoptotic cells by macrophages and dendritic cells through phosphatidylserine signaling—promotes tumor progression by modulating the immune microenvironment, recruiting regulatory T cells that suppress effector T cell activity, and upregulating immunosuppressive molecules like PD-L1 via MerTk signaling. The ERLncRNA signature correlates with tumor mutational burden, immune cell infiltration patterns, and immune checkpoint expression, providing independent predictive value for immunotherapy response. Comprehensive bioinformatic validation using multiple algorithms (CiberSort, TIMER, ssGSEA) and pathway analysis revealed that these lncRNAs regulate critical oncogenic pathways including VHL/HIF/VEGF, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, and Hippo signaling. This ERLncRNA-based model offers potential for improved prognostic assessment and personalized immunotherapeutic stratification in ccRCC patients.

title: Estrogen Promotes Endometrial Cancer Development by Modulating ZNF626, SLK, and RFWD3 Gene Expression and Inducing Immune Inflammatory Changes
summary:
# Summary

Elevated estrogen drives endometrial cancer development through coordinated regulation of three key genes: upregulation of ZNF626 and SLK, and downregulation of RFWD3. Estradiol stimulation promotes cancer cell proliferation and suppresses apoptosis by modulating these genes, with prolonged exposure increasing DNA replication errors and endometrial cell mitosis. The altered gene expression pattern shifts the tumor immune microenvironment toward immunosuppression, with ZNF626 correlating positively with pro-tumoral M2 macrophages while SLK inversely correlates with anti-tumoral immune cells including M1 macrophages and memory B cells. Mechanistically, the upregulated genes participate in complement activation and cell cycle pathways, while RFWD3 loss impairs DNA damage checkpoints. The selective estrogen receptor modulator arzoxifene reverses these effects by downregulating ZNF626 and SLK while restoring RFWD3, thereby inhibiting proliferation and promoting apoptosis, suggesting therapeutic potential for endometrial cancer treatment.

title: MiR-451 in Inflammatory Diseases: Molecular Mechanisms, Biomarkers, and Therapeutic Applications—A Comprehensive Review Beyond Oncology
summary: MiR-451, identified on chromosome 17q11.2, emerges as a critical regulator of inflammatory responses and disease progression across multiple pathological conditions through its modulation of key signaling pathways including NF-κB, AMPK, and PI3K. The microRNA exhibits distinct tissue-specific and disease-specific expression patterns: it is downregulated in plasma but upregulated in lesion tissues in endometriosis, upregulated in systemic inflammatory diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis and systemic lupus erythematosus, and downregulated in chronic cardiovascular conditions like heart failure. MiR-451 modulates inflammatory cell populations by suppressing neutrophil chemotaxis and influencing macrophage polarization and microglia activation, while also functioning in hematopoietic differentiation and erythropoiesis, particularly through miR-451a's unique Dicer-independent, Ago2-mediated maturation. The microRNA's therapeutic potential is supported by its detection across multiple biomarker sources including plasma, lesion tissues, urinary exosomes, and peripheral monocytes, and its capacity to suppress pathological processes such as ischemia-reperfusion injury through targeting mechanisms involving HMGB1 and CELF2. These findings position miR-451 as a promising diagnostic and therapeutic target for endometriosis and related inflammatory diseases.

title: Identification of AKNA Gene and Its Role for Genetic Susceptibility in Epithelial Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies AKNA, an AT-Hook transcriptional factor encoded on chromosome 9q32, as a key genetic susceptibility factor for epithelial ovarian cancer with pleiotropic effects on immune regulation and EMT control. The AKNA rs10817595 promoter variant (−1372 C>A), particularly the AA homozygous genotype, reduces both mRNA and protein expression levels and is associated with increased ovarian cancer risk in both low-grade and high-grade tumors. AKNA functions as a multi-faceted immune regulator: it enhances anti-tumor immunity through CD154-CD40 interactions, suppresses pro-inflammatory genes including MMP-9 and interferon-1β, and regulates critical pathways (JAK-STAT, NF-κB, chemokine signaling) that coordinate immune cell activation and cytokine production. Mechanistically, reduced AKNA expression impairs E-cadherin-mediated cell adhesion and fails to suppress uncontrolled EMT, hallmarks of cancer progression and poor prognosis. The pleiotropic nature of AKNA variants is evident from their association with multiple conditions including cervical cancer, primary Sjögren's syndrome, and osteoarthritis, establishing AKNA as a critical node integrating inflammatory, immunological, and epithelial integrity pathways relevant to endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer.

title: A novel telomere-associated genes signature for the prediction of prognosis and treatment responsiveness of hepatocellular carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a novel prognostic signature based on eighteen telomere-related genes (TRGs) to predict hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) prognosis and treatment responsiveness. Telomere-associated genes are deeply involved in HCC tumorigenesis, with telomere shortening contributing to genomic instability that increases cancer risk, though the relationship remains complex as shortened telomeres can paradoxically inhibit tumor formation. The authors constructed a risk stratification model incorporating key genes such as ASF1A, MMP1, and ACADS that significantly outperformed traditional clinical factors in predictive accuracy (AUC = 0.801), with low-risk patients exhibiting substantially better overall survival than high-risk patients. Analysis of the HCC tumor microenvironment revealed that high-risk patients displayed elevated tumor immune dysfunction and exclusion alongside reduced proportions of protective C3 and C4 immune subtypes. Mendelian randomization analysis established a causal relationship between ASF1A overexpression and alcoholic HCC development, identifying it as a key driver of disease in this subtype. This telomere-associated risk model provides a robust tool for HCC patient stratification and may inform personalized treatment strategies based on immune landscape profiling.

title: Integrating Bioinformatics and Experimental Validation to Identify Mitochondrial Permeability Transition-Driven Necrosis-Related lncRNAs that can Serve as Prognostic Biomarkers and Therapeutic Targets in Endometrial Carcinoma
summary: This study identifies mitochondrial permeability transition (MPT)-driven necrosis-related long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) as prognostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets in endometrial carcinoma. The researchers developed a nine-lncRNA risk model derived from 4,092 MPT-driven necrosis-related lncRNAs co-expressed with 31 necrosis-related genes, which independently predicts clinical outcomes and overall survival in endometrial carcinoma patients. High-risk patients demonstrated poor prognosis, immune evasion, and activation of oncogenic pathways including cell cycle and Hedgehog signaling, while the gene OGFRP1 plays a carcinogenic role by affecting mitochondrial membrane permeability. Conversely, low-risk patients showed enhanced immune signatures with elevated CD8+ T cells and chemokine/cytokine signaling, along with higher mutation burdens in tumor suppressor genes. The risk model also predicts differential drug sensitivity, with high-risk patients showing lower IC50 values to drugs like paclitaxel and midostaurin, while low-risk patients respond better to axitinib and docetaxel. These findings establish MPT-driven necrosis-related lncRNAs as valuable biomarkers for stratifying endometrial carcinoma patients and guiding personalized therapeutic strategies.

title: The Effect of Endometrial Polyp and Myoma Uteri on Fertility-Related Genes in the Endometrium
summary:
# Summary

This study examines how endometrial polyps and uterine fibroids (myoma uteri) alter the expression of fertility-critical genes in the endometrium. Both pathologies significantly disrupt the prokineticin signaling pathway and HOXA10 expression, which are essential for endometrial receptivity, angiogenesis, and implantation. In myoma uteri patients, PROKR1 expression dramatically increased (5.42-fold) while PROK1, PROKR2, and HOXA10 were substantially reduced, suggesting impaired vascular remodeling and endometrial preparation for pregnancy. Endometrial polyps showed elevated PROKR1 (8.66-fold) and decreased PROKR2 expression, though HOXA10 reduction was not statistically significant. These gene expression alterations in both conditions are associated with reduced fertility and may explain the high prevalence of these lesions in women with unexplained infertility, identifying potential molecular targets for improving reproductive outcomes.

title: Comparison of chromatin accessibility remodeling of granulosa cells in patients with endometrioma or pelvic/tubal infertility
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study reveals that endometrioma-associated epigenetic alterations in granulosa cells impair oogenesis through chromatin remodeling defects. Using ATAC-seq and RNA-seq, the researchers identified significant differences in chromatin accessibility and gene expression between granulosa cells from endometrioma patients and controls, with over 6,200 genes downregulated in the endometrioma group. Multi-omics analysis identified 22 significantly downregulated genes including PPIF and VEGFA, both of which correlated positively with oocyte retrieval numbers. The chromatin remodeling in granulosa cells disrupts both granulosa cell and extracellular vesicle transcriptomes, thereby impairing somatic cell-oocyte communication and compromising overall oogenesis. These epigenetic alterations, particularly affecting cellular localization pathways, represent a molecular mechanism linking endometrioma to the reproductive dysfunction observed in affected patients.

title: YAP1 and WWTR1 are required for murine pregnancy initiation
summary:
# Summary

YAP1 and WWTR1, effectors of the Hippo signaling pathway, are essential for successful pregnancy initiation in mice through their role in endometrial stromal cell decidualization and maternal preparation. Female mice carrying mutations in both Yap1 and Wwtr1 exhibit subfertility, compromised decidualization responses, decreased endometrial receptivity, and delayed embryonic development, with defects spanning from blunted expression of decidualization genes (Wnt4 and Bmp2) to reduced estrogen receptor signaling in the uterus. At the molecular level, YAP1 and WWTR1 translocate to the nucleus where they bind TEADs to regulate transcription of genes encoding extracellular and cytoplasmic matrix components, with their expression dynamically regulated throughout the estrous cycle and induced by 17β-estradiol. Transcriptomic analyses revealed that YAP1/WWTR1 dysfunction causes aberrant maternal remodeling and alters expression of genes associated with fertility-compromising pathologies including endometriosis and pre-eclampsia, affecting pathways such as integrin signaling and oxytocin receptor signaling. These findings establish YAP1 and WWTR1 as critical mediators linking estrogen signaling to Hippo pathway activation, ultimately controlling the endometrial changes necessary for successful embryo implantation and pregnancy establishment.

title: Loss of SMARCA4 induces sarcomatogenesis through epithelial–mesenchymal transition in ovarian carcinosarcoma
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that loss of SMARCA4, a SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex component, drives sarcomatogenesis in ovarian carcinosarcoma through epithelial–mesenchymal transition (EMT). SMARCA4 loss is specifically observed in the sarcomatous component of serous carcinosarcomas, while ARID1A loss predominates in endometrioid cases. Loss of SMARCA4 function triggers EMT by reducing E-cadherin expression while upregulating mesenchymal markers (vimentin, ZEB1, SNAI1, TGFβI), a process mediated by increased YAP1 expression and activation of the TGFβ and Hippo signaling pathways. In SWI/SNF-retained tumors, YAP1-dependent mechanisms promote sarcomatogenesis, whereas in SWI/SNF-reduced tumors, elevated TGFβI expression drives sarcomatoid differentiation. The study identifies EMT as a critical mechanism conferring therapeutic resistance in carcinosarcomas and suggests that the Hippo signaling pathway represents a potential therapeutic target for managing SWI/SNF-deficient ovarian carcinosarcomas.

title: Azoospermia/Oligozoospermia and Prostate Cancer Are Increased in Families of Women With Primary Ovarian Insufficiency
summary: Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) and nonobstructive azoospermia/oligozoospermia (NOA) share common genetic pathways converging on meiosis I genes involved in homologous recombination, including BRCA2, FANCA, and RECQL4. Families of women with POI show significantly elevated risks of severe male spermatogenic impairment (relative risk 2.8) in first-degree relatives and substantially increased prostate cancer incidence in both first- and second-degree male relatives (relative risk 3.5). The genetic overlap between POI and NOA is further evidenced by shared chromosomal abnormalities including X chromosome deletions and translocations, as well as mutations in DNA repair genes associated with both conditions. These findings indicate that genetic variants predisposing to female reproductive failure through meiotic dysfunction also increase male cancer susceptibility, suggesting a unified biological mechanism linking gonadal development, gametogenesis, and cancer predisposition. The study highlights the importance of recognizing familial clustering of these conditions and considering shared genetic contributions when evaluating cancer risk in relatives of POI patients.

title: Pan-cancer characterization of C–C motif chemokine ligand 5 (CCL5) identifies its role as biomarker and therapeutic target
summary:
# Summary: CCL5 as a Pan-Cancer Biomarker and Therapeutic Target

This pan-cancer analysis identifies C–C motif chemokine ligand 5 (CCL5) as a significantly elevated and prognostically important molecule across multiple cancer types, particularly renal cell carcinoma (KIRC) and esophageal cancer (ESCA). CCL5 promoter hypomethylation drives elevated expression and correlates with poorer overall and disease-specific survival independent of genetic mutations, establishing it as an epigenetically regulated biomarker. Mechanistically, CCL5 enhances tumor progression through dual pathways: direct activation of PI3K/AKT and MAPK/ERK signaling to promote cell proliferation and survival, and recruitment of immunosuppressive cells including tumor-associated macrophages and regulatory T cells while upregulating matrix metalloproteinases to facilitate invasion and metastasis. Additionally, CCL5 promotes angiogenesis by upregulating vascular endothelial growth factor, creating a permissive tumor microenvironment. Experimental knockdown of CCL5 substantially reduces cancer cell proliferation, colony formation, and migration, demonstrating its functional importance. These findings collectively position CCL5 as both a robust diagnostic indicator of tumor presence and a high-priority therapeutic target across multiple cancer types.

title: Methylation of ESRα Promoters in Benign Breast Tumors Could Be a Signature for Progression to Breast Cancer in African American Women
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies DNA methylation of estrogen receptor alpha (ESRα) promoters as an epigenetic signature predicting progression from benign breast lesions to cancer, with particular relevance for African American women. ESRα promoter hypermethylation, particularly at the P1 promoter region normally dominant in breast tissue, silences ESRα expression through CpG island methylation and correlates with loss of the estrogen receptor protein. Benign tumors such as fibroadenomas show elevated methylation of ESRα and tumor suppressor genes (RASSF1A, HIN-1) that is even higher than in some ER-negative cancers, suggesting these lesions carry malignant potential. The two ESRα promoters have distinct roles in carcinogenesis: P0 methylation associates with early-stage disease while P1 methylation occurs in later stages with poor prognosis. African American women display significantly higher frequencies of methylation in multiple cancer-related genes including HIN-1, RASSF1A, and others, and show elevated ESRα-negative phenotypes and worse outcomes. These findings support using ESRα and associated gene methylation patterns as biomarkers to identify benign breast lesions at high risk for malignant transformation, offering potential for improved cancer prevention strategies.

title: Genome-wide meta-analysis identifies novel risk loci for uterine fibroids within and across multiple ancestry groups
summary:
# Genome-Wide Meta-Analysis of Uterine Fibroids Risk Loci

This genome-wide meta-analysis identifies novel and established genetic risk loci for uterine fibroids across multiple ancestry groups, revealing significant associations with genes including TP53, SYNE1, SIRT3, PSMD13, and FOXO3, among many others. Key findings show that genetic variants associate with fibroids through pathways enriched for cancer, cell death and survival, and cellular proliferation processes, with specific involvement of DNA damage response signaling, replicative senescence, and telomere maintenance regulation. Gene expression analysis identified uterine tissue expression of HEATR3 and tissue-specific associations with ACTRT3 (pituitary), WNT4 (lung), and NQO1 (esophageal mucosa) as particularly significant contributors to fibroid risk. The study identified eleven novel associations in European ancestry populations, including SULT1E1, LRRC34, ATP1B2, and others, demonstrating ancestry-specific genetic contributions. Clinically, fibroids associate with heavy menstrual bleeding, abnormal menstrual bleeding, and pelvic pain, with established risk factors including early menarche, obesity, and premenopausal age, while parity provides protection. Notably, Black individuals have substantially higher cumulative incidence of fibroids (over 80% by age 50) compared to White individuals (nearly 70%), underscoring the importance of multi-ancestry analysis for understanding genetic predisposition.

title: The breadth and impact of the Global Lipids Genetics Consortium
summary: The Global Lipids Genetics Consortium has identified over 923 genomic loci associated with major blood lipid traits, including total cholesterol, LDL cholesterol, HDL cholesterol, triglycerides, and non-HDL cholesterol, substantially advancing understanding of the genetic architecture of lipid metabolism. Through genome-wide association studies, the consortium has mapped genetic variants that regulate lipid and lipoprotein biology with increasing precision. Notably, incorporating greater population diversity into these studies has enhanced variant discovery, improved fine-mapping of causal loci, and strengthened the predictive accuracy of polygenic scores for blood lipid levels. The publicly available GWAS summary statistics generated by the consortium have enabled broader exploration of lipid-related genetic influences beyond cardiovascular disease, extending into associations with noncardiovascular conditions. These findings underscore the critical importance of continued large-scale genetic collaboration in characterizing the complex genetic basis of lipid biology and its pleiotropic effects across human disease phenotypes.

title: Prognostic risk modeling of endometrial cancer using programmed cell death-related genes: a comprehensive machine learning approach
summary: This study developed a machine learning prognostic model for endometrial cancer using programmed cell death (PCD)-related genes, identifying ten critical genes (PTGIS, TIMP3, SRPX, SNCA, HIC1, BAK1, STXBP2, TRIB3, RTKN2, and E2F1) that predict patient risk stratification. PCD dysregulation drives both tumor development and cancer progression through mechanisms involving immune cell infiltration and checkpoint expression, with epigenetic modifications including DNA methylation and histone modifications controlling PCD-related gene expression. The StepCox[forward] + plsRcox algorithm constructed a risk score model that correlated strongly with clinical characteristics, immune cell infiltration patterns, and tumor stage, with significantly elevated risk scores in patients aged over 60 years and those with advanced disease (stages III-IV). Functional pathway analysis revealed enrichment in apoptotic signaling, autophagy regulation, mitochondrial organization, proteoglycans in cancer, and calcium signaling pathways. The integrated prognostic model achieved superior performance and demonstrated that PCD-related gene expression can predict therapeutic sensitivity, offering a comprehensive approach to patient stratification and treatment selection in endometrial cancer.

title: “Incidentally” discovered Von Hippel Lindau disease: an emerging clinical phenotype
summary:
# Summary

This case series describes an emerging clinical phenotype of incidentally discovered Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease in six patients identified through multigene panel testing without classical VHL disease presentations. Pathogenic germline variants in the VHL gene were identified in all patients, with three distinct variants detected: c.562C>G (p.L188V) in patients 1, 2, and 4; c.460C>T (p.P154S) in patient 3; and c.598C>T (p.R200W) in patients 5 and 6. Notably, two patients (1 and 6) had histories of endometriosis requiring hysterectomy, and the c.598C>T variant was specifically linked to Chuvash polycythemia with associated risks of pulmonary hypertension and thrombotic episodes. The high penetrance of VHL disease (nearly 97%) and its variable expression across gender, age, genetic background, and environmental factors highlight the importance of comprehensive surveillance in asymptomatic carriers identified through expanded genetic screening programs, particularly when multiple genetic variants are present in a single individual.

title: Analysis of cellular senescence-related genes in calcified aortic valve disease and the potential therapeutic role of β-Carotene
summary: This study identifies cellular senescence as a critical mechanism in calcific aortic valve disease (CAVD) and reveals a network of senescence-related differentially expressed genes (CS-DEGs), including MMP9, HIF1A, KLF4, MMP7, AR, FGFR3, CTSB, and others, that regulate collagen metabolism and membrane lipid distribution in diseased aortic valves. Senescent cells promote CAVD progression through multiple pathways, including secretion of inflammatory factors, extracellular matrix-degrading enzymes, and senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) factors that disrupt normal tissue homeostasis. The study demonstrates that β-carotene, a natural antioxidant, reduces oxidative stress and mitigates cellular senescence by interacting with key CS-DEGs (MMP9, MEIS2, and CTSB), while also inhibiting inflammation through NF-κB downregulation and reducing mitochondrial oxidative stress. Eight CS-DEGs including MMP9, HIF1A, and KLF4 are regulated by microRNAs, suggesting an additional layer of molecular control in CAVD pathogenesis. These findings establish β-carotene as a potential therapeutic agent for CAVD by targeting senescence-driven valve degeneration through its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

title: Big data analysis and machine learning of the role of cuproptosis-related long non-coding RNAs (CuLncs) in the prognosis and immune landscape of ovarian cancer
summary: This study identified cuproptosis-related long non-coding RNAs (CuLncs) as prognostic biomarkers in ovarian cancer by analyzing 248 CuLncs associated with 16 copper death-related genes. Cuproptosis, a copper ion-triggered cell death mechanism involving mitochondrial protein clumping and iron-sulfur cluster disruption, showed therapeutic potential when regulated by specific lncRNAs like WARS2-AS1 and MKLN1-AS. LINC01956 emerged as a key oncogenic CuLnc that promotes ovarian cancer cell proliferation, invasion, and migration, with high expression correlating with worse prognosis and increased hazard ratio, while LINC01956 knockdown significantly suppressed these malignant phenotypes in A2780 and SKOV3 cells. The developed risk score model demonstrated strong predictive power for 1-, 3-, and 5-year patient survival and outperformed traditional clinicopathological staging. High-risk patients exhibited distinct immune alterations including elevated Type I interferon and MHC I expression but paradoxically lower Type I interferon expression, along with higher immune evasion scores (TIDE), suggesting potential resistance to immunotherapy. Notably, high-risk patients showed enhanced sensitivity to drugs including JQ12, PD-0325901, and sorafenib, offering therapeutic targets for precision medicine approaches in ovarian cancer management.

title: Advancing sepsis diagnosis and immunotherapy machine learning-driven identification of stable molecular biomarkers and therapeutic targets
summary:
# Summary

This study employed machine learning algorithms to identify stable molecular biomarkers and therapeutic targets in sepsis by analyzing immune-related genes (IIRGs) and their associated pathways. Sepsis, which arises from chaotic immune responses leading to excessive immune activation and widespread inflammation, was found to be associated with dysregulated IIRGs that participate in key signaling pathways including JAK-STAT, B cell receptor signaling, and T cell receptor signaling. Multiple machine learning approaches (Random Forest, XGBoost, LASSO regression, and Boruta algorithm) identified critical predictive genes such as MAPK14, IL10, JAK2, and SOCS3 that correlate with immune cell infiltration patterns and anticancer drug sensitivity. Notably, sepsis patients exhibited reduced immune scores and ESTIMATE scores alongside elevated stromal content, with characteristic shifts in immune cell composition including increased neutrophils, regulatory T cells, and macrophages M0, while showing decreased populations of resting T cells and naive B cells compared to healthy individuals. These findings provide a comprehensive molecular framework for sepsis diagnosis and identify potential immunotherapeutic targets based on pathway-specific immune dysregulation.

title: Association of TAB2 gene polymorphism with endometrial cancer susceptibility and clinical analysis
journal: Turkish Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Article Summary

This study investigates TAB2 gene polymorphisms as genetic markers for endometrial cancer (EC) susceptibility, focusing on the inflammatory and hormonal mechanisms underlying EC development. The TAB2 protein functions as a critical scaffold in inflammatory signaling by linking TRAF6 and TAK1, which activate downstream nuclear factor-κB and mitogen-activated protein kinase pathways—cascades fundamental to inflammation and cancer progression. The rs521845 polymorphism showed significant association with EC risk, with the T allele conferring protective effects and the TT genotype correlating with lower EC risk, while the rs237028 AG genotype associated with highly differentiated tumors. Beyond inflammation, TAB2 interacts with estrogen receptors and may potentiate antiestrogen action, suggesting dual roles in both inflammatory and hormonal pathways driving EC, which typically arises from persistent estrogen stimulation without progesterone antagonism. These findings position the TAB2 gene as a potential novel genetic marker for EC susceptibility and highlight its multifaceted involvement in EC molecular pathogenesis through both inflammatory and estrogenic mechanisms.

title: Association study of interleukin-10 and P53 polymorphisms and their influence on Iranian women with recurrent abortion
journal: Turkish Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

This association study examined how IL-10 and P53 gene polymorphisms influence recurrent spontaneous abortion (RSA) in Iranian women. IL-10 promotes successful pregnancy by enhancing Th2 immune responses while suppressing Th1 cytokines, with diminished IL-10 levels linked to recurrent fetal loss; specific promoter polymorphisms including -1082(A/G), -819(C/T), and -592(C/A) were associated with increased abortion risk. The P53 gene regulates critical pregnancy processes including vasculogenesis, cell apoptosis, and trophoblast invasion, with elevated P53 expression and apoptosis observed in placental tissues of women experiencing RSA; the P53 p.Pro72Arg polymorphism notably affects this pathway through its influence on leukemia inhibitory factor expression and apoptosis rates. The study's key finding was that the combined CC homozygous genotypes for both P53 rs1042522 and IL-10 rs1800871 polymorphisms significantly increased spontaneous abortion risk, particularly in consanguineous marriages. These results suggest that genetic variations in IL-10 and P53 genes may serve as molecular markers for RSA susceptibility through dysregulated immune tolerance and excessive placental apoptosis.

title: Serum HSF1 is upregulated in endometriosis patients and serves as a potential diagnostic biomarker
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This study identifies Heat Shock Factor 1 (HSF1) as an upregulated biomarker in endometriosis (EMS) patients, with serum levels significantly elevated in advanced disease (ASRM stage III/IV) and demonstrating good diagnostic accuracy (AUC: 0.857). HSF1, a master regulator of the heat shock response with known roles in cell survival and tumorigenesis, appears to facilitate endometrial pathology by controlling downstream targets including PTGES3 and HSP90AA1 (downregulated in EMS) and HSPB1 (upregulated in EMS). Elevated serum HSF1 is identified as an independent risk factor for both EMS diagnosis and disease severity, correlating with clinical symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and pelvic pain. The findings suggest that HSF1-mediated regulation of heat shock protein pathways may be central to endometriosis pathogenesis, potentially linking the condition's molecular mechanisms to cellular stress responses and cancer-related pathways. Serum HSF1 thus represents a promising minimally invasive diagnostic biomarker that could complement traditional clinical assessment and CA125 measurement in endometriosis evaluation.

title: LncRNA GAS5 inhibits the proliferation and invasion of ovarian clear cell carcinoma via the miR‐31‐5p/ARID1A axis
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This study demonstrates that long non-coding RNA GAS5 functions as a tumor suppressor in ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) by acting as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) that sponges miR-31-5p, thereby preventing miR-31-5p from suppressing ARID1A expression. GAS5 and ARID1A are significantly reduced while miR-31-5p is markedly elevated in OCCC tissues and cell lines, with GAS5 and ARID1A showing positive correlation and both inversely correlating with miR-31-5p levels. Functional studies confirm that GAS5 overexpression or miR-31-5p inhibition suppresses OCCC cell viability, colony formation, and invasion while upregulating ARID1A, whereas miR-31-5p overexpression reverses these protective effects. Dual-luciferase reporter assays validate direct binding interactions between GAS5-miR-31-5p and miR-31-5p-ARID1A, establishing the GAS5/miR-31-5p/ARID1A regulatory axis as a critical pathway in OCCC pathogenesis. Clinically, patients with higher GAS5 or ARID1A expression and lower miR-31-5p levels demonstrate significantly improved five-year overall survival, identifying these molecules as potential prognostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets for OCCC management.

title: Shared Pathophysiological Mechanisms and Genetic Factors in Early Menarche and Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary: Early age at menarche and polycystic ovary syndrome share common pathophysiological mechanisms involving dysregulation of neuroendocrine pathways that control pulsatile gonadotropin-releasing hormone secretion, which subsequently affects gonadotropin and luteinizing hormone release. Both conditions are additionally characterized by positive energy balance and abnormal metabolic status, suggesting overlapping metabolic dysfunction in their etiology. Genome-wide association studies have identified shared genetic variants associated with both disorders, particularly in genes such as FSHB and FTO, with FTO also linked to obesity susceptibility. High-throughput sequencing has revealed rare loss-of-function variants in the DLK1 gene in women presenting with central precocious puberty, early menarche, and polycystic ovary syndrome, all associated with adverse metabolic outcomes. Broader genetic and epigenetic factors regulate both the timing of puberty onset and reproductive outcomes, suggesting that early menarche may represent an early manifestation of the same underlying genetic predisposition that drives polycystic ovary syndrome development.

title: Proteogenomic characterization of molecular and cellular targets for treatment-resistant subtypes in locally advanced cervical cancers
summary: This proteogenomic study identifies distinct molecular subtypes in locally advanced cervical cancers (LACC) with specific drivers of treatment resistance. CIC mutations trigger epithelial-mesenchymal transition through ETV4 upregulation, while immune suppression is driven by elevated checkpoint molecules PVR, FCN1, and FCGR3B, alongside extracellular matrix activation mediated by PDGFD and CXCL1-expressing fibroblasts. Three treatment-resistant subtypes (Sub3, Sub5, Sub6) share BPTF mutations and display distinct molecular signatures: Sub3 exhibits elevated MUC5AC, Sub5 is characterized by EMT and ECM remodeling with increased PGK1 and CXCL10, while all three show poor recurrence-free survival. At the genetic level, STK11, CASP8, TGFBR2, and keratin genes (KRT5/10/13/14/16) are significantly enriched in LACC, with TP53 mutations disrupting normal suppression of keratinization pathways, and N-glycosylated CD44 potentially counteracting TP53 function through MDM2 activation. These findings establish a comprehensive molecular framework for understanding treatment resistance mechanisms in cervical cancer and identifying potential therapeutic targets.
