These findings establish LINC01158 as an independent prognostic factor for breast cancer and suggest that restoration of this lncRNA or inhibition of miR-711 may represent viable therapeutic strategies for controlling breast cancer progression.

title: Building a Hand-Curated ceRNET for Endometrial Cancer, Striving for Clinical as Well as Medicolegal Soundness: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review constructs a hand-curated competing endogenous RNA network (ceRNA) for endometrial cancer, revealing how microRNAs, long noncoding RNAs, and mRNAs form regulatory axes that control protein expression and influence carcinogenesis. Key cancer-associated genes including POLE, MLH1, TP53, PTEN, PIK3CA, and CTNNB1 are dysregulated in endometrial cancer, alongside epigenetic alterations like DNA hypermethylation. The ceRNA network features prominent regulatory interactions such as TUG1 binding miR-34a to indirectly regulate L1CAM, NOTCH1, and PD-L1, while the miR-195 network shows robust involvement across eight distinct regulatory axes. Endometrial cancer risk factors include metabolic dysfunction (obesity, insulin resistance, metabolic syndrome), hormonal imbalance, and dysregulation of oncogenic pathways including Wnt, ErbB, and insulin signaling. The study emphasizes that metabolomic profiling—particularly lipid metabolites like phosphocholines and acylcholines—combined with ceRNA network analysis can improve diagnostic accuracy and enable personalized therapeutic strategies for endometrial cancer patients.

title: Screening, Analysis, and Validation of Endoplasmic Reticulum Stress-Related DEGs in Epilepsy
summary:
# Summary

This study identified endoplasmic reticulum stress (ERS) as a critical mechanism underlying epilepsy pathogenesis, triggered by abnormal neuronal discharges that increase intracellular calcium and subsequently activate unfolded protein response (UPR) pathways. ERS-induced dysregulation leads to neuronal dysfunction and apoptosis through multiple interconnected mechanisms: activation of pro-inflammatory NF-κB signaling that promotes cytokine release, direct activation of apoptotic pathways involving CHOP and caspase-12, and impaired protein homeostasis exacerbated by oxidative stress and energy failure. Gene expression analysis identified eight key differentially expressed genes associated with epilepsy, including CXCL8 (downregulated) and TLR4, MMP9, TNFRSF1A, STAT1, RELA, PTGS2, and BCL2 (upregulated in epilepsy patients), with CXCL8 emerging as the most valuable diagnostic biomarker. Functional enrichment analysis revealed that ERS-related genes predominantly regulate apoptosis signaling, inflammatory responses, and protein binding pathways, suggesting that therapeutic targeting of ERS or its downstream mediators could provide novel approaches to prevent seizure severity and associated neurological complications.

title: Identification and validation of tissue-based gene biomarkers for acute intestinal graft-versus-host disease(AIGVHD)
summary:
# Summary

This study identified and validated three critical tissue-based gene biomarkers—FCGR3A, SERPING1, and IFITM3—for acute intestinal graft-versus-host disease (AIGVHD). Differentially expressed genes in AIGVHD samples showed marked enrichment in immune and inflammatory response pathways, particularly IL-17 signaling, NOD-like receptor signaling, and B cell receptor signaling pathways. The three biomarker genes exhibited significantly elevated expression in AIGVHD samples and in an aGVHD mouse model, and their expression correlated positively with infiltration of pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages and neutrophils while correlating negatively with protective regulatory T cells. A machine learning model incorporating these three genes achieved robust predictive performance (AUC 0.9802) for AIGVHD diagnosis. Mechanistically, FCGR3A functions as an Fc receptor in immune cell activation and inflammation, IFITM3 enhances dendritic cell antigen presentation and prevents exacerbated inflammatory reactions, and SERPING1 upregulates inflammatory responses, collectively suggesting these genes drive pathological immune activation in AIGVHD through modulation of innate immune cell populations.

title: Molecular Prognostic Factors in Uterine Serous Carcinomas: A Systematic Review
summary: Uterine serous carcinomas (USCs) are characterized by frequent TP53 mutations and extensive copy number alterations, with TP53 alterations occurring in approximately 85% of cases and serving as an independent predictor of worse outcomes and shorter survival. These tumors are primarily classified within the copy number-high/p53 abnormal molecular prognostic group and are typically diagnosed at advanced stages with poor prognosis and frequent extraperitoneal recurrences. HER2 gene amplification is strongly associated with serous histology and aggressive disease features, including increased recurrence rates and worse progression-free and overall survival, while homologous recombination repair gene mutations (HRRmut), found in approximately 5% of patients, paradoxically correlate with better disease-specific survival and longer progression-free survival in advanced-stage disease. Additional molecular markers including L1CAM (associated with advanced stage and distant recurrences when combined with TP53), SNCG (linked to tumor grade and chemoresistance), and progesterone receptor expression (associated with better recurrence-free survival) have been identified as important prognostic factors. Emerging evidence supports the use of genomic signatures and molecular classification systems to further stratify patient risk, with potential therapeutic implications including immunotherapy for mismatch repair-deficient cancers and targeted approaches for HER2-positive disease.

title: Establishment of Two Novel Ovarian Tumor Cell Lines with Characteristics of Mucinous Borderline Tumors or Dedifferentiated Carcinoma—Implications for Tumor Heterogeneity and the Complex Carcinogenesis of Mucinous Tumors
summary: This study establishes two novel ovarian cell lines, HMucBOT-1 and HMucBOT-2, derived from mucinous borderline ovarian tumors that demonstrate distinct biological behaviors and genetic profiles, illuminating tumor heterogeneity in this malignancy. KRAS mutations, present in 90% of mucinous borderline tumors, activate the MAPK signaling pathway to promote cell proliferation and tumor progression, with KRAS amplification specifically associated with malignant transformation. While HMucBOT-1 retains the morphology and characteristics of benign borderline tumors, HMucBOT-2 exhibits dedifferentiated carcinoma features with significantly greater proliferation, migration, invasion, and colony-forming capacity. The aggressive phenotype of HMucBOT-2 is driven by unique genetic alterations including threefold gains in KRAS, CDK4, TERT, and other oncogenic genes, as well as frequent mutations in ARID1A, ARID1B, and other tumor suppressors. These findings indicate that KRAS amplification combined with additional copy number alterations confers enhanced tumor aggressiveness and represents a critical mechanism in the progression from borderline tumors to mucinous carcinomas. The two cell lines serve as valuable preclinical models for investigating mucinous ovarian tumor biology and exploring targeted therapeutic strategies.

title: AI-Driven Drug Target Screening Platform Identified Oncogene CACNA2D1 Activated by Enhancer Infestation in Epstein-Barr Virus-Associated Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

An AI-driven drug target screening platform identified CACNA2D1 as an oncogenic driver in Epstein-Barr virus (EBV)-associated nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC), where EBV episomes tether to enhancer regions to activate CACNA2D1 expression through increased H3K27 acetylation. CACNA2D1, a voltage-gated calcium channel subunit, is significantly upregulated in EBV-positive NPC cells and tissues compared to normal controls, and its expression correlates strongly with EBV-tethering events. Functional studies demonstrated that CACNA2D1 depletion markedly reduces tumor cell viability, migration, and in vivo tumor growth by approximately one-third, indicating its critical role in NPC pathogenesis. CACNA2D1 likely promotes cancer cell proliferation through enhanced calcium signaling that activates downstream transcription factors (NFAT, CREB) and the Ras-ERK pathway. Treatment with the BRD4 inhibitor JQ1 suppressed CACNA2D1 expression and inhibited approximately 85% of cancer cell proliferation, suggesting that targeting the EBV-enhancer-CACNA2D1 axis represents a viable therapeutic strategy for EBV-associated NPC.

title: Unraveling the Transcriptomic Profiles of Large and Small Donkey Follicles
summary:
# Summary

This transcriptomic study identified 3,291 differentially expressed genes between large and small donkey follicles, with 2,152 genes upregulated in large follicles. KEGG pathway analysis revealed significant enrichment in the PI3K-Akt signaling pathway, focal adhesion, and cancer-associated pathways, implicating these mechanisms in follicular development and selection. The EMCN gene emerged as a key regulator that positively upregulates FOXO3 within the PI3K-Akt pathway, thereby inhibiting apoptosis in granulosa cells and promoting follicular survival during the maturation phase. Donkey follicles reach exceptionally large diameters (approximately 40.7 mm at ovulation) and display distinctive FSH insensitivity compared to other species, while dominant follicles inhibit the development of smaller follicles, leading to atresia in non-selected follicles. Homeobox and zinc-finger transcription factor families were identified as potential regulatory centers controlling follicular development. These findings provide molecular insight into the transcriptional networks governing follicle selection, survival, and the development of donkey reproductive physiology.

title: Serum expression signature of TUG1, MALAT1, miR-483, and miR-141 and their targets TGF-β1 and STAT3 in severe male factor infertility
summary: This study identified a serum biomarker panel of long non-coding RNAs (TUG1 and MALAT1) and microRNAs (miR-483 and miR-141) that distinguishes severe male factor infertility subtypes, with distinct expression signatures between non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) and oligozoospermia (SO) patients. TUG1 and miR-141 are downregulated in infertile patients and show particular association with NOA risk through their involvement in spermatid differentiation and blood-testis barrier integrity, while miR-483 and STAT3 are upregulated in both NOA and SO patients. These microRNAs regulate critical spermatogenic signaling pathways, including TGF-β and JAK2/STAT3 cascades, and MALAT1 acts as a competing endogenous RNA that sponges miR-483-3p and miR-141-3p to influence these pathways. Notably, the serum TUG1/miR-141 panel discriminated NOA with an AUC of 0.93, while the TUG1/miR-483 panel distinguished SO with an AUC of 0.972, demonstrating strong diagnostic potential. Serum expression of these molecules also correlated with clinical parameters including hormone levels, testicular volume, sperm motility, and morphology, suggesting their utility as non-invasive biomarkers for stratifying male infertility etiology and severity.

title: The fourspine stickleback (Apeltes quadracus) has an XY sex chromosome system with polymorphic inversions on both X and Y chromosomes
summary: The fourspine stickleback (Apeltes quadracus) employs an XY sex determination system located on chromosome 23, with males functioning as the heterogametic sex, contrary to previous reports of a ZW system. Polymorphic inversions on both the X and Y chromosomes define a shared sex determination region (SDR) spanning 7.20-8.21 Mb, with population-specific variants including an X-linked inversion in the CT population and a Y-specific inversion in the NS population. Two novel candidate sex determination genes, rxfp2a and zar1l, were identified within this SDR and likely play central roles in sex determination. The Y chromosome in A. quadracus shows evidence of early-stage degeneration with elevated genetic diversity in males and reduced recombination in the SDR, consistent with nascent sex chromosome differentiation driven by sexually antagonistic selection. These findings demonstrate sex chromosome turnover within the stickleback family, as different species employ distinct sex determination systems on different chromosomes (chromosome 19 in Gasterosteus, chromosome 12 in Pungitius, and chromosome 7 in P. sinensis), illustrating the evolutionary plasticity of genetic sex determination mechanisms across closely related lineages.

title: A brief review of Lynch syndrome: understanding the dual cancer risk between endometrial and colorectal cancer
summary: Lynch syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by germline mutations in DNA mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS1, and PMS2) that substantially elevate lifetime risks for both colorectal and endometrial cancers, along with multiple other malignancies. These MMR gene defects lead to microsatellite instability and genomic instability, characterized by the accumulation of frameshift mutations that drive aggressive cancer progression and tumorigenesis through impaired proliferation regulation and apoptosis inhibition. EPCAM deletions represent an additional genetic mechanism in Lynch syndrome, causing MSH2 inactivation through promoter hypermethylation and conferring cancer risks comparable to direct MMR mutations. The specific gene mutated determines the cancer risk profile, with MSH6 mutations conferring particularly elevated endometrial cancer risk (16-49% lifetime risk) relative to colorectal risk, while PMS2 mutations show lower overall risks. Beyond genetic factors, lifestyle determinants including obesity and physical inactivity further exacerbate cancer susceptibility in affected individuals. Notably, tumors with microsatellite instability-high phenotypes respond favorably to immune checkpoint inhibitors targeting PD-1/PD-L1, providing an important therapeutic avenue for Lynch syndrome-associated malignancies.

title: Hypercoagulability and the A1298C MTHFR Mutation: Case Series of Unexplained Pulmonary Embolism
summary:
# Summary

The A1298C polymorphism in the methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR) gene reduces enzymatic activity in homocysteine metabolism, leading to elevated plasma homocysteine levels that promote a hypercoagulable state. This case series presents two female patients with unexplained pulmonary embolism and venous thromboembolism, one homozygous and one heterozygous for the A1298C mutation, both demonstrating markedly elevated homocysteine levels (30.7 and 182 μmol/L, respectively) without traditional thrombotic risk factors. The elevated homocysteine exacerbates thrombogenesis through multiple mechanisms including increased tissue factor expression, reduced plasmin generation, and enhanced platelet reactivity. Both patients responded favorably to anticoagulation therapy with enoxaparin and apixaban, achieving complete symptom resolution. While routine MTHFR genetic screening is not currently recommended in all venous thromboembolism cases, targeted testing may be warranted in unexplained thrombotic events, and supplementation with folic acid and vitamins B6 and B12 may lower homocysteine levels and reduce recurrence risk.

title: The phenotypic and genetic association between endometriosis and immunological diseases
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis demonstrates significant phenotypic and genetic associations with multiple immunological diseases, most notably rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, multiple sclerosis, coeliac disease, and psoriasis. Mendelson-Randomization analysis provides evidence for potential causal relationships between endometriosis and rheumatoid arthritis, with shared genetic loci including BMPR2, BSN, MLLT10, and XKR6 contributing to disease susceptibility. The pathophysiology involves a dysregulated inflammatory microenvironment wherein endometriotic implants secrete cytokines, chemokines, and prostaglandins that recruit immune cells including macrophages, T cells, neutrophils, and natural killer cells, while local estrogen signaling through aromatase and estrogen receptor beta promotes endometrial implant proliferation and impairs TNF-α-mediated apoptosis. Genetic variants associated with endometriosis function through multiple biological pathways, including oxidative stress protection (MSRA, PON2), immune cell development (BLK, ZAP70), apoptosis regulation (ATRAID, SLC35F6, XKR6), progesterone resistance (TRPS1), and invasive progression via matrix metalloproteinase-9. These findings establish endometriosis as an immunological disease with shared genetic architecture and causal pathways to systemic autoimmune and autoinflammatory conditions.

title: Mutational landscape of atherosclerotic plaques reveals large clonal cell populations
summary:
# Summary

Atherosclerotic plaques harbor extensive somatic mutations organized into large clonal populations, with mutated cells comprising over 10% of plaque samples in most cases. Mutations are significantly enriched in genes encoding contractile apparatus proteins, cancer driver genes, and desmosome components, predominantly affecting smooth muscle cells that originate from medial layer progenitors and demonstrate remarkable phenotypic plasticity spanning both the plaque cap and interior regions. Clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) mutations, particularly in epigenetic regulators like DNMT3A and TET2, render circulating myeloid cells more inflammatory and proatherogenic, and these CHIP-mutated hematopoietic clones directly infiltrate atherosclerotic plaques where they contribute to disease progression. Reactive oxygen species in the plaque microenvironment inflict DNA damage and impair repair mechanisms in smooth muscle cells, promoting asymmetric cellular proliferation that generates large mutated clones, while specific mutations in contractile genes become downregulated in symptomatic versus asymptomatic plaques. These findings demonstrate that atherosclerotic plaques evolve through clonal selection mechanisms similar to those observed in cancer and inflammatory conditions, with age-dependent accumulation of somatic mutations contributing to age-related cardiovascular disease.

title: Genome‐Wide Association Analyses in Family Triads and Dyads Following Assisted Reproductive Technology
summary:
# Summary

This genome-wide association study identifies novel genetic loci affecting fetal survival specifically in assisted reproductive technology (ART) pregnancies, revealing genetic mechanisms distinct from natural conception. Three genes—CXXC4-AS1, OPCML, and DYNLRB2-AS1—were associated with fetal survival through variants including rs1468231, rs76395305, and rs34149818, with these signals notably absent in non-ART pregnancies, suggesting ART-specific selection pressures. DYNLRB2, highly expressed in testes and involved in male meiosis, was linked to male fertility, while variants in FLI1 showed maternal and parent-of-origin effects on offspring outcomes. The study establishes that genetic factors significantly influence infertility and early pregnancy loss, with ART procedures paradoxically enhancing early fetal survival while simultaneously increasing risks for low birth weight, preterm delivery, and congenital malformations through epigenetic alterations and altered genetic selection.

title: Subtype-specific dysregulation of biogenic amine-related genes and miRNAs in breast cancer: identification of DRD2, HRH2, and HRH4 as potential therapeutic targets in TNBC and HER2+ subtypes
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals subtype-specific dysregulation of biogenic amine signaling pathways in breast cancer, with distinct molecular signatures in triple-negative (TNBC) and HER2+ subtypes. Histamine receptor genes HRH1-HRH4 and dopamine receptor DRD2 are broadly upregulated across breast cancer subtypes, while DRD5 is markedly downregulated, particularly affecting non-luminal tumors. The heightened HRH2 and HRH4 expression in TNBC correlates with loss of their regulatory microRNAs hsa-miR-30b-3p and hsa-miR-372-5p, suggesting disrupted post-transcriptional control. Additional upregulated genes including ICAM1, MAPK9, and OXT promote inflammatory responses, cell proliferation, and angiogenesis—processes fundamental to cancer progression. These findings identify DRD2, HRH2, and HRH4 as high-confidence therapeutic targets for TNBC and HER2+ breast cancers, offering opportunities for biogenic amine pathway-directed interventions.

title: Bibliometric analysis of MXRA7 gene research trajectory: trends and insights (2015–2024)
summary:
# Summary

This bibliometric analysis of MXRA7 gene research from 2015–2024 reveals that MXRA7 functions as a central mediator of extracellular matrix remodeling, tissue regeneration, and inflammatory processes, with co-expression patterns linking it to matrix metalloproteinases and collagen. Beyond its role in bone biology and osteoblast differentiation, MXRA7 emerges as a multifunctional protein involved in wound healing, angiogenesis, and pathological processes including fibrosis, psoriasis, liver injury, and cancer progression. The research demonstrates that stromal remodeling mediated by MXRA7 and related matrix genes promotes tumor cell proliferation and invasion through modulation of the tumor microenvironment, while MXRA7 expression temporally correlates with matrix metalloproteinase activity. Publication trends show substantial growth in MXRA7 research across diverse journals, with PLOS ONE leading in output, reflecting expanding interest in exploiting MXRA7 as a therapeutic target for immune diseases, fibrosis, cardiovascular disease, and oncology. These findings suggest MXRA7 represents a critical node integrating tissue remodeling pathways relevant to both physiological processes and pathological conditions including cancer-associated stromal changes.

title: Whole exome sequencing and bioinformatics reveal PMAIP1 and PDGFRL as immune-related gene markers in follicular thyroid carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study identified PMAIP1 and PDGFRL as immune-related genetic markers distinguishing follicular thyroid carcinoma (FTC) from benign follicular thyroid adenoma (FTA) using whole exome sequencing and bioinformatics analysis. PMAIP1 expression was significantly elevated in FTC tissues and FTC cell lines (FTC133 and FTC238) compared to normal controls, while PDGFRL expression was substantially reduced in the same comparisons. Both genes were identified among the top ten differentially expressed genes and are involved in critical signaling pathways including cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction and MAPK signaling, which regulate immune cell infiltration and function. The study proposes PMAIP1 and PDGFRL as novel immunodiagnostic markers that could help address the clinical challenge of accurately differentiating FTC—characterized by capsular and vascular invasion with metastatic potential—from benign FTA. These findings suggest that dysregulation of immune-related genes contributes to the aggressive phenotype of FTC and may provide new diagnostic and therapeutic targets for follicular thyroid malignancies.

title: Transcriptomic insights into the mechanism of action of telomere-related biomarkers in rheumatoid arthritis
summary: This study identifies telomere-related biomarkers and their molecular mechanisms in rheumatoid arthritis through transcriptomic analysis. Telomere shortening, significantly associated with RA risk and disease progression, correlates with increased oxidative stress and exaggerated inflammatory responses, with defects in telomerase activity leading to accelerated telomere attrition and subsequent immune dysfunction. Five key biomarkers—ABCC4, S100A8, PIM2, ISG20, and VAMP2—were identified as diagnostic indicators, with differential expression patterns in RA patients that correlate with distinct immune cell populations including activated CD4 memory T cells, plasma cells, neutrophils, and M2 macrophages. ABCC4, localized to the plasma membrane and regulated by transcription factors USF1, SP1, and NR1H4, showed strong interactions with multiple RA therapeutics, particularly methotrexate, and enriched pathways involved in cell cycle regulation and systemic autoimmunity. The biomarkers collectively implicate dysregulated immune cell activation and altered telomere maintenance as central mechanisms in RA pathogenesis, offering potential therapeutic targets beyond conventional NSAID and methotrexate approaches. These findings establish telomere biology and associated molecular signatures as key determinants of RA susceptibility and progression.

title: Cancer prognosis and treatment results in patients with PTEN Hamartoma Tumour Syndrome (PHTS)—a European cohort study
summary: Pathogenic germline PTEN variants cause PTEN Hamartoma Tumour Syndrome (PHTS), conferring substantially elevated lifetime risks of multiple malignancies including breast cancer (comparable to BRCA1/2 risk), endometrial cancer (6-22%), thyroid cancer (9-21%), and moderately increased risks of colorectal, renal, and melanoma. Somatic PTEN loss in tumors promotes proliferation, recurrence, and shortened survival, while aberrant PTEN signaling drives metastasis and treatment resistance to chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and targeted therapies like trastuzumab. Despite the elevated cancer incidence in female PHTS patients causing 3.7-fold increased overall mortality (peaking at 5.2-fold between ages 50-59), early-stage cancer detection yields excellent outcomes comparable to the general population—with stage I endometrial and thyroid cancers achieving 80% and 100% 10-year survival respectively. Notably, systemic therapies including chemotherapy and radiotherapy were associated with poorer survival in PHTS breast cancer patients, while mastectomy and hormone therapy in receptor-positive cases improved outcomes. The clinical prognosis of PHTS-related cancers overall is comparable to sporadic malignancies when detected early, underscoring the critical importance of enhanced cancer surveillance in this syndrome.

title: Identification of Important Diagnostic Genes in the Uterine Using Bioinformatics and Machine Learning
journal: Medical Journal of the Islamic Republic of Iran
summary: Using bioinformatics and machine learning approaches on the GDCA database, researchers identified three novel diagnostic biomarkers for uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma (UCEC): MEX3B, CTRP2, and AASS, which are all significantly upregulated in tumor cells. Prognostic analysis revealed that low expression of FOXL2, TCF4, and NR2F2 correlates with poor outcomes in UCEC patients, while elevated Cyclin E1 expression associates with worse overall survival. Protein-protein interaction analysis highlighted ten hub genes including JUN, GATA2, and WT1 as key players in uterine cancer pathogenesis, alongside extracellular matrix proteins (VWF, ECM2, MMRN1, SPARCL1) that demonstrate significant functional interactions. Dysregulated genes in UCEC were enriched in biological processes involving tight junction interactions, PI3K/AKT signaling, TP53-regulated G1 cell cycle arrest, and transport of small molecules, indicating multiple dysregulated pathways in endometrial malignancy. Additional prognostic markers for type 2 UCEC included LINC02418, RASGRF1, GCNT1, LEF1, and NKD1, while L1 cell adhesion molecule and estrogen receptor were associated with p53-abnormal endometrial carcinoma subtypes.

title: Analysis of the pharmacological mechanisms of Xiaoyao pill in breast cancer treatment
summary: Xiaoyao pill, a traditional Chinese medicine originally used for breast hyperplasia, demonstrates therapeutic potential in breast cancer through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The treatment primarily works by downregulating lipid metabolism pathways, including fatty acid degradation, PPAR signaling, and propanoate metabolism, while simultaneously upregulating DNA repair and replication pathways such as base excision repair and Fanconi anemia pathways. The herbal components, particularly bupleurum, exert anti-inflammatory and anti-estrogenic effects that inhibit breast tissue proliferation and restore hormone level balance, while angelica and white peony root enhance local blood circulation. Network analysis identified 24 common genes shared between Xiaoyao pill targets and breast cancer tissues that play essential roles in disease diagnosis and progression, with these genes predominantly associated with fatty acid metabolism and extracellular matrix composition. The therapeutic mechanism also involves modulation of tumor immunity, as target genes are expressed in T cells and regulate immune cell infiltration into the tumor microenvironment. Overall, Xiaoyao pill's anti-cancer activity appears to rely on a synergistic approach combining metabolic reprogramming, immune activation, and hormonal regulation.

title: Exploring the potential function of high expression of ANAPC1 in regulating ubiquitination in hepatocellular carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates ANAPC1, the largest subunit of the E3 ubiquitin ligase anaphase-promoting complex/cyclosome (APC/C), as a key regulator in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) progression. ANAPC1 is significantly upregulated in HCC tissues and cells, where it controls ubiquitin-mediated protein degradation to regulate cell cycle progression, metabolic pathways including glycolysis and pyruvate metabolism, and cell proliferation, particularly during the G2/M phase. High ANAPC1 expression correlates with poor prognosis and enhanced sensitivity to multiple tyrosine kinase inhibitors (sorafenib, dasatinib, ibrutinib, lapatinib, nilotinib, and afatinib), while also associated with cisplatin resistance. Mechanistically, ANAPC1 upregulation activates the macrophage migration inhibitory factor (MIF) pathway and promotes metabolic reprogramming toward glycolysis. ANAPC1 knockdown effectively inhibits hepatocyte proliferation, suggesting it could serve as a prognostic biomarker and potential therapeutic target for HCC patient stratification and treatment selection.

title: Correlation Between miR-27a-3p Polymorphisms and Peri-Implantitis Susceptibility: A Case-Control Study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study investigates the association between miR-27a-3p polymorphisms and peri-implantitis (PI) susceptibility. The rs895819 G allele and GG genotype of miR-27a-3p were found to significantly increase PI risk, with the GG genotype showing a 2.157-fold increased odds ratio for disease. The rs895819 polymorphism reduces miR-27a-3p expression levels, particularly in GG genotype carriers, which leads to elevated RUNX1 protein—a direct target of miR-27a-3p that promotes osteoclast formation and inflammatory cytokine production. PI patients carrying the rs895819 GG genotype exhibited markedly elevated levels of inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein, interleukin-6, white blood cell count) and RUNX1, consistent with enhanced inflammatory response and bone resorption. These findings suggest that rs895819 modulates PI susceptibility through a mechanistic pathway involving miR-27a-3p downregulation, RUNX1 upregulation, and subsequent amplification of inflammatory and bone-resorptive processes.

title: Reference genes selection and validation in yam by real-time quantitative polymerase chain reaction
summary:
# Summary

This study validated reference genes for accurate gene expression analysis in Chinese yam (*Dioscorea opposita* Thunb.) using real-time quantitative PCR (RT-qPCR), a method essential for normalizing target gene expression across different experimental conditions. Nine candidate reference genes including UBQ, ACT, GAPDH, 18S, PP2A, F-box, CHCI, EIF, and EIF-5A were evaluated across different developmental stages, tissues, and abiotic stress treatments (high temperature, low temperature, salt, drought, and abscisic acid). UBQ and PP2A emerged as the most stable and suitable reference genes for comprehensive expression analysis in yam, while EIF was identified as the least stable gene across all conditions and was deemed unsuitable as an internal reference. The stability rankings determined by RefFinder analysis showed UBQ>CHCI>F-box>ACT>GAPDH>18S>PP2A>UBA>EIF, with differential stability observed across specific stresses—for example, GUSB, ACT, GAPDH, and 18S showed optimal stability under high temperature, low temperature, salt, and drought treatments respectively. Validation using WRKY transcription factors confirmed that using stable reference genes (UBQ and PP2A) yielded consistent expression results, while the unstable reference gene EIF produced variable and unreliable expression data, demonstrating the critical importance of proper reference gene selection for accurate molecular analysis in plant research.

title: Clinical genome sequencing in patients with hereditary breast and ovarian cancer: Concept, implementation and benefits
journal: The Breast : Official Journal of the European Society of Mastology
summary:
# Summary

Clinical genome sequencing (GS) has emerged as a superior diagnostic tool for hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC), detecting pathogenic variants in established high-risk genes including BRCA1, BRCA2, TP53, PALB2, and CHEK2, with BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations accounting for up to 80% of genetically confirmed cases. Unlike traditional exome or panel sequencing approaches, GS demonstrates enhanced capacity to identify structural variants, copy number variations, deep intronic variants, and complex rearrangements—including tandem duplications in BRCA1, whole-exon inversions in BARD1, and deep intronic variants in CHEK2—that would otherwise be missed. The implementation of complementary approaches including transcriptome sequencing for variant confirmation and blood-based polygenic risk score analysis (BRIDGES-PRS) further improves diagnostic accuracy and patient stratification, particularly for cases without identifiable pathogenic variants in core genes. GS also enables detection of actionable secondary findings in cancer predisposition genes such as MLH1 and MUTYH, and permits greater flexibility for reanalysis of candidate genes and regulatory regions. Long-read genome sequencing promises additional improvements in structural variant characterization and diagnostic sensitivity, making comprehensive genomic analysis the preferred approach for HBOC genetic diagnosis.

title: Blurred by a “Puff of Smoke”—A Case-Based Review on the Challenging Recognition of Coexisting CNS Demyelinating Disease and Moyamoya Angiopathy
summary:
# Summary

This case-based review describes the rare and diagnostically challenging coexistence of moyamoya angiopathy (MMA) and multiple sclerosis (MS), conditions that share overlapping clinical presentations and underlying pathophysiological mechanisms. MMA is a cerebrovascular disease characterized by chronic progressive stenosis or occlusion of internal carotid arteries with formation of an abnormal vascular network, while MS is a chronic demyelinating disease of the central nervous system causing focal demyelinating lesions. Both disorders predominantly affect young women and frequently associate with autoimmune diseases, complicating differential diagnosis when patients present with transient neurological symptoms. The authors present two patients whose MS diagnoses were initially missed or delayed due to imaging findings suggestive of MMA, with Patient 1 also carrying the RNF213 c.12092T>C variant implicated in moyamoya pathogenesis. Genetic, inflammatory, and immunological pathways involving RNF213—which regulates angiogenesis, arterial wall remodeling, chronic inflammation, and oligodendrocyte formation—appear central to both conditions, suggesting a molecular link between vascular and demyelinating disease. Management required multimodal approaches combining antiplatelet therapy and revascularization for MMA with immunomodulatory treatments including corticosteroids and disease-modifying agents for MS.

title: The Genetic Background of Ankylosing Spondylitis Reveals a Distinct Overlap with Autoimmune Diseases: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Ankylosing spondylitis is a genetically complex autoimmune disease driven by HLA-B27 and multiple susceptibility genes including ERAP1 and IL23R, which influence immune cell function and cytokine profiles. The disease pathogenesis centers on dysregulation of the IL-23/IL-17 axis, whereby IL-23 stimulates pathogenic IL-17-producing cells (Th17, γδ T cells, MAIT cells, and ILC3s) that drive chronic inflammation and abnormal bone remodeling in the sacroiliac joints and axial skeleton. HLA-B27 misfolding triggers endoplasmic reticulum stress and the unfolded protein response, while microbial dysbiosis may trigger aberrant immune activation in genetically predisposed individuals through molecular mimicry mechanisms. The inflammatory milieu is perpetuated by key cytokines (TNF-α, IL-17, and IL-23) that promote tissue inflammation and vertebral fusion, counterbalanced only partially by anti-inflammatory IL-10. Notably, ankylosing spondylitis shares substantial genetic overlap with other autoimmune diseases, particularly rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis, suggesting common immunological pathways. Current therapeutic approaches target TNF-α inhibition with agents such as etanercept and infliximab to suppress the chronic inflammatory cascade.

title: Combined profile of the tandem repeats CAG, TA and CA of the androgen and estrogen receptor genes in breast cancer
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: This study examined tandem repeat polymorphisms in the androgen and estrogen receptor genes as genetic risk factors for breast cancer. The researchers identified that a specific combined profile of these repeats—longer CAG repeats (≥28), shorter TA repeats (<23), and shorter CA repeats (<23)—represents a potential genetic risk signature for breast cancer susceptibility. The findings confirm previous associations between longer CAG repeats in the androgen receptor gene and increased breast cancer risk, while also highlighting the importance of evaluating multiple repeat polymorphisms together rather than individually. These tandem repeat variations in hormone receptor genes may influence hormone signaling pathways relevant to breast cancer development, suggesting that a cumulative genetic profile combining CAG, TA, and CA repeat lengths could improve risk stratification. The results contribute to understanding how polymorphisms in steroid hormone receptors modulate breast cancer susceptibility, though the authors note that previous case-control studies have reported inconsistent findings regarding individual receptor polymorphisms.

title: Role of glutathione-S-transferase and codon 72 of P53
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: This study investigated genetic polymorphisms in p53 and glutathione-S-transferase genes as potential risk factors for ovarian cancer. The p53 codon 72 polymorphism emerged as a significant contributor to ovarian cancer susceptibility, with the heterozygous Arg/Pro genotype increasing cancer risk more than 2.5-fold compared to controls, while the wild-type variant was significantly less frequent in patients. In contrast, none of the glutathione-S-transferase variants examined—including GSTT1, GSTM1, GSTP1, and GSTO2—demonstrated any association with ovarian cancer risk, indicating these detoxification enzymes do not substantially influence cancer development in this context. Additionally, neither p53 nor any of the GST genotypes correlated with chemotherapy response or other clinical and pathological features in the patient population. These findings highlight p53 codon 72 polymorphism as a potentially important genetic risk marker for ovarian cancer, while suggesting that common GST polymorphisms may not play a major role in ovarian cancer susceptibility or treatment outcomes.

title: Identification of lipid metabolism-related marker genes in colorectal cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies lipid metabolism-related biomarkers in colorectal cancer through integrated bioinformatic and experimental approaches. Gene Set Enrichment Analysis revealed that differentially expressed genes were enriched in lipid-related pathways including fatty acid metabolism, adipogenesis, oxidative phosphorylation, and myogenesis. Weighted Gene Co-expression Network Analysis identified pink and yellow gene modules as significantly associated with colorectal cancer, from which six hub genes were isolated: DHCR7, FABP4, FASN, FAXDC2, PTGIS, and SLC27A6. Clinical validation showed elevated expression of DHCR7 and FASN in colorectal cancer tissues, while FAXDC2 and PTGIS were downregulated. Functional studies demonstrated that DHCR7 knockdown significantly inhibited colorectal cancer cell proliferation and induced apoptosis, implicating DHCR7 as a key player in colorectal cancer pathogenesis and a potential therapeutic target for intervention.

title: Phthalates unleashed: decoding ovarian carcinogenesis through multi-omics networks, single-cell insights, and molecular docking
summary: This multi-omics study reveals that chronic phthalate exposure (diethyl phthalate, dimethyl phthalate, dioctyl phthalate) drives ovarian carcinogenesis through multiple interconnected mechanisms centered on metabolic reprogramming and hormonal dysregulation. Three phthalate compounds demonstrated strong molecular binding affinities to key cancer-regulatory proteins including GAPDH, ESR1, CCND1, and CYCS, with dioctyl phthalate showing particularly high binding scores to PPARA and SIRT1. The study identified marked upregulation of glycolytic enzymes (GAPDH, GPI, TPI1) and estrogen signaling machinery (ESR1) alongside downregulation of metabolic regulators (PPARA, SIRT1) in ovarian tumors, indicating phthalate-induced metabolic switching toward aerobic glycolysis and HIF-1α signaling. Single-cell transcriptomics revealed coordinated expression of these hub genes across tumor cells, immune cells, mesenchymal cells, and endothelial cells, suggesting systemic tumor microenvironment reprogramming. Beyond ovarian cancer, the analysis linked phthalate exposure to reproductive dysfunction, oxidative stress, inflammatory pathways, and gynecological disorders including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome, establishing phthalates as pleiotropic environmental carcinogens affecting female reproductive health.

title: Transcriptome and proteome profile analysis of the regulation of chicken ovarian development
summary: This transcriptomic and proteomic study identifies the regulatory mechanisms of chicken ovarian development across developmental stages. FSH, LH, estradiol (E2), and progesterone (P4) act as coordinated key regulators of ovarian development, with all four hormones significantly elevated at 30 weeks of age compared to 20 weeks. Early ovarian development (20 weeks) is characterized by enrichment of extracellular matrix pathways including collagen synthesis, cell adhesion, and ECM-receptor interaction, with elevated expression of structural proteins such as collagen variants (COL12A1, COL1A2, COL5A2), annexin family members (ANXA1, ANXA2, ANXA5, ANXA7), and matrix proteins like POSTN and ITGB3. By contrast, peak egg production (30 weeks) shows significant upregulation of steroid biosynthesis and cholesterol metabolism pathways, with preferential expression of steroid-related genes including CYP17A1, HSD17B1, and OSBP2. Integration of multi-omics data revealed additional candidate genes involved in ovarian development including EDNRA, CRISPLD2, SERPINF2, YES1, and LGMN, while pathway analysis identified enrichment in ribosomal, cytokine signaling, cell cycle, oocyte meiosis, and progesterone-mediated oocyte maturation pathways.

title: Identification of DCAF5 as a novel cancer prognostic and immunotherapy biomarker through pan-cancer analysis and renal clear cell carcinoma clinical data validation
summary: This pan-cancer analysis identifies DCAF5 as a dual-functional biomarker for cancer prognosis and immunotherapy personalization, with particular validation in renal clear cell carcinoma (KIRC). DCAF5, a component of the Cul4-RING E3 ubiquitin ligase complex, regulates protein stability and prevents reformation of SMARCB1-deficient SWI/SNF complexes that would otherwise restore oncogenic gene expression patterns. DCAF5 expression is significantly reduced in KIRC and multiple other cancer types including lung adenocarcinoma, breast cancer, and endometrial cancer, with lower levels correlating with higher tumor grades and lymph node metastasis. The protein exhibits broad subcellular localization across the nucleus, cytoplasm, mitochondria, and endoplasmic reticulum, and interacts with key ubiquitin ligase components including DDB1, CUL4A, and CUL4B. Notably, DCAF5 expression levels associate strongly with immune cell infiltration and overall immune scores, positioning it as a valuable marker for predicting immunotherapy response. These findings establish DCAF5 as a prognostic indicator whose expression status could guide personalized immunotherapy strategies across multiple cancer types.

title: Pyroptosis-Related Gene Signatures Enable Robustly Diagnosis, Prognosis and Immune Responses Prediction in Uterine Corpus Endometrial Carcinoma
summary:
# Pyroptosis-Related Gene Signatures in Uterine Corpus Endometrial Carcinoma

This study identified 26 pyroptosis-related genes as robust biomarkers for diagnosing and predicting the prognosis of uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma (UCEC). A seven-gene pyroptosis-related gene risk signature (PRGRS) incorporating IRF2, TIRAP, BAK1, GSDMD, CHMP2A, GPX4, and CHMP2B effectively stratified UCEC patients and independently predicted survival outcomes while correlating with tumor immune responses. The research elucidated that pyroptosis, a pro-inflammatory programmed cell death mechanism, operates through canonical inflammasome pathways activated by pathological stimuli, leading to caspase-1 activation and GSDMD cleavage, which triggers maturation of inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18. Gasdermin proteins mediate the formation of plasma membrane pores, causing cell swelling and rupture that releases inflammatory contents, simultaneously promoting both tumor cell death and creating a pro-tumor microenvironment through inflammatory cytokine signaling. Fifteen pyroptosis-related genes including CASP6, BAK1, GSDMD, and IL-18 were significantly elevated in UCEC tumors, while eleven genes including NLRP1, SIRT1, and TIRAP showed reduced expression in tumor samples. These findings establish pyroptosis-related gene signatures as clinically relevant prognostic tools for UCEC and suggest their potential utility in predicting responses to immunotherapies such as pembrolizumab and dostarlimab.

title: Molecular methods applied to gynecological diseases: a laboratory review
summary:
# Summary

This laboratory review examines molecular methods for diagnosing gynecological diseases, with particular focus on endometriosis, cancer-associated pathways, and reproductive disorders. Endometriosis is characterized as a complex condition involving genetic factors (including PI3K, AKT, and inflammasome components like NLRP3 and Caspase-1), hormonal dysregulation, and chronic inflammation that drives tissue growth outside the uterine cavity. The review highlights that cervical cancer pathogenesis is strongly linked to HPV infection, particularly types 16 and 18, which invade basal epithelial cells and produce oncogenic E6 and E7 proteins that inhibit tumor suppressors; notably, reduced lactobacilli populations correlate with increased HPV expression and cervical inflammation. Additionally, the article discusses how BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations impair DNA repair and genomic stability leading to breast cancer risk, alongside other genes like TP53 and PIK3CA that contribute to malignant progression. Molecular techniques such as PCR-based genetic analysis are presented as valuable tools for identifying disease-associated gene alterations and guiding diagnostic approaches to these complex gynecological pathologies.

title: Integrated multi-omics analysis and predictive modeling of heart failure using sepsis-related gene signature
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a multi-omics predictive model for sepsis-induced heart failure using sepsis-related gene signatures, identifying 340 upregulated and 333 downregulated genes associated with the condition. Sepsis causes cardiac dysfunction through multiple mechanisms, including excessive catecholamine production that impairs myocardial function, tachycardia-induced decreased coronary perfusion, and elevated inflammatory mediators such as CXCL8 and endothelin-1. A six-gene signature model demonstrated superior predictive performance for heart failure risk and was associated with alterations in immune checkpoint molecules (PDCD1, CD274, CTLA4, and LAG3), reflecting significant immune infiltration changes in heart failure patients. The upregulated genes were enriched in pathways involving mitotic division and mitochondrial respiration, while downregulated genes were involved in metabolic regulation, suggesting broad systemic dysregulation during sepsis-complicated heart failure. Biomarkers including BNP, NT-proBNP, and endothelin-1 were elevated in septic patients with concurrent heart failure and associated with cardiac dysfunction. Early therapeutic intervention with beta-blockers and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists effectively reduced heart failure incidence, offering potential treatment strategies for this serious complication.

title: Identification of novel causally related genes in adenomyosis: An integrated summary data-based Mendelian randomization study and bioinformatics analysis
summary: This integrated Mendelian randomization and bioinformatics study identified 24 novel protein-coding genes causally linked to adenomyosis, including ARHGEF35, AMT, RCVRN, GMPPB, and INTS1 as the most relevant candidates. Adenomyosis, characterized by invasion and proliferation of endometrial glands and stroma into the myometrium, is a complex disorder involving hormonal, genetic, epigenetic, immune, inflammatory, and environmental factors that collectively contribute to disease pathogenesis. The study revealed distinct expression patterns: DNA2 and INTS1 are elevated in adenomyosis, while EFCAB2, HLA-DQA2, and RPS26 are reduced, with ARHGEF35 showing negative correlation with adenomyosis risk and expression-trait associations in both whole blood and uterine tissues. These identified genes function in critical biological pathways including base-excision repair, cell cycle regulation, and metabolism-related processes, suggesting multiple molecular mechanisms underlying adenomyosis development. Clinically, adenomyosis causes significant morbidity through abnormal uterine bleeding, menorrhagia, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and reduced fertility while elevating risks of pregnancy complications and adverse outcomes. These novel genetic findings provide potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets for improving diagnosis and treatment of this prevalent gynecological disorder.

title: Structural variation detection and association analysis of whole‐genome‐sequence data from 16,543 Alzheimer's disease sequencing project subjects
summary: This large-scale genomic study of 16,543 Alzheimer's disease subjects demonstrates that structural variations (SVs) play a significant role in AD susceptibility beyond common genetic variants. The analysis identified over 400,000 SVs, with singletons and homozygous deletions showing statistically significant associations with AD risk. Ultra-rare protein-altering SVs were discovered in established AD genes including ABCA7, APP, PLCG2, and SORL1, while a novel deletion near RNA5SP293 showed reproducible association with disease. Twenty-one SVs demonstrated linkage disequilibrium with known AD-risk variants, suggesting they may tag previously identified risk loci. The findings reveal that SVs are enriched in neuronal function pathways and collectively account for a portion of the unexplained genetic heritability in AD, highlighting that comprehensive structural variant detection is essential for understanding Alzheimer's disease genetics alongside the well-characterized roles of amyloid-β pathology, neurofibrillary tangles, and established risk genes like APOE.

title: The MAP kinase negative regulator DUSP2 (dual specificity phosphatase 2) is controlled by oncogenic microRNA cluster miR-17-92, miR-106a-363 and miR-106b-25
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates how oncogenic microRNA clusters suppress DUSP2, a critical negative regulator of the MAPK signaling pathway involved in cancer development. Multiple microRNAs from the miR-17-92, miR-106a-363, and miR-106b-25 clusters directly bind to the 3'UTR of DUSP2 mRNA and inhibit its expression across diverse cancer types including lymphoma, prostate cancer, and kidney carcinomas. Reduced DUSP2 levels fail to adequately suppress MAPK pathway activation, leading to dysregulated cell proliferation and poor clinical outcomes including metastasis formation. Comprehensive analysis across 32 cancer types revealed significant inverse correlations between microRNA and DUSP2 expression, with specific examples in lymphoma, leukemia, glioma, and prostate cancer showing this regulatory relationship. The findings suggest that microRNA-mediated downregulation of DUSP2 represents a key mechanism by which cancer cells escape negative feedback control of MAPK signaling. This regulatory axis may provide therapeutic opportunities through microRNA inhibition or restoration of DUSP2-mediated MAPK suppression.

title: Immune Regulation and ECM-Related Pathway Enrichment Reveal ATP2A3 as a Prognostic Biomarker for Nonspecific Orbital Inflammation: An Integrated Machine Learning and Mendelian Randomization Analysis
summary: This integrated machine learning and Mendelian randomization study identifies ATP2A3 as a key prognostic biomarker for nonspecific orbital inflammation (NSOI), a condition characterized by periorbital swelling, pain, erythema, and potentially sight-threatening complications including ophthalmoplegia and visual acuity compromise. ATP2A3, which encodes the calcium-regulating protein SERCA3, is significantly upregulated in NSOI tissues and modulates calcium homeostasis and endoplasmic reticulum function—processes fundamental to protein folding, signal transduction, and apoptosis. The study reveals that elevated ATP2A3 expression associates with immune infiltration changes, specifically increased memory B cells, M2 macrophages, regulatory T cells, and monocytes, while inversely correlating with naive B cells and plasma cells, suggesting ATP2A3 orchestrates a shift toward anti-inflammatory immune responses. Pathway enrichment analysis demonstrates that genes positively correlated with ATP2A3 are enriched in sensory perception and extracellular matrix organization, linking calcium dysregulation to inflammatory responses and tissue remodeling. Gene expression clustering identified ATP2A3, FAM46C, and KCNN4 as upregulated in NSOI, while multiple genes including TMEM30B, CSTA, and SIX1 were downregulated, establishing a distinct molecular signature predictive of disease status. These findings position ATP2A3 as a potential therapeutic target for NSOI management through restoration of calcium homeostasis and immune regulation.

title: Perineural invasion is associated with function-evoked pain and altered extracellular matrix in patients with head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Perineural invasion (PNI) in head and neck squamous cell carcinoma independently predicts function-evoked pain and worst pain intensity, emerging as a critical determinant of pain phenotype in affected patients. The molecular basis of PNI-associated pain involves upregulation of genes including FABP4, MMP10, MMP1, TFPI2, and FAP, which collectively drive disruptions in extracellular matrix organization and remodeling—key pathological hallmarks of PNI. These upregulated genes coordinately promote inflammation, extracellular matrix degradation, and neurodegenerative processes while suppressing protective pathways, with FABP4 notably inhibiting anti-nociceptive PPARγ signaling. Major oncogenic signaling cascades including TNF, IL-17, PI3K-Akt, and TGF-β are perturbed in PNI and contribute to both pain generation and tumor progression toward nodal metastasis. The dysregulation of this integrated system correlates with aggressive tumor characteristics including increased depth of invasion, nodal involvement, and lymphovascular invasion, demonstrating that PNI-associated pain reflects fundamentally altered biology linking tumor invasiveness to neuropathic pain mechanisms.

title: Drug Search and Design Considering Cell Specificity of Chemically Induced Gene Expression Profiles for Disease‐Associated Tissues
summary: This study presents a novel computational approach for drug discovery that leverages cell-specific gene expression profiling to identify therapeutic compounds. The researchers developed an inverse correlation method that identifies compounds capable of counteracting disease-specific gene expression patterns by comparing chemically induced profiles with disease-induced profiles across disease-relevant cell types. Using tissue-associated cell lines (gastric, skin, and breast cells), the method successfully detected compounds with diverse chemical structures that effectively reversed abnormal gene expression in gastric cancer and atopic dermatitis models. The approach resolved technical challenges such as missing data through tensor decomposition algorithms and demonstrated that matching cell types between disease and drug screening is essential for omics-based drug discovery. This cell-type-specific strategy identified candidate compounds for multiple diseases including endometriosis, inflammatory bowel disease, and cancer, offering a more physiologically relevant alternative to conventional drug screening methods.

title: Ferroptosis and Sterol Biosynthesis Dysregulation in Granulosa Cells of Patients with Diminished Ovarian Reserve
summary:
# Summary

Granulosa cell dysfunction in patients with diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) is characterized by ferroptosis and dysregulation of sterol and cholesterol metabolism. RNA-seq analysis revealed enrichment of cholesterol/sterol biosynthesis pathways in granulosa cells from DOR patients, accompanied by upregulation of mevalonate pathway genes (HMGCR, SQLE, SREBF2) and downregulation of ferroptosis-protective factors GPX4 and SLC7A11, with increased iron accumulation indicative of ferroptosis. Metabolomics of follicular fluid further demonstrated enrichment in steroid hormone biosynthesis, tryptophan metabolism, and fatty acid oxidation in DOR patients. The granulosa cell dysfunction manifests as decreased proliferation, elevated apoptosis, and marked downregulation of key reproductive signaling molecules including FSHR, CYP19A1, NR5A1, and phosphorylated CREB, resulting in impaired androgen-to-estrogen conversion and elevated androgen levels. These molecular abnormalities correlate with clinical DOR markers including elevated baseline FSH, reduced AMH levels, and decreased antral follicle counts, suggesting that ferroptosis-driven disruption of sterol metabolism and hormone signaling contributes to the pathophysiology of diminished ovarian reserve.

title: Unique Function in Cancer Stemness and Prognostic Significance of EMX2 in Esophageal Squamous Cell Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

EMX2, a homeobox transcription factor normally critical for central nervous system and genitourinary development, plays a paradoxical role in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) where its expression is significantly reduced compared to normal tissues. Despite lower overall expression in ESCC, EMX2 expression in the remaining tumor cells is strongly associated with enhanced cancer stemness, increased colony formation capacity, and notably poor patient prognosis across disease-specific survival, progression-free survival, and disease-free survival metrics. EMX2 regulates cellular differentiation, migration, and proliferation through transcriptional programs and specifically controls the side population fraction—a marker of cancer stem cells—with enforced EMX2 expression decreasing the side population ratio while paradoxically increasing colony formation in the remaining stem cell population. The EMX2 splice variant 1 (sp1) is predominantly expressed in ESCC cell lines, and EMX2 expression correlates with 5-fluorouracil sensitivity and increased tumor-initiating capacity in xenograft models. These findings identify EMX2 as a unique stamped marker of poor prognosis in ESCC and suggest its critical involvement in regulating tumor stemness, distinct from traditional proliferation markers.

title: Differential Expression of lncRNAs in Ovarian Tissue of Meigu Goats During the Sexually Immature and Mature Periods
summary: This study identified 966 novel long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) in goat ovarian tissue and demonstrated their critical involvement in the transition from sexual immaturity to sexual maturity. Among 95 differentially expressed lncRNAs (DElncRNAs) identified between immature and mature periods, their target genes were enriched in key reproductive pathways including JAK-STAT signaling, Wnt/β-catenin signaling, progesterone-mediated oocyte maturation, and PI3K-Akt signaling. Two lncRNAs, MSTRG.15120.9 and MSTRG.15110.2, emerged as major regulators of ovarian development through interactions with genes including JAK3, INSL3, and inhibin subunits. The JAK-STAT pathway was specifically highlighted as essential for preserving primordial follicle reserves and supporting granulosa cell proliferation, while Wnt signaling regulated follicular development, ovulation, and granulosa cell function. These findings establish lncRNAs as integral molecular regulators of reproductive maturation through their modulation of hormone secretion and follicular development processes.

title: RNA-Seq Uncovers Association of Endocrine-Disrupting Chemicals with Hub Genes and Transcription Factors in Aggressive Prostate Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This RNA-Seq study identifies molecular mechanisms linking endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) to aggressive prostate cancer development through dysregulation of hub genes and transcription factors. EDCs promote prostate cancer aggressiveness and poor prognosis by inducing DNA mutations, chromosomal abnormalities, and inflammation, while disrupting androgen receptor (AR) signaling—a critical pathway in which AR binds testosterone and DHT to drive cancer cell survival and proliferation. Key hub genes including CCNB1, BUB1B, and CCNA2 are significantly elevated in prostate cancer samples and strongly associated with high Gleason scores, functioning in chromosome segregation and mitotic regulation. Transcription factors E2F4, MYC, and YBX1 coordinately regulate differentially expressed genes involved in cancer progression, while upregulated genes (NCAPH, RAD54L, E2F7, MKI67) correlate with immune cell infiltration including neutrophils and macrophages. Overall, these hub genes and their associated transcription factors collectively predict overall survival and represent critical nodes in EDC-driven prostate cancer pathogenesis.

title: The Prognostic Value of the Hedgehog Signaling Pathway in Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

The hedgehog signaling pathway functions as a major regulator of cell growth and differentiation and plays a critical role in ovarian cancer pathogenesis, with dysregulated hedgehog signaling driving malignant transformation, clonal growth, and proliferation in ovarian tumors. Multiple hedgehog pathway genes display elevated expression in ovarian carcinomas, including SHH, DHH, PTCH1, PTCH2, SMO, and GLI1, with specific genes exhibiting divergent prognostic significance. Activation of ligands and receptors in the canonical pathway—particularly SHH, PTCH1, and PTCH2—correlates with improved progression-free and overall survival in ovarian cancer patients, especially in advanced FIGO stages III-IV. Conversely, elevated expression of negative regulators and alternative effectors, particularly SUFU and GLI3, consistently associates with adverse outcomes, worse progression-free survival, and poorer overall survival across multiple cancer cohorts. The hedgehog pathway therefore emerges as a stratified prognostic indicator in ovarian cancer, with distinct gene products serving as either favorable or unfavorable prognostic markers that could inform treatment selection and patient risk assessment in this disease.

title: A Proteogenomic View of Synchronous Endometrioid Endometrial and Ovarian Cancer
journal: Clinical cancer research : an official journal of the American Association for Cancer Research
summary:
# Summary

Synchronous endometrial and ovarian cancer (SEOC) represents clonally related malignancies that typically present as low-grade endometrioid histology in younger premenopausal women and paradoxically carry a favorable prognosis despite affecting two anatomic sites. Proteogenomic analysis of 27 SEOC cases revealed that 25 were clonally related, with the most frequently mutated genes being ARID1A, PTEN, PIK3CA, and KRAS—mutations associated with unopposed estrogen exposure and chromatin remodeling dysregulation. The tumor proteome of SEOC clustered more closely with single endometrial endometrioid cancer than with single ovarian endometrioid cancer, while the stromal proteome distinguished SEOC from single cancers, suggesting a distinct microenvironment in dual disease. Mutational signatures and proteome profiling strongly indicate that SEOC originates from the endometrium, with ovarian tumors representing metastases from a minor subclone within the primary uterine lesion rather than independent primary tumors. These findings highlight the clinical importance of distinguishing SEOC from single-site endometrioid cancers, as patients with dual disease benefit from different surgical and treatment considerations despite their overall favorable outcomes.

title: Parity modifies the effect of genetic variants associated with gestational duration and birth weight
summary:
# Parity Modification of Genetic Effects on Gestational Duration and Birth Weight

This study demonstrates that parity significantly modifies the effects of maternal genetic variants on gestational duration, with the strongest evidence showing that polygenic prediction of the maternal genome exhibits substantial interaction with parity status. The maternal genome plays a larger role in determining gestational duration, while the fetal genome predominantly influences birth weight, both of which are linked to short- and long-term adverse health outcomes in mothers and offspring, including preterm birth as a leading cause of neonatal mortality. Multiple genetic loci associated with gestational duration were identified, including previously known variants in WNT4, EBF1, TET3, ADCY5, HAND2, and KCNAB1, along with newly discovered loci in NDST4, WWTR1, HMGA1, and WT1. Notably, first pregnancies show shorter gestational duration and lower birth weight with higher genetic variance, whereas later pregnancies show reduced genetic variance for gestational duration but higher genetic variance for birth weight. The study reveals mechanistic insights through functional analysis: parity downregulates WNT signaling in mammary cells by reducing WNT4 ligand expression and oestrogen/progesterone receptor levels, while genetic variants in WNT4 interact with estrogen receptors and influence uterine size—a key determinant of birth weight. These findings suggest that physiological changes in uterine size and immune function associated with parity status fundamentally alter how genetic variants influence reproductive outcomes across successive pregnancies.

title: Plin2 Coordinates Immune and Metabolic Reprogramming in Lacrimal Gland Aging
summary: This study identifies Plin2 (Perilipin-2) as a master regulator coordinating immune and metabolic reprogramming during lacrimal gland aging. Aging causes progressive tear production decline, gland atrophy, and inflammatory infiltration, accompanied by immune imbalance characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory populations (naïve B cells, memory B cells, M1 macrophages) and reduced regulatory immune cells (plasma cells, M2 macrophages, dendritic cells). Plin2 expression, which is predominantly localized to fibroblasts and monocyte/macrophage/dendritic cell populations, is significantly downregulated in aged tissues and inversely correlates with pro-senescent immune subsets while positively correlating with anti-inflammatory regulators. The decline in Plin2 accelerates inflammaging and metabolic reprogramming through multiple pathways including T/B cell receptor signaling, cytokine interactions, JAK-STAT signaling, and leukocyte migration. These findings establish Plin2 as a critical protective factor against immune-metabolic aging, suggesting that Plin2 restoration or activation may represent a therapeutic strategy to combat age-related lacrimal gland dysfunction and dry eye syndrome.

title: Integrated genetic analysis of Alzheimer's disease and stroke subtypes: insights from LDSC, PLACO, and MR studies
summary: This integrated genetic analysis reveals substantial shared biological mechanisms and genetic architecture between Alzheimer's disease and stroke subtypes. Stroke acts as an independent risk factor for Alzheimer's disease progression, with approximately one-third of stroke patients developing dementia with AD-like symptoms within three years, suggesting a causal relationship between these conditions. Common pathophysiological pathways involving excitotoxicity-induced neuronal loss, amyloid-beta and tau deposition, neuroinflammation, and microglia activation underlie both diseases, while genetic variants in APP, PSEN1, and PSEN2 directly cause early-onset familial AD. Genome-wide association studies identified multiple shared genetic loci (including ALDH1A2, ANKRD22, ANTXR1, HDAC9, MMP3, MMP12, and others) associated with both AD and stroke subtypes, with particularly strong positive genetic correlations observed between AD and all major stroke subtypes (AIS, AS, LAS, SVS). Chromosomes 11 and 19 emerge as critical regions harboring significantly correlated single nucleotide polymorphisms across multiple AD-stroke subtype pairs, with specific variants such as rs3818361 and rs11988 reaching genome-wide significance. These findings indicate substantial genetic overlap driving shared susceptibility to both neurodegenerative and cerebrovascular disease, supporting the hypothesis that AD patients carry elevated stroke risk through common genetic mechanisms.

title: Screening and validation of long non-coding RNAs associated with colorectal cancer based on random forest and LASSO regression algorithm
summary: This study employed random forest and LASSO regression algorithms to identify and validate five long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) associated with colorectal cancer: NCAL1, CRNDE, HMGA1P4, EPIST, and MT1JP. Three lncRNAs—NCAL1, CRNDE, and HMGA1P4—were significantly upregulated in colorectal cancer tissues, while EPIST and MT1JP were downregulated compared to normal tissues. These five lncRNAs demonstrated diagnostic potential, with MT1JP showing the highest performance (AUC 0.919, sensitivity 86.3%, specificity 83.6%), positioning them as candidate biomarkers for colorectal cancer detection. Additionally, the study identified multiple miRNA interactions for each lncRNA, including CRNDE binding to six distinct miRNAs, suggesting these molecules regulate cancer-related pathways through competing endogenous RNA mechanisms. The expression patterns of HMGA1P4 and MT1JP also correlated with TNM staging, with higher expression in early-stage tumors, indicating potential prognostic utility in risk stratification.

title: Integrated eQTL-pQTL Mendelian randomization and single-cell sequencing reveal therapeutic targets in ovarian clear cell cancer
summary: Ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) presents a therapeutic challenge due to its poor response to platinum-based chemotherapy, immunotherapy, and PARP inhibitors, combined with rapid development of chemoresistance and an immune-cold phenotype. Through integrated eQTL-pQTL Mendelian randomization and single-cell sequencing analysis, the study identified PPP1R14A and PTGS2 as key protective genes, with reduced expression in OCCC patients correlating with lower disease risk. Both genes showed tissue-specific expression patterns—PPP1R14A predominantly in epithelial cancer cells and PTGS2 in immune cell populations—and participate in critical pathways including ovarian steroidogenesis and VEGF signaling. PTGS2 demonstrated particular druggable potential based on drug prediction and molecular docking analyses, while both genes showed synergistic interactions with tumor progression markers (ATM, BRCA1, MET, and TP53). These findings establish PPP1R14A and PTGS2 as promising therapeutic targets relevant to tumor immunotherapy, offering a potential avenue to overcome OCCC's notorious treatment resistance. Additionally, the study identified 154 genes associated with increased OCCC risk, including F5, NUDT2, and CCR3, providing a comprehensive genetic landscape for future therapeutic development.

title: Comprehensive bioinformatics analysis of the common mechanism of atherosclerosis and atrial fibrillation: emphasizing mitochondrial metabolic disorder and immune inflammation
summary: This bioinformatics study identifies shared molecular mechanisms linking atherosclerosis and atrial fibrillation, two clinically intertwined cardiovascular disorders. Both diseases involve dysregulation of mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation and immune-inflammatory pathways, suggesting that impaired mitochondrial metabolism drives pathological immune activation in these conditions. The analysis identified MRPS23, a mitochondrial ribosomal protein, as a key biomarker that bridges mitochondrial dysfunction and immune dysregulation in both atherosclerosis and atrial fibrillation; its downregulation in atherosclerotic tissues correlates with loss of mitochondrial ribosomal integrity and reduced function of immune cell subsets. Additional hub genes including CASP8 show strong associations with immune cells and disease pathology. Both atherosclerosis and atrial fibrillation significantly increase stroke risk through distinct mechanisms—atherosclerosis causes structural arterial damage while atrial fibrillation promotes thromboembolism—and statin therapy effectively reduces atherosclerosis-associated mortality. These findings suggest that therapeutic strategies targeting mitochondrial metabolism and immune regulation may address the common pathophysiological basis underlying both cardiovascular conditions.

title: Identification and validation of key target genes of penning formula for treating rat chronic endometritis model
summary: Chronic endometritis, a persistent inflammatory condition of the endometrium primarily caused by *Escherichia coli*, leads to chronic lower abdominal pain and infertility. This study identified three key target genes—Bhmt, Scn10a, and Esr2—that are significantly upregulated in chronic endometritis and downregulated by Penning Formula (PNF), a traditional herbal remedy. Bhmt participates in one-carbon metabolism, Scn10a encodes the Nav1.8 sodium channel involved in pain transmission, and Esr2 encodes estrogen receptor 2 which modulates estrogen signaling and inflammasome complex interactions. PNF exerts its therapeutic effects by inhibiting the TLR4 signaling pathway and downregulating MyD88 and NF-κB, while its active ingredients including baicalin and quercetin directly bind to Esr2. These findings elucidate the molecular mechanisms by which PNF treats chronic endometritis through modulation of inflammation, pain signaling, and estrogen-mediated immune responses.

title: Investigation of LBX1, TIMP2, GPR126 and CHD7 Gene Polymorphisms in Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis Patients
summary: This study investigates genetic polymorphisms in four candidate genes—LBX1, TIMP2, GPR126, and CHD7—as potential contributors to adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) in a Turkish population. The LBX1 gene, which plays critical roles in neuromuscular development, muscle precursor cell migration, and somatosensory relay neuron development, showed strong associations with AIS, particularly the rs11190870 polymorphism and its T allele, with higher frequency in females. GPR126, which regulates skeletal growth and human height, and TIMP2, which inhibits matrix metalloproteinases and remodels the extracellular matrix during endochondral ossification, are implicated in the disproportionate vertebral growth patterns characteristic of AIS. The study reveals that dysregulation of these genes may lead to asymmetric paraspinal muscles, dysfunctional somatosensory systems, and altered anterior vertebral column growth contributing to thoracic hypokyphosis. Additionally, hormonal factors, particularly estrogen's effects on bone development through suppression of osteoclastogenic cytokines and promotion of osteoblast proliferation, help explain the female predominance of AIS. The findings underscore that AIS involves complex interactions among genetic polymorphisms, epigenetic modifications, hormonal influences, and environmental factors such as biomechanical stress.

title: Identification of neutrophil extracellular trap-related biomarkers in ulcerative colitis based on bioinformatics and machine learning
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies neutrophil extracellular trap (NET)-related biomarkers in ulcerative colitis through integrated bioinformatics and machine learning approaches. Three core genes—IL1B, MMP9, and DYSF—were consistently overexpressed in UC patients and showed strong associations with neutrophil infiltration and immune cell populations including CD4+ memory T cells and M2 macrophages. These genes are centrally involved in NET formation and mediate UC pathology through leukocyte activation, migration, and multiple inflammatory signaling pathways including IL-17, TNF, and NF-κB cascades. NETs themselves contribute to disease by disrupting the intestinal epithelial barrier, promoting inflammation through release of damaging components (myeloperoxidase, lactoferrin, calprotectin), and triggering production of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-17A. Machine learning analysis predicted gevokizumab, canakinumab, and carboxylated glucosamine as potential therapeutic candidates targeting these NET-related pathways in UC.

title: Biomarker discovery for non-invasive diagnosis of inflammatory bowel disease using blood transcriptomics
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies blood transcriptomic biomarkers for non-invasive diagnosis of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), which presents clinically with abdominal pain, diarrhea, and intestinal inflammation.
