Blood transcriptome analysis revealed characteristic immune alterations in IBD patients, including elevated M0 macrophages, regulatory T cells, CD4 naïve T cells, CD8 T cells, and neutrophils, alongside decreased memory B lymphocytes and activated natural killer cells. A three-gene biomarker panel consisting of IL4R, EIF5A, and SLC9A8 achieved 84% diagnostic accuracy, with IL4R and SLC9A8 upregulated and EIF5A downregulated in IBD patients. The study identified mitochondrial complex I dysfunction as a critical mechanism, with NDUFB2 downregulation impairing electron transport and elevating reactive oxygen species production, contributing to systemic inflammation and oxidative stress. Additionally, IBD patients demonstrated significantly reduced hemoglobin and vitamin D levels, elevated inflammatory markers (white blood cells, C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate), and dysregulation of oxidative phosphorylation pathways, consistent with the Th1/Th17 inflammatory profile also observed in rheumatoid arthritis.

title: Barrier genes are associated with preterm birth
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies multiple barrier genes associated with preterm birth through analysis of the maternal genome, with NOTCH1 emerging as a particularly critical regulatory hub. NOTCH1 and related barrier genes (LAMA4, F11R, MAGI1, MAGI2, TJP1, PARD3, claudins, and others) are essential for maintaining epithelial and endothelial integrity through tight junction regulation, vascular barrier function, and cell-cell adhesion. NOTCH1 signaling plays multifaceted roles in pregnancy, including decidualization progression, trophoblast invasion, spiral artery remodeling, and placental angiogenesis through its interaction with Delta-like ligand 4 and subsequent regulation of target genes like PGR and SLC2A1. Disruption of NOTCH1 signaling is associated with numerous reproductive complications including preeclampsia, intrauterine growth restriction, endometriosis, adenomyosis, and infertility, suggesting that barrier gene dysfunction compromises the maternal-fetal interface. These findings highlight how impaired barrier function—mediated by defects in tight junction proteins (F11R, TJP1, MAGI1) and signaling molecules—may contribute to preterm birth pathogenesis by destabilizing the endometrial and placental barriers critical for pregnancy maintenance.

title: Characterizing healthcare resource utilization in two rare diseases (Kleefstra syndrome and SLC6A1 epileptic encephalopathy) using multimodal real-world data
summary: This article characterizes healthcare resource utilization (HRU) patterns in Kleefstra syndrome and SLC6A1 epileptic encephalopathy, two rare genetic neurodevelopmental disorders with distinct molecular etiologies. Kleefstra syndrome results from loss-of-function mutations in EHMT1, which encodes a histone methyltransferase involved in epigenetic regulation, leading to altered DNA methylation patterns and impaired neuronal development that manifests as developmental delays, intellectual disability, hypotonia, and dysmorphic facial features. SLC6A1 epileptic encephalopathy stems from pathogenic variants in SLC6A1, which encodes GAT-1, a GABA transporter expressed in brain GABAergic neurons and astrocytes; loss of GAT-1 function reduces GABA reuptake and causes seizures and hypotonia. Post-diagnosis, Kleefstra syndrome patients experienced increased cardiology, sleep medicine, and radiology encounters, with significantly elevated cardiac imaging studies (echocardiograms and ECGs), reflecting previously undiagnosed cardiac abnormalities. Both conditions showed decreased genetics encounters post-diagnosis, though current symptomatic therapies such as levetiracetam for SLC6A1 and polyethylene glycol for Kleefstra syndrome do not address the underlying genetic etiology and thus likely do not substantially reduce disease burden. The findings underscore that disease-modifying therapies targeting the genetic basis would be needed to meaningfully decrease healthcare resource utilization, in contrast to conditions like cystic fibrosis and spinal muscular atrophy that have benefited from such approaches.

title: Immunogenic cell death-related biomarkers in heart failure probed by transcriptome and single-cell sequencing
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies immunogenic cell death (ICD) as a key mechanism in heart failure progression and reveals three monocyte/macrophage-expressed biomarkers—CD163, FPR1, and VSIG4—with significant diagnostic value for heart failure. Single-cell RNA sequencing demonstrated that these biomarkers are primarily localized to monocytes and macrophages, where they show dynamic expression patterns: CD163 and VSIG4 decrease over time while FPR1 increases. These three markers correlate strongly with CD8+ T cells and M2 macrophages, and communicate with cardiac fibroblasts, suggesting their involvement in immune-mediated cardiac remodeling. Pathway analysis revealed that candidate genes enriched in the ICD-related network are involved in inflammatory response regulation, phagosome formation, and neutrophil extracellular trap formation, implicating antimicrobial and inflammatory immunity in heart failure pathophysiology. The discovery that compounds like carbachol and etynodiol interact with all three biomarkers provides potential therapeutic targets for modulating ICD-driven heart failure progression.

title: Shared genetic loci connect cardiovascular disease with blood pressure and lipid traits in East Asian populations
summary: This study identified shared genetic loci connecting cardiovascular diseases with metabolic and hemodynamic traits in East Asian populations through large-scale genetic correlation analysis. Myocardial infarction and heart failure exhibited strong positive genetic correlations with systolic and diastolic blood pressure, as well as adverse lipid profiles characterized by elevated low-density lipoprotein and triglycerides but reduced high-density lipoprotein. Multiple genes central to lipid metabolism—including APOB, LDLR, APOE, and SURF4—emerged as shared genetic factors influencing both lipid levels and cardiovascular disease risk. Notably, tissue-specific analysis revealed that myocardial infarction-associated variants enriched in coronary arteries while blood pressure variants enriched in aortic tissues, suggesting organ-specific genetic regulation. Several pleiotropic loci were identified, including APOB (jointly associated with heart failure and myocardial infarction), QSOX2 (linked to heart failure and systolic blood pressure through effects on oxidative protein folding), and GUCY1A1/B1 (involved in nitric oxide signaling affecting both myocardial infarction and blood pressure). These findings demonstrate that cardiovascular diseases share common genetic architecture with blood pressure and lipid traits, providing mechanistic insight into their comorbidity patterns in East Asian populations.

title: Aryl hydrocarbon receptor interacting protein and syndromic gene variants detected in Turkish isolated pituitary adenoma families by whole exome sequencing
summary:
# Summary

This whole exome sequencing study of Turkish families with familial isolated pituitary adenomas (FIPA) identified both known and novel genetic variants underlying inherited pituitary tumor susceptibility. Mutations in AIP, the major FIPA gene, were associated with somatotrophinomas, somatolactotrophinomas, and lactotrophinomas that present as large, aggressive tumors in younger patients and demonstrate resistance to somatostatin receptor ligands. Beyond AIP, the researchers detected pathogenic variants in syndromic genes including NF1, TSC1, SDHB, and CDH23, each segregating with specific adenoma phenotypes (GH-secreting, PRL-secreting, or ACTH-secreting). Additionally, variants in genes such as ATP2B3, SQSTM1, LGALS3, NR5A1, PKD1, DRD4, and SYTL3 were identified in patients with particular tumor types, suggesting broader genetic heterogeneity in FIPA pathogenesis. Mechanistically, the study highlights the Hedgehog signaling pathway (PKA/SUFU/GLI1) as a driver of pituitary adenoma cell proliferation and survival, while Galectin-3 emerged as a biomarker for aggressive prolactin-secreting tumors. These findings expand the genetic landscape of FIPA and support molecularly informed approaches to diagnosis and treatment selection in familial pituitary adenoma families.

title: Transcriptomics insight into occupational exposure to engineered nanoparticles
summary: This transcriptomic study reveals that occupational exposure to nanoparticles from welding and mechanical machining processes triggers significant alterations in immune and cancer-related pathways in exposed workers. Inhalation of nanoparticles leads to lung injury through oxidative stress and induces molecular changes including decreased expression of stress-response genes DDIT4 and FKBP5 that correlate with exposure dose. The welding group exhibited elevated levels of carcinogenic microRNAs (miR-30d-5p and miR-3613-5p) and substantial mRNA deregulation affecting immune response, antigen processing, and natural killer cell cytotoxicity, while the machining group showed minimal miRNA changes despite nanoparticle generation. Two distinct gene networks emerged: Cluster 1 encompassed immune-related hub genes (KLRC1, KLRD1, KIR2DL1, CD160), while Cluster 2 contained cancer-pathway genes (ZBTB16, KIT, FLT3) associated with acute myeloid leukemia and mast-cell leukemia pathways. Chronic nanoparticle exposure also triggers epigenetic modifications through DNA methylation alterations, suggesting that occupational nanoparticle exposure poses both acute immunotoxic and long-term carcinogenic risks requiring workplace intervention strategies.

title: Recognition of pivotal immune genes NR1H4 and IL4R as diagnostic biomarkers in distinguishing ovarian clear cell cancer from high-grade serous cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies NR1H4 and IL4R as key diagnostic biomarkers for distinguishing ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) from high-grade serous cancer (HGSC), with both genes showing elevated expression in OCCC and strong diagnostic accuracy (AUC values of 0.809 and 0.840). These biomarkers correlate with specific immune cell populations including memory T cells, natural killer cells, and neutrophils, suggesting their role in shaping the distinct immunological landscape of OCCC. The underlying pathogenic mechanisms in OCCC involve HNF-1β activation of STAT-3 and NF-κB signaling cascades, which drive immunosuppression through production of IL-6 and IL-8 and induction of checkpoint ligands PD-L1 and B7-H1. OCCC tumors exhibit enrichment of plasma cells, M2 macrophages, and neutrophils alongside elevated checkpoint markers (PD-1, CTLA-4, Tim-3, LAG-3) and reduced HLA expression, creating an immunologically "cold" phenotype compared to HGSC. Molecularly, OCCC is characterized by frequent ARID1A and PIK3CA mutations but lacks typical P53 and BRCA1/2 alterations, and exhibits platinum-based chemotherapy resistance with two distinct subtypes—epithelioid OCCC associated with early-stage disease and SWI/SNF mutations, and mesenchymal OCCC linked to advanced disease with heightened immune pathway activation.

title: Investigation of HLA-B –21 M/T Dimorphism and Its Potential Role in COVID-19
summary:
# Summary

The HLA-B –21M/T dimorphism significantly influences natural killer (NK) cell function by controlling the availability of HLA-B leader peptides that stabilize HLA-E expression, which then interacts with the inhibitory NKG2A/CD94 receptor on NK cells. The methionine (–21M) variant promotes efficient HLA-E stabilization and NK cell education, while the threonine (–21T) variant reduces HLA-E surface expression and impairs NK cell function. In COVID-19 patients, individuals carrying the protective M/M genotype show increased HLA-E surface expression, enhanced NKG2A+ NK cell functionality, and higher interferon-gamma production, resulting in less severe disease and reduced need for mechanical respiratory support. Multiple genetic factors including HLA alleles (HLA-A*11:01, HLA-C*04:01 associated with severe disease; HLA-DRB1*04:01 associated with protection), NK cell receptor genes (KLRC2, KIR2DS2, KIR2DL1), and viral entry genes (ACE2, TMPRSS2, SLC6A20) collectively determine COVID-19 severity through modulation of innate immune responses. The study demonstrates that this genetic dimorphism represents an important immunogenetic determinant of susceptibility to severe COVID-19, with implications for understanding both antiviral and antitumor NK cell immunity.

title: Expression pattern changes of three homeologs in chemokine activity enhance antiviral response to herpesvirus infection in a newly synthesized alloheptaploid
summary:
# Summary

A newly synthesized alloheptaploid fish (CaA7n) combining genetic material from two parental species exhibits enhanced resistance to cyprinid herpesvirus (CaHV) through reconfiguration of homeolog expression networks, particularly those governing chemokine activity. CaA7n inherited 100% resistance from its pathogenic-resistant parent M. amblycephala while maintaining chromosomal content from the susceptible C. gibelio parent, resulting in dramatically lower viral loads compared to the highly susceptible C. gibelio clone. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that three homeologs derived from M. amblycephala and associated with chemokine activity exhibited altered expression patterns following CaHV infection, with eigengenes in a "blue module" positively correlated with viral resistance and enriched in chemokine-related gene ontology terms. This antiviral enhancement was achieved through coordinated activation of Toll-like receptor signaling, viral protein-cytokine interaction pathways, and C-type lectin receptor signaling in infected CaA7n, contrasting sharply with minimal transcriptional changes in resistant M. amblycephala and massive dysregulation in susceptible C. gibelio. These findings demonstrate that allopolyploidy-driven genome reconfiguration can generate novel antiviral defenses, with implications for understanding pathogen resistance mechanisms in polyploid organisms.

title: Identification and analysis of pyroptosis-related key genes in heart failure
summary:
# Summary

This bioinformatics study identifies pyroptosis as a pivotal mechanism in heart failure pathogenesis and pinpoints seven pyroptosis-related genes—SNORD76, RPS3A, SNORD1A, CCDC159, AMT, RANBP6, and CRAT—as key diagnostic and therapeutic targets with superior diagnostic performance in heart failure. Most of these genes (SNORD76, SNORD1A, CCDC159, AMT, and RANBP6) are significantly upregulated in heart failure, while the transcription factor PHF8 regulates both CRAT and AMT expression. The pyroptosis mechanism involves inflammasome activation (particularly NLRP3) and gasdermin D-mediated cell death, which triggers innate immune responses and is associated with five distinct immune cell types. Functional enrichment analysis reveals that dysregulated genes participate in protein targeting, cholesterol metabolism, fatty acid metabolism, and steroid hormone biosynthesis—pathways critical to cardiac dysfunction. Notably, AMT, RPS3A, and CRAT correspond to eight potential therapeutic drugs, offering translational opportunities for targeting pyroptosis-driven heart failure.

title: Early NOTCH1 mutation is positively selected but epistatically suppresses evolution of later esophageal squamous-cell carcinoma drivers
summary:
# Early NOTCH1 Mutations and Esophageal Squamous-Cell Carcinoma Evolution

NOTCH1 mutations are strongly positively selected during early esophageal tissue development and clonal expansion in histologically normal epithelium, paradoxically inhibiting malignant progression despite driving clonal growth. The study reveals that NOTCH1 mutations exhibit antagonistic epistasis with canonical tumor suppressors TP53 and RB1, meaning that early NOTCH1 alterations actively suppress the selective advantage of these later driver mutations. While other genes like NOTCH2 and FAT1 similarly inhibit tumorigenesis, NOTCH1 specifically demonstrates a unique evolutionary role where its early acquisition creates a cellular state refractory to further oncogenic mutations. This discovery reshapes understanding of esophageal squamous-cell carcinoma development by showing that early "driver" mutations can actually impede subsequent malignant evolution through epistatic interactions. The findings suggest that somatic mutations accumulating in normal tissues may establish pathways that fundamentally constrain rather than promote cancer development, highlighting the complex selective pressures governing the multi-step progression from normal epithelium to invasive malignancy.

title: Multi-omics analysis of the anti-cancer effects of curcumol in endometrial carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics study demonstrates that curcumol, a natural sesquiterpenoid compound from *Curcuma longa*, exhibits significant anticancer effects against endometrial carcinoma through inhibition of the JAK2/STAT3 signaling pathway and direct binding to progesterone receptor (PGR) and ribosomal protein S6 kinase A1 (RPS6KA1). Molecular docking confirmed strong binding affinities between curcumol and these core targets, which are regulated upstream by transcriptional factors MAZ and long non-coding RNAs KCNQ1OT1 and chr22-38_28785274–29006793.1. The expression profiles showed that PGR is significantly downregulated in endometrial carcinoma tissues compared to normal tissue, while RPS6KA1 is upregulated; elevated PGR and RPS6KA1 expression correlated with prolonged patient survival. Mechanistically, PGR suppresses cell cycle progression and DNA replication while activating metabolic pathways including butanoate and fatty acid metabolism, whereas RPS6KA1 promotes metabolic reprogramming through glycolysis and nucleotide sugar metabolism while inhibiting cancer-related pathways. These findings provide a comprehensive molecular basis for curcumol's therapeutic potential in treating endometrial carcinoma through modulation of key regulatory networks involved in cell survival and proliferation.

title: Genetic Predisposition in Müllerian Malformations: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Multiple genes involved in embryonic development regulate normal formation of the female reproductive tract, and mutations in these genes cause Müllerian malformations with significant clinical consequences. Key developmental regulators include HOX genes (particularly HOXA10 and HOXA11), which control uterine patterning and endometrial maturation, along with MRKH syndrome-associated genes such as LHX1, HNF1B, TBX6, WNT4, and GREB1L. Specific mutations produce distinct malformations: TBX6 mutations cause mesodermal defects leading to bicornuate and septate uteri, while HOXA9 and HOXA13 mutations associate with complex polymalformative syndromes including arm agenesis and cardiac defects. Müllerian malformations significantly impact reproductive health, leading to infertility, first-trimester pregnancy losses, premature labor, and fetal growth abnormalities, and are linked to endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease. The case presented involved a 16p11.2 microdeletion contributing to Müllerian aplasia, demonstrating the value of chromosomal microarray and genomic sequencing in identifying causative variants. Comprehensive genetic analysis combined with detailed family history and genetic counseling is recommended for screening patients with hereditary predisposition to these malformations.

title: Mutation profile and chromosomal abnormality in adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis is characterized by somatic mutations in the adenomyotic epithelium, with KRAS mutations being the most frequent (34.1%), followed by PIK3CA, ARID1A, and FBXW7 mutations at lower frequencies. A novel finding is the recurrent gain of chromosome 1q in adenomyotic epithelium, which may promote disease development. KRAS mutations, in particular, are associated with progesterone resistance through decreased progesterone receptor expression and appear to drive the clonal expansion and spread of adenomyotic lesions across multiple sites. The study reveals an oligoclonal origin for adenomyosis, with an ancestral clone carrying cancer-associated gene mutations that expands from normal endometrium into the myometrium, while the adenomyotic epithelium and stroma maintain distinct mutational profiles. These genetic alterations directly contribute to the severe clinical symptoms of adenomyosis, including heavy menstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility.

title: Bioinformatics analysis reveals major hub genes involved with extracellular matrix and inflammatory and endocrine pathways associated with intracranial aneurysm tissue
summary: This bioinformatics study identified eleven hub genes central to intracranial aneurysm pathogenesis, with eight validated candidates including COL1A, CXCR4, IL10, CXCL8, ESR1, APOE, RN1, and IGF1. These hub genes converge on three critical biological processes: extracellular matrix degradation, inflammatory response, and muscle contraction, which collectively characterize the molecular landscape of aneurysm tissue. The analysis revealed significant involvement of immunologic pathways and extracellular matrix remodeling, supporting the long-standing hypothesis that intracranial aneurysm development is driven by complex interplay between genetic predisposition and focal extracellular dysfunction. Additionally, the study identified estrogen receptor signaling and cholesterol metabolism as important endocrine-related processes contributing to aneurysm pathogenesis, suggesting hormonal and metabolic factors modulate disease susceptibility. These findings establish a comprehensive molecular framework for understanding intracranial aneurysm biology and potentially identify therapeutic targets within inflammatory, structural, and endocrine pathways.

title: Integrated Transcriptomic Analysis Provided Diagnostic and Pathophysiological Insights for Epilepsy
summary:
# Summary

This integrated transcriptomic analysis identifies immune dysregulation as a critical pathophysiological mechanism underlying epilepsy, with particular emphasis on T cell dysfunction. SCN1A gene mutations cause neuronal hyperexcitability through disrupted sodium channel and GABA function, while inflammatory mediators like interleukin-1β amplify excitatory signaling by enhancing N-methylaspartate receptor-mediated calcium influx and neuronal excitability. Key immune-related genes (CD3D, CD3G, CTSW, JCHAIN) are significantly downregulated in epilepsy samples and function as critical hub genes in T cell receptor signaling, immune checkpoint regulation, and T helper cell differentiation pathways. The study reveals that suppression of these T cell-associated genes impairs immune function and exacerbates epilepsy progression, suggesting that compromised T cell-mediated autoimmunity may paradoxically contribute to seizure susceptibility. These findings provide diagnostic biomarkers and identify potential immunotherapeutic targets for epilepsy management through restoration of adaptive immune function.

title: Long noncoding RNA LINC00339 promotes osteoporosis development via modulating of regulator CDC42 by binding PARP1
summary: This study identifies a novel pathogenic mechanism in osteoporosis involving the long noncoding RNA LINC00339, which is significantly upregulated in bone tissue from osteoporosis patients and correlates with disease severity as measured by clinical T-scores. LINC00339 functions through a regulatory axis with the DNA repair protein PARP1, which together suppress the expression of CDC42, a key regulator of cellular migration, adhesion, and cytoskeletal organization. Functionally, elevated LINC00339 inhibits osteoblast differentiation and osteogenic gene expression, effects that are reversed by LINC00339 knockdown, which enhances expression of critical osteogenic markers including RUNX2, alkaline phosphatase, and osteocalcin, and accelerates matrix mineralization. The cytoplasmic localization of LINC00339 enables its interaction with PARP1, which collectively modulates transcriptional programs essential for bone homeostasis. These findings establish the LINC00339-PARP1-CDC42 regulatory axis as a critical epigenetic mechanism underlying osteoporosis development and suggest potential therapeutic targets for bone remodeling disorders.

title: Integrating Bulk RNA and Single-Cell Sequencing Data Reveals Genes Related to Energy Metabolism and Efferocytosis in Lumbar Disc Herniation
summary:
# Summary

This integrative analysis of bulk and single-cell RNA sequencing data identifies critical molecular mechanisms in lumbar disc herniation centered on the interplay between energy metabolism, apoptosis, and immune dysfunction. Six interconnected hub genes—IL6R, TNF, MAPK13, ELANE, PLAUR, and ABCA1—are upregulated in herniated discs and primarily function in inflammatory response, chemokine production, and cellular energy metabolism, with predominant expression in chondrocyte-like cells. Mitochondrial dysfunction and energy deficits drive intervertebral disc cell apoptosis, while inefficient clearance of apoptotic cells through efferocytosis leads to secondary necrosis, perpetuating inflammation and tissue degeneration. Elevated infiltration of activated dendritic cells, neutrophils, and gamma delta T cells exacerbates this pathology, with inflammatory cytokines driving pain and further disc degradation. The study reveals that inflammation itself impairs mitochondrial energy metabolism, creating a vicious cycle where impaired homeostatic processes—particularly defective efferocytosis—prevent resolution of inflammation and accelerate disease progression.

title: Genetic Association of PCSK5 and MUC2 Gene Polymorphisms with Recurrent Pregnancy Loss (RPL)
summary: This study identifies genetic polymorphisms in PCSK5 and MUC2 genes as significant biomarkers for recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) through whole-exome sequencing analysis. The PCSK5 rs1110222 G>A polymorphism and its A allele combinations were associated with reduced RPL risk, while the MUC2 rs10902088 C>T polymorphism increased RPL susceptibility, particularly in women with multiple prior losses. PCSK5 encodes a protein involved in proprotein processing critical for embryo implantation and ovarian follicle development, whereas MUC2 supports pregnancy health through mucus layer formation and is dysregulated in the endometrium of women with implantation failure. The study reveals overlapping genetic pathways between endometriosis and RPL, highlighting that polymorphisms in these endometriosis-associated genes contribute to both conditions. Specific allele combinations, including the A-T pairing of PCSK5 rs1110222 and MUC2 rs10902088, demonstrated particularly strong protective effects against RPL, suggesting these genetic variants may serve as prognostic biomarkers for pregnancy outcomes in affected women.

title: Multi- and Transgenerational Histological and Transcriptomic Outcomes of Developmental TCDD Exposure in Zebrafish (Danio rerio) Ovary
summary:
# Summary

Developmental TCDD (2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin) exposure causes multi- and transgenerational reproductive dysfunction in zebrafish, manifesting as subfertility, ovarian atresia, impaired folliculogenesis, and premature ovarian failure in females, alongside decreased sperm concentration and reduced epididymal reserves in males. TCDD disrupts ovarian steroidogenesis by upregulating thyroid hormone (T3) and estradiol (E2) levels through aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) signaling in gonadal tissues, with downstream effects on the RXFP2-INSL3 pathway that regulates androstenedione and estrogen production. Transcriptomic analysis revealed dysregulation of critical pathways including Rho GTPase signaling, neutrophil degranulation, and epigenetic regulators (hdac7, ywhae, mapkapk3) in both F0 and F2 generations, suggesting persistent epigenetic modifications across generations. The study identifies molecular candidates linked to human reproductive pathology, including SPATA22 loss-of-function variants associated with primary ovarian insufficiency and YWHAE alterations observed in gynecological sarcomas. Notably, dioxin-like compounds, including TCDD, promote endometriosis development—a syndrome characterized by infertility, pain, and irregular menstruation—demonstrating direct relevance to human reproductive disease pathogenesis.

title: Identification of a key smooth muscle cell subset driving ischemic cardiomyopathy progression through single-cell RNA sequencing
summary: Using single-cell RNA sequencing of 4,184 smooth muscle cells, this study identified C6 S100A4+ smooth muscle cells (SMCs) as a pathogenic subpopulation critically driving ischemic cardiomyopathy progression. C6 S100A4+ SMCs, which are predominantly enriched in ischemic cardiomyopathy patients, exhibit elevated ferroptosis, oxidative stress, and inflammation, along with reduced differentiation capacity and stemness-associated gene expression including EPAS1, CTNNB1, MYC, and HIF1A. These cells interact with endothelial cells through the PTN-NCL pathway and are characterized by high oxidative phosphorylation metabolism and expression of stress-response genes (IER2, ZFP36, PPP1R15A) while downregulating protective genes like FABP4 and AQP1. The Bach1/HO-1 pathway emerges as a key regulator promoting ferroptosis-driven oxidative stress in these cells, while key transcription factors KLF2, FOS, FOSB, and JUNB orchestrate pathogenic gene expression patterns. These findings identify S100A4 as a potential therapeutic target and suggest that ferroptosis-driven mechanisms in SMCs represent a central mechanism in ischemic cardiomyopathy pathogenesis.

title: Long-read RNA-sequencing reveals transcript-specific regulation in human-derived cortical neurons
summary:
# Summary

This long-read RNA-sequencing study reveals extensive transcript-level complexity in human-derived cortical neurons, identifying over 15,000 transcripts in stem cell-derived cortical neurons compared to approximately 13,000 in fibroblasts and 12,700 in induced pluripotent stem cells. Alternative splicing emerges as a critical regulatory mechanism underlying neuronal development, controlling key processes including neurogenesis, synaptogenesis, axon guidance, and neural plasticity through coordinated action of RNA-binding proteins and transcription factors. Differential transcript usage analysis highlighted disease-relevant genes including APP (Alzheimer's disease), KIF2A (neuronal migration), and multiple hereditary spastic paraplegia-associated genes (BSCL2, REEP1, UCHL1, KIF5A), suggesting that transcript-specific variants may underlie neurodegenerative pathology. The reprogramming of patient-derived fibroblasts into cortical neurons involves coordinated regulation of mRNA processing, translation, and chromatin organization, with enriched signaling through Rho GTPases and focal adhesion components. Specific transcript isoforms such as GAP43-201 (neuronal growth and plasticity), TUBA1A-202 (cytoskeletal organization), and BEX1-201 (neuronal differentiation) were prominently expressed, while long noncoding RNAs like GAS5 showed extraordinary transcript diversity with 45 variants, indicating their potential regulatory roles in neural differentiation.

title: A Systematic Evidence‐Based Review Regarding miRNA Polymorphisms in Recurrent Implantation Failure
summary:
# Summary

MicroRNAs regulate fundamental biological processes including post-transcriptional gene silencing, cell proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis, and dysregulated miRNA expression has been linked to impaired fertility and recurrent implantation failure (RIF). Altered microRNA activity contributes to increased RIF susceptibility, endometriosis, and impaired endometrial receptivity by disrupting gene regulatory networks involved in endometrial receptivity, immune modulation, and hormone signaling. Specific miRNA polymorphisms are strongly associated with RIF risk, including single variants (miR-34a, miR-449b, miR-196a2) and protective genotypes (miR-1302-3), with certain haplotype combinations demonstrating substantially elevated or reduced risk of implantation failure. Additionally, several miRNA variants correlate with altered hemostatic and endocrine parameters including coagulation factors, FSH, TSH, and blood urea nitrogen levels, suggesting pleiotropic effects on multiple reproductive and metabolic pathways. These findings establish miRNA polymorphisms as potential genetic biomarkers for identifying women at increased RIF risk and suggest therapeutic opportunities targeting dysregulated miRNA expression to improve implantation outcomes.

title: SLC2A1 and MPST as diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers of potential endometrial cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies SLC2A1 and MPST as dual diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers for uterine corpus endometrial cancer (UCEC). Both genes show strong associations with immune cell infiltration and are regulated by DNA methylation patterns at CpG islands, establishing their value for cancer classification and prognosis prediction. SLC2A1, a glucose transporter, is broadly overexpressed across 22 cancer types including UCEC and activates the PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway to promote cell proliferation and survival; it also interacts with KRAS to enhance the Warburg effect in tumorigenesis. MPST functions as a key hydrogen sulfide biosynthesis enzyme involved in critical cellular processes including tRNA sulfuration, oxidative stress protection, and mitochondrial function, and is similarly elevated across multiple cancer tissues including endometrial tumors. The integrated analysis of these two biomarkers provides complementary mechanistic insights into endometrial cancer biology through glucose metabolism and hydrogen sulfide signaling pathways, offering improved diagnostic accuracy and prognostic stratification.

title: Analysis of histone modifications in key cellular subpopulations in the context of azoospermia using spermatogenic single-cell RNA-seq data
summary:
# Summary

This study analyzed histone modifications across testicular cell subpopulations in non-obstructive azoospermia using single-cell RNA-sequencing, revealing that aberrant epigenetic regulation drives male infertility. In healthy spermatogenesis, histone modifications orchestrate chromatin remodeling through HDAC2-mediated deacetylation and Sirt1-dependent H4 acetylation, processes essential for proper germ cell differentiation and sperm production. In azoospermic tissues, multiple pathological changes emerged: spermatogenic cells, spermatogonia, and spermatids were significantly reduced, while non-reproductive cell populations (endothelial, interstitial, and vascular smooth muscle cells, macrophages) accumulated abnormally. Dysregulated histone modifiers, particularly elevated EZH2 and IL-6 expression with strong positive correlation, drove pathogenic WNT and NOTCH signaling activation in Leydig and peritubular myoid cells, disrupting critical cell-cell interactions. Additionally, altered Leydig cell subpopulation dynamics showed enrichment in immune signaling (T-cell and B-cell receptors) and metabolic dysfunction, while Sertoli cell functional strength declined. These findings establish that epigenetic dysregulation in specific testicular cellular compartments, coupled with inflammatory pathway activation, underlies the pathogenic mechanisms of non-obstructive azoospermia and male infertility.

title: Multi-omics and Mendelian randomization study explores potential therapeutic targets for meningiomas
summary: This multi-omics and Mendelian randomization study identified ten genes with potential therapeutic significance in meningiomas: BET1L, COL17A1, CFAP43, SH3PXD2A, TTC28, ZNRF3, SLK, AKR1C3, NRXN3, and RSPO3. Among these, BET1L and SH3PXD2A showed elevated expression in meningioma tissues compared to controls, while AKR1C3 and NRXN3 demonstrated causal genetic relationships with meningioma development based on Mendelian randomization analysis. Network analysis revealed that NRXN3 directly interacts with CHEK2, a gene associated with meningioma risk, suggesting a shared biological mechanism. Additionally, plasma proteins including fibrinogen degradation products and the antigen TXNDC16 were elevated in meningioma patients and may promote tumor progression. The study also identified altered immune cell profiles, with M1 macrophages reduced and resting mast cells elevated in meningioma tissue, contributing to the tumor microenvironment. These findings provide multiple targetable pathways for therapeutic intervention in meningioma management.

title: Identification of key genes associated with infertile endometriosis based on bioinformatic analysis
summary: This bioinformatic study identified key genes associated with infertile endometriosis by analyzing dysregulated mitotic pathways. Endometriosis affects 20-50% of subfertile women and reduces implantation rates, clinical pregnancy rates, and ovarian response primarily through chronic inflammation and impaired endometrial receptivity and decidualization. Eight mitosis-related genes—including CCNA2, CENPE, BUB1B, KIF4A, NEK2, NCAPG, KIF11, and FBXO5—are significantly downregulated in endometriosis, with CCNA2 and CENPE showing particular diagnostic value for infertile endometriosis by disrupting the endometrial secretory phase transition necessary for pregnancy. The WNT4/WNT5A signaling pathway, which regulates embryo implantation and uterine development, exhibits abnormal expression patterns linked to endometriotic lesion initiation. Novel pharmacological agents including letrozole, GnRH modulators, progestogen-based drugs, and cordycepin—which inhibits endometriosis cell proliferation and improves oocyte quality—represent promising treatment approaches for restoring fertility in endometriosis patients.

title: Epithelial characteristics of ovarian clear cell carcinoma at single-cell resolution
summary: This single-cell transcriptomic study reveals that ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) arises from endometrial-type epithelial cells associated with endometriosis, with a distinctive cellular composition characterized by expanded epithelial and myeloid populations alongside reduced fibroblasts. The PAX8/KRT7 double-positive epithelial cells, particularly the EP1 subcluster, emerge as malignant-like populations enriched in both primary (pOCCC) and recurrent (rOCCC) tumors. Three transcription factors—STAT3, KLF5, and TRIM28—and their downstream targets (including TMBIM1, SLC2A1, LAMC1, and PTHLH) drive OCCC pathogenicity through coordinated regulation of genes involved in transcription and protein synthesis. Functional validation demonstrates that knockdown of STAT3, KLF5, or TRIM28 substantially inhibits cancer cell proliferation and migration, identifying these factors as potential therapeutic targets. These findings establish a molecular framework connecting endometriosis-derived epithelial transformation to OCCC development and progression, with implications for understanding endometriosis-associated cancer risk and developing treatment strategies.

title: Integrative analysis of efferocytosis- and invasion-related genes as potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets in breast cancer
summary: This integrative analysis identifies efferocytosis and invasion-related genes as key drivers of breast cancer progression through multiple mechanistic pathways. IL33 emerges as a critical prognostic marker that suppresses tumor growth while simultaneously enhancing immune-mediated tumor destruction, highlighting the importance of immune engagement in controlling cancer cells. The study reveals a mechanistic feed-forward loop wherein Collagen I triggers YAP activation in basal-like breast cancer cells, and YAP subsequently drives enhanced Collagen I alignment and tension, sustaining collective invasion and metastatic capacity. ANO6 and PLGRKT are identified as significantly upregulated prognostic markers in breast cancer that participate in bleb assembly and phagocytosis, with functional enrichment revealing their association with the KEAP1/NFE2L2 and TP53 metabolic pathways. Additionally, efferocytosis-related genes facilitate the shift of tumor-associated macrophages toward the pro-tumoral M2 phenotype, thereby promoting tumor progression through altered immune regulation. These findings collectively position efferocytosis and invasion-related genes as promising biomarkers for stratifying breast cancer patients and potential therapeutic targets to disrupt pro-tumoral immune microenvironments and metastatic pathways.

title: The role and mechanism of ARID1A in immunotherapy of gastric cancer
summary:
# Summary

ARID1A, a chromatin-remodeling component of the SWI/SNF complex, is frequently lost in gastric cancer (42.4% of cases) and its deficiency is associated with aggressive clinicopathological features, increased invasion depth, distant metastasis, and reduced overall survival. Loss of ARID1A promotes tumor aggressiveness through activation of the PI3K/AKT pathway, which upregulates PD-L1 expression and facilitates immune evasion by blocking T-cell cytotoxicity via PD-L1-PD-1 interaction. Paradoxically, while ARID1A deficiency predicts poor prognosis, it paradoxically enhances sensitivity to PD-1 inhibitor immunotherapy, with negative ARID1A expression and elevated PD-L1 levels serving as predictive biomarkers for improved therapeutic response. Independent risk factors for poor PD-1 inhibitor efficacy include unresected primary tumors, positive ARID1A expression, low PD-L1 combined positive score (CPS < 10), and intact mismatch repair status. These findings identify the ARID1A-PI3K/AKT-PD-L1 axis as a promising therapeutic target, suggesting that PI3K/AKT inhibitors combined with immunotherapy may optimize treatment outcomes in ARID1A-deficient gastric cancers.

title: Single-cell RNA and bulk sequencing analysis reveals that formononetin inhibits GTSF1 to exert anti-osteosarcoma effects
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that formononetin, a natural polyphenol derived from Spatholobi Caulis, exerts potent anti-osteosarcoma effects by downregulating GTSF1 expression. GTSF1 is significantly elevated in osteosarcoma cells and tissues compared to normal bone cells, and its overexpression promotes cell proliferation while inhibiting apoptosis. Formononetin treatment effectively suppresses osteosarcoma cell proliferation, migration, and invasion while promoting apoptosis in a concentration-dependent manner, with these anti-tumor effects mediated through GTSF1 downregulation. Single-cell and bulk RNA sequencing analyses revealed that formononetin modulates the osteosarcoma immune microenvironment, including effects on macrophages, monocytes, and other immune cell populations. These findings identify GTSF1 as a novel therapeutic target and suggest formononetin as a promising natural compound for osteosarcoma treatment, complementing conventional chemotherapy agents such as cisplatin, doxorubicin, and methotrexate.

title: Identification of M2 macrophage-related biomarkers for a predictive model of interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy after kidney transplantation by machine learning algorithms
summary:
# Summary

This study identified M2 macrophage-related biomarkers predictive of interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy (IFTA), the leading cause of kidney graft loss after transplantation. Using machine learning algorithms, the researchers discovered three hub genes—ALOX5, ARL4C, and MS4A6A—that are significantly elevated in IFTA and strongly correlated with graft failure. ALOX5 promotes leukotriene B4 production, which drives M2 macrophage infiltration and polarization, while ARL4C regulates macrophage trafficking and migration. M2 macrophages contribute to fibrosis through TGF-β secretion, which activates SMAD3-dependent pathways and facilitates macrophage-to-myofibroblast transition via ATF6/TGF-β/SMAD3 and JAK/STAT6 signaling. The study revealed that IL-4 and IL-13 induce M2 polarization, contrasting with IFN-γ-induced M1 differentiation, and identified two gene co-expression modules associated with IFTA and distinct immune infiltration patterns linked to rejection episodes. These findings establish a molecular framework for developing early diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers for post-transplant fibrosis.

title: A multi-omics strategy to understand PASC through the RECOVER cohorts: a paradigm for a systems biology approach to the study of chronic conditions
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics study employs a comprehensive systems biology approach to characterize post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC/Long COVID), a condition associated with widespread morbidity, rising healthcare costs, and multiple distinct molecular subtypes. Genome-wide association studies identified loci including FOXP4, LZTFL1, and ABO as genetic risk factors for acute COVID-19 and PASC, with FOXP4 expression detected in Type II alveolar cells and granulocytes, suggesting pulmonary and immune involvement. Epigenetic modifications, particularly altered DNA methylation patterns at loci such as IFI44L (involved in interferon responses) and the long non-coding RNA LEF1-AS1, correlate with COVID-19 severity and mortality risk. The study emphasizes that circulating viral load, immune mediators (cytokines, chemokines, antibodies, coagulation factors, growth factors, complement components), and potential cofactors like Epstein-Barr virus reactivation collectively contribute to PASC pathophysiology and patient stratification. This integrated multi-omics framework demonstrates how systems-level analyses can uncover the heterogeneous molecular basis of complex post-viral conditions and inform precision medicine approaches to chronic disease.

title: Differential causal networks highlight sex-based differences in human tissues
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals significant sex-based differences in gene expression patterns across human tissues, with critical implications for disease susceptibility and progression. Sex chromosomes and hormones modulate differential gene expression between males and females, influencing the onset and severity of diverse diseases. Pancreatic tissue exhibits the highest number of sex-specific differentially expressed genes, particularly in immune-related pathways, which are implicated in both diabetes and pancreatic cancer through inflammatory mechanisms. Key pathways enriched in sex-biased networks include immunoglobulin and B cell receptor signaling, blood microparticle dynamics, and the HOXA10 gene pathway, which is notably associated with both endometriosis in women and prostate cancer. The XIST and TSIX genes demonstrate bidirectional regulatory interactions in X-chromosome inactivation, a process specific to females that may underlie sex-specific disease vulnerabilities. Additionally, sex-specific differences in adipose tissue and metabolic pathways influence cardiovascular and metabolic homeostasis, with chronic activation of acute-phase responses contributing to elevated inflammatory states in metabolic diseases.

title: A Late-Onset and Mild Phenotype of Mitochondrial Complex I Deficiency Due to a Novel Reported Variant Within the ACAD9 Gene
summary:
# Summary

This study reports a novel ACAD9 gene variant (c.1636G>A p.Val546Met) associated with late-onset, mild mitochondrial complex I deficiency. ACAD9 is a mitochondrial enzyme with dual functions: catalyzing the rate-limiting step of fatty acid β-oxidation and serving as an assembly factor for respiratory complex I. The identified V546M variant reduces complex I specific activity by over 50% while preserving the protein's mitochondrial localization, cellular energy production, and complex I protein content, indicating a selective functional impairment in complex I assembly rather than global mitochondrial dysfunction. Unlike previously reported ACAD9 mutations that cause severe infantile-onset disease with acute metabolic acidosis, encephalomyopathy, and cardiomyopathy, this patient presented with exercise intolerance and muscle weakness, demonstrating a markedly milder phenotype. The study suggests that specific ACAD9 variants can dissociate the enzyme's two biological roles, with the V546M mutation affecting complex I assembly function while sparing fatty acid oxidation capacity. Treatment with riboflavin, which augments mitochondrial FAD cofactors, represents a potential therapeutic approach for ACAD9 deficiency.

title: Epigenetics of Endometrial Cancer: The Role of Chromatin Modifications and Medicolegal Implications
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial cancer development involves both genetic mutations and epigenetic dysregulation, with multiple tumor-suppressor and oncogenic genes including POLE, MLH1, TP53, PTEN, PIK3CA, CTNNB1, and ARID1A consistently implicated in disease pathogenesis. Chromatin modifications—particularly histone post-translational modifications (acetylation, methylation, phosphorylation, ubiquitination) and DNA methylation—play pivotal roles in altering gene accessibility and expression patterns critical to cancer development. Type I endometrial cancers display elevated DNMT1 and DNMT3B expression with frequent hypermethylation of tumor suppressors (PTEN, RASSF1, HAND2, MLH1), while Type II cancers show reduced DNA methyltransferase levels; notably, MLH1 promoter hypermethylation correlates with microsatellite instability affecting 17-25% of cases. Mutations in POLE and PTEN are associated with favorable outcomes and may support fertility-sparing treatment with progestins such as medroxyprogesterone acetate and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine devices, whereas p53, L1CAM, HER2, and FGFR2 alterations predict poor prognosis. Emerging evidence suggests that bioactive compounds like tea polyphenols and D-chiro-inositol with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties warrant investigation as potential therapeutic approaches targeting these epigenetic mechanisms.

title: Stress granule clearance mediated by V-ATPase-interacting protein NCOA7 mitigates ovarian aging
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that NCOA7, a stress-response protein abundant in ovarian granulosa cells, is critical for mitigating ovarian aging through its role in clearing accumulated stress granules via autophagic degradation. Loss of NCOA7 expression or function—whether through deleterious mutations associated with premature ovarian insufficiency or experimental deletion—accelerates oxidative stress-related cellular senescence, reduces estradiol production, and promotes age-related fertility decline in mice characterized by elevated FSH, decreased follicle numbers, and ovarian fibrosis. NCOA7 operates by interacting with the G3BP1–V-ATPase complex to facilitate stress granule autophagy, and therapeutic approaches such as rapamycin treatment or mRNA nanoparticle delivery of NCOA7 effectively accelerate stress granule clearance and alleviate senescence in human granulosa cells. These findings establish stress granule accumulation as a pathogenic mechanism in ovarian aging and identify NCOA7-based interventions as potential therapeutic strategies to delay reproductive aging.

title: LncRNA IGF2-AS serves as a miR-106b-5p sponge to induce apoptosis and inflammatory reaction of bronchial epithelial cells in COPD
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies the long non-coding RNA IGF2-AS as a critical regulator of bronchial epithelial cell dysfunction in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), operating through a competitive inhibition mechanism with microRNA-106b-5p. Cigarette smoke extract induces elevated IGF2-AS expression in bronchial epithelial cells, which suppresses miR-106b-5p levels and subsequently prevents miR-106b-5p-mediated suppression of its target SIX1. IGF2-AS downregulation reverses the inhibitory effects of cigarette smoke on cell proliferation while simultaneously abolishing smoke-induced apoptosis and reducing inflammatory cytokine production (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), with miR-106b-5p inhibition blocking these protective effects. Clinically, serum IGF2-AS levels are significantly elevated in both stable COPD and acute exacerbation COPD patients compared to healthy controls, correlating inversely with miR-106b-5p expression and disease severity, positioning IGF2-AS as a potential diagnostic biomarker for COPD. These findings establish an IGF2-AS/miR-106b-5p/SIX1 regulatory axis as a key molecular mechanism underlying smoke-induced epithelial injury and inflammation in COPD pathogenesis.

title: Identification of hub genes and prediction of the ceRNA network in adult sepsis
summary: This study identifies key molecular mechanisms underlying sepsis pathogenesis through transcriptomic and network analysis. Sepsis causes life-threatening organ dysfunction through a dysregulated host immune response characterized by concurrent hyperinflammation and immunosuppression, with upregulated genes enriched in neutrophil migration, chemotaxis, and pro-inflammatory signaling pathways (IL-17, Toll-like receptor, and TNF), while downregulated genes are associated with impaired T cell and lymphocyte differentiation. Four hub genes—CLEC4D, GPR84, S100A12, and HK3—demonstrate significant prognostic value in sepsis and are predicted to be regulated by a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) network involving long non-coding RNA NEAT1 and microRNA hsa-miR-495-3p targeting ELF1. Multiple therapeutic approaches including anti-TNF-α, recombinant-activated protein C, TLR4/MD2 antagonists, and immunoglobulin have been tested for sepsis management. Early recognition and rapid antibiotic administration substantially improve survival outcomes in septic patients, highlighting the clinical importance of timely intervention in this dysregulated immunological state.

title: FEN1 plays a key role in the transition from HSIL to CSCC
summary:
# Summary: FEN1's Role in Cervical Cancer Progression

This study identifies FEN1 (Flap Endonuclease 1) as a critical driver of the transition from cervical high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (HSIL) to cervical squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC). FEN1 is significantly elevated in both HSIL and CSCC, where it functions in DNA replication and mitosis alongside co-expressed PCNA, and its elevated expression correlates with poor CSCC prognosis. FEN1 promotes immune escape by interacting with PCNA to suppress anti-tumor immunity, reducing M1 macrophages and CD8+ T cells while promoting myeloid-derived suppressor cell accumulation and upregulating immunosuppressive checkpoints (PD-1, TIGIT, CTLA4). Functionally, FEN1 knockdown significantly inhibits cervical cancer cell proliferation, and molecular docking analysis shows FEN1 binds small molecule inhibitors with substantial affinity, suggesting therapeutic potential. The progression pathway involves dysregulation of DNA replication, cell cycle, and p53 signaling pathways, with an M1/M2 macrophage imbalance favoring tumor development. These findings establish FEN1 as a central hub linking proliferative signaling to immunosuppression during cervical cancer progression, making it a promising therapeutic target for preventing or treating advanced cervical malignancy.

title: Regulation of RNA splicing in endometrial tissue and its association with endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that alternative RNA splicing dysregulation in the endometrium is a key molecular mechanism underlying endometriosis risk and pathology. The authors analyzed transcript isoform-level changes across the menstrual cycle and found that the mid-secretory phase exhibits the most pronounced splicing abnormalities in endometriosis samples, including differential splicing in 62 genes and altered transcript usage in multiple pathways regulating cell adhesion, extracellular matrix organization, and mesenchymal cell function. Integrating splicing quantitative trait loci (sQTLs) with genome-wide association study data identified GREB1 and WASHC3 as endometriosis-risk genes with genetically regulated splicing events, along with functionally important genes like ZNF217 and FBLN2 that show altered exon usage affecting protein domains critical for hormone signaling and tissue remodeling. Notably, 67.5% of genes with splicing variation were not detected by conventional gene-level expression analysis, demonstrating that transcript isoform-level and splicing-specific analyses reveal endometriosis-associated dysregulation that would be missed by standard genomic approaches. These findings establish alternative splicing as a novel regulatory layer in endometriosis pathogenesis, with potential implications for understanding the disease's association with infertility and chronic pelvic pain.

title: Challenges and proposed solutions to the adoption of cell free DNA in screening, detecting and prognosticating colorectal cancer
summary: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) represents a promising liquid biopsy approach for colorectal cancer detection and monitoring, functioning as a non-invasive biomarker that circulates at elevated levels in cancer patients compared to healthy individuals. CfDNA originates primarily from apoptosis and necrosis of hematopoietic cells, as well as active release via exosomes and extracellular vesicles, and carries cancer-specific genetic alterations including single-nucleotide variations, copy number variations, and DNA methylation patterns that enable cancer detection. The circulating tumor DNA component of cfDNA correlates with tumor burden and metastatic disease, while cfDNA levels during treatment predict oncologic outcomes and can outperform imaging modalities in detecting recurrent tumors. Multiple cfDNA sources beyond blood—including stool, urine, cerebrospinal fluid, and saliva—enhance detection capabilities, with stool-derived cfDNA proving particularly advantageous for colorectal cancer screening, as demonstrated by the Cologuard test achieving 92.3% sensitivity and 86.6% specificity. Advanced error-corrected next-generation sequencing methods now enable identification of low-frequency cancer-associated mutations, including hotspot variants in genes like ERBB2, ERBB3, PIK3CA, and FBXW7 that appear in normal epithelium but are selected during malignant transformation. These findings establish cfDNA analysis as a clinically viable approach for colorectal cancer screening, prognostication, and surveillance with potential to improve patient outcomes.

title: Machine learning based screening of biomarkers associated with cell death and immunosuppression of multiple life stages sepsis populations
summary:
# Summary

This machine learning study identified age-specific programmed cell death (PCD) pathways as critical biomarkers in sepsis progression. Pyroptosis emerged as the dominant PCD mechanism in neonatal sepsis, with NLRC4 serving as the strongest predictor (AUC=0.902), positively correlating with neutrophil infiltration while suppressing CD8+ T cells. In children, ferroptosis predominated through elevated expression of ferroptosis-associated genes (AKR1C3, GCLM, PEBP1, CARS, MAP1LC3B, SCL11A2), with MAP1LC3B showing superior predictive capability and coordinating shifts toward M0-macrophage accumulation. Adult sepsis featured autophagy activation via TSPO, HTRA2, and USP10, with TSPO regulating oxidative stress, iron homeostasis, and cholesterol transport while promoting M1-macrophage polarization. These distinct PCD pathways drive sepsis pathophysiology by triggering cytokine storms and subsequent immune paralysis, creating a mechanistic link between different life stages and differential therapeutic vulnerabilities. The findings suggest that age-stratified targeting of specific PCD pathways may improve sepsis management by addressing stage-specific immune dysregulation.

title: Clarification of the clinical significance of an intron variant in a case of Peutz–Jeghers syndrome with abnormal RNA splicing of STK11
summary:
# Summary

Germline mutations in STK11, a tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 19p13 that regulates apoptosis, cell cycle arrest, cell proliferation, cell polarity, and energy metabolism, cause Peutz–Jeghers syndrome, an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by intestinal polyposis and mucocutaneous pigmentation. This case report identifies a novel intron variant, STK11 c.375–10 A > G, located in the conserved U12-type minor intron 2, which suppresses normal RNA splicing and causes intron 2 retention leading to frameshift mutations that disrupt gene function. Functional analyses using minigene and luciferase reporter assays confirmed that this variant significantly impairs splicing efficiency compared to wild-type controls. The proband presented with mucocutaneous pigmentation and constipation with a positive family history, while her father exhibited characteristic gastrointestinal polyps and lentigines. This study clarifies that splicing abnormalities in rare U12-type introns represent an important pathogenic mechanism in Peutz–Jeghers syndrome beyond classical haploinsufficiency. The findings underscore the clinical significance of investigating intron variants and their effects on RNA processing in STK11-associated disease.

title: TET1 methylation and mRNA expression in renal cell carcinoma: implications for tumor staging and prognosis
summary: This study demonstrates that TET1 methylation plays a critical role in renal cell carcinoma (RCC) progression through epigenetic silencing of TET1 expression. TET1 expression is significantly reduced in RCC tissues compared to normal tissues, with higher expression preserved in early-stage tumors but markedly decreased in advanced stages. Increased TET1 DNA methylation inversely correlates with TET1 mRNA expression and associates with larger tumor size, advanced stage, and elevated serum biomarkers (CA125, NSE, Ki67), while negatively correlating with the cell cycle inhibitor p21. The authors propose that hypermethylation-induced TET1 silencing promotes malignant RCC cell proliferation by dysregulating p21 and Ki67 signaling pathways, ultimately driving rapid cancer cell proliferation. Notably, TET1 methylation levels display gender-specific patterns, with females showing higher methylation than males. Combined assessment of TET1 DNA methylation and mRNA expression demonstrates strong diagnostic accuracy (AUC = 0.876) for RCC detection, positioning TET1 methylation as a promising early-stage diagnostic and prognostic biomarker in RCC patients.

title: High SNHG expression may contribute to poor cervical cancer prognosis, based on systematic reviews and meta-analyses
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that elevated expression of the small nucleolar host gene (SNHG) family members is significantly associated with poor cervical cancer prognosis. High SNHG expression correlates with advanced TNM staging, increased lymph node metastasis, and larger tumor size, with statistical evidence supporting these clinicopathological associations. At the molecular level, specific SNHG family members promote cervical cancer progression through distinct mechanisms: SNHG14 induces cell proliferation and invasion by downregulating pro-apoptotic factors like Bax and JAK2-STAT3 signaling, while SNHG16 enhances these malignant phenotypes by upregulating the epithelial-mesenchymal transition factor ZEB1 through microRNA sponging of miR-216-5p. More broadly, SNHG molecules regulate tumor cell proliferation, migration, invasion, apoptosis, and drug resistance, operating either through direct signaling pathway modulation or indirectly as competitive endogenous RNAs that sequester microRNAs. The SNHG family's oncogenic roles extend beyond cervical cancer to multiple malignancies including colorectal, lung, breast, and gastric cancers, suggesting SNHG members represent promising prognostic biomarkers and potential therapeutic targets across diverse cancer types.

title: Prevalence and Clinical Characteristics of the
summary:
# Summary

The LRRK2 p.L1795F variant is a genetic risk factor for Parkinson's disease primarily identified in individuals of European ancestry, where it accounts for approximately 3.23% of familial PD cases with no presence in control populations. This variant enhances LRRK2 kinase activity through increased Rab10 phosphorylation (5-fold increase) and autophosphorylation at p.S1292, while reducing phosphorylation at p.S935, resulting in altered protein signaling. Carriers of p.L1795F present with a distinctive aggressive phenotype characterized by early-onset akinetic-rigid PD without tremor, early onset of severe dyskinesia and motor fluctuations, postural instability, and a narrow therapeutic window responsive to levodopa in early stages. The clinical presentation is also marked by prominent nonmotor symptoms including orthostatic hypotension and urinary dysfunction (83% prevalence each), as well as neuropsychiatric features such as anxiety and depression (67% prevalence). MAPT gene variations may further modify the age of disease onset in LRRK2-associated PD carriers. This phenotypic profile indicates that p.L1795F contributes significantly to PD pathogenesis in Central European populations through enhanced LRRK2 kinase activity.

title: Using proteomics and single-cell sequencing to analyze the pathogenesis of recurrent implantation failure associated with uterine natural killer cells
summary:
# Summary

This proteomics and single-cell sequencing study identified three key genes—AMPD3, H6PD, and PAK2—as central to recurrent implantation failure (RIF) through their dysregulation in uterine natural killer cells. AMPD3 and H6PD are upregulated in RIF patients, while PAK2 is downregulated; notably, the transcription factor HR strongly regulates PAK2 expression. The study reveals that bacterial infections, particularly Gram-negative bacteria like *Escherichia coli*, contribute to RIF by inducing endometrial dysfunction through lipopolysaccharide-mediated production of inflammatory mediators including selectin E, CXCL1, and CXCL13, which elevate iNOS, nitric oxide, and COX-2. PAK2 reduction increases reactive oxygen species production and impairs embryonic development through increased chromosomal abnormalities and reduced blastocyst formation, while elevated inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, CCL5, CCL3) and growth factors (VEGFA) drive NK cell dysfunction and aberrant cell-cell interactions. These findings establish that RIF pathogenesis involves interconnected dysregulation of immune gene expression, bacterial-induced inflammation, and impaired endometrial decidualization, with implications for potential therapeutic targeting of these pathways.

title: Implication of CDKN2A in cuproptosis through defining cuproptosis-related gene signature in ovarian cancer
summary: This study identifies cuproptosis—a copper-dependent form of programmed cell death—as a therapeutic mechanism in ovarian cancer through a novel gene signature. The researchers identified 11 cuproptosis-related genes (CRGs) including PUM3, KYAT1, AMT, REPS1, CD40LG, CXCR2, AGFG1, FGF23, AHSG, and HAAO that are significantly associated with ovarian cancer prognosis and patient survival, with CDKN2A emerging as a central regulatory hub in protein-protein interaction networks. Mechanistically, copper binds to lipoylated proteins in the tricarboxylic acid cycle, triggering cuproptosis through mitochondrial dysfunction and reactive oxygen species generation that activates apoptotic pathways. Treatment with the copper-chelating agent CuET induced cuproptosis in ovarian cancer cells, resulting in upregulation of CDKN2A and significant suppression of cell viability, migration, invasion, and tumor volume. Clinical factors including age greater than 65 years and advanced FIGO stage III-IV were associated with increased ovarian cancer risk and reduced survival. The study's findings suggest that cuproptosis-based therapeutics targeting the CDKN2A-regulated pathway could provide a novel treatment strategy for ovarian cancer, with potential relevance to the endometriosis-cancer link given associations between ovarian endometriosis cysts and malignant transformation.

title: A urinary DNA methylation assay using two genes enables noninvasive detection and prognostic prediction in urothelial carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study developed and validated a noninvasive urinary DNA methylation assay targeting two genes, HIST1H4F and SOX1-OT, for detecting and monitoring urothelial carcinoma (UC). Both genes demonstrate significantly elevated methylation levels in UC tumor tissues, with methylation status showing strong correlation to tumor grade and invasiveness, displaying higher methylation in high-grade and invasive tumors compared to low-grade disease. The assay achieved excellent diagnostic performance, with 85.2% positive rate in UC patients versus no positives in healthy controls, and demonstrated superior sensitivity and specificity when applied to stratified UC populations (92.4-94.1% sensitivity for high-grade and invasive disease). Most clinically relevant, the assay showed exceptional ability to predict UC recurrence, detecting recurrence in 98.5% of patients who subsequently developed recurrent disease following surgical treatment. This biomarker-based approach offers a non-invasive alternative or complement to conventional diagnostic methods such as cystoscopy and urine cytology, and may enable early detection of disease recurrence in post-operative surveillance.

title: Identification and validation of oxidative stress signature genes in the trabecular meshwork of glaucoma
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress drives glaucoma pathogenesis through excessive reactive oxygen species production and compromised antioxidant defenses in the trabecular meshwork, the tissue most sensitive to oxidative damage. Multiple inflammatory signaling pathways including IL-17, TNF, and NF-κB converge to amplify oxidative stress, while ROS-induced damage to DNA, lipids, and proteins leads to trabecular meshwork fibrosis and impaired aqueous humor outflow. Key signature genes associated with this pathology include downregulation of the chemokine CXCL1 and adhesion molecule VCAM1, upregulation of the inflammatory mediator CCL3, and altered expression of NF-κB signaling components including NFKBIA and NFKB1. These hub genes are regulated by the microRNA hsa-miR-34a-5p and transcription factors RELA and NFKB1, establishing interconnected regulatory networks that modulate immune cell infiltration and inflammatory responses. Drug-gene interaction analysis identified therapeutic candidates including the TNF inhibitor Infliximab and Vitamin B6, which interact with disease-associated genes to potentially ameliorate glaucomatous damage through suppression of oxidative stress and inflammatory signaling.

title: To Study the Correlation of Methylenetetrahydrofolate Reductase C677T and MTR (A2756G) Gene Polymorphism in Endometriosis in Women of Reproductive Age Group
summary: This study investigated the association between methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR) C677T and methionine synthase (MTR) A2756G gene polymorphisms and endometriosis in women of reproductive age. The MTHFR C677T polymorphism was significantly associated with endometriosis risk, with the T allele and CT/TT genotypes showing higher prevalence in endometriosis patients compared to controls; the homozygous 677C>T variant was twice as frequent in the endometriosis group. Conversely, the MTR A2756G polymorphism showed no significant association with endometriosis. The MTHFR C677T variant reduces enzyme activity, leading to impaired 5-methyltetrahydrofolate formation, elevated homocysteine levels, and increased inflammatory cytokines—all mechanisms implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis. The MTHFR C677T polymorphism was also significantly associated with disease severity, dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, with these clinical features most pronounced at advanced disease stages. These findings suggest that MTHFR C677T polymorphism represents a genetic risk factor for endometriosis through disruption of one-carbon metabolism and methylation processes.

title: From Petri Dish to Primitive Heart: How IVF Alters Early Cardiac Gene Networks and Epigenetic Landscapes
summary:
# Summary

Assisted reproductive technology (ART) and in vitro culture (IVC) induce widespread epigenetic alterations—including aberrant DNA methylation, histone modifications, and chromatin accessibility changes—that disrupt the coordinated gene regulatory networks essential for cardiac development. Critical cardiac transcription factors including NKX2-5, GATA4, TBX5, ISL1, MEF2C, and HAND1/2 show reduced expression and diminished enhancer occupancy in ART embryos, while key signaling pathways (BMP, FGF, WNT, Notch, and TGFβ/Smad) that normally orchestrate cardiac patterning and morphogenesis become dysregulated. Imprinted genes such as H19, Igf2, Peg3, Mest, and Dlk1 exhibit aberrant methylation patterns, and dysregulation of imprinting control genes (Trim28, Zfp57, Zac1) further compromises developmental fidelity. Additionally, cardiac-regulatory microRNAs (miR-1, miR-133a, miR-208a, miR-499) show dysfunction, while aberrant expression of epigenetic machinery enzymes (Dnmt3a/b, Tet1, Hdac3, Kdm6a, Ezh2) perpetuates these alterations. These cumulative epigenetic derangements and gene expression dysregulation provide a mechanistic explanation for the significantly increased incidence of congenital heart abnormalities observed in ART-conceived children, with placental epigenetic dysfunction potentially serving as an additional contributor to altered fetal cardiac development.

title: A multilevel genomic approach to uncover causal connections between CPFE and lung cancer subtypes: A two-sample Mendelian randomization study
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identifies causal genetic associations between combined pulmonary fibrosis and emphysema (CPFE) and distinct lung cancer subtypes. CPFE substantially increases lung cancer risk and is associated with both squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma, pathogenic processes driven partly by underlying pulmonary inflammation and immune imbalance. The study identifies multiple risk-modifying genes: five genes (ADGRE1, CA4, C1orf162, GZMH, GIMAP6) enhance squamous cell carcinoma risk while four (CPPED1, ASF1A, CDK2AP2, FADS1) provide protection; for adenocarcinoma, KLRF1 and GSTO1 increase risk while CYP1B1, FAM167A, CD33, CEBPA, and FOXO4 are protective; and for small cell lung cancer, seven genes confer risk (including CD300LF) while three (ARRDC2, DOK4, H2BC12) are protective. Notably, CPPED1 acts as a serine/threonine phosphatase that inhibits the PI3K-AKT pathway by dephosphorylating AKT1 and PAK4, explaining its consistent protective effect against squamous cell carcinoma, while FAM167A (DIORA-1) is highly expressed in lung tissue including alveolar macrophages and bronchial epithelium, suggesting roles in immune homeostasis and cancer prevention.

title: Regulatory Effects of Endometriosis-Associated Genetic Variants: A Multi-Tissue eQTL Analysis
summary: This multi-tissue expression quantitative trait loci (eQTL) study identified how endometriosis-associated genetic variants regulate gene expression across multiple tissues, revealing tissue-specific mechanisms underlying this estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease. Endometriosis involves multiple pathogenic factors including genetic susceptibility, aberrant immune surveillance, localized estrogen production, and inflammation, with retrograde menstruation serving as the primary initiating mechanism. The analysis identified key regulatory variants on chromosomes 8, 6, 11, and 4 (including rs10917151, rs56319427, and rs71575922) that modulate expression of critical genes such as CDKN2B-AS1, WNT4, and SMAD3. Tissue-specific patterns emerged, with immune and epithelial signaling genes predominating in intestinal and blood tissues, while reproductive tissues showed enrichment for genes involved in hormonal response, tissue remodeling, and adhesion. Three hub genes—MICB, CLDN23, and GATA4—were consistently linked to hallmark endometriosis pathways: MICB to immune evasion, CLDN23 to angiogenesis, and GATA4 to proliferative signaling, with CLDN23 and GATA4 particularly associated with tissue invasion and inflammatory responses. This multi-tissue eQTL mapping provides mechanistic insights into how genetic variants contribute to endometriosis through tissue-specific gene regulation networks.

title: Expression Analysis of miR-519a-3p and miR-379-5p in Colorectal Cancer Patients: A Combined Experimental and Bioinformatic Approach
summary: This study identifies dysregulated microRNAs in colorectal cancer with opposing expression patterns: miR-379-5p is significantly upregulated while miR-519a-3p is downregulated in tumor tissues. Both microRNAs participate in carcinogenesis-related pathways including p53, TGF-β, and FoxO signaling, and interact with key transcription factors such as ESR1 and FOXA1. miR-379-5p specifically targets CASP3, while miR-519a-3p targets APC and AKT3, genes crucial for apoptosis regulation and colorectal cancer development. Clinical associations reveal that elevated miR-379-5p correlates with older patient age (≥55 years), rectal tumor location, advanced TNM stage, and tumors ≤6 cm, suggesting its potential as a prognostic biomarker. Beyond colorectal cancer, these microRNAs display tissue-specific regulatory roles, with miR-379-5p demonstrating tumor-suppressive functions in endometrial cancer while being downregulated in multiple other cancers including ovarian, oral squamous cell, and lung adenocarcinoma. This comprehensive miRNA-target network analysis provides potential diagnostic and therapeutic opportunities for colorectal cancer management through microRNA-based interventions.

title: NR4A1 Acts as a Nutrient Sensor That Inhibits the Effects of Aging
summary:
# Summary

NR4A1 functions as a nutrient sensor that declines with age and protects against aging-related pathology through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The nuclear receptor regulates critical longevity pathways including SIRT1 and SIRT3 activation, GSK-3β signaling, and mTOR-mediated muscle mass maintenance, while NR4A1 deficiency results in decreased survival, cardiac fibrosis, and enhanced disease susceptibility. NR4A1 suppresses pro-inflammatory responses by inhibiting NFκB signaling and M1 macrophage differentiation, while simultaneously promoting beneficial angiogenesis and tissue repair through VEGF-induced pathways and integrin induction. Natural ligands of NR4A1—including resveratrol, quercetin, and kaempferol—exhibit protective effects against endometriosis and cancer, suggesting therapeutic potential. Beyond inflammation, NR4A1 regulates metabolic homeostasis by inhibiting adipogenesis, controlling hepatic lipogenesis, and enhancing insulin secretion through β-cell expansion. The receptor's age-dependent decline in expression correlates with impaired long-term memory and increased vulnerability to neurodegenerative and cardiovascular diseases, making NR4A1 activation a potential intervention strategy against multiple age-related pathologies.

title: Differential expression profiles and bioinformatics analysis of microRNAs in brown adipose tissue dysfunction induced by chronic intermittent hypoxia in obstructive sleep apnea
