journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) causes brown adipose tissue (BAT) dysfunction through chronic intermittent hypoxia (CIH)-induced alterations in microRNA expression profiles. CIH triggers metabolic syndrome, insulin resistance, sympathetic activation, and inflammation, while also inducing hepatic inflammation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and oxidative stress that collectively impair metabolic homeostasis. Key findings include significant upregulation of miRNAs such as mmu-miR-3102-3p, mmu-miR-135a-5p, and mmu-miR-92b-3p, alongside downregulation of mmu-miR-185-3p, mmu-miR-505-3p, and mmu-miR-222-5p in CIH-injured BAT. Specifically, upregulated miR-135a-5p targets insulin signaling pathways to promote metabolic dysregulation, while elevated miR-210 impairs brown adipocyte differentiation and thermogenic function. These aberrant miRNA-regulated target genes dysregulate metabolic processes and signal transduction pathways, with pathway enrichment analysis revealing that upregulated miRNAs regulate the arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy pathway, explaining OSA's association with increased cardiovascular disease risk. These findings identify miRNA dysregulation as a critical molecular mechanism linking OSA-induced BAT injury to systemic metabolic complications and cardiovascular comorbidities.

title: Associations of Matrix Metalloproteinase-7 Promoter Genotypes With Asthma Risk in Taiwan
summary: This study investigated the association between the MMP-7 promoter polymorphism -181A>G and asthma risk in a Taiwanese population. Matrix metalloproteinase-7 is upregulated in asthmatic airway tissue and contributes to disease pathogenesis through multiple mechanisms, including airway tissue remodeling, immune cell recruitment, epithelial barrier dysfunction, and mucus overproduction via EGFR activation. The G allele carriers (AG and GG genotypes) showed significantly increased asthma susceptibility and severity, with the polymorphism demonstrating particular predictive value for younger-onset asthma in individuals aged 25-40 years. Genetic ablation of MMP-7 in animal models confirmed its pathogenic role by reducing airway hyperresponsiveness, eosinophil accumulation, and allergic inflammation. These findings suggest that the MMP-7 -181A>G polymorphism and the G allele serve as potential genetic biomarkers for asthma risk stratification and may identify individuals predisposed to more severe disease phenotypes.

title: The circRNA-mediated ceRNA molecular regulatory network in fatigue-type type 2 diabete
summary:
# Summary

This study identified a dysregulated circular RNA (circRNA)-mediated competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) network in fatigue-type type 2 diabetes mellitus (F-T2DM), characterized by significant upregulation of circRNAs such as hsa_circ_0078539 and hsa_circ_0026239. These circRNAs regulate critical metabolic and cellular processes through miRNA sponging mechanisms, including key axes like hsa_circ_0078539/hsa-mir-4695-3p/SLC7A14, hsa_circ_0044623/hsa-mir-129-5p/MYLK3, and hsa_circ_0002622/hsa-mir-200b-3p/RAB21. The dysregulated ceRNA network impairs glucose transport, mitochondrial energy metabolism, myocardial contractility, and insulin signaling, while enriching pathways involving actin cytoskeleton remodeling, AMPK signaling, tricarboxylic acid metabolism, and oxidative stress responses. Clinical associations with elevated fasting plasma glucose and glycosylated hemoglobin levels were observed, correlating with multiple metabolic derangements in F-T2DM patients. This comprehensive ceRNA regulatory framework provides molecular insight into the pathogenesis of fatigue-associated type 2 diabetes and suggests therapeutic targets within the circRNA-miRNA-mRNA regulatory axis.

title: Potential role of Fanconi anemia pathway in the pathogenesis of endometrial cancer (Review)
summary:
# Summary

The Fanconi anemia (FA) pathway is a critical DNA damage repair mechanism involving multiple FA genes that maintain genome stability through coordinated control of interstrand crosslink (ICL) repair, homologous recombination, and trans-lesion synthesis. Mutations or deletions in FA genes impair DNA damage repair capacity, leading to genomic instability characterized by somatic copy number alterations and microsatellite instability, which are major drivers of endometrial cancer development. Type II endometrial cancers are particularly associated with extensive somatic copy number alterations and TP53 mutations, while Type I tumors exhibit microsatellite instability linked to cell cycle abnormalities. The FANCE gene, a component of the FA core complex, is frequently downregulated in endometrioid adenocarcinomas, and reduced FANCE expression promotes genomic instability and cancer progression. Conversely, FANCE overexpression enhances DNA repair capacity and inhibits endometrial cancer growth by inducing cell cycle arrest in the S and G2/M phases. These findings highlight the FA pathway as a key regulator of endometrial cancer pathogenesis and suggest that FA gene dysfunction may be a therapeutic target in endometrial malignancies.

title: Hirschprung's Disease and Thoracic Limb Amputation by Amniotic Bands in a Patient With Down Syndrome: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a male infant with Down syndrome (trisomy 21) presenting with three concurrent congenital conditions: Hirschsprung's disease, amniotic band sequence with left limb amputation, and multiple cardiac defects. Hirschsprung's disease arose from the congenital absence of ganglion cells in the myenteric and submucosal plexuses, resulting in intestinal obstruction and life-threatening enterocolitis at one month of age. The molecular basis for the increased susceptibility to Hirschsprung's disease in Down syndrome involves aberrant expression of genes located on chromosome 21q22, particularly DSCAM, RET, and COL6A1; elevated collagen VI levels in affected colon tissue impair enteric neural crest cell migration during embryogenesis. The authors hypothesize that maternal progesterone therapy used to prevent miscarriage may have contributed to amniotic band sequence formation, establishing an unusual multifactorial etiology for this patient's severe congenital anomalies. This case underscores the dramatically elevated gastrointestinal and skeletal morbidity in Down syndrome patients and highlights potential iatrogenic contributions from prenatal pharmacotherapy.

title: Immunogenic Cell Death Genes Related Prognostic Biomarker in Hepatocellular Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study identified a six-gene immunogenic cell death (ICD)-related prognostic signature comprising CFHR3, G6PD, IGHM, KPNA2, PON1, and SERPINE1 that effectively stratifies hepatocellular carcinoma patients into high-risk and low-risk groups with distinct clinical outcomes. Therapy-induced ICD stimulates antitumor immune responses through the release of danger-associated molecular patterns such as ATP and calreticulin, which recruit antigen-presenting cells to facilitate immune activation. High-risk patients exhibited significantly poorer overall survival, reduced immune cell infiltration, elevated immunosuppressive signaling (including TGFβ1 and TGFβ2), and increased m6A modification-related gene expression, features associated with the wound-healing immune subtype and enhanced immune escape mechanisms. Conversely, low-risk patients demonstrated markedly higher infiltration of activated immune cells including effector memory CD8+ T cells, activated B lymphocytes, and natural killer cells, corresponding to the inflammatory immune subtype. The risk model effectively predicted sensitivity to anticancer drugs and overall survival when integrated with clinical factors into a nomogram, offering a comprehensive framework for patient stratification and treatment optimization in HCC.

title: Biomarkers for colorectal cancer detection: An insight into colorectal cancer and FDA-approved biomarkers
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review identifies key molecular biomarkers for colorectal cancer detection and treatment stratification. The APC gene, mutated in approximately 85% of CRC patients, regulates cell proliferation and adhesion through the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway, while TP53 mutations—particularly elevated in distal bowel cancers—suppress critical cell cycle checkpoints and correlate with distant metastasis. Additional oncogenic drivers include PIK3CA mutations (10-15% frequency) involved in the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, SMAD4 mutations affecting TGF-β signaling and tumor spread, and KRAS mutations (highly prevalent in CRC) that promote tumorigenesis and angiogenesis through the RAS signaling pathway. The BRAF gene, mutated in approximately 10% of CRC cases, controls cell proliferation and apoptosis, with BRAFV600E mutations associated with female gender, advanced TNM stage, and poor differentiation. Clinically, cetuximab and panitumumab treat wild-type KRAS/NRAS tumors, while BRAF-mutant CRCs respond to encorafenib, binimetinib, cetuximab, and sorafenib combinations. Microsatellite instability-high tumors, occurring in 15% of CRC patients and associated with Lynch syndrome, demonstrate favorable prognosis and respond to immune checkpoint inhibitors including pembrolizumab, nivolumab, and ipilimumab.

title: Identification of lactylation-related biomarkers in osteoporosis from transcriptome and single-cell data
summary: This study identifies lactylation-related biomarkers in osteoporosis by analyzing transcriptomic and single-cell data, revealing that histone lactylation—mediated by lactate derived from glycolysis and endothelial cells—activates osteogenic gene expression and promotes bone formation in mesenchymal stem cells. CSRP2 and FUBP1 emerge as key biomarkers for osteoporosis risk prediction, with CSRP2 downregulated and FUBP1 upregulated in osteoporotic samples, both proteins showing strong binding affinities for environmental toxins (bisphenol A and tetrachlorodibenzodioxin) and association with inflammation regulation and immune cell differentiation. The study demonstrates that osteoporosis pathogenesis involves an imbalance between osteoclast-mediated bone resorption and osteoblast-driven bone formation, with immune dysregulation and aging as contributing factors, and identifies bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells, T cells, natural killer cells, and hematopoietic stem cells as key cell types involved in disease progression. Emerging mesenchymal stem cell therapies counter osteoporosis through paracrine growth factor secretion and bone remodeling, while established biomarkers including PINP and BALP for bone formation and β-CTX for bone resorption enable therapeutic monitoring. The identified candidate genes EMG1, RRP1B, and FUBP1 cluster functionally around ribosome biogenesis pathways, providing molecular targets for understanding osteoporosis etiology and developing interventions.

title: GADD45A
journal: Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies GADD45A as a critical negative regulator of granulosa cell differentiation and ovarian reserve in diminished ovarian reserve (DOR). GADD45A is significantly upregulated in granulosa cells from DOR patients and functions as a DNA damage-responsive gene that suppresses the expression of key fertility factors FSHR and CYP19A1, which are essential for estrogen production and oocyte development. Functionally, GADD45A overexpression inhibits granulosa cell proliferation and differentiation through S-phase cell cycle arrest, while GADD45A knockout leads to restoration of these processes. In vivo, Gadd45a knockout mice recapitulate core DOR features including irregular estrous cycles, reduced ovarian reserve, decreased anti-Müllerian hormone levels, elevated testosterone, and compromised oocyte developmental competence, establishing GADD45A as a pathogenic factor in ovarian aging. These findings suggest that dysregulation of GADD45A-mediated DNA damage responses in ovarian follicles contributes to impaired granulosa cell function and ovarian reserve depletion in DOR.

title: Novel estrogen-related gene variants identified by whole-exome sequencing in pregnancy-associated intrahepatic cholestasis
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies novel estrogen-related gene variants associated with intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP) through whole-exome sequencing, revealing critical mechanisms linking abnormal estrogen metabolism to this gestational complication. Three missense mutations in CYP17A1—encoding the key steroid synthesis enzyme P450c17—were identified in ICP patients: p.Pro28Thr (in the membrane attachment domain), p.Phe93Leu (in the steroid-binding domain), and p.Arg347Leu (in the redox partner interaction domain). ICP patients demonstrated significantly reduced CYP17A1 mRNA expression compared to healthy controls, despite elevated circulating estrogen levels contributing to ICP pathogenesis, suggesting impaired estrogen metabolism rather than biosynthesis deficiency. Additional variants in estrogen metabolism genes (CYP1A2, ESR2) and signaling receptors (ESR1, ESR2) were also associated with ICP risk and adverse outcomes including preterm birth. The study highlights that estrogen-metabolizing enzyme dysfunction, particularly in genes controlling biosynthesis (CYP17A1, CYP19A1) and metabolism (CYP1A2, CYP1B1, CYP3A4, COMT), contributes to ICP pathogenesis and shares mechanistic overlap with other estrogen-dependent gynecological conditions including endometriosis, endometrial cancer, and ovarian cancer.

title: Actionable Genes and Carcinogenic Pathways for Gastric Cancer in Latinos
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies distinct molecular and genetic signatures of gastric cancer in Puerto Rican Hispanics compared to predominantly non-Hispanic populations. TP53, CDH1, ARID1A, and KMT2D mutations are significantly elevated in Puerto Rican patients and associate with specific gastric cancer subtypes: TP53 mutations predominate in intestinal-type cancers linked to chromosomal instability, while CDH1 mutations characterize diffuse-type and genomically stable tumors. Hypermutated and microsatellite instability-high tumors exhibit distinct mutational profiles including KMT2C and LRP1B alterations, whereas the CLDN18-ARHGAP26 fusion predominates in non-hypermutated cases. Beyond genetic factors, multiple viral and bacterial pathogens—including EBV, Salmonella enterica, and Bartonella—are detected in Puerto Rican gastric cancers. Key carcinogenic pathways including TGF-β, PI3K, and WNT/β-catenin signaling promote tumor progression, epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, and therapeutic resistance, with implications for precision treatment strategies targeting HER2-positive and immunotherapy-responsive tumors in this population.

title: Tamoxifen induces PI3K activation in uterine cancer
summary:
# Summary

Tamoxifen, while effective for treating estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer, paradoxically activates the phosphoinositide 3-kinase (PI3K) pathway in the uterus through ER-agonistic effects, contributing to increased risk of secondary uterine cancer in a dose-dependent manner. Unlike de novo uterine cancers, which frequently harbor activating PIK3CA mutations and show co-occurring PTEN mutations, tamoxifen-associated uterine cancers are characterized by significantly lower frequencies of PIK3CA and PIK3R1 alterations and demonstrate mutual exclusivity with tamoxifen exposure, suggesting tamoxifen itself serves as an alternative mechanism for PI3K activation independent of genetic mutations. The study identified four significantly mutated genes (PTEN, KRAS, TP53, and ARID1A) across uterine cancer specimens, establishing the multistep model of carcinogenesis. Mechanistically, tamoxifen promotes endometrial epithelial proliferation more potently than physiologic estradiol, and this pro-proliferative effect is ER-dependent, distinguishing the drug's uterotrophic activity from natural estrogen signaling. These findings explain the distinct molecular etiology of tamoxifen-associated versus de novo uterine cancers and highlight that extended tamoxifen therapy carries an approximately twofold increased risk with prolonged use.

title: Identification and experimental validation of circular RNA-associated ceRNA networks in intrauterine adhesion
summary: This study identifies circular RNA (circRNA)-associated competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) networks implicated in intrauterine adhesion (IUA), a pathological condition characterized by endometrial fibrosis that causes reduced menstrual flow, secondary amenorrhea, and infertility. Comprehensive transcriptomic analysis revealed 44 differentially expressed circRNAs, 41 miRNAs, and 640 mRNAs in IUA tissues, with two hub circRNAs—hsa_circ_0000994 and hsa_circ_0000439—significantly upregulated in IUA samples. Functional validation demonstrated that silencing hsa_circ_0000994 substantially decreased expression of fibrosis markers α-SMA and COL1A1 in human endometrial stromal cells, indicating this circRNA plays a pivotal role in promoting fibrotic changes. Gene ontology and pathway analysis identified enrichment in extracellular matrix structure and collagen fibril organization, along with dysregulation of critical signaling pathways including NF-κB, apoptosis, and Notch pathways. Hub network analysis identified key regulatory nodes including miRNAs (hsa-miR-205-5p, hsa-miR-149-3p, hsa-miR-449b-3p) and mRNAs (SRSF11, PAPOLA, CPSF6) that integrate the circRNA-miRNA-mRNA regulatory cascade. These findings establish hsa_circ_0000994 as a promising therapeutic target for preventing or reversing IUA-associated endometrial fibrosis.

title: Comprehensive analysis of a lipid metabolism-related gene signature for ulcerative colitis
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies dysregulated lipid metabolism genes as key drivers of ulcerative colitis pathogenesis. Multiple lipid metabolism-related genes, including ALOX15, ACSL1, HK2, CD38, and FCER1G, are significantly elevated in UC patients, while ABCD3, NR3C2, and PIGN are downregulated, with ABCD3 demonstrating the highest diagnostic value (AUC=0.9185). These dysregulated genes are enriched in pathways governing fatty acid metabolism, natural killer cell-mediated cytotoxicity, calcium signaling, and adipocytokine signaling, collectively contributing to inflammatory pathology. The lipid metabolism gene signature strongly correlates with infiltration of multiple immune cell types, including CD8 T cells, macrophages, dendritic cells, and plasma cells, suggesting a central role for lipid dysregulation in driving immune-mediated inflammation. Mechanistically, inhibition of FABP5 and optimization of secondary bile acids and short-chain fatty acid metabolism reduce inflammatory responses in colonic tissues, while therapeutic intervention with mirikizumab improves histological outcomes. These findings establish lipid metabolism dysregulation as a targetable mechanism in UC and suggest that lipid-modulating therapeutic strategies warrant further clinical investigation.

title: 28th Annual Conference of the United Mitochondrial Disease Foundation Abstracts - Mitochondrial Medicine 2025 St. Louis, MO – June 18-21, 2025
summary:
# Summary

This conference abstract collection reveals multiple molecular mechanisms underlying mitochondrial dysfunction and its systemic consequences. In primary mitochondrial diseases caused by POLG mutations, patient-derived organoids demonstrate profound downregulation of mtDNA-encoded genes (MT-ND5, MT-TL1, MT-RNR1/2) alongside activation of NOTCH and JAK-STAT pathways leading to aberrant astrocytosis. Metformin treatment partially reverses these pathologies by restoring neuronal populations, improving mitochondrial mass and Complex I levels through AMPK/mTOR activation. At the cellular level, transfer of mitochondria from oral squamous cell carcinoma induces hypoxic stress and ROS production in CD4+ T cells, promoting glycolytic metabolism and T cell exhaustion. Novel therapeutic approaches targeting mitochondrial dynamics—specifically the Drp1/Fis1 interaction using the small molecule CVP-350—prevent mitochondrial fragmentation and provide superior cardioprotection in ischemia-reperfusion injury models. Additionally, pathogenic MARS2 variants impair oxidative phosphorylation through reduced Complex I and IV enzymatic activity, causing developmental delays and sensory deficits that correlate with abnormal EEG and early seizure onset.

title: Druggable Targets for Pelvic Inflammatory Disease: Mendelian Randomization and Experimental Validation
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) is a bacterial infection of the female genital tract caused by pathogens such as *Staphylococcus aureus* and *Escherichia coli*, involving dysregulated immune responses and inflammatory pathways that can lead to infertility, chronic pelvic pain, and tissue adhesion if untreated. Mendelian randomization and experimental validation in a PID rat model identified six key druggable genes, with albumin (ALB), interleukin-6 (IL6), and CD4 emerging as primary targets: ALB and IL6 are reduced in PID while CD4 is elevated, indicating disrupted immune regulation and systemic inflammation. IL6 exhibits context-dependent effects—promoting pathogen clearance and tissue repair at appropriate levels but driving immune dysfunction, anti-endometrial antibody production, and fibroblast-mediated collagen deposition when chronically elevated, while albumin's antioxidant and anti-inflammatory functions are impaired during persistent inflammation. Drug enrichment analysis identified sunitinib and everolimus as therapeutic candidates that effectively bind all three target proteins, with everolimus showing particularly stable IL6 binding and both compounds possessing established anti-inflammatory and anti-infective properties. These findings provide genetic and mechanistic evidence supporting ALB, IL6, and CD4 as viable therapeutic targets for PID treatment, offering an alternative to prolonged antibiotic use that carries risks of pathogen resistance and iatrogenic complications.

title: Association between MTHFR polymorphisms and vitamin D status in infertile women: a mediation analysis
summary: This mediation analysis in infertile women reveals that MTHFR C677T polymorphisms increase vitamin D deficiency risk substantially through elevated homocysteine levels. MTHFR, a key enzyme regulating folate metabolism and homocysteine methylation, shows that individuals carrying the 677CT and 677TT variants have significantly lower serum 25(OH)D levels and higher serum homocysteine compared to those with the CC genotype. Homocysteine mediated a notable proportion of this association—15.8% for the CT variant and 41.6% for the TT variant—demonstrating its critical intermediate role in the MTHFR-vitamin D relationship. The findings underscore how impaired MTHFR enzyme activity disrupts both folate metabolism and homocysteine regulation, creating a pathological pathway that compromises vitamin D status in infertile women. Since both folate deficiency and vitamin D deficiency are independently associated with female reproductive disorders and adverse pregnancy outcomes, this study suggests that MTHFR polymorphisms may contribute to reproductive dysfunction through a dual metabolic mechanism involving disrupted one-carbon metabolism and consequent vitamin D insufficiency.

title: Weighted Single-Step GWAS Reveals Genomic Regions Associated with Female Fertility in the Spanish Retinta Beef Cattle
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

This genome-wide association study identified genomic regions controlling female fertility in Spanish Retinta beef cattle by analyzing age at first calving, calving interval, and reproductive efficiency. Key candidate genes involved in ovarian folliculogenesis (ACVR1B, AMHR2), steroidogenesis (CYP27B1), cell cycle regulation (CDK2), and immune modulation (IFNG) were discovered as significant contributors to fertility traits. The weighted single-step GWAS analysis identified 96 genomic regions explaining over 1% of additive genetic variance, with chromosomes 2 and 5 showing particularly strong associations with female fertility. Long non-coding RNAs were also detected with potential regulatory roles in reproductive processes, and environmental factors such as herd-year-season interactions substantially influenced reproductive performance. These findings indicate that selection strategies must account for the negative trade-off between growth and reproductive traits to optimize overall herd productivity in beef cattle breeding programs.

title: Identification of Key Genes Related to Both Lipid Metabolism Disorders and Inflammation in MAFLD
summary: This study identified four key genes—FADS1, FADS2, GLB1, and PNPLA3—as critical regulators that simultaneously control both lipid metabolism and inflammation in metabolic-associated fatty liver disease (MAFLD). All four genes were significantly upregulated in MAFLD patients and mouse models compared to controls, with single-cell RNA sequencing revealing their elevated expression specifically in MAFLD-associated natural killer/T cells, suggesting immune involvement in disease pathogenesis. The genes localize to distinct cellular compartments: FADS1, FADS2, and PNPLA3 reside in the endoplasmic reticulum (and plasma membrane for FADS1/FADS2), while GLB1 localizes to the Golgi apparatus and lysosomes, positioning them at metabolic hubs within hepatocytes. The study links abnormal hepatic lipid accumulation to oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction, which trigger hepatocyte death, activate macrophages, and perpetuate chronic inflammation—ultimately driving progression to fibrosis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. Notably, estradiol was computationally predicted as a potential activator of these four genes, indicating possible sex-hormone-dependent regulation of MAFLD pathogenesis. These findings establish FADS1, FADS2, GLB1, and PNPLA3 as potential therapeutic targets that simultaneously address the dual pathogenic drivers of lipid metabolism dysregulation and inflammatory activation in MAFLD.

title: Impact of BRCA Status on Reproductive Outcomes in Breast Cancer Patients in Romania: A Retrospective Study
summary:
# Summary

Pathogenic variants in BRCA1 and BRCA2, which encode DNA double-strand break repair proteins, are strongly associated with early-onset, aggressive breast cancer and ovarian cancer, with BRCA1 mutations particularly linked to triple-negative and hormone receptor-negative tumors. BRCA1/2 germline mutations impair DNA repair mechanisms in oocytes, leading to accelerated oocyte aging, increased apoptosis, and reduced oocyte reserve, which has significant implications for reproductive planning in affected women. Despite these concerns, this Romanian retrospective study found that BRCA mutation status did not significantly influence reproductive outcomes in breast cancer survivors, with pregnancies progressing safely to term in both BRCA-positive and BRCA-negative groups, and no adverse effects on maternal prognosis or birth outcomes. BRCA1 mutation carriers showed a 100% live birth rate and demonstrated shorter intervals between diagnosis and pregnancy compared to BRCA-negative patients, suggesting preserved reproductive capacity despite the genetic mutation. The findings suggest that pregnancy after breast cancer treatment may be safe for BRCA mutation carriers, though the study was limited by small cohort size and selection bias, and further investigation into the effects of gonadotoxic chemotherapy on reproductive function is warranted.

title: DNA Damage and Repair in Ovarian Cancer: Focus on MicroRNAs
summary:
# Summary

MicroRNAs regulate critical DNA damage response and repair pathways in ovarian cancer by targeting essential genes involved in homologous recombination, mismatch repair, and checkpoint control. Multiple miRNAs including miR-9, miR-1255b, miR-193b, and miR-148b converge on BRCA1 downregulation, while miR-203a-3p targets ATM, a central kinase coordinating the DNA damage response and promoting cell cycle arrest through CHEK2 phosphorylation. Additional miRNA-mediated dysregulation affects PARP1 (by miR-519a-3p, let-7e, and miR-216b), RNF8 (by miR-214), and p53 regulators like MDM2 and MDMX, thereby compromising genomic stability and promoting cancer progression. Notably, miR-203a-3p reduction of ATM expression paradoxically suppresses ovarian cancer cell proliferation and apoptosis, while miR-214 upregulation induces genomic instability through RNF8 downregulation. These miRNA-mediated disruptions of DNA repair pathways create vulnerabilities exploitable by PARP inhibitors such as olaparib, which achieve therapeutic synthetic lethality in homologous recombination-deficient tumors by trapping PARP at damaged DNA and preventing single-strand break repair.

title: Integrated In Silico and Experimental Validation of Antrocin as a Plant-Derived Multi-Target Therapeutic for BRAF/MEK/PI3K-Driven Colorectal Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies the BRAF/MEK/PI3K signaling axis as a critical driver of colorectal cancer progression through multiple oncogenic mechanisms including sustained proliferation, growth evasion, angiogenesis, and metastasis. BRAF mutations downregulate the tumor suppressor AXIN2, disrupting Wnt/β-catenin signaling, while MEK upregulates FRRS1 to promote proliferation and PI3K upregulates OLFM4 to suppress apoptosis; these mutations are associated with poor overall survival and therapy resistance. The study demonstrates through integrated computational docking and experimental validation that antrocin, a plant-derived compound from Antrodia cinnamomea, potently binds all three pathway components with favorable binding energies and suppresses colorectal cancer cell viability, migration, and spheroid formation in HCT116 and RKO cell lines while reducing BRAF/MEK/PI3K pathway expression. Additionally, antrocin treatment inhibits the pro-tumoral immune microenvironment by reducing M2 macrophage polarization and cancer-associated fibroblast differentiation, and delays tumor growth in mouse xenograft models. These findings establish antrocin as a promising multi-target therapeutic candidate that overcomes resistance to current BRAF/MEK/PI3K inhibitors by simultaneously engaging all three kinases and modulating the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment.

title: Integrated Analysis, Machine Learning, Molecular Docking and Dynamics of CDK1 Inhibitors in Epithelial Ovarian Cancer: A Multifaceted Approach Towards Targeted Therapy
summary: CDK1 emerges as a critical oncogenic driver in epithelial ovarian cancer, where its overexpression is strongly associated with poor prognosis, adverse survival outcomes, and uncontrolled cell proliferation through its role in regulating G2/M phase transition and mitotic entry via interaction with Cyclin B1. A comprehensive integrated analysis identified a dysregulated gene signature in ovarian cancer characterized by elevated expression of cell cycle regulators (CDK1, CCNB1, CCNA2, TOP2A, RRM2, TYMS) and reduced expression of protective genes including estrogen receptor alpha (ESR1) and apoptotic factors (BCL2), with CDK1 exhibiting robust interactions with multiple cell cycle checkpoint proteins. Multiple CDK1 inhibitors, including Naringin, Avotaciclib, Seliciclib, and others, demonstrated high-affinity binding to CDK1 and its regulatory partner WEE1, with molecular docking and dynamics studies confirming their ability to inhibit CDK1 kinase activity and prevent G2/M progression while maintaining minimal predicted toxicity. These findings provide a mechanistic rationale for CDK1-targeted therapy in ovarian cancer, particularly in endometriosis-associated Type I epithelial ovarian cancers, where interrupting cell cycle control offers therapeutic benefit. The integrated bioinformatics and computational approach validates CDK1 as a central hub gene and promising therapeutic target in this disease.

title: Archaic adaptive introgression in modern human reproductive genes
summary: This study identifies evidence of adaptive introgression from Neanderthals and Denisovans into modern human reproductive genes, with 118 genes showing signatures of archaic ancestry. The researchers discovered 47 archaic segments enriched in reproductive tissues, with 327 archaic variants acting as expression quantitative trait loci that regulate gene expression in ovaries, prostate, testes, uterus, and vagina. Notably, a Neanderthal haplotype in the PGR gene is associated with reduced miscarriages and bleeding during pregnancy, while archaic alleles on chromosome 2 are protective against prostate cancer but involved in endometriosis and preeclampsia susceptibility. The archaic variants show distinct population distributions, with higher frequencies in American and East Asian populations, Denisovan ancestry enriched in Oceanic populations, and minimal representation in African populations. Pathway analysis reveals that these introgressed genes are enriched in developmental and cancer-related pathways, particularly carbon metabolism and DNA replication. The AHRR locus emerged as the strongest candidate for adaptive introgression, highlighting how ancient gene flow has shaped modern human reproductive biology and disease susceptibility.

title: Multi-omics data reveal the origin of cardiac myxoma
summary:
# Summary

Multi-omics analyses of cardiac myxoma reveal that this common benign cardiac tumor originates from endothelial cells undergoing aberrant endothelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EndMT), driven by activation of TGF-β and Wnt/β-catenin pathways. Single-cell transcriptomics identified two distinct tumor subtypes: Subtype 1 characterized by MAPK/WNT/EGFR pathway activation and Subtype 2 by ribosomal and oxidative phosphorylation signatures with immune-evasive programs. The MET–PTK2 signaling axis emerged as a critical driver of tumor initiation and progression, promoting cell proliferation, migration, and invasion, while extracellular matrix remodeling and dysregulated purine and nicotinamide metabolism contribute to pathogenesis. Metabolomic and transcriptomic profiling identified disease-associated markers including SOX4/SOX5, GATA4, and CDH11, with abnormal phosphodiesterase expression linked to tumor growth. Surgical resection remains the standard treatment with excellent prognosis, achieving 80–98% survival rates post-intervention.

title: Analysis of diagnostic apoptosis-related biomarkers and immune cell infiltration characteristics in endometriosis by integrating bioinformatics and machine learning
summary: This study identified three key apoptosis-related genes—FAS, PRKAR2B, and CSF2RB—as critical diagnostic biomarkers for endometriosis through integrated bioinformatics and machine learning analysis. FAS and CSF2RB are significantly downregulated in endometriosis tissue compared to controls, and reduced FAS expression impairs the FAS-FASL signaling pathway, leading to apoptosis resistance of ectopic endometrial cells that normally undergo elimination. The dysregulated apoptosis-related genes correlate with abnormal immune infiltration patterns characteristic of endometriosis, including elevated gamma delta T cells, immature dendritic cells, monocytes, and natural killer cells, while activated B cells and effector memory CD4+ T cells are reduced. CSF2RB functions as a shared receptor subunit for GM-CSF, IL-3, and IL-5 signaling, which regulate immune cell proliferation and monocyte activation through JAK/STAT and MAPK pathways. The study identified potential therapeutic agents targeting these biomarkers, including aspirin and GnRH agonists for FAS, and theophylline and leridistim for PRKAR2B and CSF2RB respectively, offering new strategies for endometriosis treatment through restoration of apoptosis and immune homeostasis.

title: Link between the ESR2 (rs3020449) polymorphism and breast cancer risk in Bangladeshi women
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates the ESR2 gene polymorphism rs3020449 and its association with breast cancer risk in Bangladeshi women, focusing on estrogen receptor beta's role as a tumor suppressor. The rs3020449 variant, located in the ESR2 promoter region, demonstrated a protective effect against breast cancer development, with the heterozygous AG genotype showing reduced risk compared to the AA genotype (OR = 0.58). The polymorphism was also associated with lower tumor aggressiveness, reduced tumor grade advancement, and decreased cancer staging progression in affected women. Mechanistically, the SNP likely modulates transcription factor binding affinity and ESR2 expression levels, influencing critical cancer-related cellular processes including cell proliferation, apoptosis, and tumor invasiveness. The findings suggest that ESR2 promoter polymorphisms, particularly rs3020449, could serve as genetic biomarkers for stratifying breast cancer risk, while also supporting broader evidence that estrogen signaling pathways significantly influence hormone-related cancer susceptibility.

title: Dissecting immune cell-specific genetics in migraine: a multi-omics framework for target discovery and therapeutic prioritization
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary: This study employed a multi-omics framework to dissect the immune genetic basis of migraine by identifying 25 immune cell-specific expression quantitative trait loci (eGenes) causally associated with migraine susceptibility. Key genes including CDC42, NELFCD, HOXB3, HAX1, and FHL3 were identified across multiple immune cell types, with CD4 naive/central memory T cells showing the highest number of associated variants. The research established that T cells, B cells, natural killer cells, and their secreted cytokines and chemokines contribute to migraine pathogenesis through neuroinflammatory mechanisms, complementing existing therapies targeting serotonin and calcitonin gene-related peptide receptors. Pleiotropy analyses revealed that migraine-associated eGenes overlap genetically with comorbid conditions including depression, gastrointestinal disorders, and hypertension, suggesting shared immunological mechanisms. Drug repurposing identified FDA-approved agents such as hydroxychloroquine sulfate and bazedoxifene as potential therapeutic candidates targeting CDC42 and NELFCD, while pathway analysis revealed minimal adverse associations for candidate genes like GINM1 and TMA7, supporting their therapeutic prioritization. These findings provide a comprehensive immune cell-centric map for migraine drug discovery and highlight immune dysregulation as a tractable therapeutic target beyond current neurovascular-focused approaches.

title: Hallmarks of primary headache: part 3 – cluster headache
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary:
# Summary

Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder classified within trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias, characterized by severe unilateral pain and cranial autonomic symptoms mediated through activation of the trigeminal-autonomic system and release of neuropeptides. The hypothalamus plays a central role in cluster headache pathophysiology by regulating circadian rhythms and pain discharge patterns; repeated hypothalamic activation in chronic cases may cause neurotransmitter exhaustion that disturbs circadian hormonal rhythmicity. Multiple genetic variants contribute to cluster headache susceptibility, including genes involved in circadian regulation (HCRTR2, CLOCK, CRY1/CRY2), alcohol metabolism (ADH4), CGRP receptor signaling (RAMP1), and microglial inflammation (MERTK), with elevated MERTK mRNA and its ligand galectin-3 observed in patient serum and trigeminal ganglia. Environmental triggers including smoking, alcohol consumption, and sleep disrupt attacks that exhibit characteristic circadian peaks (21:00–03:00) and circannual patterns aligned with temperature and daylight changes. Current therapeutic options remain limited, with acute treatments comprising sumatriptan and oxygen, prophylactic agents including verapamil and steroids, and emerging neuromodulation approaches, though some patients develop drug-resistant chronic cluster headache. Strong familial clustering and increased risk among first-degree relatives (5–18 fold) underscore the significant genetic contribution to disease susceptibility.

title: Molecular analysis of the evolutionary history of endometrial and ovarian carcinoma in Lynch syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals the molecular evolutionary pathways linking Lynch syndrome to endometrial and ovarian cancers through defective DNA mismatch repair. Lynch syndrome, caused by germline mutations in MMR genes (MLH1, MSH2, and MSH6), substantially elevates cancer risk, with lifetime endometrial carcinoma incidence ranging from 37-49% depending on the affected gene, and ovarian carcinoma incidence of 11-17%. Nearly all endometrial (98%) and ovarian (98%) carcinomas and atypical hyperplasias (91%) displayed MMR deficiency, indicating this is a hallmark feature of disease progression. The study identified a stepwise progression model wherein atypical endometrial hyperplasia serves as a precursor lesion, with 20-40% eventually developing into carcinoma, and demonstrated that somatic variants in cancer-associated genes like ARID1A, KTM2C, PTEN, and PIK3CA accumulate during this transition. Notably, frameshift mutations predominated in these genes due to microsatellite instability, and shared somatic variants were frequently detected between paired hyperplasia and carcinoma samples, as well as in histologically normal endometrium years before malignant transformation. These findings establish that Lynch syndrome-associated gynecological cancers result from the combination of inherited MMR deficiency and progressive acquisition of somatic mutations in chromatin remodeling and cell cycle regulation genes.

title: Genomic Insights into the Origin, High Fecundity and Environmental Adaptation of Hu Sheep
summary:
# Summary

This genomic study of Hu sheep reveals how a livestock breed adapted to warm, humid environments while maintaining exceptionally high reproductive output. Three genes—BMPR1B, UNC5C, and GRID2—synergistically drive the breed's high fecundity trait through direct protein interactions, with the FecB mutation in BMPR1B nearly fixed in the population. During their migration approximately 1,330 years ago from northern to southern China, Hu sheep experienced strong selection for environmental adaptation, with distinct alleles at chromosomal loci chr6:30090758 and chr10:29526684 conferring humidity versus dry environment adaptation. Environmental adaptation genes show associations with reproductive phenotypes, suggesting coevolution between climate response pathways and fecundity traits mediated by genes like BMPR1B and RXFP2. Temperature and humidity adaptation involved multiple biological processes including circadian entrainment, lipid metabolism, and Hippo signaling, demonstrating the complex genomic architecture underlying Hu sheep's dual capability for year-round estrus and environmental resilience.

title: Identification of Potential Diagnostic Biomarkers and Drug Targets for Endometriosis from a Genetic Perspective: A Mendelian Randomization Study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study identified seven genes as potential diagnostic biomarkers for endometriosis, with distinct protective and pathogenic roles. Four genes—ADK, EEFSEC, INO80E, and LDAH—are downregulated in endometrial tissue affected by endometriosis and exert protective effects through multiple mechanisms including selenoprotein incorporation (EEFSEC), chromatin remodeling and DNA repair (INO80E), and adenosine metabolism regulation (ADK). Conversely, three genes—HCG22, RAP1GAP, and RSPRY1—are elevated in diseased endometrial tissue and promote endometriosis through inflammation, immune dysregulation, and abnormal cell proliferation signaling pathways. Virtual drug screening identified FDA-approved compounds with high binding affinities to these target genes, including netupitant and bedaquiline for EEFSEC, avapritinib for HCG22, midostaurin for INO80E, and ubrogepant for RSPRY1, representing potential therapeutic candidates. These findings establish a genetic framework linking dysregulated gene expression to endometriosis pathogenesis and suggest specific molecular targets for diagnostic and therapeutic intervention.

title: Identification of Immune Candidate Genes in Post-Sepsis Syndrome: Linking Innate Immunity to Long-Term Autoimmune Responses
summary: Post-sepsis syndrome is characterized by persistent immune dysregulation involving dysregulated monocyte gene networks that contribute to both immune suppression and autoimmune manifestations. Three key hub genes—CD4, C1QA, and HLA-DRA—involved in T-cell regulation, complement activation, and antigen presentation respectively, are significantly elevated during the recovery phase and reduced in post-sepsis syndrome mice compared to healthy controls. Functional studies demonstrate that CD4 silencing worsens sepsis outcomes and reduces survival, while CD4 overexpression improves outcomes, establishing CD4 as a critical mediator of immune recovery. Transcriptomic analysis identified 168 overlapping hub genes associated with post-sepsis clinical features, with upregulated genes enriched in immune activation processes (cell chemotaxis, neutrophil activation) and downregulated genes involved in glutathione metabolism and apoptosis. Pathway analysis revealed that autoimmune-related pathways including systemic lupus erythematosus, graft-versus-host disease, and autoimmune thyroid disease are normalized during recovery, suggesting post-sepsis syndrome shares mechanistic overlap with autoimmune conditions. These findings establish monocyte dysregulation as a central driver of the dual immune dysregulation phenotype in post-sepsis syndrome, with potential therapeutic implications through CD4-targeting interventions.

title: Laminin-gamma2 promotes oral squamous cell carcinoma growth by activating the p-ERK/c-Myc signaling pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that laminin-gamma2 (LAMC2), an essential component of the epithelial basement membrane protein laminin-332, is significantly upregulated in oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) tissues and cell lines, where it correlates with poorer survival rates and increased mortality risk. LAMC2 overexpression enhances OSCC cell proliferation and invasion capacity by activating the p-ERK/c-Myc signaling axis, while LAMC2 knockdown suppresses these oncogenic phenotypes and reduces tumor formation in vivo. Treatment with ERK inhibitors can reverse LAMC2-induced OSCC progression by blocking ERK phosphorylation, suggesting a tractable therapeutic target. Beyond OSCC, LAMC2 is aberrantly expressed across multiple cancer types including pancreatic, colorectal, lung, ovarian, and thyroid carcinomas, highlighting its broad role in cancer biology. These findings position LAMC2 as a crucial oncoprotein in OSCC pathogenesis and suggest that small molecule inhibitors targeting LAMC2 or its downstream signaling could serve as effective therapeutic strategies for OSCC patients.

title: LncRNA LINC02381/miR-133b axis promotes tumorigenesis and predicts survival in pancreatic cancer
summary: This study identified the long non-coding RNA LINC02381 as a critical oncogenic factor in pancreatic cancer, operating through a competing endogenous RNA mechanism with microRNA-133b. LINC02381 is dramatically elevated in pancreatic cancer tissues and cell lines, where it directly binds and sequesters miR-133b, thereby suppressing this tumor-suppressive microRNA. Knockdown of LINC02381 significantly inhibited pancreatic cancer cell proliferation, migration, and invasion in multiple cell lines, effects that were reversed by miR-133b inhibitor, confirming the functional LINC02381/miR-133b axis. Clinically, elevated LINC02381 expression correlated with adverse features including lymph node metastasis, advanced TNM stage, and perineural invasion, and served as an independent prognostic factor predicting poor overall survival. The study demonstrates that LINC02381 represents a promising therapeutic target and biomarker for pancreatic cancer prognosis, with potential implications for understanding similar ceRNA networks in other cancers including endometriosis-associated malignancies.

title: Exploring the Causal Relationship Between Circulating miRNAs and Sepsis Through Mendelian Randomization Analysis
summary: This Mendelian randomization study identifies circulating microRNAs as causal regulators of sepsis risk through distinct mechanistic pathways. Three miRNAs—hsa-miR-125a-5p, hsa-miR-494-3p, and hsa-miR-885-5p—demonstrate protective effects against sepsis, while hsa-miR-196b-5p, hsa-miR-27b-3p, and hsa-miR-598-3p increase sepsis susceptibility. These miRNAs regulate their effects by targeting genes involved in bacterial infection responses, FoxO signaling, and inflammatory pathways, with hsa-miR-27b-3p displaying the most extensive gene regulation through 439 target genes. Mechanistically, protective miRNAs suppress pro-inflammatory cascades and reactive oxygen species production, while risk-increasing miRNAs promote inflammation and apoptosis in critical tissues including lungs, kidneys, and heart. The study establishes miR-150 and miR-4772-5p-iso as biomarkers for distinguishing sepsis from systemic inflammatory response syndrome, and reveals that miR-146a and miR-223 levels correlate with sepsis disease stages. These findings suggest circulating miRNAs as potential therapeutic targets and diagnostic tools for sepsis management.

title: Expression and prognosis of CXCL13 in uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma based on bioinformatics analysis
summary:
# Summary

This bioinformatics study identifies CXCL13 (C-X-C motif chemokine ligand 13) as a key prognostic biomarker in uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma (UCEC). CXCL13 is significantly elevated in UCEC tissues compared to normal endometrial tissue and functions as a ligand for CXCR5, promoting recruitment of B cells, T cells, and other immune cells to the tumor microenvironment. Elevated CXCL13 expression is independently associated with favorable overall survival, progression-free survival, and disease-specific survival in UCEC patients, with particularly strong prognostic benefit observed in younger patients and those with early-stage disease. The chemokine's protective effect correlates strongly with infiltration of multiple immune cell populations including CD8+ T cells, B cells, CD4+ T cells, dendritic cells, and neutrophils, suggesting CXCL13 promotes antitumor immunity through tertiary lymphoid structure formation alongside CXCL9. CXCL13 expression also correlates with immune checkpoint genes (TIGIT, LAG3, PDCD1, CTLA4) and other immune regulatory markers, and the most favorable prognosis occurs when high CXCL13 expression coincides with elevated B cell and CD8+ T cell infiltration in UCEC tissue.

title: Pharmacogenomic analysis of alarelin acetate-induced hepatotoxicity: a case report and literature review
summary: This case report documents the first documented severe hepatotoxicity associated with alarelin acetate, a GnRH analogue widely used to treat endometriosis and hormone-sensitive malignancies. A 37-year-old female developed acute hepatocellular injury with marked transaminitis (ALT elevations exceeding 1,100 U/L) following subcutaneous administration during a phase I clinical trial. Pharmacogenomic profiling identified specific genetic variants associated with this adverse event, including NUDT15 *3/*3 homozygosity and HLA alleles HLA-DRB1*15:01 and HLA-DQB1*06:01, which are known to influence drug-specific immune responses and drug metabolism. The hepatotoxicity was reversible upon drug discontinuation and treatment with hepatoprotective agents including glycyrrhizin and polyene phosphatidylcholine, with normalization of liver enzymes within 18 days. These findings underscore the importance of pharmacogenomic screening and highlight HLA polymorphisms and nucleotide metabolism genes as potential predictive biomarkers for drug-induced liver injury risk, similar to patterns observed with amoxicillin-clavulanic acid and antituberculosis drugs.

title: Genetic Association and Drug Target Exploration Between Inflammation-Related Proteins and the Risk of Primary Ovarian Insufficiency
summary: This study identifies inflammation-related proteins as critical modulators of primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) risk through genetic association analysis and drug target exploration. The research reveals a complex inflammatory landscape where certain cytokines and chemokines increase POI risk—including IL-18, IL-18R1, MCP-1/CCL2, and ARTN—while others provide protection, such as CXCL10, CX3CL1, IL-17C, and TRANCE. Mechanistically, risk-associated proteins converge on key signaling pathways including the IL-18, JAK-STAT, and oncostatin M pathways, with IL-18 directly promoting granulosa cell pyroptosis and diminished ovarian reserve. Protective proteins suppress inflammatory signaling through NF-κB inhibition and immune modulation, while dysregulation of TGF-β1 and LIF-R signaling further contributes to POI pathogenesis. The study identifies genistein, melatonin, and tretinoin as promising therapeutic candidates that target ferroptosis to counteract POI development. These findings establish a comprehensive framework linking inflammation-related protein dysregulation to ovarian dysfunction and suggest multiple intervention strategies for POI treatment.

title: Identification of Novel Disease-Modifying Agents for Pelvic Organ Prolapse by Systematic Druggable Genome-Wide Mendelian Randomization
summary: Using genome-wide Mendelian randomization, this study identified novel druggable gene targets for pelvic organ prolapse (POP) by analyzing genetic associations with disease risk. Key protective genes—ESR1, DES, SMAD3, and DDX5—showed that increased expression decreases POP risk, suggesting these represent promising therapeutic targets. Conversely, overexpression of genes including SLC12A2, EHMT2, TGFB2, PLA2G6, and ADAMTS5 was associated with elevated POP risk, indicating that their inhibition could be disease-modifying. The identified genes participate in vaginal development, glycosaminoglycan binding, intracellular ion homeostasis, and estrogen signaling pathways, highlighting the multi-system molecular basis of POP pathogenesis. These findings provide a systematic, genetically-validated approach to prioritize drug candidates for POP, with ESR1 and DES emerging as particularly strong therapeutic targets based on multiple lines of evidence. This resource offers a translational framework for developing novel treatments for this common pelvic floor disorder.

title: Integrated transcriptomic and single-cell RNA-seq analysis identifies CLCNKB, KLK1 and PLEKHA4 as key gene of AKI-to-CKD progression
summary:
# Summary

This integrated transcriptomic and single-cell RNA-seq study identified CLCNKB, KLK1, and PLEKHA4 as key biomarkers associated with acute kidney injury (AKI) and its progression to chronic kidney disease (CKD). The three genes are significantly associated with multiple immune cell types and are commonly targeted by tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, suggesting potential environmental or toxicological involvement in AKI-CKD progression. CLCNKB participates in oxidative phosphorylation and amino acid degradation pathways, while PLEKHA4 appears to play a particularly important regulatory role in both AKI and CKD development. The transition from AKI to CKD is substantially influenced by immune-inflammatory responses and kidney fibrosis, with specific cell types including Type A intercalated cells and collecting duct-principal cells identified as key contributors. These findings provide potential therapeutic targets for preventing the transition from acute to chronic kidney pathology and mitigating associated complications including hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

title: Striae gravidarum in the Han Chinese pregnant population: identifying genetic markers and risk factors through a prospective cohort study
summary: This prospective cohort study in Han Chinese pregnant women identifies striae gravidarum (stretch marks) as a multifactorial condition involving both clinical risk factors and genetic determinants. Multiple physiological parameters—including younger maternal age, higher pre-pregnancy and gestational weight gain, increased BMI, and larger abdominal girth—show strong associations with striae gravidarum development. Genome-wide association studies identified numerous genetic variants across genes including PTPRD, PPARGC1A, FGF12, RAB38, MUC16, ELN, and others involved in collagen/elastic fiber metabolism and cellular signaling. Histopathologically, striae gravidarum involve disruption of collagen bundle organization and elastic fiber networks with abnormal tropoelastin deposition, suggesting compromised dermal structural integrity during pregnancy. The condition demonstrates significant psychological impact, with striae gravidarum associated with postpartum anxiety and depression. These findings establish striae gravidarum as a pregnancy-related dermatological condition shaped by convergent maternal metabolic factors, genetic predisposition, and inherent mechanisms of rapid skin expansion.

title: ARID1A Loss plus CD8+ T-Cell Infiltration Associate with Favorable Clinical Outcomes in Urothelial Carcinoma
summary: This study demonstrates that ARID1A loss combined with high CD8+ T-cell infiltration identifies a urothelial carcinoma subgroup with exceptional clinical outcomes. ARID1A, a chromatin-remodeling component of the switch/sucrose nonfermentable complex, is frequently mutated in urothelial carcinoma and its loss triggers a type I interferon response through the cGAS-STING pathway, promoting T-cell infiltration and antitumor cytotoxicity. Patients with ARID1A loss and high CD8+ T-cell infiltration exhibit an inflamed tumor microenvironment characterized by increased tertiary lymphoid structures, elevated effector molecules (IFN-γ and perforin), upregulated chemokines (CCL5, CCL21, CXCL13), and reduced immune-suppressive populations including DC-SIGN+ macrophages and B7-H3/B7-H4-expressing cells. This molecular phenotype is associated with higher tumor mutational burden and neoantigen load, conferring improved overall survival, lower recurrence rates, and superior responses to both chemotherapy and immunotherapy, including atezolizumab treatment. These findings establish ARID1A loss and CD8+ T-cell infiltration as predictive biomarkers for favorable prognosis and treatment response in urothelial carcinoma, supporting their potential clinical utility in patient stratification and therapeutic decision-making.

title: LncRNA FTX/OCT4/miR-122-5p orchestrates self-renewal and lineage commitment of human dental pulp stem cells by directly targeting FOXO3
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates a regulatory pathway governing human dental pulp stem cell (hDPSC) pluripotency through a long noncoding RNA-microRNA-transcription factor axis. The lncRNA FTX suppresses hDPSC proliferation and differentiation by inhibiting OCT4, which in turn represses miR-122-5p expression; this miR-122-5p downregulation allows increased FOXO3 expression, a transcription factor that further inhibits cell proliferation and differentiation. Overexpression of miR-122-5p partially rescues the inhibitory effects of FTX, confirming miR-122-5p's role as a functional antagonist in this regulatory circuit. The FTX/OCT4/miR-122-5p/FOXO3 pathway represents a critical mechanism controlling the balance between self-renewal and lineage commitment in hDPSCs, with implications for directing stem cell fate decisions. Given that lncRNA FTX has previously been implicated in cancer cell proliferation and apoptosis, this axis may provide important mechanistic insights relevant to both regenerative medicine applications in dentin regeneration and understanding aberrant stem cell regulation in disease contexts.

title: Specific Marker Gene Analysis for Primary Central Nervous System Lymphoma Based on Methylation Difference and Development of Detection Primers
summary:
# Summary

Primary central nervous system lymphoma (PCNSL) is a high-mortality CNS malignancy with variable presentation and diagnostic challenges, typically occurring in patients with a median age of 56-61 years and often associated with immunosuppression. Through epigenome-wide methylation analysis using 450K microarray and RRBS methodologies, the study identified promoter hypermethylation of the RHEB gene as a specific biomarker for PCNSL, with methylation levels significantly elevated in tumor tissue compared to healthy controls. Custom primers targeting the RHEB promoter methylation site were successfully developed and validated for distinguishing PCNSL from normal CNS and non-CNS tissues, demonstrating potential for clinical plasma-based detection. Beyond RHEB, seven additional genes—FAM83B, B3GAT1-DT, PLOD2, DNMBP-AS1, GNG7, and TEAD1—were identified as methylation-associated markers correlating with poor patient prognosis. Treatment with the demethylating agent decitabine reversed RHEB hypermethylation in PCNSL cell lines, reducing methylated region expression while increasing unmethylated region expression, suggesting therapeutic potential for epigenetic intervention. These DNA methylation markers represent promising biomarkers for improved PCNSL diagnosis, prognostication, and treatment monitoring compared to traditional histopathology approaches.

title: Endometrial Steroid Receptor Dysregulation and Its
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates the dysregulation of endometrial steroid receptors in recurrent implantation failure (RIF), revealing a pathological imbalance characterized by elevated estrogen receptor-alpha (ESR-α) and reduced ESR-beta and progesterone receptor B (PGR-B) expression. This receptor imbalance promotes excessive endometrial inflammation and impairs implantation by suppressing the integrin ITGB3 and activating pro-inflammatory pathways, while simultaneously reducing anti-inflammatory progesterone signaling. Notably, women with RIF demonstrate decreased biomarkers of reproductive competence—including anti-mullerian hormone (AMH) and vitamin D3—alongside elevated inflammatory markers (hs-CRP), establishing reciprocal negative correlations between these parameters. The dysregulated ESR-α-to-ESR-β ratio promotes an estrogen-dominant, pro-inflammatory endometrial microenvironment that impairs decidualization and embryo implantation, effects partially mediated through suppression of the vitamin D receptor and reduced progesterone receptor functionality. These findings suggest that restoring steroid receptor balance and vitamin D3 levels may represent therapeutic strategies to overcome implantation failure and restore immune tolerance in the endometrium.

title: Protein markers of ovarian cancer and its subtypes: insights from proteome-wide Mendelian randomisation analysis
summary:
# Summary

This proteome-wide Mendelian randomisation study identified novel protein markers associated with ovarian cancer and its subtypes. Follitropin subunit beta (FSHB) and its complex partner CGA showed robust associations with endometrioid ovarian cancer, consistent with the hypothesis that prolonged gonadotropin exposure increases risk, similar to late menopause and reduced ovulation. Beyond endometrioid disease, additional proteins including LRRC37A2, PECAM1, ROBO4, and vascular adhesion molecules showed weaker associations with serous and epithelial ovarian cancer subtypes. The study identified several druggable targets, including the FSHR antagonist suramin and the tissue factor-directed therapy tisotumab vedotin-TFTV, offering potential therapeutic opportunities. Four proteins (MFAP2, SEMG2, DLK1, and NTNG1) demonstrated potential causal links with ovarian cancer, while associations for eleven others were attributed to genetic pleiotropy from ABO and MAPT-AS1 loci rather than direct causation. These findings expand understanding of the molecular pathways underlying ovarian cancer pathogenesis and suggest new avenues for biomarker development and therapeutic intervention.

title: Integrative transcriptomic analysis identifies shared EndMT-related gene signatures in endometriosis and recurrent miscarriage
summary:
# Summary

This integrative transcriptomic analysis reveals that endothelial-mesenchymal transition (EndMT) represents a shared pathological mechanism linking endometriosis and recurrent miscarriage. The study identified 13 EndMT-related genes significantly associated with both conditions, including key regulators FGF2, ITGB1, VIM, NR4A1, MAPK1, SMAD1, TUBB3, and CDH11, with ITGB1 and VIM emerging as hub genes dysregulated in both diseases. EndMT dysregulation promotes tissue remodeling and fibrosis in endometriosis while impairing spiral artery remodeling critical for placental development in recurrent miscarriage, processes mediated through PI3K-Akt, MAPK, and SMAD signaling pathways. The analysis also identified distinct immune signatures, including gamma-delta T cells and monocytes in endometriosis, and natural killer cells and T follicular helper cells in recurrent miscarriage, underscoring the role of EndMT in modulating immune interactions and cytokine regulation. These shared molecular signatures suggest that EndMT-targeted interventions may have therapeutic potential for both reproductive disorders affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age.

title: DNA methylation profiles and cancer in children conceived after assisted reproductive technology
summary:
# Summary

Assisted reproductive technology induces widespread DNA methylation modifications during periconceptional development, with children conceived through in vitro fertilization showing increased leukemia risk, particularly acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Multiple genes show concordant hypermethylation patterns in both ART-exposed children and leukemia cases, including TP73 (a p53 homologue involved in tumor suppression), PRSS16 (regulating immune development), and several imprinted loci such as ATP10A, CHD2, and GNAS associated with loss of imprinting in cancer. Oxidative stress from in vitro culture and ovarian stimulation procedures, along with global DNA hypomethylation resembling cancer epigenotypes, represent key mechanisms linking ART to pediatric cancer risk. The study identified specific methylation-dysregulated genes that are known somatic or germline targets in leukemia, providing potential biomarkers for future pediatric leukemia risk assessment. Underlying parental infertility conditions including endometriosis, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and advanced maternal age may independently contribute additional epigenetic perturbations alongside ART-induced modifications.

title: Multi-omics integration to identify immune-associated biomarkers and potential therapeutics in periodontitis
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics study identified immune-associated biomarkers and signaling mechanisms underlying periodontitis pathogenesis through integrated genomic and epigenetic analysis. Periodontitis development is primarily driven by an excessive or imbalanced host immune response to oral microorganisms, with disease progression involving dysregulated inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-8, and IFN-γ. Nine key diagnostic genes (ATP2C2, FAM43B, FOXA3, HSPA12A, KIF1C, NCS1, PGM1, RASSF6, and SH2B2) and aberrant immune cell populations (elevated astrocytes, monocytes, neutrophils, and plasma cells; reduced platelets) were identified as disease biomarkers. DNA methylation emerges as a critical epigenetic regulator affecting cytokine-encoding, interferon-encoding, and chemokine-encoding genes, with differentially methylated genes enriched in NF-κB, PI3K-Akt, and HIF-1 signaling pathways that orchestrate inflammatory responses and tissue destruction. Network pharmacology analysis identified bisphenol A, acetaminophen, and valproic acid as potential therapeutic candidates targeting these dysregulated pathways.

title: Genomic landscape of endometrial polyps
summary:
# Genomic Landscape of Endometrial Polyps

Endometrial polyps represent benign tumor-like lesions with a complex genomic architecture involving recurrent chromosomal alterations and gene mutations that drive their development and malignant potential. Chromosomal rearrangements affecting HMGA1 and HMGA2 occur in 74% of polyps, leading to upregulation of these high mobility group proteins and their downstream target PLAG1, with these rearrangements involving partners such as LRMDA, RAD51B, and TRAF3IP2. Beyond HMGA alterations, the most frequently mutated genes include KRAS, PIK3CA, PIK3R1, PTEN, ARHGAP35, and the novel candidate driver UBE2A, which shows the highest variant allelic fraction among recurrently mutated genes and is implicated in human tumorigenesis. Estrogen and aromatase overexpression promote polyp development alongside inflammatory pathways, while risk factors such as tamoxifen treatment increase malignant transformation potential, with up to 3% of polyps progressing to endometrial cancer. These lesions clinically manifest as abnormal uterine bleeding and infertility, and hysteroscopic polypectomy serves as the primary minimally invasive treatment approach. The genomic alterations identified connect endometrial polyps to established endometrial cancer pathways, suggesting these polyps represent important precursor lesions with actionable molecular targets.

title: Low‐Allele‐Frequency Somatic Variants in a Cohort of Sporadic Saccular “Berry” Cerebral Aneurysms
journal: Journal of the American Heart Association: Cardiovascular and Cerebrovascular Disease
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies low-allele-frequency somatic variants in sporadic saccular cerebral aneurysms, revealing mutations in genes involved in critical vascular pathways. The identified variants cluster into functional categories including angiogenesis (PKD1, XDH), DNA repair (MLH1, DCC, SF3B3), extracellular matrix remodeling (COL4A1, COL4A5, FBN1, MMP8), and cancer-related genes (ERBb4, PTCH1). Multiple genes linked to hereditary vascular syndromes—such as COL4A1 in HANAC syndrome, COL4A5 in Alport syndrome, and FBN1 in Marfan syndrome—harbor somatic mutations in aneurysms, suggesting shared molecular mechanisms between familial and sporadic disease. Notably, the tyrosine kinase ERBb4 preserves blood-brain barrier integrity through MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling pathways and was identified as a novel aneurysm-associated gene. The findings demonstrate that somatic mosaicism, potentially interacting with germline predisposition, contributes to aneurysm development through dysregulation of cell proliferation and vascular remodeling pathways.

title: Development of a prognostic prediction signature for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis by integrating multiple programmed cell death-related genes and machine learning algorithms
summary: This study developed a prognostic prediction signature for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis by identifying seven key genes—TLR2, PDCD1LG2, AKT3, SLC1A4, ANTXR2, HTRA1, and TIMP1—that are significantly upregulated in IPF patients and associated with poor outcomes. Multiple forms of programmed cell death, including apoptosis, ferroptosis, necroptosis, and immunogenic cell death, play central roles in IPF pathogenesis, with excessive apoptosis of alveolar epithelial cells triggering fibroblast activation and pulmonary fibrosis progression. The enriched molecular pathways include apoptosis, TNF signaling, and NF-κB signaling, which regulate epithelial cell survival and inflammatory responses. Immune profiling revealed that monocytes and activated natural killer cells are significantly elevated in high-risk IPF patients and correlate with worse overall survival, with these immune populations showing strong co-expression with signature genes. Drug screening identified 25 potential therapeutic molecules, including histone deacetylase inhibitors like vorinostat that promote fibroblast apoptosis, as well as mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists and acetylcholinesterase inhibitors. The proposed seven-gene prognostic signature, validated in a bleomycin-induced pulmonary fibrosis mouse model, provides a machine learning-based tool for predicting IPF patient outcomes and identifying candidates for targeted therapeutic interventions.

title: Integrated bioinformatics analysis and machine learning identifies FZD4, SRPX2, and COL8A1 as angiogenesis hub genes in endometriosis
summary: Angiogenesis, the formation of new blood vessels, is essential for endometrial lesion survival in endometriosis and constitutes a critical component of the pathogenic "3A" sequence (Attachment, Aggregation, Angiogenesis). Through integrated bioinformatics and machine learning approaches, FZD4, SRPX2, and COL8A1 were identified as hub genes that drive angiogenesis in endometriosis and are significantly upregulated in affected patients. FZD4 promotes neovascularization via the WNT/β-catenin signaling pathway, leading to activation of pro-angiogenic factors VEGF and Cyclin D1, while SRPX2 functions as an extracellular matrix protein that enhances endothelial cell migration and tube formation through FAK/integrin signaling cascades. COL8A1, a basement membrane-localized short-chain collagen, contributes to angiogenesis by enhancing VEGF secretion. All three genes are coordinately regulated by the transcription factor FOXC1 and miRNA-31-5p, and their expression patterns correlate with altered immune infiltration, including reduced regulatory T cells and increased pro-inflammatory immune populations. These findings establish FZD4, SRPX2, and COL8A1 as potential diagnostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets for endometriosis.

title: MX1 is a novel crucial prognostic and therapeutic target inducing chemoresistance in right-sided colon cancer: insights from machine learning-based multi-omics analysis
summary: Machine learning analysis of multi-omics datasets identified MX1, an interferon-induced GTPase, as a novel prognostic biomarker and therapeutic target in right-sided colon cancer (RCC). MX1 is predominantly expressed in epithelial cells and promotes chemoresistance, angiogenesis, and tumor progression through activation of the VEGFA-VEGFR2 pathway, while also enhancing cancer cell proliferation and migration in RCC cell lines. A seven-gene primary location signature incorporating MX1 and six other genes (MLLT6, PEDS1, STK4, TOMM34, SLC35A2, and LZTS3) was used to develop the PLriskScore, which independently predicts overall survival, TNM staging, and chemotherapy response in RCC patients. Elevated PLriskScore correlates with poorer prognosis, advanced disease stage, increased stromal infiltration, and enrichment of the VEGF pathway, with high-risk RCC patients exhibiting significantly higher mortality and shorter survival times. These findings implicate MX1 in immune evasion mechanisms and suggest that MX1 represents a promising therapeutic target to overcome chemoresistance in right-sided colorectal cancer.

title: In Vivo Models of Cardiovascular Disease: Drosophila melanogaster as a Genetic Model of Congenital Heart Disease
summary: Drosophila melanogaster serves as a powerful genetic model for studying congenital heart disease due to the evolutionary conservation of cardiac developmental mechanisms between insects and vertebrates. A complex regulatory network of transcription factors, including Hox genes, tinman, odd-skipped, even-skipped, and Mef-2, directs the specification and differentiation of cardiac cell populations during dorsal vessel formation. Key signaling pathways such as FGF and Slit/Robo regulate cardiac cell migration and convergence, while extracellular matrix proteins like pericardin and Viking stabilize the developing cardiac structure through interactions with alary muscles. The tinman gene in Drosophila is orthologous to the vertebrate NKX2 gene family, particularly NKX2.5, establishing a molecular link between invertebrate and mammalian cardiac development that enables identification of disease-causing mutations. Ancient Hox gene duplications have been associated with the emergence of increasingly complex cardiac chambers in vertebrates, and approximately 35% of human congenital heart diseases have identifiable genetic causes that can be discovered through investigation of simplified Drosophila cardiac gene networks.

title: The Zebrafish miR-183 Family Regulates Endoderm Convergence and Heart Development via S1Pr2 Signaling Pathway
summary:
# Summary

The miR-183 family (miR-183, miR-96, and miR-182) regulates early heart development in zebrafish by controlling endoderm convergence and cardiac precursor cell migration through the S1Pr2 signaling pathway. Overexpression of these microRNAs downregulates sox32 and multiple cardiac specification genes (gata4, gata5, nkx2.5, cmlc2, vmhc, amhc, nppa), leading to endoderm defects with characteristic holes and cardia bifida, a congenital heart defect characterized by bifurcated cardiac chambers. The miR-183 family directly targets and reduces S1Pr2 expression; S1Pr2 normally couples with Gα13 to activate RhoGEF signaling, which stabilizes fibronectin and activates cell surface integrins essential for coordinated endoderm convergence and cardiomyocyte migration. Loss of S1Pr2 signaling phenocopies miR-183 overexpression, confirming this pathway is critical for cardiac tube formation. These findings establish miR-183 family members as developmental regulators with potential implications for understanding congenital heart disease mechanisms and cardiovascular pathologies in postnatal life.

title: HOXA10 and HOXA11 Methylation: Epigenetic Barriers to Endometrial Receptivity in ART
summary:
# Summary

Impaired endometrial receptivity, a critical barrier to successful embryo implantation in assisted reproductive technology, significantly contributes to recurrent implantation failure. The homeobox genes HOXA10 and HOXA11 emerge as key regulators of endometrial receptivity, controlling progesterone receptor expression and regulating stromal decidualization and leukocyte infiltration necessary for embryonic implantation. Aberrant DNA methylation of HOXA10 and HOXA11 promoters disrupts their normal expression, and this epigenetic dysregulation is particularly prominent in endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and uterine fibroids—all conditions associated with reduced HOXA10/HOXA11 expression and implantation failure. Two natural compounds, epigallocatechin-3-gallate and indole-3-carbinol, show promise as therapeutic agents by upregulating HOXA10 and HOXA11 expression to restore endometrial receptivity. These findings underscore the critical role of epigenetic regulation in reproductive pathology and identify tractable molecular targets for improving fertility outcomes in women with gynecological diseases affecting implantation.

title: Computer-Aided Molecular Design Meets Network Toxicology and Molecular Docking: A Joint Strategy to Explore Common Molecular Mechanisms of Phthalates on Human Breast Cancer and Structure–Activity Relationship
summary:
# Summary
