This study employed computer-aided molecular design and molecular docking to elucidate how phthalate esters (PAEs)—specifically dibutyl phthalate, benzyl butyl phthalate, and diethylhexyl phthalate—promote breast cancer through interactions with key molecular targets. The three PAEs collectively bind to 47 common target genes including ESR1, TP53, PPARA, CTNNB1, and CDKN2A, with benzyl butyl phthalate demonstrating the strongest binding affinity. PAEs act as xenoestrogens by strongly binding estrogen receptors and promoting tumor sphere formation and breast cancer stem cell expansion through multiple signaling pathways including AhR/HDAC6/c-Myc and SPHK1/S1P/S1PR3 cascades. Structural analysis revealed that alkyl chain length and phenyl groups are critical determinants of PAE-hub gene binding affinity, with C17 alkyl chain elongation and phenyl group removal significantly reducing binding strength. The study's findings demonstrate that PAEs promote breast cancer progression primarily through transcription factor-mediated pathways and endocrine disruption, supporting the epidemiological association between PAE exposure and increased breast cancer risk, with relevance to understanding PAE impacts on estrogen-dependent tissues including the endometrium.

title: Identification and Validation of Iron Metabolism-Related Biomarkers in Endometriosis: A Mendelian Randomization and Single-Cell Transcriptomics Study
summary: This study identifies dysregulated iron metabolism as a central pathological mechanism in endometriosis, with iron concentration in peritoneal fluid directly associated with disease severity. Using Mendelian randomization and single-cell transcriptomics, the researchers identified BMP6 and SLC48A1 as key iron metabolism-related biomarkers that are significantly elevated in endometriosis tissues compared to controls. The lncRNA NEAT1 regulates BMP6 expression through microRNAs hsa-mir-22-3p and hsa-mir-124-3p, while SLC48A1 is modulated by hsa-mir-423-5p, hsa-mir-19a-3p, and hsa-mir-19b-3p, establishing critical gene regulatory networks. Single-cell analysis revealed macrophages and stromal stem cells as pivotal cellular components, with BMP6 negatively correlating with monocytes and SLC48A1 positively correlating with activated natural killer cells. The study further demonstrated that iron overload drives ferroptosis through hemosiderin deposition, linking dysregulated iron metabolism to cell death pathways that perpetuate endometriosis pathology. These findings establish iron metabolism-related genes as potential therapeutic targets for preventing and managing endometriosis-associated symptoms including pain, infertility, and psychological comorbidities.

title: Identification of metabolic reprogramming-associated biomarkers in endometriosis through integrated bioinformatics analysis
summary: This integrated bioinformatics study identified metabolic reprogramming as a central hallmark of endometriosis, wherein ectopic lesions exhibit enhanced aerobic glycolysis coupled with suppressed oxidative phosphorylation, similar to cancer cells. Glucose metabolic reprogramming drives endometriosis pathology through multiple mechanisms, including compromised oocyte quality and reduced endometrial receptivity that contribute to infertility, while also modulating macrophage polarization within lesions. Key biomarkers were identified through analysis of downregulated genes in ectopic endometrium, including HNRNPR, SYNCRIP, HSP90B1, HSPA4, HSPA8, CCT2, and CCT5, with immunohistochemistry confirming reduced expression of CCT2, HSP90B1, and SYNCRIP in endometriosis lesions. HSP90B1 emerged as particularly significant, as its overexpression upregulated glucose transporter GLUT1, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and COX-2, promoting glycolytic metabolism. The metabolic reprogramming-associated genes were enriched in functional pathways involving RNA splicing, protein folding, and ATP hydrolysis, and showed associations with CD8+ T cells, regulatory T cells, and mast cells in the endometriotic microenvironment. These findings establish metabolic reprogramming-related genes as potential diagnostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets for endometriosis.

title: Deciphering ESR1-driven transcription in human endometrial stromal cells via transcriptome, cistrome, and integration with chromatin landscape
summary:
# Summary

This study comprehensively characterized ESR1 (estrogen receptor alpha) function in human endometrial stromal cells by integrating transcriptomic, cistromic, and chromatin architecture analyses. ESR1, the dominant estrogen receptor in the endometrium, regulates both ligand-dependent and ligand-independent transcriptional programs through binding to classical estrogen response elements and non-canonical genomic sites in collaboration with transcription factors including Jun-AP1, RUNX, and TEAD. The research identified ESR1 as a critical regulator of decidualization—the transformation of stromal fibroblasts into immunotolerant decidual cells—by upregulating key genes including FOXO1, progesterone receptor, and prolactin signaling components through both direct promoter binding and distal chromatin looping interactions. Notably, ESR1 also activates cancer-related pathways and upregulates oncogenic genes including LINC01016, ERRFI1, NRIP1, and EPAS1, explaining how dysregulated estrogen signaling contributes to endometrial cancer and endometriosis. These findings reveal ESR1 as a central node controlling endometrial homeostasis with dual roles in normal pregnancy establishment and pathological disease progression.

title: Integrated analysis of single-cell and bulk RNA sequencing identifies GSTM4 as a tumor suppressor and prognostic biomarker in breast cancer
summary:
# Summary

This integrated single-cell and bulk RNA sequencing study identifies GSTM4 (Glutathione S-transferase Mu 4) as a tumor suppressor and prognostic biomarker in breast cancer. GSTM4 expression is significantly diminished in breast cancer tissues and positively correlates with improved patient outcomes, increased drug sensitivity, and enhanced antitumor immune responses. The mechanistic analyses reveal that GSTM4 suppresses breast cancer progression through its roles in glutathione metabolism, detoxification, and antioxidant defense, while in vitro studies confirm its inhibitory effects on cancer cell proliferation, invasion, and stemness. Additionally, GSTM4 negatively correlates with immune checkpoint gene expression, promoting antitumor immunity, and may be epigenetically silenced through promoter deletion mutations in cancer tissues. Beyond breast cancer, GSTM4 demonstrates tumor suppressor functions in hepatoma and pancreatic cancer while also mitigating endometriosis progression, highlighting its broader therapeutic potential as a biomarker for predicting treatment response and clinical outcomes.

title: Genomic Landscapes of Endometrioid and Mucinous Ovarian Cancers and Morphologically Similar Tumor Types
summary:
# Genomic Landscapes of Endometrioid and Mucinous Ovarian Cancers

This whole-genome sequencing study identifies distinct genomic alterations distinguishing endometrioid and mucinous ovarian cancers from morphologically similar tumors. Ovarian endometrioid carcinomas are characterized by frequent mutations in ARID1A, PIK3CA, PTEN, TP53, and CTNNB1, along with mismatch repair deficiency, and additionally harbor alterations in NF1, NF2, RAD51C, NOTCH genes, JAK1, and SMARCA variants—genes previously unreported in these tumors. The study identifies several novel gene fusions in endometrioid cancers, including ATAD1-PTEN, NF1-RAB11FIP4, UBXN6-NF2, and IPO11-CDK7, the latter potentially driving cell-cycle progression through CDK7 activation. Ovarian mucinous carcinomas differ significantly from gastrointestinal mucinous tumors in their mutation and methylation patterns, with recurrent alterations in KRAS, PIK3CA, NF1, MED1, and SMARCA4. Notably, ovarian endometrioid and uterine endometrioid carcinomas show genomically indistinguishable profiles, while ESR1 alterations in uterine endometrioid tumors, including tamoxifen-resistance hotspot mutations, suggest estrogen-driven biology. These findings reveal therapeutic opportunities, including rapamycin sensitivity in TSC2-deleted tumors and CDK7 inhibitors for tumors with kinase fusions.

title: Mendelian Randomization Analysis Reveals No Causal Genetic Link Between Dentofacial Anomalies and Dental Caries
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study investigated whether genetic variants associated with dentofacial anomalies, including malocclusion, causally influence dental health outcomes. Although observational studies report associations between dentofacial anomalies and conditions such as dental caries, periodontal disease, and oral ulcers, the genetic prediction analysis revealed no statistically significant causal relationships between malocclusion and these dental health problems. The study examined multiple genetic loci and candidate genes (AMELX, ENAM, and DSPP) involved in enamel and dentin formation, which regulate both craniofacial development and caries susceptibility, but found insufficient evidence for direct causal pathways. While dentofacial anomalies may indirectly contribute to poor oral health through mechanisms such as plaque accumulation and compromised oral hygiene, the genetic architecture underlying dentofacial development appears distinct from that driving caries and periodontal disease. These findings suggest that the observed clinical associations between dentofacial anomalies and dental problems likely reflect shared environmental and behavioral risk factors rather than direct genetic causation.

title: Whole-genome resequencing uncovers population structure and candidate molecular markers for litter size in hetian sheep
summary: This whole-genome resequencing study identified eleven candidate genes associated with litter size in Hetian sheep through genome-wide association analysis. The genes—LOC101120681, LOC106990143, LOC101114058, GALNTL6, CNTNAP5, SAP130, EFNA5, ANTXR1, SPEF2, ZP2, and TRERF1—are implicated in reproductive processes including growth, follicular development and maturation, cell proliferation, and apoptosis. Functional enrichment analysis revealed that these candidate genes are significantly involved in carbohydrate derivative metabolic processes and glycoprotein biosynthesis, with notable localization to the Golgi membrane and organelle envelope membranes. These molecular markers provide potential targets for selective breeding to improve reproductive performance in sheep and contribute to understanding the genetic basis of litter size in livestock.

title: HeraNorm: an R shiny application for identifying optimal endogenous controls for miRNA and RNA assays in qPCR and ddPCR
summary: HeraNorm is an R Shiny application designed to address a critical problem in molecular diagnostics: the widespread misuse of unsuitable endogenous controls in qPCR and ddPCR assays. Commonly used reference genes such as miR-16, GAPDH, RNU44, and RNU48 exhibit high expression variability and correlate with technical variables and disease progression rather than remaining stable, leading to erroneous results in cancer and other pathologies. In an endometriosis case study, the application identified miR-92a-3p and miR-421 as stable endogenous controls while appropriately excluding miR-16-5p due to excessive variability, and normalization to these optimal controls revealed significant differences between patient cohorts that were obscured by conventional reference genes. For endometrial RNA-seq analysis, RPS11 emerged as a superior stable control compared to canonical choices like TBP and GAPDH. By systematically selecting context-appropriate endogenous controls based on stability criteria, HeraNorm restored diagnostic significance to endometriosis and other disease studies, offering researchers a computational tool to optimize the validity and reproducibility of gene expression quantification across diverse biological contexts.

title: Comprehensive bioinformatic analysis reveals sorafenib response-related prognostic signature in hepatocellular carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This bioinformatic study identified a five-gene prognostic signature (DNASE1L3, ACSL6, ACAN, BRSK1, and CD68) that predicts sorafenib response in hepatocellular carcinoma, with DNASE1L3 and ACSL6 specifically associated with HCC progression and metastasis. Sorafenib, a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting serine/threonine and receptor tyrosine kinases, represents a first-line therapy for advanced HCC; sorafenib responders showed higher frequencies of FGF3/4 and VEGFA amplifications, while resistance-associated genes included IGF2R, C2orf27A, PON1, and CFB. Pathway analysis revealed that sorafenib response involves metabolic pathways, complement and coagulation cascades, drug metabolism-cytochrome P450 activity, negative regulation of cell proliferation, and apoptosis—with metabolic shifts toward aerobic glycolysis playing a key role. The risk stratification model showed that patients in the low-risk group had superior sorafenib sensitivity and better overall survival, with differential enrichment of nine immune cell types and 30 signaling pathways between risk groups. These findings provide a molecular framework for patient stratification and personalized prediction of sorafenib treatment outcomes in HCC.

title: Uterine NK Cell Polarization Associates with Chronic Endometritis and Predisposition to Recurrent Implantation Failure
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies uterine natural killer (uNK) cell polarization as a critical factor linking chronic endometritis to recurrent implantation failure, revealing that an elevated uNK2/uNK3 ratio disrupts endometrial immune homeostasis. The transcription factors EOMES and ELF4 drive the differentiation of cytotoxic uNK2 cells, while ELK4 and IRF1 promote uNK3 cells that support tight junction formation and platelet activation, with distinct marker genes (AFAP1L2, KLRC1, SOCS1 for uNK2; SAMD3 for uNK3) becoming elevated in pathological conditions. In chronic endometritis, heightened populations of pro-inflammatory immune cells—including CD68+ macrophages, CD83+ dendritic cells, CD8+ T cells, and FoxP3+ regulatory T cells—accompany elevated inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-4, IL-6, IL-10, IFN-γ, TNF-α), collectively creating a hostile microenvironment. Hyperactive uNK cells shift from their normal pro-pregnancy phenotype toward a pro-inflammatory state, potentially attacking semi-allogeneic embryos and disrupting endometrial receptivity markers, directly contributing to implantation failure. These findings establish uNK cell polarization as a mechanistic bridge between endometrial inflammation and reproductive failure, with specific transcriptional regulation and marker gene signatures offering potential diagnostic and therapeutic targets.

title: Role of Long Noncoding RNA Dio3os in Glycolipid Metabolism
summary: Long noncoding RNA Dio3os, located at chromosomal locus 14q32.31 within the imprinted DLK1/DIO3 cluster, emerges as a critical regulator of glycolipid metabolism and glycolysis in both normal and pathological contexts. Dio3os functions as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) that sequesters microRNA-122, thereby preventing miR-122 from inhibiting its targets including ALDOA and PDK4, ultimately promoting glycolysis and lipid metabolism particularly in cancer cells. The lncRNA exhibits a multifaceted regulatory architecture involving interactions with proteins such as PTBP1 to stabilize LDHA mRNA and NONO to suppress hepatocellular carcinoma stemness, alongside its inverse correlation with the epigenetic modifier EZH2 across multiple cancer types. Beyond cancer, Dio3os expression is dysregulated in metabolic and neurodegenerative conditions, showing reduced levels in atherosclerotic plaques and brown adipose tissue of offspring from obese mothers, while its elevation associates with obesity and diabetic peripheral neuropathy through MAPK cascade activation. In estrogen-deprived breast cancer cells, Dio3os upregulation drives aerobic glycolysis and cancer cell proliferation, a process modulated by m6A demethylase ALKBH activity, highlighting its potential as a metabolic adaptation mechanism during endocrine therapy resistance. These findings position Dio3os as a central metabolic hub influencing both malignant progression and systemic metabolic homeostasis.

title: Subtype Characterization of Ovarian Cancer Cell Lines Using Machine Learning and Network Analysis: A Pilot Study
summary: This pilot study applied machine learning and network analysis to characterize molecular subtypes of ovarian cancer cell lines based on transcriptomic data. The analysis identified distinct molecular profiles across major ovarian cancer subtypes: high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma is characterized by TP53 mutations, homologous recombination deficiency, and DNA repair deficiency; clear cell carcinomas show enrichment in hypoxia-inducible factor and oxidative stress response pathways along with PI3K/AKT and ARID1A activation; endometrioid carcinomas exhibit WNT/β-catenin pathway activation and ARID1A involvement; and mucinous carcinomas display gastrointestinal-like transcriptional programs. The study employed multiple machine learning approaches including random forests, support vector machines, and logistic regression combined with dimensionality reduction techniques (variance filtering, correlation-based selection, matrix factorization) and feature selection methods (LASSO regression, recursive feature elimination, SelectKBest) to classify subtypes from gene expression data. Network-based analysis using weighted gene co-expression network analysis and Pearson correlation-based co-expression networks linked molecular modules to immune cell infiltration and identified drug resistance-associated transcriptional programs with receptor tyrosine kinase activation, providing a comprehensive framework for molecular stratification of ovarian cancer subtypes.

title: Diabetes-associated differentially expressed genes as prognostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets in endometrial cancer: a comprehensive molecular analysis
summary: This comprehensive molecular analysis identified diabetes-associated differentially expressed genes (DM-DEGs) as prognostic biomarkers in endometrial cancer. Diabetes mellitus is not only a recognized risk factor for uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma (UCEC) but also significantly influences disease prognosis, with metabolic dysregulation contributing to cancer development. Through integrated bioinformatics analysis, researchers identified a five-gene prognostic signature comprising TRPC1, SELENOP, and CDKN2A as poor prognosis indicators, while GSN and PGR were associated with favorable outcomes, successfully stratifying patients into high- and low-risk groups. The identified DM-DEGs are predominantly involved in glucose and lipid metabolism, glycolysis/gluconeogenesis, PI3K-Akt signaling, and cellular proliferation pathways, mechanistically linking the diabetic milieu to tumor aggressiveness. A personalized nomogram integrating clinical parameters with this five-gene risk score demonstrated robust predictive capability for endometrial cancer survival rates. These findings establish diabetes-related molecular signatures as therapeutic targets and effective tools for patient risk stratification in endometrial cancer management.

title: Somatic mutation and selection at population scale
summary: This study introduces NanoSeq, an advanced duplex sequencing technology with ultra-low error rates, to detect somatic mutations at population scale and characterize selection pressures driving clonal expansion in normal tissues. Using optimized DNA fragmentation methods and error-correction protocols, NanoSeq achieved full-genome coverage while maintaining error rates below five per billion bases, enabling accurate detection of driver mutations even in damaged tissue samples. Analysis of over 600 donors revealed that somatic mutations accumulate linearly with age in a tissue-specific manner and are influenced by exposures such as tobacco and alcohol. The study identified both positive selection on driver genes like TP53, NOTCH1, and PIK3CA in oral epithelium—with NOTCH1 showing characteristic EGF-repeat mutations that disrupt ligand binding—and negative selection against truncating mutations in essential genes like SF3B1 and CHD4. Strikingly, certain cancer-associated genes including CDKN2A and PTEN show neutral or weak selection in normal oral epithelium despite driving head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, suggesting these genes may facilitate later carcinogenic stages through mechanisms such as immune escape. These findings establish NanoSeq as a powerful tool for studying early carcinogenesis and the molecular mechanisms underlying tissue-specific patterns of somatic selection during aging.

title: LINC01871 is exclusively expressed in T and NK cells and is highly induced upon CD4+ T cell activation
summary: LINC01871 is a cytoplasmic long intergenic noncoding RNA (lincRNA) exclusively expressed in lymphoid cells, particularly NK cells and T cells, with highest expression in effector and memory T cells. This lincRNA is substantially upregulated upon CD4+ T cell activation but is suppressed by the anti-inflammatory cytokine TGF-β in a dose-dependent manner, indicating its role in promoting T cell-mediated immunity. LINC01871 functions as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) by binding to and sequestering miR-142-3p, thereby upregulating the miRNA target ZYG11B and supporting IL-2 secretion, a key cytokine for T cell proliferation. The expression of LINC01871 correlates strongly with effector T cell markers including KLRB1, IL32, and CD160, positioning it as a molecular signature of activated T cell states. The suppression of LINC01871 results in modest reductions in IL-2 secretion, demonstrating its functional contribution to T cell activation responses. Additionally, elevated LINC01871 expression has been observed in CD4+ T cells from prediabetic children with autoimmune progression, suggesting potential relevance to immune-mediated pathologies including endometriosis.

title: Investigating the
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates how nonsynonymous single nucleotide polymorphisms (nsSNPs) in the VEGFA gene affect the protein's structure and function, with implications for angiogenesis-related diseases. Five disease-associated mutations (C266Y, P246L, R262Q, V258M, and V239M) located in the highly conserved PDGF/VEGF domain were identified and found to destabilize the VEGFA protein. Molecular dynamics simulations revealed that the R262Q and C266Y mutations cause particular structural disruptions, including dimer instability, altered receptor-binding conformations, electrostatic imbalances, and disulfide bridge disruption. The R262Q variant increased protein flexibility and conformational mobility, while C266Y induced transient overstabilization, both mechanisms potentially impairing VEGF receptor recognition and signaling. Since VEGFA is a critical regulator of angiogenesis operating through binding to VEGFR-1 and VEGFR-2, these mutations could compromise the normal vascular permeability and cell migration processes essential for proper vascular development and potentially influence tumor-related angiogenesis. This computational analysis suggests that certain VEGFA variants may represent disease risk factors by functionally compromising a key angiogenic pathway.

title: Research progress and potential therapeutic targets of a novel disulfide stress-driven cell death-disulfidptosis in gynecological tumors and other gynecological disorders
summary: This review identifies disulfidptosis, a novel NADPH deficiency-driven cell death pathway, as a potential therapeutic target in gynecological cancers and endometriosis. In gynecological tumors including ovarian, cervical, and endometrial cancers, disulfidptosis is orchestrated by dysregulated SLC7A11 and involves the thioredoxin system and Rac-WRC-Arp2/3-mediated actin network collapse, with SLC7A11 expression correlating with patient prognosis and chemoresistance. Therapeutic strategies to induce disulfidptosis include glucose deprivation, G6PD inhibition, and nanodelivery systems like FeOOH@Fe-Ap@Au, which can be synchronized with ferroptosis and potentially combined with immune checkpoint inhibitors. Beyond malignancies, disulfidptosis-related genes such as PDLIM1 and ACTB regulate ectopic lesion progression in endometriosis through immune-metabolic crosstalk mechanisms. Multiple disulfidptosis-related genes including GYS1, NCKAP1, PRDX6-AS1, and EMSLR show strong correlations with patient prognosis across these gynecological conditions. This emerging cell death pathway represents a promising avenue for developing novel therapeutics targeting both gynecological cancers and endometriosis-related pathology.

title: Identification of immune-relevant candidate genes in atherosclerosis by WGCNA and single-cell analysis
summary:
# Summary

Atherosclerosis is a systemic inflammatory disease characterized by immune cell infiltration and activation that leads to serious cardiovascular complications including myocardial infarction, stroke, and coronary artery disease. Low-density lipoprotein accumulation and oxidation by reactive oxygen species promotes macrophage infiltration into arterial walls, where macrophages engulf lipids to form foam cells that deposit in the endothelium. Using weighted gene co-expression network analysis and single-cell profiling, the study identified five immune-relevant biomarkers—SYK, PTPRC, ITGAL, FGR, and IL10RA—that are predominantly expressed in macrophages and monocytes and collectively regulate adaptive immune responses, cell activation, and T cell activation. Single-cell analysis revealed that atherosclerotic plaques exhibit elevated foamy macrophages and disease-associated macrophages alongside a shift in immune cell composition, with increased M0 macrophages and memory B cells but decreased M2 macrophages, naive B cells, and resting immune cells. The five biomarker genes show dynamic expression patterns during macrophage phenotypic transition from antigen-presenting cells toward foam cells, suggesting their involvement in disease pathogenesis. These findings identify potential drug targets, with existing therapeutics available for SYK (28 drugs), PTPRC (12 drugs), ITGAL (17 drugs), and FGR (6 drugs).

title: Key genes and associated mechanisms of PCOS and EC comorbidity: A bioinformatics analysis
summary:
# Summary: Key Genes and Mechanisms in PCOS and Endometrial Cancer Comorbidity

This bioinformatics study identified five hub genes—SYTL1, PARVG, ID4, IL1RN, and S100A9—that are significantly associated with comorbidity between polycystic ovary syndrome and endometrial cancer, conditions with documented elevated co-occurrence. These genes regulate critical pathways including TGF-β signaling, insulin signaling, GnRH signaling, and Wnt signaling, with abnormal expression patterns contributing to the shared pathophysiology. The five genes activate multiple downstream signaling cascades including P53 and JAK-STAT pathways, which likely mediate cellular proliferation and immune dysregulation. Immune profiling revealed that patients with PCOS-EC comorbidity display elevated proportions of pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages, plasma cells, T follicular helper cells, and regulatory T cells, with the key genes showing specific correlations to these immune populations. These findings suggest that dysregulation of the identified genes and their associated signaling networks drives both immune system imbalance and pathological cellular transformation in comorbid disease.

title: Immune Response and Lipid Metabolism Genes Associated with Acute Cerebral Circulatory Failure in Pre-Eclampsia in a Central Asian Population
summary: This study examines genetic polymorphisms in immune response and lipid metabolism genes associated with acute cerebrovascular disease in pre-eclampsia within a Central Asian (Kazakh) population. The research identifies TLR4, a key innate immune receptor on macrophages, dendritic cells, and neutrophils, as a trigger for pro-inflammatory cytokine production; notably, the low frequency of TLR4 minor alleles in Kazakhs (3.3%) correlates with reduced inflammatory reactions. Lipid metabolism genes including LPL, APOE, and FTO were found to influence disease risk through multiple pathways: LPL variants affect triglyceride metabolism and dyslipidemia, while FTO variations associate with obesity and metabolic syndrome, both established pre-eclampsia risk factors. The study also highlights PLEKHG1's role in vascular endothelial function through cytoskeleton reorganization and cell adhesion processes, with endothelial dysfunction representing a key mechanism in pre-eclampsia pathogenesis. Collectively, dysregulation of these genes leads to increased inflammatory responses, dyslipidemia, and impaired vascular tone regulation, which converge to increase the risk of acute cerebrovascular complications including ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke in eclamptic women. The findings underscore how genetic susceptibility, mediated through immune and metabolic pathways, modulates cerebrovascular disease risk in pre-eclampsia, with population-specific allele frequencies suggesting ethnic variations in genetic predisposition.

title: Machine learning-based tumor associated macrophages polarity signature predicts prognosis and treatment response in hepatocellular carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) play a critical role in shaping the hepatocellular carcinoma microenvironment and driving disease progression, with their polarity—particularly the CXCL9:SPP1 signature—emerging as a key prognostic indicator. Using machine learning (XGBoost), researchers developed a 17-gene TAM polarity-related signature that effectively stratified HCC patients into high-risk and low-risk groups with significantly different survival outcomes, demonstrating superior predictive accuracy compared to traditional clinical variables. High-risk patients exhibited enrichment in glycan metabolism, DNA repair, and oncogenic pathways alongside elevated immunosuppressive cell infiltration and immune checkpoint expression, while low-risk patients showed elevated lipid and amino acid metabolism. Mechanistic analysis revealed that SPP1 acts as a key signaling mediator in disrupted macrophage-immune interactions, with TTC1 and G6PD promoting HCC cell proliferation. Drug sensitivity analysis identified CDK1 inhibitors, PLK1 inhibitors, and statins as potential therapeutic targets for high-risk patients. This TAM polarity-based signature provides both improved prognostic value and potential therapeutic guidance for HCC patient stratification and treatment selection.

title: Establishment of novel cholangiocarcinoma cell lines with ARID1A deficiency and preclinical validation of synthetic lethality therapies
summary: This study established two novel cholangiocarcinoma cell lines, SiSP-K01 and SiSP-K05, derived from intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma with ARID1A heterozygous loss and near-complete protein depletion. ARID1A, a component of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex, normally antagonizes the PI3K-AKT pathway and participates in DNA repair and metabolism; its loss-of-function mutations are associated with poor prognosis in multiple cancer types. Both novel cell lines exhibited derangement of PI3K/AKT signaling and demonstrated synthetic lethality to PI3K/AKT inhibitors (Buparlisib and MK-2206) and PARP inhibitors (Olaparib), showing hypersensitivity compared to ARID1A-wildtype controls. The cell lines expressed typical cholangiocarcinoma markers and achieved 100% in vivo tumorigenesis when xenografted, confirming their authenticity and utility as preclinical models. These findings validate that ARID1A loss sensitizes cholangiocarcinoma cells to synthetic lethal therapies targeting the PI3K/AKT pathway and DNA repair mechanisms, offering promising therapeutic strategies for ARID1A-deficient cholangiocarcinomas.

title: Can classical statistics and deep learning converge on explainable, causally driven target discovery?
journal: DNA Research: An International Journal for Rapid Publication of Reports on Genes and Genomes
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how classical statistical methods and deep learning can be integrated to identify causal genetic targets in complex diseases like endometriosis. Traditional approaches including Mendelian randomization, fine-mapping methods (SuSiE, FINEMAP), and colocalization analyses identify causal variants and their associated genes by leveraging large biobank cohorts and expression quantitative trait loci (eQTL) databases such as GTEx. Deep learning approaches complement these methods by modeling high-order genetic interactions and integrating multi-omics data through autoencoders and transformer-based models that assign interpretable weights to molecular features. The authors highlight that genetic variants influence disease susceptibility through context-dependent gene expression regulation shaped by chromatin accessibility, histone modifications, and metabolic conditions—complexities that deep learning can capture through nonlinear pattern recognition. A critical case study demonstrates that therapeutic agents with solid genetic evidence (such as drugs targeting genes identified through GWAS and functional integration methods like PolyFun + SuSiE) have substantially higher clinical success rates than those lacking genetic support. The convergence of explainable deep learning with causal inference frameworks promises to transform target discovery by moving beyond single-variant assumptions toward comprehensive mechanistic models that integrate genetic, transcriptomic, epigenomic, and proteomic data.

title: Genome-wide association study of 398,238 women unveils seven loci associated with high-grade serous ovarian cancer
summary: This genome-wide association study of nearly 400,000 women identified seven genetic loci contributing to high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC), the most common form of epithelial ovarian cancer. The TP53 3'-UTR variant rs78378222-T emerged as a particularly important risk factor, increasing cancer risk 1.44-fold per allele by impairing TP53 3'-end processing and reducing p53 protein levels, thereby decreasing apoptosis. A polygenic score incorporating 64,518 variants showed strong predictive value with an odds ratio of 1.46 per standard deviation, indicating that HGSOC susceptibility involves cumulative effects across multiple genetic variants. Several newly identified loci harbor genes involved in critical cancer-related pathways: CCNE1 amplification drives genomic instability through replication stress; ARID1A loss creates synthetic lethality with GCLC inhibition; and TNFSF13/TNFSF12 variants influence tumor necrosis factor signaling. The study also revealed pleiotropic effects, with the TP53 variant and TNFSF13/TNFSF12 locus showing associations with other cancers and uterine fibroids, suggesting shared genetic architecture across gynecologic conditions. These findings provide new targets for therapeutic intervention, exemplified by PKMYT1 inhibitors that selectively target CCNE1-overexpressing cells.

title: A SWI/SNF complex-related genes signature predicts prognosis and immune infiltration in ccRCC with KCNK5 as a novel biomarker
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a prognostic signature based on SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex-related genes (SCRGs) in clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), identifying KCNK5 as a novel therapeutic biomarker. SCRG expression stratified patients into two clusters, with SCRGcluster B showing poor prognosis, elevated immune infiltration, and enrichment in pro-tumorigenic pathways including epithelial-mesenchymal transition and IL-6/JAK/STAT3 signaling. Through machine learning integration, five core genes (TMCC3, TOP2A, EPS8, PIK3R3, and KCNK5) were identified as independent prognostic factors, with KCNK5 demonstrating tumor-suppressive functions by inhibiting ccRCC cell proliferation, migration, and invasion when overexpressed. The resulting risk score successfully stratified patients into high- and low-risk groups with distinct survival outcomes and immune characteristics, with high-risk patients showing elevated immune checkpoint expression and TIDE scores indicating potential immune evasion. These findings suggest that the SCRG signature provides prognostic value in ccRCC and that KCNK5 restoration may represent a therapeutic strategy.

title: Bioinformatic analysis and validation of candidate genes in the eutopic endometrium reveal differential expressions in diffuse adenomyosis, endometrioma, and their co-existence
summary: This bioinformatic study identified distinct molecular signatures differentiating adenomyosis, endometriosis, and their co-existence through gene expression analysis in the eutopic endometrium. Matrix metalloproteinases—particularly MMP7 and MMP9—emerged as key biomarkers, with enhanced extracellular matrix remodeling characterizing adenomyosis and showing strong diagnostic discrimination (AUC=0.93) between adenomyosis and endometriosis. MMP7 expression positively correlated with uterine volume in adenomyosis, while hub genes including IGFBP5, SERPINA1, and THBS1 were implicated in adenomyosis pathogenesis alongside serine-type endopeptidase activity. Both conditions involve dysregulation of multiple molecular pathways—including transforming growth factor-beta signaling, IL6/MAPK pathways in adenomyosis, and inflammatory cascades plus epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition in endometriosis—which promote endometrial invasion, apoptosis dysregulation, and steroid hormone signaling abnormalities. The co-existent adenomyosis-endometriosis phenotype displayed distinct expression profiles with elevated KRAS mutations and demonstrated reduced pregnancy rates, suggesting these conditions involve overlapping yet mechanistically distinct hormone-dependent pathogenic mechanisms requiring differentiated clinical approaches.

title: Differentially expressed genes and therapeutic targets for nonalcoholic fatty liver disease from transcriptomic techniques
summary: This transcriptomic study identifies five key genes as therapeutic targets in nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD): four upregulated genes (ZFP36L1, ENTPD2, GBP1, and TCIRG1) and one downregulated gene (ENO2). ZFP36L1 regulates inflammatory responses by modulating cytokine mRNA stability, while ENTPD2 controls purinergic signaling and restrains cholangiocyte proliferation through extracellular ATP scavenging, with elevated expression correlating with activated immune cells. GBP1, induced by interferon-gamma, inhibits cellular and endothelial proliferation while promoting neutrophil activation and inflammasome responses in chronic inflammation. TCIRG1, encoding a lysosomal V-ATPase component, regulates autophagy flux and acidification, with restoration of lysosomal acidification improving lipid droplet accumulation, mitochondrial function, and insulin sensitivity. In contrast, ENO2, a glycolytic enzyme, is downregulated in NAFLD and negatively correlates with activated dendritic cells. The study demonstrates that NAFLD pathogenesis involves coordinated dysregulation of inflammatory, immune, metabolic, and lysosomal pathways, with lifestyle interventions such as weight loss providing clinical benefit through histological improvement.

title: Loss of miR-424 and miR-503 promotes decidualization of human endometrial stromal cells by increasing SCARA5 expression
summary:
# Summary

Decidualization of human endometrial stromal cells (HESCs) is a critical process for successful implantation that is driven by progesterone and cAMP signaling. During decidualization, expression of miR-424 and miR-503 microRNAs decreases, which permits upregulation of the transcription factor FOXO1 and its target gene SCARA5, both essential markers of the decidualized phenotype. When miR-424 and miR-503 are overexpressed, they directly bind to and suppress FOXO1, preventing its accumulation and causing its cytoplasmic mislocalization, which in turn reduces SCARA5 expression and suppresses key decidual marker genes including PRL, IGFBP1, and WNT4. This miRNA-mediated inhibition impairs morphological differentiation of HESCs and increases cell mobility, phenotypes characteristic of undifferentiated stromal cells. These findings reveal that loss of miR-424 and miR-503 constitutes a critical regulatory mechanism promoting decidualization through the FOXO1-SCARA5 axis, and dysregulation of this pathway may contribute to decidualization defects associated with implantation failure and miscarriage.

title: The Role of Vitamin D Metabolism-Related Genes in Recurrent Pregnancy Loss and Their Immune Microenvironmental Changes
summary: This study identifies vitamin D metabolism-related genes as key regulators of recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) through alterations in the immune microenvironment. Machine learning analysis identified DOCK11 and ETV2 as biomarkers for RPL, with both genes enriched in natural killer (NK) cells and involved in inflammatory pathways including interferon-gamma responses and TNF-α/NF-κB signaling. Vitamin D metabolism genes CYP2R1 and VDR normally suppress inflammatory responses and maintain immune homeostasis; however, reduced expression of CYP27B1 in women with recurrent miscarriage disrupts the activation of the active metabolite 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3, which binds to VDR to exert immunoregulatory effects. Vitamin D deficiency is associated with increased NK cell activity and heightened susceptibility to pathogenic challenges, while single-cell analysis revealed dysregulated immune interactions in RPL characterized by elevated NK and cytotoxic CD8+ T cell abundance alongside reduced macrophage populations. These findings suggest that impaired vitamin D metabolism promotes RPL through enhanced inflammatory signaling and dysregulated NK cell-mediated immune responses.

title: The Emerging Role of FAM171A2 in Gynecological Malignancies: Bioinformatic Insights from UCEC and Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This bioinformatic study investigates FAM171A2, a poorly characterized transmembrane protein, across gynecological malignancies, particularly uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma (UCEC) and ovarian cancer (OV). FAM171A2 is implicated in cellular signaling, vesicle trafficking, and extracellular matrix interactions, with elevated expression observed in multiple cancer types including gynecologic tumors, thyroid carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, and lung adenocarcinoma. The research reveals that FAM171A2 expression is regulated post-transcriptionally by multiple microRNAs (miR-15b-5p, miR-16-5p, miR-497-5p) and long non-coding RNAs (PVT1, UCA1, NEAT1, SNHG12), and is significantly associated with TP53 mutations, patient age, BMI, race, and menopausal status. FAM171A2 interacts with neuronal and immune-related protein modules, including NPTX1 (involved in synaptic remodeling) and ZNF696 (transcriptional/immune regulation), suggesting broader roles in cancer progression through epithelial-mesenchymal transition and genomic instability. These findings position FAM171A2 as a potential biomarker and therapeutic target in gynecological malignancies.

title: Clinical Spectrum, Surgical Management, and Outcomes of NR5A1-Related 46,XY Differences of Sex Development: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

NR5A1 gene mutations impair the production of steroidogenic factor-1 (SF-1), a nuclear receptor critical for gonadal differentiation, sex determination, and reproductive endocrine regulation. SF-1 acts as a master transcription factor that regulates essential genes including SRY, SOX9, AMH, and steroidogenic enzymes (STAR, CYP11A1), orchestrating the cascade of testicular development and testosterone synthesis necessary for male sexual differentiation. Mutations in NR5A1 cause 46,XY differences of sex development (DSD) through multiple mechanisms, including gonadal dysgenesis, Leydig cell dysfunction leading to incomplete masculinization, and defective steroidogenesis, clinically manifesting as undervirilization, hypospadias, and spermatogenic failure. Individuals with NR5A1-related DSD carrying streak-like intra-abdominal gonads face increased germ cell tumor risk and require appropriate surgical and endocrine management. Beyond gonadal effects, NR5A1 mutations are also associated with secondary conditions including premature ovarian insufficiency in 46,XX individuals, adrenal tumorigenesis, cryptorchidism, and emerging evidence suggests potential links to endometriosis and male infertility. This comprehensive understanding of NR5A1 function enables better clinical diagnosis, prognostication, and management of these complex developmental disorders.

title: Investigating the molecular mechanisms of resveratrol in treating diabetic foot ulcers: a comprehensive analysis of network pharmacology and experiment validation
summary: This comprehensive network pharmacology study elucidates how resveratrol treats diabetic foot ulcers by identifying and validating key molecular targets. Through bioinformatic analysis and machine learning, researchers identified cytidine deaminase (CDA) and ornithine decarboxylase 1 (ODC1) as critical resveratrol-binding proteins abnormally upregulated in diabetic foot ulcer tissues. Molecular docking confirmed strong binding affinity between resveratrol and these target genes, while pathway enrichment analysis revealed that CDA and ODC1 mediate immune microenvironment remodeling and alterations in the pathological microenvironment of ulcers. The therapeutic benefits of resveratrol stem from its multi-faceted properties including anti-inflammatory activity, promotion of angiogenesis, and reduction of inflammatory infiltration, which collectively support wound repair through fibroblast proliferation, keratinocyte migration, and immune cell polarization. Immunohistochemical validation confirmed abnormal expression of these resveratrol-associated genes in diabetic foot ulcer tissues, providing experimental evidence for the computational predictions. These findings establish resveratrol as a promising therapeutic agent that targets specific molecular drivers of diabetic foot ulcer pathogenesis, particularly the hyperglycemic microenvironment-induced persistent inflammation and vascular dysfunction.

title: A focused evaluation of genetic and epigenetic contributions to common infertility conditions
summary:
# Summary

This review examines genetic and epigenetic mechanisms underlying common infertility conditions. Epigenetic alterations, including abnormal DNA methylation in genes such as DNMT3A/B/L, H19, MEST, SOX30, and the piRNA pathway, along with histone modifications and noncoding RNAs, are strongly associated with infertility by reducing sperm quality and impairing embryo health. Chromosomal abnormalities including Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) cause male infertility through germ cell apoptosis, seminiferous tubule fibrosis, and hyalinization leading to azoospermia, though microsurgical sperm extraction combined with in vitro fertilization or intracytoplasmic sperm injection can restore fertility. Turner syndrome causes female infertility via gonadal dysgenesis and premature ovarian failure through accelerated oocyte apoptosis and disrupted folliculogenesis involving genes like BMP15, KDM6A, ZFX, and USP9X, with treatment options including oocyte cryopreservation and gamete donation. Additional chromosomal conditions such as Down syndrome, balanced translocations, and 46,XX testicular disorder of sex development contribute to infertility through gonadal impairment, recurrent miscarriages, and azoospermia, respectively.

title: Polymorphic Variants of FOXP3 Gene (rs 3761548) and (rs 3761549) are Significantly Associated with the Risk for Recurrent Pregnancy Losses. A Study in High Incidence Zone (Kashmir, North India)
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that polymorphic variants in the FOXP3 gene, specifically rs3761548 (−3279 C/A) and rs3761549 (−2383 C/T), are significantly associated with increased recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) risk in a Kashmiri population. FOXP3 is a critical transcription factor controlling the development and function of regulatory T cells (Tregs), which maintain fetal-maternal immunological tolerance through immune suppression. The A allele at rs3761548 and TT genotype at rs3761549 confer 1.7- to 3-fold increased RPL susceptibility, with heightened risk in women under 30 years without family history or consanguinity. FOXP3 polymorphisms dysregulate gene expression through altered transcription initiation, reducing Treg suppressive capabilities and disrupting the critical immunological barrier at the fetal-maternal interface. The resulting imbalanced immune response and decreased CD4+CD25+ Treg cell populations in peripheral blood and decidua create an unfavorable pregnancy environment, culminating in recurrent abortions.

title: Statistical methods to disentangle genetic effects influencing infertility and early fetal viability with a genome-wide application
summary:
# Summary

This genome-wide association study developed statistical methods to distinguish between parental genetic effects on fertilization success and fetal genetic effects on early pregnancy survival. The researchers identified multiple genetic loci associated with early fetal viability, including SNPs in MDC1, MICB, HCP5, and NOTCH4 that significantly influence fetal survival in naturally conceived pregnancies. Male infertility-associated genes such as ACTB, FSCN1, and RNF216 showed parental interaction effects particularly evident in assisted reproductive technology (ART) pregnancies, while female infertility genes including TBPL2 and CCDC201 demonstrated distinct mechanisms affecting reproductive outcomes. The study highlights that genetic variants influence infertility and early pregnancy loss through diverse biological pathways, ranging from chromosomal abnormalities to sperm dysfunction and ovulation disorders, establishing that fetal genotype critically determines embryonic survival once conception is achieved.

title: Female Mice with HSD17B1 Inactivation Show Mild Hyperandrogenism without Notable Impact on Reproductive Function or Bone
summary:
# Summary

HSD17B1 inactivation via a Ser143Ala mutation eliminates the enzyme's ability to convert estrone (E1) to estradiol (E2), resulting in markedly elevated intraovarian and circulating E1, androstenedione, and testosterone levels in female knock-in mice, accompanied by increased luteinizing hormone. The mutation disrupts the catalytic function at the active site while preserving mRNA expression, and compensatory mechanisms involving increased precursor steroid substrates and alternative HSD17B enzymes maintain circulating E2 at normal levels. Despite the significant androgenic shift in the steroid profile, HSD17B1-inactivated females show remarkably mild reproductive consequences, including normal puberty onset, fertility, and genitalia development, with only modest prolongation of the metestrous phase and slight ovarian weight reduction at six months. Transcriptomic analysis reveals widespread upregulation of genes involved in steroidogenesis, mitochondrial metabolism, and oxidative stress responses in mutant ovaries, suggesting metabolic compensation for the blocked estrogen synthesis pathway.

title: Case Report: Blood single-cell analysis of a IVB high-grade serous ovarian cancer patient presenting a favorable prognosis
summary:
# Summary

This case report presents single-cell blood analysis of an advanced high-grade serous ovarian cancer patient with an exceptional clinical response despite stage IVB disease. The patient's favorable prognosis was driven by multiple reinforcing factors: pathogenic BRCA2 and PALB2 mutations conferring platinum sensitivity, complete cytoreduction achieving R0 status, and notably high IL-12 production across immune cell populations (B cells, T cells, NK cells, monocytes) that enhanced anti-tumor immune responses and T cell/NK cell cytotoxic function. Circulating tumor cell analysis revealed distinct epithelial and epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) phenotypes, with epithelial CTCs expressing VEGFA to support the bevacizumab component of therapy, while EMT CTCs upregulated DNA repair genes and mTOR pathway components (RPTOR, RICTOR, MTOR), suggesting adaptive survival mechanisms that paradoxically retained platinum sensitivity. The tumor demonstrated heightened glycolytic metabolism through upregulation of multiple glycolytic enzymes (PKM, PGK1, LDHA, SLC2A1, PFKFB3, GAPDH, ENO1) coupled with downregulation of ribosomal genes, representing a metabolic adaptation linked to mTOR pathway activation and HIF-1α signaling. Collectively, the combination of platinum sensitivity, complete surgical cytoreduction, anti-VEGF therapy, and a robust endogenous anti-tumor immune milieu characterized by elevated IL-12 production achieved prolonged remission in this initially high-burden disease case.

title: Regulatory Role of Long Noncoding RNAs LINC00449 in Hepatocellular Carcinoma Mechanism and Prognosis Via Sponge miR-329-3p/KIF5A Axis
summary: This study elucidates a critical regulatory axis in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) in which the long noncoding RNA LINC00449 acts as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) to sponge miR-329-3p, thereby derepressing its target KIF5A and promoting HCC progression. LINC00449 is significantly elevated in HCC tissues and associates with poor patient prognosis, tumor size, vascular invasion, and advanced TNM stage. Silencing LINC00449 suppresses HCC cell proliferation, migration, and invasiveness while inducing apoptosis, effects that are mechanistically mediated through upregulation of the tumor-suppressive miR-329-3p, which subsequently downregulates KIF5A, a microtubule motor protein implicated in poor outcomes across multiple cancers. The LINC00449/miR-329-3p/KIF5A regulatory network mediates HCC deterioration, as confirmed by rescue experiments showing that miR-329-3p inhibition or KIF5A overexpression reverses the growth-inhibitory effects of LINC00449 silencing. These findings establish LINC00449 as a novel prognostic biomarker and potential therapeutic target in HCC, with the regulatory axis controlling fundamental processes of tumor cell biology.

title: Machine Learning-Derived Diverse Regulated Cell Death Patterns for Therapeutic Target Identification in Glaucoma
summary: This study employed machine learning to identify regulated cell death (RCD) patterns as therapeutic targets in glaucoma, a neurodegenerative disease characterized by progressive retinal ganglion cell death. The pathophysiology of glaucoma involves interconnected mechanisms including oxidative stress, glial cell activation, and mitochondrial dysfunction, with diverse RCD modes—including apoptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis, ferroptosis, and PANoptosis—contributing to neurodegeneration. The research identified 358 RCD-related differentially expressed genes (DEGs) and systematically validated 33 hub genes, notably PLEC, DLGAP4, and GPI, which were significantly downregulated in glaucoma patients and enriched in pathways controlling cytoskeletal organization, cell adhesion, and metabolic processes. Immune cell infiltration emerged as a critical driver of retinal ganglion cell damage, with elevated T cells, CD4 naïve cells, and activated NK cells in glaucoma patients showing significant correlations with hub gene expression. Using a ridge regression model achieving an average AUC of 0.889, the authors developed predictive diagnostic models based on RCD-related genes, providing novel molecular targets for both glaucoma detection and development of neuroprotective therapeutic strategies.

title: Case Report: Genomic characterization of a rare skull-base plasmacytoma
summary:
# Summary

This case report presents the genomic characterization of a rare skull-base plasmacytoma, identifying pathogenic variants in chromatin remodeling genes (ARID1A and KMT2D), B-cell neoplasm-associated genes (BCL7A), and oncogenic signaling genes (PTPN11 and NUP214), alongside a germline ATM mutation. These molecular alterations converge to disrupt epigenetic regulation, enhance proliferative signaling through RAS/MAPK activation, and impair DNA-damage responses, resulting in substantial genomic instability marked by 1q amplification and extensive copy number alterations associated with poor prognosis. The tumor's genomic profile closely parallels multiple myeloma, reflecting shared mechanisms of plasma cell transformation and suggesting heightened risk for disease progression to disseminated disease. Therapeutically, the patient received multimodal treatment combining radiotherapy with Dara-VRD (daratumumab-bortezomib-lenalidomide-dexamethasone), targeting proteasome function and immune activation, which effectively reduced tumor burden and mass effects. This case underscores the clinical utility of personalized genomic profiling in identifying actionable vulnerabilities and tailoring treatment strategies for extramedullary plasma cell malignancies that carry high transformation potential.

title: Expanding the genetic landscape of endometriosis: Integrative -omics analyses implicate key genes and pathways in a multi-ancestry study of over one million women
summary: This large-scale multi-ancestry genome-wide association study identified novel genetic loci and mechanisms underlying endometriosis in over one million women. The study discovered new ancestry-specific loci at 2p13.3 and 20q13.2 in African-ancestry populations, plus 49 total associated loci including the genes GRB14, ZNF536, and CHD6, while implicating key biological pathways including Wnt signaling, immune cell dysfunction, and cellular proliferation in disease pathogenesis. Gene expression analyses revealed dysregulation of critical genes such as ESR1 (most frequently elevated), GREB1 (most consistently upregulated), and ADK/ZBTB2 (most consistently downregulated), with stage-specific expression patterns in immune and stromal cell populations. Multi-omics integration identified genes with pleiotropic effects—notably KDR and RSPO3—supported by evidence across multiple molecular layers including transcriptomics, methylation, and splicing. The study identified 135 genetically correlated phenotypes, revealing associations with psychiatric comorbidities (anxiety disorders), metabolic traits (triglycerides, hyperlipidemia), and symptomatic outcomes (severe pain, fertility challenges, sleep disturbances), suggesting endometriosis shares underlying genetic architecture with multiple complex conditions. These findings substantially expand the genetic landscape of endometriosis and highlight therapeutic targets within Wnt signaling and immune regulation pathways.

title: Monogenic Contributions to Familial Endometriosis: A Scoping Review
summary: This scoping review identifies multiple monogenic contributions to familial endometriosis through examination of 16 families reported across eight studies. The identified genes regulate critical endometriosis pathways including estrogen metabolism (CYP1A1, UGT2B28), oxidative stress and detoxification (GSTM1, GSTT1), inflammatory signaling (TNFRSF1B, ADGRG7, TFG), immune regulation (CIITA), and cell proliferation/invasion (CDKN2BAS, FGFR4, WNT4). Notable variants include a CIITA missense variant that enhances endometrial cell migration and invasion, FGFR4 and NALCN variants following autosomal dominant inheritance patterns, and deletions in UGT2B28 and USP17L2 affecting hormone metabolism and cellular survival. The findings underscore that familial endometriosis involves both genetic and environmental factors, with first-degree relatives of affected women having sevenfold elevated risk, and highlight the disease's mechanistic connection to endometrial cancer pathogenesis through dysregulation of these monogenic loci.

title: Potential prognostic biomarkers in restenosis of peripheral arteries identified through comprehensive ceRNA network analysis
summary: This study employs ceRNA network analysis to identify prognostic biomarkers in restenosis of peripheral arteries (RPA), a cardiovascular complication characterized by recurrent vessel narrowing. The authors identified substantial transcriptomic alterations in RPA samples, including 1,854 upregulated and 225 downregulated genes, along with 562 upregulated and 57 downregulated long non-coding RNAs, reflecting dysregulation of the ceRNA regulatory axis where lncRNAs act as molecular sponges for microRNAs to modulate downstream mRNA expression. Six key genes—KCNC1, FRMPD4, ATP2B2, NOS1, SHC3, and FAIM2—were identified as central to RPA pathogenesis through network integration, with most showing significantly elevated expression in restenotic lesions and involvement in calcium signaling, vascular smooth muscle contraction, and cardiac conduction pathways. Notably, ATP2B2 demonstrated strong positive correlation with activated dendritic cells in RPA samples, while UPCAT1, PLA2G1B, and SMMPD4 were specifically elevated in rapidly progressing RPA cases, suggesting distinct molecular subtypes. The six key genes showed diagnostic accuracy (AUC >0.8) and represent potential therapeutic targets, with several genes possessing known pharmacological interactions that could be exploited for RPA treatment and prevention.

title: FCGR3A Drives Innate Immune Activation via M1 Macrophage Polarization in Pediatric IBD
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies FCGR3A as a critical driver of innate immune activation in pediatric inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) through its promotion of M1 macrophage polarization. FCGR3A expression is significantly upregulated in IBD patients and correlates strongly with increased M1 macrophage infiltration and enrichment in intestinal tissues, with FCGR3A knockdown effectively inhibiting this pro-inflammatory macrophage phenotype. Mechanistically, activated M1 macrophages triggered by FCGR3A signaling secrete inflammatory mediators that induce epithelial apoptosis, impair tight junction integrity, and drive disease progression in both in vitro and DSS-induced murine colitis models. FCGR3A variants were associated with reduced clinical responses to infliximab therapy, suggesting a pharmacogenetic role in IBD treatment outcomes. The study demonstrates that shifting macrophage polarization from M1 toward the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype—shown through antigen B treatment—alleviates IBD manifestations, highlighting FCGR3A-driven M1 polarization as a therapeutic target. With an AUC of 0.968 in ROC analysis, FCGR3A shows strong potential as a diagnostic and predictive biomarker for pediatric IBD.

title: Prenatal alcohol exposure induces circular and linear long non-coding RNAs, and protein-coding genes linked to proinflammatory neuroimmune function, promoting nerve injury-induced allodynia following morphine treatment
summary: Prenatal alcohol exposure (PAE) induces persistent neuroimmune dysregulation that increases susceptibility to opioid-induced allodynia following nerve injury. In PAE mice, morphine treatment significantly prolonged nerve injury-induced allodynia through day 24, whereas morphine had no effect on allodynia development in control mice. PAE upregulates proinflammatory circular RNAs (circPan3, circRab11) and long non-coding RNAs (lncSnhg14) while simultaneously downregulating protective circRNAs (circNr3c2, circAnkrd12), establishing a pro-allodynic transcriptional state. This dysregulated ncRNA landscape drives activation of damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) molecules including heat shock proteins (Dnaja1, Hsph1) and Hmgb1, which amplify IL-1β production and TLR4-mediated inflammation in the spinal cord. Key protein-coding genes associated with inflammatory responses (Nfkbia, Map3k6) and downregulation of genes supporting synaptic plasticity (Ephb3, Fzd6) further contribute to chronic pain sensitization. These findings demonstrate that PAE creates a vulnerable neuroimmune state through coordinated dysregulation of coding and non-coding RNAs, rendering exposed individuals paradoxically more susceptible to opioid-exacerbated pain rather than analgesia.

title: NAP1L4 in hepatocellular carcinoma progression and treatment from gene expression to clinical impact
summary: This study identifies NAP1L4, a senescence-related gene, as a critical prognostic marker and therapeutic target in hepatocellular carcinoma. NAP1L4 is highly elevated in HCC tissues and its increased expression strongly associates with poor overall survival and adverse prognosis through remodeling of the tumor microenvironment, including expansion of immunosuppressive M0 macrophages and regulatory T cells. Functionally, NAP1L4 knockdown significantly inhibits HCC cell proliferation and migration while activating the NOD-like receptor pathway, suggesting its role in promoting tumor progression. The study demonstrates that reducing NAP1L4 expression markedly enhances sensitivity to standard chemotherapy agents (cisplatin) and targeted therapy (axitinib), offering potential therapeutic strategies. Additionally, the research reveals broader connections between senescence-related genes and HCC pathogenesis through multiple mechanisms including senescence-associated secretory phenotypes (SASPs) that promote inflammation and tumor progression, as well as gut microbiota alterations regulated by angiopoietin-1 signaling. These findings establish senescence-related genes as pivotal factors governing HCC advancement and treatment response, with NAP1L4 serving as a promising biomarker for patient stratification and therapeutic intervention.

title: Integrative Analysis of eQTL Genes Reveals Key Biomarkers and Mechanisms for Early Diagnosis of Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This integrative analysis identified eight causal eQTL-regulated genes associated with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) susceptibility, with five hub genes—CTSC, SMYD3, MFGE8, IGFBP7, and POC1B—showing statistically significant causal relationships. CTSC emerged as a central driver, activating the ECM-integrin-PI3K/AKT signaling axis through co-expression with integrins ITGAV and ITGB3, thereby promoting apoptosis resistance, ECM remodeling, and metastatic capacity. Additionally, SMYD3 activates Ras/ERK signaling and transcriptional amplification, while MFGE8 facilitates immune evasion through PD-L1 trafficking, and IGFBP7 regulates TGF-β signaling to suppress proliferation. A machine learning-derived diagnostic nomogram incorporating these genes achieved excellent predictive performance (C-index = 0.92), substantially outperforming the conventional biomarker CA19-9 for early PDAC diagnosis. These findings establish eQTL-regulated genes as critical mediators of PDAC pathogenesis and provide biomarkers for improved clinical detection and therapeutic targeting.

title: Integrate bulk RNA and single-cell sequencing to identify prognostic genes associated with dietary restriction and circadian rhythm in colorectal cancer and conduct experimental verification
summary:
# Summary

This study integrated bulk and single-cell RNA sequencing to identify five prognostic genes (SOCS3, MMP3, SLC3A1, FABP4, and SERPINE1) associated with dietary restriction and circadian rhythm alterations in colorectal cancer. Dietary restriction regulates circadian rhythm function and reduces insulin resistance and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6), while circadian rhythm disruption accelerates cancer progression by impairing cell cycle regulation and DNA repair through loss of heterozygosity in the tumor suppressor APC. A risk stratification model incorporating these five genes demonstrated independent prognostic value for patient survival, with high-risk patients exhibiting elevated immune infiltration, stromal involvement, and immune evasion markers (TIDE score), alongside enrichment in complement and coagulation cascade pathways. Single-cell analysis revealed fibroblasts as key producers of FABP4 and SOCS3, while protein-protein interaction networks identified FN1, IL6, and LOX as central hub genes involved in extracellular matrix organization and inflammatory signaling. All five prognostic genes showed excellent diagnostic performance (AUC > 0.7) and differential drug sensitivity profiles, providing therapeutic targets for personalized colorectal cancer management.

title: Exploring the genetic architecture of multiple long-term conditions using a genome-wide association study in the UK Biobank population
summary: This genome-wide association study identified the genetic architecture underlying multiple long-term conditions (MLTC) in UK Biobank participants, revealing that the HLA region on chromosome 6 harbors the majority of significant variants, with rs9272539 near HLA-DQA1 being the most significantly associated SNP. Pathway analysis demonstrated that T cell activation and apoptosis signaling pathways are central to MLTC susceptibility, with HLA class II molecules (HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DRA) and apoptotic regulators (ATF6B, LTA, HSPA1A/B/L) playing key roles. Additional loci implicated genes associated with metabolic regulation (TCF7L2, FTO, ATXN2) and cell cycle control (MAD1L1), highlighting shared genetic mechanisms across diseases. The study identified five distinct disease clusters: metabolic diseases (Factor 1), mental illness (Factor 2), cancer and autoimmune disorders (Factor 3), musculoskeletal and inflammatory conditions (Factor 4), and digestive system diseases (Factor 5), with TLK1 implicated in cancer genesis. These findings underscore the substantial contribution of immune regulation and metabolic pathways to the genetic overlap observed among multiple chronic conditions.

title: When 25% Feels Like 100%: Confronting Recurrent Cystic Fibrosis Risk Across Consecutive Pregnancies
summary:
# Summary

Cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the CFTR gene on chromosome 7, which encodes a protein responsible for chloride and bicarbonate transfer across epithelial tissues. CFTR mutations impair chloride transport, leading to thick, sticky mucus and characteristic symptoms including chronic cough, recurrent lung infections, poor growth, digestive issues, and complications such as pancreatic insufficiency, diabetes, and hepatic dysfunction. This case study describes a family where compound heterozygous CFTR mutations—the F508del mutation and an exons 4-11 deletion—segregated across three pregnancies, with prenatal diagnosis by chorionic villus sampling identifying affected fetuses in consecutive pregnancies. The article emphasizes that accurate diagnosis requires comprehensive molecular testing including multiple ligation-dependent probe amplification (MLPA) or next-generation sequencing with copy number variant detection capability, as standard exome sequencing can miss large deletions. Variable expressivity and penetrance of CFTR mutations complicate genetic counseling, particularly for variants associated with residual CFTR activity that may result in milder phenotypes such as congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens rather than classic cystic fibrosis disease.

title: Updates on the role of miR-193b in the pathogenesis of human cancers and other diseases
summary: miR-193b is a microRNA with context-dependent roles in cancer pathogenesis, functioning primarily as a tumor suppressor through regulation of gene expression, proliferation, and apoptosis. In most cancers, including gastric, cervical, osteosarcoma, lung adenocarcinoma, and esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, miR-193b is downregulated and normally suppresses oncogenic targets such as CCND1, ETS1, PLAU, KRAS, and LAMC2, as well as key signaling pathways including PI3K-AKT, WNT/β-catenin, and c-Met. Conversely, miR-193b is elevated in esophageal and bladder cancers and acts as an oncomiR in colorectal cancer, where it positively correlates with TRF2 expression and promotes tumor progression. Beyond oncology, miR-193b dysregulation is associated with diverse non-malignant diseases including systemic sclerosis, diabetes, osteoarthritis, neurodegeneration, and autoimmune conditions. The expression of miR-193b is controlled by transcriptional regulators such as TRF2 and CTCF, while transcriptional repressors like c-Jun suppress its expression, and its own downregulation can be mediated by promoter hypermethylation. This dual functionality and broad tissue involvement position miR-193b as an important regulator with therapeutic potential across multiple disease contexts.

title: Differential Expression of IFI16, IL-33 and CD55 Link Potential Common Pathogenic Mechanisms for COVID-19 and Ulcerative Colitis
summary: This study identifies shared pathogenic mechanisms between COVID-19 and ulcerative colitis by analyzing differentially expressed genes in both conditions. Eleven hub genes—including IFI16, IL-33, CD55, OAS2, IFIT3, IFITM2, UBE2L6, SAMD9L, CCL18, SLC22A5, and ANPEP—were found to be dysregulated in both diseases, with most upregulated to promote innate immune and interferon signaling responses. Key regulatory pathways involve interferon signaling, neutrophil activation, cytokine-mediated responses, and IL-23-driven inflammation, with STAT1 and miR-155-5p identified as critical transcriptional regulators. The dysregulated inflammatory responses and tissue injury mechanisms shared between these conditions suggest that SARS-CoV-2 may exacerbate ulcerative colitis through enhanced cytokine storms and lymphocyte depletion. Drug repurposing analysis identified retinoic acid, cyclosporine, and TD-139 as potential therapeutic candidates targeting these common mechanisms, with the hub genes also showing associations with endometriosis, colon cancer, and other immune-mediated diseases.

title: Unveiling mitochondrial and PANoptosis-related biomarkers for premature ovarian insufficiency
summary:
# Summary

Mitochondrial dysfunction is a central pathogenic mechanism in premature ovarian insufficiency (POI), operating through decreased ATP production and reactive oxygen species (ROS) accumulation that trigger inflammation, apoptosis, and oocyte quality decline. Mitochondrial tRNA gene mutations and POLG genetic disorders further exacerbate this dysfunction and oxidative stress. PANoptosis, a programmed cell death pathway integrating pyroptosis, apoptosis, and necroptosis, accelerates ovarian reserve depletion and correlates positively with mitochondrial phenotype scores in POI. Six candidate biomarkers—four mitochondrial-related genes (ACSM3, ALDH1L1, MAOB, OSBPL1A) and two PANoptosis-related genes (NOS2, UCHL1)—are significantly downregulated in POI and human granulosa cells, with NOS2 emerging as the most clinically significant. NOS2 overexpression markedly suppresses both cell death and ROS production in ovarian tissues, suggesting a protective mechanism, while cranberry-derived exosomes relieve POI symptoms by reducing PANoptosis-related factor expression. These findings establish mitochondrial and PANoptosis pathways as interconnected drivers of POI pathology and identify six genes as potential diagnostic and therapeutic targets.

title: Genome-wide profiling of m6A modification and its role in the transcriptome of sequelae of pelvic inflammatory disease
summary:
# Summary

This genome-wide study reveals that N6-methyladenosine (m6A) methylation plays a critical role in sequelae of pelvic inflammatory disease (SPID), a chronic condition characterized by tissue adhesions, scarring, chronic pain, and infertility. Patients with SPID exhibited significantly elevated m6A methylation levels with 28,578 m6A peaks across 14,065 modified genes, predominantly located in the 3' untranslated regions and coding sequences of mRNAs. METTL3-mediated m6A methylation regulates inflammatory responses and ectopic implantation by affecting mRNA splicing, translation, and stability, while differentially methylated genes are enriched in pathways controlling neutrophil activation, the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling cascade, and immune system processes. Key findings identified six genes with significant m6A methylation-expression correlations in SPID: TET1 and ADAM23 were upregulated with m6A hypomethylation, while NUF2 was downregulated with hypermethylation, the latter affecting cell proliferation and apoptosis through the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway. These results suggest that dysregulated m6A methylation mediates SPID pathogenesis by altering the transcriptome, inflammatory immune responses, and cellular processes, offering potential therapeutic targets for managing this debilitating sequela of pelvic inflammatory disease.

title: Decoding Potential Cuproptosis-Related Genes in Sarcopenia: A Multi-Omics Network Analysis
summary: This multi-omics study identifies cuproptosis, a copper-dependent form of regulated cell death, as a novel contributor to sarcopenia development through disruption of mitochondrial function and energy metabolism. The research reveals that dysregulated copper metabolism and accumulation of intracellular copper ions trigger cuproptosis, leading to the downregulation of 14 critical sarcopenia-cuproptosis-related genes including SLC25A12, PABPC4, GOT1, and GOT2, which are essential for mitochondrial energy production and cellular respiration. These key genes are predominantly involved in mitochondrial pathways, particularly the citric acid cycle and oxidative phosphorylation systems, and regulatory network analysis identified FOXC1 and miR-16-5p as upstream modulators of these processes. In sarcopenia samples, dysregulated pathways shift toward inflammatory and proliferative processes, including elevated classical complement pathway and adipocyte differentiation, while normal energy-generating pathways become suppressed. The study demonstrates that abnormal copper metabolism contributes to muscle wasting through cuproptosis-mediated mitochondrial dysfunction, suggesting that copper homeostasis may represent a therapeutic target for addressing the reduced mobility, impaired quality of life, and comorbidities associated with sarcopenia.

title: Non-Invasive Methods for Early Diagnosis of Endometriosis—A Comprehensive Narrative Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines non-invasive molecular biomarkers for early endometriosis diagnosis, highlighting epigenetic and genetic mechanisms underlying disease pathogenesis. Aberrant DNA methylation, mediated by upregulated DNMTs (DNMT1, DNMT3A, and DNMT3B) in endometriotic lesions, dysregulates critical genes including ESR2, PR-B, HOXA10, and aromatase, contributing to altered gene expression patterns. Multiple signaling pathways—including WNT, PI3K-Akt, and MAPK—drive endometriosis pathology, with somatic mutations in genes such as KRAS, ARID1A, and PTEN playing significant roles; notably, KRAS activates SIRT1 and BCL6, which suppress p53 and impair leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) expression critical for embryo implantation. Promising non-invasive diagnostic approaches include circulating cell-free DNA (cfDNA) methylation patterns, dysregulated miRNAs detectable in saliva, and 16S rRNA profiling revealing gut microbiota dysbiosis characterized by pathogenic bacteria like *Escherichia coli* and depletion of beneficial *L. ruminococcus*.
