These molecular signatures collectively establish a foundation for developing non-invasive diagnostic tests that could enable earlier endometriosis detection and improve clinical management of this debilitating condition.

title: Interplay Between Dysregulated Immune System and the Footprints of Blood-Borne miRNAs in Treatment Naive Crohn’s Disease and Ulcerative Colitis Patients
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a complex interplay between dysregulated immune responses and circulating microRNAs in treatment-naive inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) patients. Dysregulated T-cell-mediated responses characterized by elevated IL-22 and IFN-γ, combined with upregulation of pro-inflammatory Th17 and Th1/Th2 pathways alongside diminished regulatory T-cell function, drive intestinal inflammation and barrier disruption in both Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. The researchers identified 141 elevated and 120 reduced miRNAs in IBD patients, with specific miRNAs—particularly miR-155, miR-223, miR-21, and miR-146a—orchestrating inflammatory signaling through NF-κB, JAK-STAT, and Toll-like receptor pathways. Notably, circulating miRNA levels correlated strongly with cytokine profiles and T-cell subset distribution, suggesting these miRNAs serve as biomarkers for disease pathogenesis. Additionally, several miRNAs including miR-21, miR-155, and miR-223 promote colitis-associated colorectal cancer through activation of tumor-promoting NF-κB and STAT3 signaling, linking chronic IBD inflammation to malignant transformation.

title: Identification of novel variants underlying non-syndromic primary ovarian insufficiency using a targeted NGS gene panel
summary: Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI), affecting 1-4% of women, is characterized by amenorrhea, hypoestrogenism, elevated follicle-stimulating hormone, and progressive loss of ovarian function before age 40, leading to infertility and long-term complications including osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease, and mental health disorders. This study employed targeted next-generation sequencing to identify novel genetic variants underlying non-syndromic POI, revealing mutations in genes functioning across three primary biological pathways: DNA repair, meiosis, and signal transduction. The most frequently mutated genes included DNAH5 (axonemal dynein), LAMC1 (laminin), ADAMTS1/19 (extracellular matrix remodeling), HSD17B4 (peroxisomal beta-oxidation), and Fanconi anemia complementation group genes (FANCM, FANCC, FANCG), along with X-linked variants in AR and PCDH11X. Gene Ontology analysis demonstrated that identified mutations critically impact oocyte differentiation, folliculogenesis, and follicular maturation. The study proposes an integrated diagnostic approach combining clinical assessment, ultrasound parameters, biochemical markers (OvAge method), and genetic analysis to enable early identification of women at POI risk and facilitate timely fertility preservation strategies such as oocyte cryopreservation.

title: Identification and validation of biomarkers related to centrosome replication in ulcerative colitis based on bulk transcriptome, single-cell RNA sequencing and experiments
summary: This study identifies centrosome amplification as a pathogenic mechanism in ulcerative colitis and validates six associated biomarkers—TEX11, SLC16A1, OVOL1, EDNRA, HEPACAM2, and SPIRE2—through integrated bulk transcriptomics, single-cell RNA sequencing, and experimental validation. Four biomarkers (SLC16A1, OVOL1, TEX11, and HEPACAM2) were significantly downregulated in UC, while EDNRA was elevated, indicating distinct dysregulation patterns. Centrosome amplification arises from cell division failure and improper regulation of centrosome replication proteins, leading to genomic instability and disease progression. Immune infiltration analysis revealed that these biomarkers correlate with differential immune cell populations, particularly neutrophils and M0 macrophages, suggesting immune-stromal interactions in UC pathogenesis. Single-cell analysis highlighted undifferentiated and enteroendocrine cells as key cell types implicated in disease. These biomarkers are functionally associated with cell adhesion molecule and oxidative phosphorylation pathways, which may drive inflammatory responses in UC.

title: Multiomic analysis of CCNE1 amplification associated molecular and immune features in gynecological cancers
summary:
# Summary

CCNE1 amplification is a recurrent molecular alteration in gynecological cancers, particularly enriched in aggressive histological subtypes including ovarian carcinosarcoma (12.46%), high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (9.84%), and uterine carcinosarcoma, while being absent in low-grade serous tumors. CCNE1 is predominantly expressed in epithelial cells where it drives proliferative pathways and is frequently co-altered with TP53, PIK3CA, and genes in the PI3K-AKT pathway, leading to replication stress, genomic instability, and increased metastatic potential. CCNE1-amplified tumors exhibit enhanced cell cycle, DNA replication, and p53 signaling pathway activation, and are linked to epithelial-stroma segregation and immune exclusion, establishing CCNE1 as a marker of tumor aggressiveness. Clinically, WEE1 kinase inhibitors such as adavosertib demonstrate durable responses in CCNE1-amplified platinum-resistant tumors, while mTOR pathway inhibitors like everolimus and vistusertib reduce tumor growth and restore PARP inhibitor sensitivity. These findings underscore CCNE1 amplification as a therapeutic target and prognostic indicator in gynecological malignancies, with distinct molecular profiles and treatment opportunities based on cancer histotype and co-alteration patterns.

title: Multi-omics analysis reveals shared diagnostic and therapeutic targets in endometriosis and recurrent implantation failure
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics study identifies shared molecular pathways underlying endometriosis and recurrent implantation failure (RIF), revealing that occult endometriosis accounts for approximately one-third of idiopathic RIF cases. Through comprehensive analysis, the authors identified five hub genes—SRPRB, RBM3, INSIG2, GYG1, and FBXW2—that are significantly downregulated in both conditions and serve as strong diagnostic biomarkers. These genes regulate critical processes including protein targeting to the endoplasmic reticulum (SRPRB), mRNA stability (RBM3), cholesterol metabolism (INSIG2), glycogen biosynthesis (GYG1), and protein degradation (FBXW2), with dysregulation disrupting endometrial receptivity and blastocyst attachment. The study further demonstrates that endometriosis and RIF share hyperactivated PI3K/AKT and dysfunctional Wnt/β-catenin signaling, mitochondrial oxidative stress, and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β and TNF-α) alongside aberrant immune infiltration characterized by increased CD8+ T cells and M1 macrophages. These findings establish a mechanistic etiological continuum between the two conditions and suggest that targeting these shared molecular abnormalities and immune dysregulation could provide unified diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

title: Closing the evidence loop—membrane-lipid homeostasis and vesicular transport link DEHP exposure to endometriosis
summary: This study establishes a mechanistic link between DEHP (di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate) exposure and endometriosis through disruption of membrane-lipid homeostasis and vesicular transport. DEHP activates the LXR/SREBP-1c and PPAR-α/γ signaling pathways and directly binds to seven key metabolic enzymes (UGT8, ELOVL6, HMGCR, EPHX1, SLC1A5, LYPLA1, and VAMP2), creating coordinated dysregulation of lipid synthesis and remodeling. The authors identified reciprocal regulatory interactions—including ELOVL6–UGT8 synergy in acyl-chain elongation and sphingolipid conjugation, HMGCR–LYPLA1 antagonism between cholesterol biosynthesis and depalmitoylation, and EPHX1–SLC1A5 coupling of detoxification with glutamine uptake—that reshape the endometriotic lesion microenvironment. A seven-gene diagnostic signature (UGT8, EPHX1, ELOVL6, HMGCR, LYPLA1, SLC1A5, VAMP2) provides clinically applicable stratification and identifies HMGCR and SLC1A5 as therapeutic targets. The work integrates genomic, computational docking, and molecular dynamics data to propose that DEHP-driven lipid reprogramming, vesicular trafficking dysfunction, and impaired detoxification jointly drive endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Construction and validation of nomogram prediction model for recurrent spontaneous abortion based on the expression of MALAT1, miR-515-5p, and MCL1 mRNA
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies a molecular regulatory pathway underlying recurrent spontaneous abortion (RSA) and develops a predictive nomogram model based on three key biomarkers. The researchers established that MALAT1, a long noncoding RNA, functions as a competing endogenous RNA that sequesters miR-515-5p, thereby attenuating miR-515-5p's inhibitory effect on MCL1 mRNA—a gene encoding an anti-apoptotic protein essential for trophoblast survival and placental development. Elevated MALAT1 expression and reduced miR-515-5p and MCL1 expression emerged as independent risk factors for RSA, alongside clinical factors including advanced maternal age, multiple previous abortions, and low progesterone levels in early pregnancy. The MALAT1/miR-515-5p/MCL1 regulatory axis disrupts the balance between trophoblast apoptosis and proliferation while impairing endometrial receptivity and immune tolerance. A nomogram prediction model incorporating expression levels of these three molecules significantly improves early diagnosis and risk stratification for RSA patients.

title: Detection and Molecular Characterization of Listeria monocytogenes and Listeria spp. Isolates Recovered From Cattle Farms in Mpumalanga Province, South Africa
summary: This study investigated Listeria monocytogenes and related Listeria species isolated from cattle farms in South Africa's Mpumalanga Province, characterizing the pathogen's prevalence, virulence mechanisms, and clinical significance. L. monocytogenes causes listeriosis, a serious infection associated with multiple adverse outcomes in cattle and humans, including abortion, preterm birth, encephalitis, meningitis, mastitis, and notably endometriosis. The organism is ubiquitous in agricultural environments, particularly found in silage, feed, water, soil, and animal feces, with poorly fermented silage facilitating its survival and multiplication. Pathogenesis is mediated by virulence genes including hlyA and surface-associated internalins (inlA, inlB, inlC, and inlJ) that enable epithelial cell invasion. Farm size was identified as a significant risk factor for L. monocytogenes detection, with the highest prevalence observed in silage samples and fecal material from cattle. The isolation of serotype 1/2a, associated with human listeriosis, highlights the zoonotic potential and public health implications of this pathogen in livestock production systems.

title: Individuals whose phenotype deviates from genetic expectation defined by common variation are enriched for rare damaging variants in genes that cause rare disease
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that individuals whose measured phenotypes deviate substantially from polygenic score predictions are significantly enriched for rare damaging variants in genes causing Mendelian disorders. By analyzing nine complex traits with both polygenic and monogenic heritability components—including height, bone mineral density, lipid levels, and age at menopause—the researchers identified specific gene-trait associations in phenotypically "misaligned" individuals. For example, individuals with shorter-than-expected stature carried enriched loss-of-function variants in ACAN, IGF1, and SHOX, while those with unexpectedly low LDL cholesterol harbored variants in PCSK9 and APOB. The findings establish that common-variant polygenic risk can modulate the penetrance of rare monogenic variants and suggest that common polygenic scores may be insufficient to identify disease risk in individuals carrying pathogenic rare variants. This approach provides a practical framework for enriching rare variant discovery in complex disease populations and may improve diagnosis of monogenic conditions that present atypically within polygenic disease cohorts.

title: Machine learning screening for disulfidptosis genes-associated immunosuppression status in osteosarcoma and rhabdomyosarcoma
summary:
# Summary

This machine learning study investigated disulfidptosis, a recently identified form of regulated cell death, as a key driver of immunosuppression in osteosarcoma and rhabdomyosarcoma, two aggressive pediatric cancers. Using the Ridge algorithm among 100 machine learning approaches, the researchers identified a five-gene prognostic signature (ACTN4, MYH9, FLNA, MYH10, and IQGAP1) that stratified patients into distinct immunosuppressive and non-immunosuppressive subtypes. The immunosuppressive subtype was characterized by elevated PD-1 and CTLA-4 checkpoint expression, low tumor immune scores, and reduced immune cell infiltration, correlating strongly with macrophage accumulation. Notably, IQGAP1 emerged as an independent prognostic factor in rhabdomyosarcoma, with high IQGAP1-expressing cells exhibiting elevated PD-L1 expression and immunosuppressive features. These findings suggest that disulfidptosis-related genes and their associated immunosuppressive phenotype represent novel targets for overcoming chemotherapy resistance and developing combinatorial therapies with immune checkpoint inhibitors.

title: Decoding cross-talk between Mpox and HIV: insights from transcriptomics and network-based analyses
summary: This transcriptomic and network analysis study reveals shared molecular mechanisms underlying HIV and Mpox co-infection, a clinically significant concern given that 28-47% of Mpox patients are HIV-positive. Through differential gene expression analysis, researchers identified 41 common differentially expressed genes (DEGs) shared between HIV and Mpox infections, which are primarily involved in hematopoietic cell lineage differentiation, immune response pathways (Th1/Th2 differentiation), and metabolic regulation. Eight hub genes were identified with opposing expression patterns between the two viral infections: SOCS3, NFKBIA, PLK3, PTGS1, ZFP36L2, and RGS16 are upregulated in HIV but downregulated in Mpox, while IFIT1 and CCNB2 show the reverse pattern, suggesting distinct but interconnected pathogenic mechanisms. These hub genes interact with key transcription factors (NFKB1, STAT6, HIF1A, CREB1) and microRNAs (hsa-miR-30 family members), which regulate inflammatory and stress responses characteristic of both infections. The study's molecular docking analyses identified compounds that bind to these hub genes, suggesting potential for simultaneous therapeutic intervention targeting shared pathways in HIV-Mpox co-infected patients.

title: Unveiling the mechanistic links between plasticizers and gastric cancer via network toxicology and molecular docking approaches
summary:
# Summary

Plasticizers including BBP and DEHP promote gastric cancer tumorigenesis through multiple interconnected molecular mechanisms, as revealed by network toxicology and molecular docking analyses. These compounds directly bind key proteins—BBP targets AKR1C1 and GRM2 while DEHP binds TDO2—leading to dysregulation of critical genes involved in cancer progression. Differential gene expression analysis identified upregulation of GRM2, MMP7, and TDO2 alongside downregulation of protective factors AKR1C1, CPB1, and CA2 in gastric cancer tissues. The pathogenic mechanisms converge on lipid metabolism pathways, where linoleic and arachidonic acid metabolites promote tumor growth, angiogenesis, invasion, and metastasis, while simultaneously activating proliferative signaling through Notch1 and GABA receptor pathways. DEHP specifically exacerbates epithelial-mesenchymal transition via mTOR and Smad2 pathway activation, and co-exposure with Helicobacter pylori substantially increases gastric cancer risk. These findings establish plasticizers as environmental risk factors that compromise gastric mucosal integrity and drive malignant transformation through coordinated disruption of metabolic and signaling networks.

title: Genetic pleiotropy underlying obesity and autoimmune disorders: a large-scale cross-trait gwas analysis in European ancestry populations
summary: This large-scale genome-wide association study reveals substantial genetic overlap between obesity and multiple autoimmune disorders, including rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, hypothyroidism, and multiple sclerosis, with obesity conferring a 30-50% increased risk for several conditions. The analysis identified 133 pleiotropic genes (including CLN3, SH2B1, and MMEL1) enriched in hematopoietic cell differentiation and immune homeostasis pathways, suggesting shared genetic mechanisms underlying both metabolic and autoimmune pathology. Bidirectional Mendelian randomization established causality in both directions: obesity directly increases risk for hypothyroidism, psoriasis, and multiple sclerosis, while type 1 diabetes increases obesity risk. Tissue-specific heritability analysis highlighted the spleen, whole blood, and EBV-transformed lymphocytes as key contributors, with IgD+ CD38- B cell-related traits identified as critical pathological conduits linking obesity to autoimmune disease. The relationship involves complex interactions among chronic inflammation, hormonal imbalances, and gut dysbiosis. These pleiotropic loci represent potential therapeutic targets for simultaneous prevention and treatment of both obesity and autoimmune conditions.

title: Identification and validation of programmed cell death-related biomarkers in severe pneumonia: A diagnostic observational study using bioinformatics analysis
summary: This bioinformatics study identifies four key programmed cell death-related biomarkers—BCL2, CDKN2D, DYRK2, and S100A8—that are significantly dysregulated in severe pneumonia, with CDKN2D and S100A8 upregulated while BCL2 and DYRK2 are downregulated in disease samples. These biomarkers participate in multiple critical pathways including graft-versus-host disease, complement and coagulation cascades, and apoptotic signaling, and the study identified 1,397 differentially expressed genes associated with severe pneumonia that regulate apoptosis, T cell receptor signaling, and NF-κB and Th17 cell differentiation pathways. BCL2 emerges as a central antiapoptotic regulator whose expression is controlled by interferon-gamma signaling and mitochondrial PKM2 phosphorylation, while CDKN2D functions as a cell cycle inhibitor involved in G1 phase control and cellular senescence. The immune microenvironment analysis revealed altered immune cell composition with distinct correlations between M1 and M0 macrophages, as well as reciprocal relationships between neutrophils and monocytes in severe pneumonia. These findings establish a comprehensive molecular framework linking programmed cell death dysfunction to severe pneumonia pathogenesis and identify BCL2 as a potential therapeutic target, supported by evidence that compounds like EGCG can upregulate BCL2 and ameliorate lipopolysaccharide-induced pneumonia.

title: Machine learning-driven glycolytic subtyping and exosome-based PKM splicing modulation overcome drug resistance in hyper-glycolytic myeloid leukemia
summary:
# Summary

This study employs machine learning to stratify acute myeloid leukemia patients into glycolytic subtypes, identifying a high-glycolysis C1 subtype driven by elevated expression of glycolytic enzymes including PKM, PGAM1, PGK1, and ALDOA, with HIF1A and MIF serving as key regulatory nodes. The C1 subtype is characterized by increased malignant cell proportion, genomic instability, and poor prognosis, whereas the C2 subtype exhibits greater immune cell infiltration and sensitivity to venetoclax. To overcome imatinib resistance mediated by pro-tumorigenic PKM2 isoform expression, the authors developed IL3-Lamp2b-engineered exosomes to deliver vivo-morpholino oligomers that reprogram PKM splicing toward the tumor-suppressive PKM1 isoform, effectively inhibiting leukemia growth. The C1 subtype demonstrates enhanced sensitivity to panobinostat, MK-2206, 17-AAG, and anti-PD-1 immunotherapy, providing subtype-specific therapeutic strategies. This integrated approach combining glycolytic subtyping with exosome-mediated PKM splice modulation addresses a major clinical challenge of chemotherapy resistance in high-glycolysis myeloid malignancies.

title: Integrative blood profiling uncovers inflammatory network signatures in high-altitude pulmonary edema
summary:
# Summary

High-altitude pulmonary edema (HAPE) is characterized by a complex inflammatory network signature driven by hypoxia-triggered immune activation. Hypoxia initiates an inflammatory response involving elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, leukotriene metabolites, and High mobility group Box 1 protein, which collectively activate inflammatory pathways and recruit immune cells, particularly neutrophils that produce oncostatin M—a cytokine involved in inflammation and extracellular matrix degradation. Integrative blood profiling identified 515 differentially expressed genes and eighteen hub genes including TLR2 and FOXO3, with key pathways involving immune response, leukocyte adhesion, and NF-κB signaling, alongside dysregulated long noncoding RNA-mRNA interactions associated with disease pathogenesis. Clinical manifestations include impaired gas exchange due to excessive capillary leakage, presenting as reduced peripheral oxygen saturation and elevated respiratory parameters that correlate with HAPE severity. Upper respiratory tract infections increase HAPE susceptibility, while glucocorticoid treatment with dexamethasone demonstrates therapeutic efficacy against this life-threatening condition.

title: A de Novo 2q23.1–2q23.3 duplication in a neonate with anemia, thrombocytopenia, and hypospadias: clinical and genomic characterization
summary: This case report characterizes a novel de novo 1,500 kb duplication at chromosome 2q23.1–2q23.3 in a neonate presenting with a complex phenotype of anemia, thrombocytopenia, hypospadias, hypothyroidism, hyperbilirubinemia, hypokalemia, and hypoproteinemia. The duplicated region contains multiple genes with documented roles in developmental and neurological processes, including LYPD6, which regulates cholinergic system function and when duplicated associates with developmental delay, hypotonia, and autistic features, and CNTN6, which mediates axonal connections and whose copy number variations have been linked to ADHD, seizures, and autism spectrum disorder. Additionally, the region harbors CHL1, a gene previously implicated in ovarian endometriosis, as well as genes involved in mitochondrial vitamin B12 metabolism and cytoskeletal organization. The genomic region 2q21.1–2q23.3 more broadly has been associated with agenesis of the corpus callosum and congenital cardiac defects, highlighting the clinical significance of copy number variations in this interval. Management of this patient's multiple metabolic and hematologic abnormalities required coordinated therapeutic interventions including blood and immunoglobulin transfusions, phototherapy, electrolyte supplementation, levothyroxine replacement, and ATP administration. This case expands the phenotypic spectrum of 2q23.1–2q23.3 duplications and underscores the importance of comprehensive genomic characterization in understanding neonatal multisystem disorders.

title: Bioinformatics analysis identifies AURKB as a prognostic biomarker across multiple human cancers
summary: Aurora kinase B (AURKB) emerges as a critical prognostic biomarker across multiple human cancer types through its dysregulation of cell cycle control and mitotic progression. AURKB is significantly overexpressed in breast, lung, liver, kidney, colorectal, and prostate cancers compared to normal tissues, while remaining relatively low in normal cerebral cortex, kidney, and testis. Elevated AURKB expression correlates with advanced clinical stages, reduced overall survival, decreased disease-free survival, and chemotherapy resistance, with an epigenetic mechanism involving promoter hypomethylation contributing to its overexpression. At the molecular level, AURKB regulates chromosome alignment and segregation during mitosis through its localization in the nucleoplasm and midbody, maintaining chromosomal stability and controlling cell cycle progression alongside related genes including CCNB1, CDC20, and TP53. Beyond its direct mitotic functions, elevated AURKB expression suppresses CD8+ T cell infiltration into tumors, enhancing immune suppression within the tumor microenvironment and potentially contributing to therapeutic resistance. These findings establish AURKB as a valuable prognostic indicator and potential therapeutic target for improving cancer patient outcomes.

title: TMT1A inhibits lung adenocarcinoma progression by suppressing M2 macrophage polarization
summary: TMT1A functions as a tumor suppressor in lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) through multiple interconnected mechanisms involving immune regulation and cell cycle control. TMT1A expression is significantly reduced in LUAD tissues compared to normal tissues, with promoter hypermethylation at CpG sites cg04477962 and cg26333317 contributing to this downregulation. Elevated TMT1A expression inhibits LUAD cell proliferation, migration, and invasion by suppressing epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition markers (downregulating Vimentin and ZEB1 while upregulating E-cadherin) and activating the P53/P21 cell cycle checkpoint pathway. A critical mechanism of TMT1A's anti-tumor activity involves inhibiting M2 macrophage polarization and downregulating PD-L1 expression, thereby promoting CD8+ T cell infiltration and adaptive immune responses. TMT1A acts as an independent prognostic factor, with higher expression correlating with longer overall and progression-free survival. Conversely, TMT1A knockdown activates oncogenic PI3K-Akt and TGF-β signaling pathways while suppressing antigen processing and presentation, underscoring TMT1A's essential role in preventing immunosuppressive tumor microenvironments.

title: Comparisons of iron phenotypes and reports of menses, pregnancies, and live births in women with HFE p.C282Y homozygosity and HFE wt/wt
summary:
# Summary

This study examines the relationship between HFE p.C282Y homozygosity, a common genetic variant associated with hemochromatosis, and reproductive outcomes in women. The HFE gene regulates iron homeostasis through its control of the hepatic hormone hepcidin, and individuals homozygous for the p.C282Y mutation exhibit significantly elevated transferrin saturation, serum ferritin, and provisional iron overload compared to wild-type carriers. Hemochromatosis and iron overload are associated with serious maternal and fetal complications, including hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, venous thromboembolism, advanced hepatic fibrosis, and cirrhosis. The study compares iron phenotypes and reproductive outcomes (menses patterns, pregnancies, and live births) between HFE p.C282Y homozygous women and wild-type controls, with implications for understanding how iron dysregulation may affect fertility and pregnancy outcomes. These findings suggest that iron status and HFE genotype should be considered in reproductive health management, particularly regarding the foetomaternal risks associated with maternal hemochromatosis.

title: Identification of key genes related to metabolic cell death in hepatic ischemia-reperfusion injury from transcriptome data and mechanism research using single-cell data
summary:
# Summary

This study identified ferroptosis and cuproptosis as key metabolic cell death pathways in hepatic ischemia-reperfusion injury (HIRI), with four core regulatory genes—ATF3, TNFAIP3, IL1B, and KDM6B—significantly upregulated in HIRI samples and primarily expressed in mononuclear phagocytes and immune tissues. The mechanistic pathway involves CD4+ T cells driving hepatocellular ferroptosis through IFN-γ-dependent STAT1 activation, which promotes ACSL4-mediated lipid peroxidation, while reactive oxygen species bursts during reperfusion induce oxidative stress that impairs mitochondrial function and triggers additional ROS production. These molecular events are interconnected with NF-κB signaling and immune cell infiltration, where neutrophils amplify parenchymal damage through inflammatory responses. The study validated ferroptosis inhibitors, particularly ferrostatin-1, as promising therapeutic agents that reduce serum transaminases by over 50% in HIRI models, providing a potential therapeutic target distinct from current limited strategies like ischemic preconditioning and antioxidant agents.

title: Prognostic value of brown adipocyte-related genes in colorectal cancer: a multi-omics and Mendelian randomization study
summary: This multi-omics study identifies brown adipocyte-related genes (BARGs) as prognostic biomarkers in colorectal cancer, establishing a predictive model with clinical utility. Brown adipose tissue influences tumorigenesis through its unique metabolic properties mediated by UCP1-driven thermogenesis and affects tumor progression via modulation of lipid metabolism, cytokine signaling, and immune cell recruitment. Four key prognostic genes—ADD2, PDE1B, LZTS1, and PCSK5—were identified, with PDE1B showing significant positive correlation with regulatory T cells and enhanced predictive value. The study reveals that low-risk group patients respond more effectively to chemotherapeutic drugs, while high-risk and low-risk groups are distinguished by differential pathway activation in cell microenvironment remodeling and migration regulation. Colorectal cancer risk is driven by obesity and lifestyle factors including high-fat diets, chronic inflammation, and sedentary behavior, which may be partially mitigated through bariatric surgery. These findings provide fresh mechanistic perspectives on brown adipocyte involvement in colorectal cancer pathogenesis and offer potential therapeutic targets for risk stratification and treatment optimization.

title: APOL1-mediated kidney disease: a narrative review of the lessons learnt from the past 15 years
summary:
# Summary

APOL1 risk variants on chromosome 22 are strongly associated with kidney disease, particularly in individuals of African ancestry, causing increased disease risk across multiple nephropathies including focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, HIV-associated nephropathy, and lupus nephritis. APOL1, primarily produced by the liver and secreted into circulation, exerts cytotoxic effects primarily through podocyte dysfunction but also through endothelial cell dysfunction, where risk variants induce mitochondrial dysfunction, impair autophagic flux, and activate the NLRP3 inflammasome. The G2 variant particularly triggers mitophagy defects, endothelial inflammation, and increased vascular permeability, contributing to hypertension and preeclampsia. IFN-γ upregulates APOL1 expression, and studies using kidney organoids have revealed that G2 variants suppress fatty acid metabolism and lipid droplet biogenesis genes, providing new mechanistic insights into APOL1-mediated pathology. These findings demonstrate that APOL1 risk variants cause disease through multiple cellular mechanisms involving ion homeostasis imbalance, cellular stress, and inflammatory activation rather than through a single pathway.

title: Exploratory research on therapeutic agents combined with early diagnostic biomarkers for colorectal cancer
summary: This exploratory study identifies a panel of 15 survival-associated differentially expressed genes (including MELK, NFE2L3, MCM2, MAD2L1, AUNIP, CXCL3, GLDN, GREM2, ALDH1A1, CILP, FABP4, AOC3, CNN1, ANGPTL1, and DES) as potential early diagnostic biomarkers for colorectal cancer prognosis. Functional enrichment analyses revealed that upregulated genes participate in cell cycle progression, chromosome segregation, and chemokine signaling, while downregulated genes are involved in metabolic and absorption pathways. Through drug-repositioning and molecular docking analyses, the study identified SB-225002, a chemokine receptor antagonist, as a promising therapeutic agent that forms specific hydrogen bonds with key target proteins (MELK, NFE2L3, MCM2, MAD2L1, and AUNIP) and demonstrates dose-dependent inhibition of colorectal cancer cell proliferation. Additionally, the study explored alternative therapeutic approaches including terbinafine (targeting SQLE to promote apoptosis) and AT9283 (targeting MKK3-driven pathways), while evaluating limitations of more target-specific inhibitors. Overall, this work provides a rational framework combining molecular biomarkers with chemical therapeutics for improved colorectal cancer diagnosis and treatment.

title: Diagnostic, Prognostic, and Immunomodulatory Roles of Prostaglandin E Receptor 1 (PTGER1): A Pan-Cancer Study
summary:
# Summary

This pan-cancer study examines the prostaglandin E2 receptor 1 (PTGER1), a G protein-coupled receptor that mediates prostaglandin E2 signaling and regulates inflammation, pain perception, and cell proliferation. PTGER1 expression patterns vary dramatically across cancer types, with significant upregulation in glioblastoma, head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, and uterine corpus endometrial cancer, while marked downregulation occurs in kidney, bladder, lung, and gastric cancers. Elevated PTGER1 expression associates with poor prognosis in most cancers (kidney renal cell carcinoma, lung squamous cell carcinoma, uterine endometrial cancer, and mesothelioma) but favorable outcomes in select malignancies (breast, esophageal, and thyroid cancers). PTGER1 also correlates with multiple immune cell populations including CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, dendritic cells, macrophages, and neutrophils, suggesting immunomodulatory roles that vary by cancer type. These findings establish PTGER1 as a cancer-type-specific biomarker with diagnostic, prognostic, and potential therapeutic implications through immune regulation of prostaglandin E2-mediated pathways.

title: Long noncoding RNA BMPR1B-DT promotes anoikis and reduces proliferation in ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

Long noncoding RNAs regulate critical processes in ovarian cancer progression, particularly through their effects on anoikis, a form of programmed cell death triggered by detachment from the extracellular matrix. The lncRNA BMPR1B-DT is upregulated in ovarian cancer tissues and cell lines, where it paradoxically acts as a protective factor by promoting anoikis and inhibiting cell proliferation under suspension culture conditions. Resistance to anoikis is a hallmark of metastatic progression, enabling cancer cells to survive detachment and establish peritoneal metastasis, which this study addresses through identification of an anoikis-related lncRNA prognostic signature comprising nine independent prognostic indicators, including AC125611.3, AL022068.1, AL023583.1, USP30-AS1, AC007389.5, AP005233.2, AL713998.1, and AC138904.1. High-risk ovarian cancer patients characterized by decreased BMPR1B-DT expression exhibited significantly lower five-year overall survival rates, confirming the clinical value of anoikis-related lncRNA models in predicting survival outcomes. Differential expression analysis identified 698 differentially expressed anoikis-related lncRNAs between tumor and normal tissues, with 451 upregulated and 247 downregulated, highlighting the extensive involvement of lncRNAs in orchestrating cell growth, programmed cell death, and metastatic potential. This work demonstrates that anoikis-related lncRNA signatures offer strong predictive performance for ovarian cancer patient stratification and survival prediction.

title: Identifying the Genetic Basis of Fetal Loss in Cows and Heifers Through a Genome-Wide Association Analysis
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary: This genome-wide association study identified 16 genomic regions and 27 candidate genes associated with fetal loss in Holstein cattle, with distinct genetic architectures between heifers (16 loci) and primiparous cows (44 loci) operating under recessive inheritance models. The most significant locus on chromosome BTA25 contains transmembrane and zinc finger proteins—including TMEM225B, ZNF655, and ZNF789—that have established roles in fertility through regulation of immune function, gene expression, and cellular processes critical to pregnancy maintenance. Additional candidate genes on BTA26 include microRNA regulators like MIR1307 and PDCD11-associated genes that modulate ovarian function, endometrial receptivity, and embryo-maternal communication, with dysregulation affecting cellular proliferation and progesterone responsiveness. Although individual loci explain modest proportions of genetic variance (0.003–0.005%), fetal loss heritability ranges from 0.01 to 0.18 across parities, indicating meaningful potential for genetic improvement through selective breeding. These findings enable genomic selection strategies to reduce fetal loss rates, which currently range from 2–12% in heifers and 4–24% in primiparous cows and substantially impact dairy industry profitability and animal welfare.

title: Genetic, Epigenetic, and Non-Genetic Factors in Testicular Dysgenesis Syndrome: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Testicular dysgenesis syndrome (TDS) is a developmental disorder characterized by cryptorchidism, hypospadias, and infertility, with significantly elevated testicular cancer risk. The condition arises from a complex interplay of genetic, epigenetic, and environmental factors, including maternal metabolic diseases (obesity and diabetes), lifestyle exposures (smoking and alcohol), and endocrine-disrupting chemicals that exert estrogenic and antiandrogenic effects. Key genetic contributors include mutations in INSL3, RXFP2, NR5A1, and AR genes that disrupt androgen signaling and testicular descent, along with alterations in genes regulating germ cell maturation (ATRX, PIWIL1, CPEB1) and testis development (DMRT1). Epigenetic dysregulation, particularly altered DNA methylation patterns and AR promoter hypermethylation, appears to suppress androgen receptor function and expression. Environmental endocrine disruptors suppress INSL3 expression and reduce androgen sensitivity through CAG repeat polymorphisms in the AR gene, while maternal exposures to phthalates, pesticides, and cigarette smoke during critical developmental windows increase cryptorchidism risk. The convergence of impaired androgen signaling, dysregulated estrogen pathways, and environmental insults during fetal development creates the molecular basis for TDS pathogenesis.

title: Gene regulatory and co-expression networks reveal novel hub genes and regulatory mechanisms in rapeseed (Brassica napus L.) under drought stress
summary:
# Summary

This study employed co-expression network analysis to identify hub genes and transcription factors regulating drought stress response in rapeseed (*Brassica napus* L.), a crop increasingly threatened by drought-induced productivity losses. The brown module, elevated under drought conditions, coordinates multiple adaptive pathways including abscisic acid (ABA) signaling, stomatal regulation, lipid mobilization, cell-wall reinforcement, and protein homeostasis, with key hub genes such as RAB18, LTP4, LEA30, DAA1, and EXLB1 directly involved in drought response. Conversely, the black and cyan modules suppress growth, defense, and metabolic processes during stress, with downregulated genes in energy pathways like the TCA cycle indicating resource reallocation. Transcription factors BHLH122-1 and HHO6 upregulate ABA signaling and protein homeostasis genes critical for stress adaptation, while BHLH128 and GTL1 suppress nutrient transport and cell wall modification genes to conserve energy. These findings reveal a coordinated transcriptional regulatory network where multiple hub genes and transcription factors orchestrate physiological trade-offs between growth and stress survival, providing molecular targets for improving drought tolerance in rapeseed.

title: Unveiling novel potential drug targets for lung cancer through Mendelian randomization analysis
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identified 352 expression quantitative trait loci (eQTLs) and 31 protein quantitative trait loci (pQTLs) causally associated with lung cancer risk. Seven key drug targets—SERPING1, TDRD9, GBAP1, FAM241A, ZKSCAN4, ZKSCAN3, and Z94721.1—were consistently identified across multiple analytical approaches, with colocalization analysis confirming 20 robust signals including BRAT1, B3GNT5, and COPS3. Notably, SERPING1 is underexpressed in non-small cell lung cancer and plays roles in complement activation and coagulation, while TDRD9 is upregulated in lung adenocarcinoma and associated with poor prognosis. Virtual drug screening revealed that valproic acid exhibits strong binding interactions (binding energies ranging from −4.2 to −6.2 kcal/mol) with eight targets including BRAT1, IREB2, PTGFR, and SERPING1, representing a promising repositioning candidate. A comprehensive target-drug network of 275 nodes and 692 edges was constructed, linking the identified genes to 257 potential therapeutic compounds for lung cancer intervention.

title: Mining Potential Therapeutic Targets for T Cell Exhaustion in Osteoarthritis by Integrating Mendelian Randomization and Single‐Cell Sequencing
summary: This study integrated Mendelian randomization and single-cell sequencing to identify therapeutic targets for T cell exhaustion in osteoarthritis, identifying four key biomarkers: GPR65, LDLR, TRIM14, and PLIN2. Mendelian randomization analysis revealed that GPR65, LDLR, and TRIM14 function as risk factors for osteoarthritis, while PLIN2 acts as a protective factor by regulating lipid metabolism and formation of lipid droplets. Single-cell sequencing demonstrated that T cells and mast cells are central to osteoarthritis pathology, with GPR65 and PLIN2 highly expressed in these cell types, and all four biomarkers converging on the lysosomal pathway. The study identified puromycin and gossypol as potential therapeutic candidates through molecular docking, with puromycin binding strongly to LDLR and gossypol to TRIM14. Additional regulatory networks involving microRNAs and long non-coding RNAs were discovered, such as PLIN2-miR-373-3p interactions, suggesting multi-layered mechanisms controlling T cell exhaustion and osteoarthritis progression. These findings provide a comprehensive molecular framework for developing targeted interventions against T cell exhaustion-mediated osteoarthritis.

title: A multi-ancestry meta genome-wide association study of migraine among veterans: associations with traumatic brain injury, depression, and post-traumatic stress disorder
summary: This multi-ancestry genome-wide association study identified 283 genes associated with migraine in veterans, including novel associations with MAML3, CELF4, IRX1, ASXL1, and SPOCD1, alongside established migraine genes like LRP1, TRPM8, and PRDM16. The study reveals that migraine in this population is strongly associated with traumatic brain injury, post-traumatic stress disorder, major depressive disorder, and anxiety, with migraine emerging as a powerful suicide risk factor among veterans. Sex hormones, particularly estrogen and progesterone, appear to modulate migraine susceptibility, and migraine-associated genes show enrichment in brain regions involved in pain processing and emotion regulation, including the anterior cingulate cortex, nucleus accumbens, and basal ganglia. Functional analysis revealed that migraine genes are implicated in immune regulation and cytotoxic T lymphocyte function, as well as association with obesity, lipidosis, neuroticism, and irritability. Therapeutic approaches including losmapimod and TLR4 antagonists represent potential drug targets for migraine symptom reduction. The findings underscore the complex interplay between military service-related trauma, neurobiological mechanisms, psychiatric comorbidities, and migraine pathogenesis in the veteran population.

title: Single‐Cell Transcriptomics Reveals Biomarkers for NK Cell Dysfunction in Endometriosis‐Associated Immune Dysregulation
summary: Single-cell transcriptomics reveals that endometriosis is characterized by profound natural killer (NK) cell dysfunction, a key mechanism enabling immune escape and persistent lesion growth. The study identifies GNLY, PRF1, and ENTPD1 as critical biomarkers—GNLY and PRF1 are predominantly expressed in NK and CD8+ T cells and strongly correlate with activated cytotoxic phenotypes, while their reduced expression in endometrial lesions reflects impaired immune surveillance. ENTPD1, highly expressed in endometrial stromal cells, exhibits dual pathogenic functions: it promotes stromal cell migration and invasion while simultaneously modulating NK cell function through adenosine signaling and suppressing activated immune populations. Endometriotic lesions display characteristic immune dysregulation with significantly reduced resting and activated NK cells, diminished CD8+ T cell activation, and elevated immunosuppressive M2 macrophages, eosinophils, and neutrophils. The transcriptomic profile also reveals estrogen-mediated downregulation of NK cytotoxic markers CD16 and granzyme B, alongside reduced extracellular matrix genes in fibroblasts. In silico analyses identified resveratrol, ibuprofen, and danazol as potential therapeutic agents targeting this ENTPD1-adenosine pathway.

title: Genotype-to-phenotype mapping of somatic clonal mosaicism via single-cell co-capture of DNA mutations and mRNA transcripts
summary:
# Summary

This study employs single-cell co-capture of DNA mutations and mRNA transcripts to map somatic clonal mosaicism in phenotypically normal esophageal tissue, revealing how specific driver mutations shape clonal expansion and differentiation patterns with age. NOTCH1 mutations dominate the clonal landscape in aging esophagus by conferring competitive advantage while maintaining epithelial integrity through stalled differentiation, whereas TP53 mutations promote larger clonal expansions via both differentiation blockade and increased proliferation. The key regulatory genes controlling epithelial differentiation include TP63, SOX2, and COL17A1, with distinct expression patterns across basal, suprabasal, and differentiated cell layers marked by keratin and differentiation markers. Double mutant clones combining NOTCH1 with TP53 or FAT1 were detected in some donors, suggesting sequential acquisition of mutations. These findings demonstrate that somatic clonal mosaicism, driven by recurrent mutations in genes previously implicated in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, progressively accumulates in phenotypically normal aging esophagus, with clonal diversity and wild-type cell reduction increasing with donor age.

title: Multi-omics analysis: Gut microbial metabolites in ovarian lesions
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics study reveals that gut microbial metabolites influence ovarian cancer progression through regulation of key oncogenic and immune pathways. Gut microbiota-derived metabolites regulate critical cancer-associated genes including TP53 (a tumor suppressor), AKT1 (which promotes cell proliferation and chemotherapeutic resistance), and inflammatory cytokines IL6, TNF, and IL1β that collectively drive tumor microenvironment formation, immune escape, and angiogenesis. The mechanism involves activation of Toll-like receptor signaling and neutrophil extracellular trap (NET) formation, which promote tumor-associated inflammation and metastasis, while genes such as GSK3B, BCL2, PPARG, and TLR9 modulate the immunosuppressive microenvironment. Drug-likeness screening identified several bioactive bacterial metabolites with therapeutic potential, including butyric acid and indole-3-propionic acid as positive agents, while arachidonic acid functions as a precursor for inflammatory mediators and diosmetin exhibits anti-inflammatory activity. These findings establish the gut-ovarian axis as a critical regulator of cancer biology and identify specific microbial metabolites and signaling pathways as potential therapeutic targets for ovarian cancer intervention.

title: A housekeeping gene search to analyze expression changes
journal: Vavilov Journal of Genetics and Breeding
summary: This study identified and validated candidate housekeeping genes for use as reference standards in gene expression analysis across multiple tissues in rhesus macaques. Eight genes—ARHGDIA, CYB5R1, NDUFA7, RRAGA, TTC1, UBA6, VPS72, and YWHAH—demonstrated stable and consistently high expression across brain, peripheral blood, spleen, and lymph nodes, making them suitable for normalizing quantitative gene expression studies. The researchers systematically excluded genes associated with disease pathology, including those linked to oncological diseases (AHSA1, B4GALT3, HPCAL1, TBP, TMED9, SSR2), neurological conditions (CSNK2B, DIAPH1, MAPKAPK2, NDUFA1, RAD23A, UBB), eye diseases (ARL2, PRPF8), and age-related expression decline (HSPA8), as well as genes with pseudogene variants (LDHB, RPL37, RPS27A, SNRPA, SUI1, RPL19). The study highlights that traditional housekeeping genes such as beta-actin and GAPDH show significant tissue-specific expression variation and are therefore unsuitable as universal reference standards. This work is particularly relevant for rhesus macaque research models, which exhibit disease phenotypes comparable to human cancer, cardiovascular, neurological, reproductive, skeletal, and ophthalmologic conditions, enabling more reliable gene expression quantification in disease studies.

title: Prognostic impact of the lipid metabolism gene AGPAT4 in the tumor immune microenvironment of thyroid cancer
summary:
# Summary

AGPAT4, a key lipid metabolism enzyme that catalyzes lysophosphatidic acid conversion to phosphatidic acid, is significantly downregulated in thyroid cancer tissues compared to normal tissue, with expression levels correlating strongly with pathological stage and patient survival outcomes. Patients with high AGPAT4 expression demonstrate improved progression-free intervals and overall survival, positioning this gene as a promising diagnostic and prognostic biomarker for thyroid cancer. AGPAT4 participates in multiple metabolic pathways including glycerophospholipid metabolism, glycerolipid metabolism, and phospholipase D signaling, while also influencing extracellular matrix organization and cellular adhesion processes critical to tumor biology. The protein interacts functionally with related lipid metabolism genes including AGPAT1-5, LCLAT1, and LPIN1, forming a coordinated network that affects tumorigenesis. AGPAT4 expression correlates significantly with immune infiltration patterns, including associations with activated dendritic cells, natural killer cells, and regulatory T cells in the tumor microenvironment. These findings establish AGPAT4 as a multifunctional regulator linking lipid metabolism to thyroid cancer progression and immune biology, with substantial clinical potential for patient stratification and therapeutic targeting.

title: Study on biomarkers associated with epigenetic factors in endometriosis combining transcriptome with experimental validation
summary:
# Summary

This study identified three epigenetic biomarkers—HDAC9, YAF2, and CDC6—that are significantly elevated in endometriosis tissues compared to normal controls. Through transcriptomic analysis and experimental validation, these biomarkers were found to regulate key pathways including focal adhesion, oxidative phosphorylation, and epigenetic gene regulation through histone modification and DNA methylation. The biomarkers are highly expressed in endothelial cells and functionally implicated in broader metabolic and signaling processes including thermogenesis, insulin resistance, circadian rhythm regulation, and AMPK signaling. These epigenetic factors likely contribute to endometriosis pathology by modulating estrogen and progesterone receptor expression, which in turn drives the chronic pain and reproductive dysfunction characteristic of the disease. The findings suggest that HDAC9, YAF2, and CDC6 represent novel therapeutic targets for endometriosis intervention.

title: The dynamic landscape and conserved regulation of the m6Am and m6A methylomes during the human and mouse oocyte-to-embryo transition
summary: This study maps the dynamic landscape of m6A and m6Am RNA methylation during the oocyte-to-embryo transition in humans and mice, revealing conserved regulatory mechanisms essential for early development. Both m6A and m6Am modifications are highly involved in RNA metabolic processes and strongly associated with zygotic genome activation, a critical developmental transition where the methylomes undergo dramatic remodeling. M6A-marked transcripts display higher expression and translation levels than unmarked transcripts, with m6A playing a dual role in stabilizing maternal mRNAs while promoting decay of two-cell-specific transcripts, while m6Am primarily marks the 5′ transcription start site through CMTR1 and PCIF1-catalyzed biosynthesis. Mettl3, the primary m6A writer, is essential for fertility, as its conditional knockout results in reduced ovary size and impaired oocyte maturation. The study identifies stage-specific m6A and m6Am-marked genes involved in chromosome segregation, mitochondrial activity, and embryonic development (including FGFR4, GATA6, and Cdx2), demonstrating that dysregulation of these modifications is mechanistically linked to human diseases including cancer and metabolic disorders. The newly developed MeLACE-seq technique surpasses previous methods in sensitivity, enabling more comprehensive detection of these critical RNA modifications that regulate post-transcriptional processes controlling early embryonic development.

title: Coagulation-related genes COL3A1 and MMP1 influence the development of osteoarthritis and the surrounding immunological environment
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies coagulation-related genes COL3A1 and MMP1 as core diagnostic markers for osteoarthritis, revealing a critical mechanistic link between the coagulation system and joint degeneration. COL3A1, a major vascular matrix protein, promotes platelet adhesion and thrombus formation while simultaneously inhibiting chondrocyte proliferation and viability, thereby driving cartilage breakdown and disease progression. MMP1 bridges extracellular matrix degradation and coagulation signaling by directly activating protease-activated receptor 1 (PAR1), a key thrombin receptor that regulates inflammatory responses and tissue damage. The coagulation cascade, initiated by thrombin and fibrin formation, compromises cartilage integrity and amplifies the osteoarthritic microenvironment characterized by synovial inflammation, osteophytes, and immune dysregulation. These findings implicate coagulation-inflammation-fibrosis cross-talk as a central pathogenic mechanism in degenerative joint diseases, with clinical support from epidemiological data showing that vitamin K antagonists increase osteoarthritis risk. The characterization of COL3A1 and MMP1 status offers potential for improved disease classification and development of targeted therapeutic strategies beyond current symptomatic treatments.

title: Assessing the role of serum prolactin levels and coding region somatic mutations of the prolactin gene in Saudi uterine leiomyoma patients
summary: Uterine leiomyomas are benign smooth muscle tumors highly associated with elevated prolactin levels, with 88% of Saudi patients in this study demonstrating elevated serum prolactin, which positively correlates with both the number and size of leiomyomas. Although no somatic mutations were identified in the prolactin gene coding region, four germline variants (c.570G>A, c.205-102T>A, c.312+177T>C, and c.269C>T) were detected, with c.269C>T predicted to be damaging. These genetic variants alter regulatory motifs in genes including CCNT2 and STAT, suggesting they may influence prolactin expression through transcriptional mechanisms. The prolactin gene's role in leiomyoma development appears mediated through its involvement in immune regulation and angiogenesis rather than through direct somatic mutations, while leiomyoma pathogenesis also involves multiple other genetic and epigenetic factors including HMGA2, MED12, and signaling pathways such as TGF-β, Wnt, and JAK-STAT. Clinically, elevated prolactin levels are associated with the characteristic symptoms of leiomyomas including heavy vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, and anemia in affected patients.

title: Urobiota analysis and genome-wide association study in pediatric recurrent urinary tract infections and vesicoureteral reflux
summary: This genome-wide association study identified genetic variants that influence urinary microbiota composition in children with vesicoureteral reflux (VUR) and recurrent urinary tract infections. Children with VUR exhibited dysbiosis characterized by decreased microbial diversity and increased abundance of opportunistic pathogens including Pseudomonas and Enterococcus. The CXCL12 locus on chromosome 10 associated with Pseudomonas abundance encodes a chemokine critical for immune responses, bladder development, and ureteric bud branching; CXCL12 is induced following uropathogenic bacterial infection and regulates intercalated cell distribution for urine acidification and antimicrobial defense. Additional loci in ROBO1 and ABCC1 associated with Clostridia and Bacilli abundance respectively, with ROBO1 implicated in immune cell function and VUR pathogenesis. Notably, the genetic variants associated with urinary dysbiosis also showed pleiotropic effects with cardiovascular disease, endometriosis, diverticulosis, and cancer phenotypes, suggesting shared biological pathways between urinary tract development, microbiota control, and systemic disease susceptibility.

title: A single biomarker may not yield a reliable binary clinical answer: B cell lymphoma 6 testing and resulting interventions for endometriosis in infertility care
summary: This study evaluates B cell lymphoma 6 (BCL6) as a biomarker for endometriosis in patients with recurrent pregnancy loss and recurrent implantation failure. Although BCL6 is associated with endometrial endometriosis and is expressed in immune cell populations—particularly in infiltrated macrophages, monocytes, and stromal cells—the researchers found no significant upregulation of BCL6 or its downstream targets (including IRF4, PRDM1, TP53, and CDKN1A) in endometriosis cases compared to controls. Notably, BCL6 expression was also elevated in control endometrial macrophages and in other endometrial pathologies such as chronic endometritis, adenomyosis, and uterine fibroids, limiting its specificity. Despite these findings, many patients received interventions based on positive BCL6 results, including leuprolide acetate (86%), aromatase inhibitors (39%), and laparoscopy; however, implantation rates, miscarriage rates, and ongoing pregnancy rates did not differ between treated and untreated groups. The authors conclude that BCL6 testing alone is insufficient as a diagnostic marker for endometriosis-related infertility and that additional biomarkers are needed for reliable clinical decision-making.

title: Identification of endometrial cancer biomarkers using weighted gene coexpression network analysis and machine learning
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies myelin and lymphocyte protein (MAL) as a critical biomarker for endometrial cancer using integrated weighted gene coexpression network analysis and machine learning approaches. High MAL expression is significantly associated with poor prognosis, shorter overall survival, and reduced progression-free survival in endometrial cancer patients, with elevated expression predominantly found in tumor tissues compared to normal endometrial tissue. Functionally, elevated MAL expression promotes both proliferation and invasion of endometrial carcinoma cells (ECC-1), while MAL knockdown effectively suppresses these malignant phenotypes, demonstrating its direct role in driving tumor progression. MAL regulates multiple cellular processes including apical trafficking of membrane proteins and DNA methylation, and its expression correlates with aggressive tumor subtypes and advanced clinical stages. These findings establish MAL as a potential molecular marker for predicting disease progression and survival outcomes in endometrial cancer patients, with emerging evidence suggesting sensitivity to targeted treatments such as Navitoclax.

title: Correction: Met Is the Most Frequently Amplified Gene in Endometriosis-Associated Ovarian Clear Cell Adenocarcinoma and Correlates with Worsened Prognosis
summary: This study identifies Met as the most frequently amplified gene in endometriosis-associated ovarian clear cell adenocarcinoma (OCCC), establishing it as a critical molecular driver in this malignancy. Met amplification strongly correlates with worsened prognosis in OCCC patients, indicating its prognostic significance. Gene expression analysis revealed a distinctive pattern in OCCC cell lines, with preferential upregulation of AKT2 compared to the lower expression of AKT1, suggesting activation of specific phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase signaling pathways. These findings highlight how Met-driven oncogenic signaling may promote aggressive behavior in endometriosis-transformed cancers. The results underscore the molecular link between benign endometriotic lesions and malignant transformation to OCCC, with Met serving as both a key amplified driver and a potential therapeutic target in this context.

title: Expanding the spectrum of AFF2 carcinoma: clinical, morphological, immunohistochemical, and molecular characteristics of five cases harboring alternate fusions
summary:
# Summary

This study expands the recognized spectrum of AFF2-rearranged carcinomas by characterizing five head and neck tumors harboring distinct AFF2 fusion partners—H3-3A, EWSR1, CHD4, and NUCKS1—identified through RNA sequencing. Each fusion partner associates with distinct morphological and immunophenotypic patterns: H3-3A and NUCKS1 fusions exhibit transitional cell-like morphology, EWSR1 fusion presents as high-grade adenocarcinoma with neuroendocrine features, and CHD4 fusions demonstrate neuroendocrine differentiation. All tumors demonstrated consistent AFF2 immunoreactivity, suggesting this protein as a useful diagnostic marker for identifying AFF2-rearranged carcinomas regardless of fusion partner. While AFF2-rearranged tumors typically arise in sinonasal and skull base regions, this series confirms that these malignancies form a spectrum of carcinomas with considerable morphological heterogeneity depending on their specific fusion partner. The findings underscore that AFF2 immunohistochemistry combined with molecular confirmation of fusion partners is essential for accurate diagnosis and classification of this emerging tumor category.

title: TRIM26 deficiency drives gastric cancer lymph node metastasis via TGF-β signaling activation and modulates gemcitabine response
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies TRIM26, an E3 ubiquitin ligase, as a critical suppressor of gastric cancer lymph node metastasis whose loss drives aggressive disease and chemoresistance. TRIM26 deficiency triggers TGF-β signaling activation, TP53 network dysregulation, and metabolic reprogramming of taurine and pantothenate/CoA metabolism, while promoting pro-metastatic intercellular crosstalk involving SELE-CD44 and SPP1-CD44/integrin interactions between epithelial, endothelial, and immune cells. Low TRIM26 expression correlates with reduced gemcitabine sensitivity and worse clinical outcomes, whereas TRIM26 overexpression suppresses tumor growth and invasiveness while restoring chemotherapy responsiveness. The protective mechanisms involve TRIM26-mediated regulation of TGF-β signaling and tumor microenvironment remodeling, establishing TRIM26 as a potential biomarker for predicting treatment response and a therapeutic target for improving outcomes in metastatic gastric cancer.

title: Genetic landscape of human oocyte/embryo defects
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive genetic study identifies the molecular basis of human oocyte and embryo defects as Mendelian disorders, revealing mutations in over 120 genes that cause infertility through diverse mechanisms. TUBB8 emerges as the most frequently mutated gene, accounting for 47% of positive cases through its role in spindle assembly, while PATL2 represents the second most common cause at 9.4%, acting through maternal mRNA regulation. The study delineates distinct pathogenic pathways including spindle assembly defects (TUBA4A, KIF11, HAUS6, TACC3, KIF18A), maternal mRNA homeostasis (LHX8, TBPL2, ZFP36L2, PABPC1L), cell cycle regulation (CDC20, TRIP13, CDC23), and subcortical maternal complex formation (TLE6, PADI6, OOEP, NLRP2, NLRP5, NLRP7, KHDC3L). Additionally, mutations in genes controlling oocyte death (PANX1), ferroptosis (COX15), zona pellucida structure (ZP1-3), meiotic recombination (REC114, TOP6BL, MEI1, MEI4), and chromosome segregation (INCENP) collectively explain the molecular etiology of oocyte maturation arrest, fertilization failure, embryonic arrest, and infertility.

title: A comprehensive multi-layered analysis reveals genetic pleiotropy underlying coronary artery calcification and bone mineral density
summary:
# Summary

This multi-layered genetic analysis reveals shared molecular mechanisms underlying the paradoxical association between coronary artery calcification and bone mineral density. Multiple genes exhibit pleiotropy affecting both traits through distinct pathways: SMG6 and MRAS associate with both coronary artery disease and bone mineral density, while RUNX2 and BMP2 function as critical regulators bridging bone formation and vascular calcification. Key mechanistic pathways include the OPG-RANK-RANKL signaling system governing bone remodeling and vascular homeostasis, TGF-β1-mediated osteoblast and osteoclast differentiation, and the opposing roles of PRRX1 (inhibiting osteoblast differentiation) and ARID5B (promoting vascular calcification). Oxidative stress emerges as a central integrator, where elevated reactive oxygen species induce RUNX2 degradation and increase bone resorption while simultaneously promoting osteochondrogenic trans-differentiation of vascular smooth muscle cells. The ubiquitin-proteasome system and oxidative stress regulation are identified as critical biological pathways underlying this calcification paradox, suggesting that dysregulation of quality control mechanisms and cellular stress responses simultaneously promote pathological bone loss and cardiovascular calcification.

title: Identification and Validation of cGAS‐STING Pathway‐Associated Predictive and Therapeutic Models for Esophageal Squamous Cell Cancer Patients via Artificial Intelligence and Multi‐Omics
summary: This study identifies the cGAS-STING pathway as a central regulator in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) progression, where dysregulation contributes to tumor advancement, microenvironment reprogramming, and resistance to both radiotherapy and chemotherapy. Through multi-omics analysis, the researchers identified PRKDC and SLC25A13 as hub genes that regulate the cGAS-STING pathway and promote tumor development in ESCC pathogenesis. The study demonstrates that targeting these pathway components with small molecule inhibitors—specifically BX-912 (targeting PRKDC) and Navitoclax (targeting SLC25A13)—represents a viable therapeutic strategy for ESCC patients. These findings establish the cGAS-STING pathway as both a prognostic marker for risk stratification and a promising therapeutic target, offering potential clinical benefit for patients with this highly prevalent cancer in Asia where environmental and genetic factors drive disease incidence.

title: Expression Analysis of VEGF-Related Hub Genes and Pathways in Breast Cancer: A Comprehensive Bioinformatics Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This bioinformatics analysis identified key hub genes and pathways driving angiogenesis and progression in breast cancer, with matrix metalloproteinases MMP9 and MMP14 showing the strongest upregulation (logFC 2.75 and 1.07 respectively) alongside moderate elevation of VEGFA and APOE. These genes coordinate multiple pro-tumoral mechanisms including angiogenesis, extracellular matrix remodeling, and inflammatory responses through interconnected signaling pathways such as HIF-1, NF-κB, IL-17, and AGE-RAGE signaling. VEGF and MMPs drive angiogenesis by stimulating endothelial cell proliferation and ECM disassembly, which are essential for tumor expansion and metastasis, while TNF promotes breast cancer stem-like cells and inflammatory responses that further support tumor growth. The HIF-1 signaling pathway integrates hypoxia responses through coordinated action of HIF1A, VEGFA, and MMP9, demonstrating how tumors adapt to low oxygen environments. These findings designate MMP9, MMP14, VEGFA, and associated pathway components as potential therapeutic targets and prognostic biomarkers for breast cancer management.

title: The Bacteriophage VMY 22 Has Enhanced the Stability of Its Functional Proteins via Adaptive Evolution in a Temperature-Varying Environment
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that bacteriophage VMY22, isolated from the Mingyong Glacier, undergoes adaptive evolution of its functional proteins in response to temperature fluctuations. Temperature critically affects microbial viability and phage infectivity, driving mutations predominantly in functional genes encoding ATPase and endolysin rather than structural proteins. Phages enriched at different temperatures (4°C, 15°C, 28°C, and 32°C) exhibited temperature-dependent infectivity, with each phage variant showing optimal infectivity matching its enrichment temperature, demonstrating precise host-phage temperature coevolution. Molecular analysis revealed that ATPase and endolysin proteins accumulated distinct mutations enabling temperature-specific structural adaptations: ATPase variants enhanced surface charge or hydrophobicity at their respective temperatures, while endolysin variants, particularly at 4°C, underwent extensive mutations improving cold adaptability. These temperature-driven evolutionary changes primarily affected post-adsorption infection processes, establishing a novel mechanism by which environmental temperature selects for phage variants with enhanced functional protein stability through adaptive mutations.

title: Potential Biological Processes Related to Brain SLC13A5 Across the Lifespan: Weighted Gene Co-Expression Network Analysis from Large Human Transcriptomic Data
summary:
# Summary

This weighted gene co-expression network analysis reveals that SLC13A5, a citrate transporter, shows developmentally dynamic expression across the brain and liver, with peak cerebral expression during infancy and sustained hepatic upregulation postnatally. SLC13A5 functions within a tightly coordinated gene module involved in mitochondrial bioenergetics, particularly oxidative phosphorylation and ATP synthesis, co-expressing with key neuronal and mitochondrial proteins including CYP46A1, ITM2B, NRGN, and GABRD. Loss-of-function mutations in SLC13A5 cause developmental epileptic encephalopathy 25, characterized by neonatal seizures, developmental delay, and distinctive tooth hypoplasia, with antiseizure medications including benzodiazepines and phenobarbital providing treatment; conversely, Slc13a5 overexpression induces pro-epileptogenic neuronal hyperexcitability, white matter disruption, and systemic inflammation. The SLC13A5 module eigengene strongly correlates with brain maturation, positioning SLC13A5 as a critical regulator of citrate-dependent metabolic processes essential for normal neurodevelopment and mitochondrial function.

title: From Gas Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry (GC–MS) to Network Pharmacology: System-Level Insights into the Multi-Target Biological Potential of Flaveria trinervia (Spreng.) C. Mohr
summary:
# Summary

Flaveria trinervia, a traditional Mexican medicinal plant, contains eleven bioactive compounds including phytol, germacrene D, caryophyllene oxide, squalene, and various pinenes, which exhibit antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and cytotoxic properties. Network pharmacology analysis reveals that these plant metabolites converge on key nuclear receptors and co-regulators—ESR1, RXRA/B/G, NCOA2, and CYP19A1—which are central hubs regulating apoptosis, cell proliferation, and immune modulation through nuclear receptor-mediated signal transduction. The compounds induce significant reprogramming of lipid and steroid homeostasis while activating xenobiotic detoxification pathways, processes relevant to endometriosis-associated inflammation and aberrant cell proliferation. Identified metabolites like oleanolic acid contribute additional hepatoprotective, analgesic, and anti-inflammatory effects, supporting the plant's traditional use in treating gastrointestinal and respiratory disorders. The multi-target mechanism of Flaveria trinervia suggests therapeutic potential for conditions characterized by excessive cell proliferation, inflammatory dysregulation, and hormonal imbalance, including endometriosis-related pathology.

title: Wnt5a Regulates Embryonic Müllerian Duct Development Through the Non-Canonical Wnt PCP Pathway
summary:
# Summary

WNT5A is a critical regulator of Müllerian duct development that functions primarily through the non-canonical Wnt planar cell polarity (PCP) pathway to control posterior development, posterior-to-anterior patterning, and proper tissue morphogenesis of the female reproductive tract. Ablation of Wnt5a in mice results in severe developmental abnormalities including vaginal agenesis, hypoplastic uterine horns (>60% shorter than wild-type), and disrupted glandular genesis, accompanied by dysregulation of the PCP pathway and reduced cell proliferation. The loss of Wnt5a causes epithelial remodeling with altered PAX2 expression (cuboidal rather than columnar morphology), abnormal smooth muscle differentiation, and ectopic CTNNB1 expression in mesenchymal cells. WNT5A is also required for estrogen-directed regulation of downstream factors including WNT7A, HOXA10, and HOXA11, processes independent of the canonical Wnt/Lef1 pathway. In humans, WNT5A variants are associated with Robinow syndrome, a congenital condition characterized by skeletal and genital abnormalities, highlighting the evolutionary conservation of this signaling pathway in reproductive development.

title: Genetic Determinants of Radiosensitivity: Evidence of Radioresistance-Associated SNP Enrichment in Occupational Workers Chronically Exposed to Low-Dose Radiation
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated genetic determinants of radiosensitivity in occupational workers chronically exposed to low-dose ionizing radiation, identifying significant enrichment of radioresistance-associated single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in exposed populations. Key genetic variants in TP53 and CDKN1A/p21—including the TP53 intron 3 insertion, TP53 intron 6 A allele, TP53 Pro72 allele, and p21 codon 31 A allele—were found at elevated frequencies in radiation-exposed workers and functionally associated with enhanced DNA repair efficiency, modified apoptotic responses, and improved checkpoint activation. The TP53 Pro72 variant specifically inhibits mitochondrial p53 translocation and weakens apoptotic activity while promoting G1/S checkpoint control and DNA repair pathways, contrasting with the Arg72 variant which induces apoptosis more efficiently. These findings align with a polygenic model of radiosensitivity in which inter-individual variability in DNA damage response pathways, mediated by intronic regulatory elements and alternative splicing events (such as Δ40p53 isoform expression), collectively determine radiation resistance. The enrichment of these protective alleles in uranium industry workers suggests that individuals with enhanced DNA repair and checkpoint control capacity are selectively maintained in chronically exposed occupational cohorts.

title: The Association Between Estrogen Receptor-α and PIWIL3/piR-651/piR-823 Complex Regulates MI to MII Transposition in Normoresponder and Diminished Ovarian Reserve Cases
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates how estrogen receptor-α (ER-α) and the PIWIL3/piRNA-651/piRNA-823 complex regulate oocyte maturation from metaphase I to metaphase II (MI to MII), comparing normoresponder (NOR) and diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) cases. The findings demonstrate that ER-α, PIWIL3, piR-651, and piR-823 are significantly downregulated at both gene and protein levels in DOR samples compared to NOR controls, with these molecules showing strong positive correlations in normal oocytes. ER-α directly participates in oocyte maturation, steroidogenesis, and follicle growth, while piR-651 and piR-823 indirectly regulate MI to MII transition through estrogen-dependent mechanisms and by promoting cell survival and growth. The dysregulation of this ER-α/PIWIL3/piRNA axis in DOR contributes to impaired oocyte competence and reproductive dysfunction, suggesting this regulatory network is critical for normal ovarian function and fertility. These molecular alterations provide insights into the pathogenic mechanisms underlying diminished ovarian reserve and represent potential targets for understanding endometriosis-associated reproductive pathology.

title: Epigenetic Landscape of Female Infertility: An Integrated Bioinformatics Perspective on DNA Methylation, MicroRNAs, and Gene Regulatory Networks Across PCOS, Endometriosis, and Diminished Ovarian Reserve
summary: This bioinformatics study identified an eight-gene signature (H19, SULT1A4, HCK, SPI1, CARD16, NFE2, LST1, and KRT8) dysregulated across three female infertility conditions—PCOS, diminished ovarian reserve, and recurrent implantation failure—suggesting shared pathogenic mechanisms. In endometriosis, downregulation of microRNAs miR-449 and miR-200 promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition and fibrosis by releasing inhibition of these processes, contributing to the characteristic tissue remodeling. The conserved eight-gene signature predominantly regulates innate immune activation and inflammatory responses through master regulators of myeloid function (HCK, SPI1), inflammasome components (CARD16), and immune cell adhesion mediators (LST1), indicating that dysregulated immune signaling represents a common pathological feature. In PCOS specifically, inflammatory markers S100A9 and CSF3R are elevated while hemoglobin genes (HBB, HBA1, HBA2) are reduced, disrupting oxidative stress response and metabolic homeostasis.
