This integrated analysis reveals that DNA methylation, microRNA dysregulation, and gene network perturbations converge on immune dysfunction and epigenetic dyscontrol as fundamental mechanisms underlying female reproductive disorders.

title: Construction and initial validation of key gene network for progesterone resistance in endometrial cancer based on genome-wide CRISPR screening
summary:
# Summary

This study employed genome-wide CRISPR screening to identify key genes regulating progesterone sensitivity and resistance in endometrial cancer, establishing a comprehensive gene network validated in xenograft models. Progesterone normally inhibits endometrial adenocarcinoma by binding progesterone receptors and suppressing cell cycle progression, angiogenesis, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition while promoting apoptosis; however, resistance develops through reduced PR expression, PR subtype imbalance, and epigenetic alterations. The researchers identified NNMT as a progestin-resistance gene and SOX17 as a progestin-sensitivity gene, with progesterone sensitivity further involving DNA synthesis, RNA synthesis, and metabolic pathways. Multiple pathways contribute to progesterone resistance, including TGF/EGFR/MAPK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, oxidative stress, and lipid metabolism via SREBP-1 and MGLL. Notably, high expression of AMPD3 and KCNRG associated with unfavorable prognosis, while DTX3L expression correlated with favorable outcomes, and knockdown of resistance genes like ABCC2 increased apoptosis and enhanced therapeutic sensitivity. The findings suggest combination strategies, such as pairing progesterone therapy with PI3K/AKT/mTOR inhibitors or epigenetic regulators, may overcome resistance in endometrial cancer.

title: Single-cell and proteomic profiling unveil aberrant immune phenotypes in gestational diabetes following assisted reproductive technology
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) following assisted reproductive technology is characterized by aberrant immune phenotypes involving enhanced CD8+ T cell responses and systemic inflammation. Women with ART-conceived pregnancies and GDM show significantly elevated CD8+ T cell proportions along with heightened plasma levels of inflammatory mediators including CD6, CXCL5, MMP10, and 4E-BP1. The cytotoxic CD8+ T cell subsets display enhanced effector and cytotoxic activity, with specific populations (CD8+ CTL-GNLY, CD8+ NKT, and CD8+ CTL-GZMK) showing elevated expression of genes involved in cytotoxicity and T cell receptor signaling. Concurrently, regulatory T cells in GDM patients exhibit reduced expression of transcription factors JUN, FOS, and EGR1, indicating compromised immunoregulatory capacity. Monocytes and neutrophils in GDM display proinflammatory transcriptional profiles with upregulated TNF-α and NF-κB signaling pathways, alongside increased IL-8+ IL-1β+ monocytes and CXCL8+ FCGR3B+ neutrophils. These findings suggest that immune dysregulation, potentially driven by hormonal disturbances and fertility-enhancing drugs during ART treatment, contributes fundamentally to GDM pathogenesis through enhanced systemic and T cell-mediated immune activation.

title: Tumor-intrinsic B4GALNT3 expression drives a protective immune microenvironment in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies B4GALNT3, a glycosyltransferase expressed by tumor cells, as a novel protective biomarker in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer (EAOC), which predominantly manifests as endometrioid and clear cell histotypes. High tumor-intrinsic B4GALNT3 expression significantly correlates with improved overall survival and drives an anti-tumor immune microenvironment enriched in plasma B cells, memory B cells, and activated dendritic cells while reducing immunosuppressive regulatory T cells and M2 macrophages. Through single-cell RNA sequencing and pathway analysis, the researchers identified B4GALNT3 as part of a core network of hub genes involved in cell division, mitotic cell cycle, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition, demonstrating robust diagnostic potential with an area under the curve of 0.962. These findings suggest that B4GALNT3 acts as a potential immune checkpoint through epithelial-immune interactions, positioning it as a valuable prognostic biomarker and therapeutic target for stratifying EAOC patients and optimizing immune-based treatments.

title: GREB1 ‐rearranged uterine tumour shares a common DNA methylation signature with ESR1 ‐rearranged UTROSCT
summary:
# Summary

Uterine tumors resembling ovarian sex cord tumors (UTROSCT) represent a diverse group of neoplasms driven by recurrent gene fusions, with GREB1 and ESR1 rearrangements defining molecularly distinct subtypes that share overlapping DNA methylation signatures. GREB1-rearranged tumors form fusion partnerships with multiple genes including NCOA1-3, SS18, and NR4A3, while ESR1-rearranged tumors primarily fuse with NCOA2 and NCOA3. GREB1-rearranged UTROSCTs display more aggressive biological features, including greater genomic complexity and instability, predominant diffuse growth patterns lacking overt sex cord differentiation, higher mitotic activity, larger tumor size, and presentation in older patients, resulting in reduced disease-free survival compared to ESR1-rearranged counterparts. In normal endometrium, GREB1 is regulated by progesterone and facilitates hormone-responsive gene expression, whereas in endometriosis tissue, estrogen stimulates GREB1 to activate estrogen-induced transcriptional programs. Despite their molecular and phenotypic differences, GREB1- and ESR1-rearranged tumors cluster together based on DNA methylation profiles, distinguishing them as a separate entity from uterine smooth muscle tumors and supporting their classification within the UTROSCT spectrum.

title: Intravenous miR-30c therapy confers dual neurovascular protection and improves long-term recovery after ischemic stroke
summary: This study demonstrates that intravenous delivery of miR-30c mimics provides dual neuroprotection following ischemic stroke by targeting two critical pathological cascades. miR-30c, which is normally highly expressed in cortical and hippocampal neurons but becomes downregulated after stroke, directly inhibits PAI-1 expression in cerebral endothelium, thereby preventing intravascular fibrin and platelet deposition that would otherwise lead to downstream microvascular thrombosis and infarct progression. Simultaneously, miR-30c suppresses neuronal endoplasmic reticulum stress and downstream mitochondrial apoptotic signaling, reducing neuronal cell death. When administered post-stroke as a PEGylated liposome-encapsulated formulation, miR-30c therapy restored brain miR-30c levels, significantly reduced infarct volume, and improved long-term neurological function. The findings are clinically relevant given that reduced miR-30c expression is associated with major stroke risk factors including hyperlipidemia, atherosclerosis, and diabetes. Overall, this work establishes miR-30c as a promising therapeutic candidate that addresses multiple pathogenic mechanisms of ischemia-reperfusion injury simultaneously.

title: Integrated bulk and single-cell transcriptomic profiling reveals bromocriptine sensitivity genes and cellular targets in adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

This integrated transcriptomic study identified bromocriptine as a therapeutic agent for adenomyosis, a gynecological disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue within the myometrium that causes dysmenorrhea, abnormal bleeding, and infertility. Bromocriptine exerts its effects primarily through inhibition of prolactin secretion and correction of hormonal dysregulation, including elevated prolactin and estrogen levels in adenomyotic tissues, while also modulating angiogenesis and fibrosis. Single-cell analysis revealed nine candidate bromocriptine-sensitivity genes—including ADAM12, HOXA11, SFRP1, SESN3, and others—that are enriched in specific cell types (epithelial cells, smooth muscle cells, and fibroblasts) and regulate critical pathways controlling proliferation, apoptosis, and extracellular matrix organization. These genes coordinate key signaling cascades including Wnt, TGF-β, and mTOR pathways, and show elevated expression in ectopic lesions that normalizes with bromocriptine treatment. The findings support a model in which bromocriptine restores hormonal homeostasis and modulates fibroblast activity and extracellular matrix composition to reverse the aberrant tissue remodeling characteristic of adenomyosis.

title: Loss of ARID1A expression promotes lung adenocarcinoma metastasis and predicts a poor prognosis
summary: Loss of ARID1A, a critical subunit of SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complexes, serves as a tumor suppressor in lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD), and its deficiency significantly promotes cancer progression and metastasis. ARID1A knockdown enhances LUAD cell proliferation, migration, and invasion in vitro while promoting metastasis in vivo, partly through activation of the Akt signaling pathway and downregulation of metastatic suppressors. Clinically, ARID1A mutations and loss of expression correlate with poor differentiation, shorter overall survival, and a poor prognosis, with mutations showing frequent co-occurrence with EGFR mutations in young LUAD patients. Loss of ARID1A expression increases the degree of malignancy in LUAD cells and enables primary tumor cells to acquire metastatic potential, suggesting that ARID1A status is an effective prognostic marker independent of EGFR mutation status. These findings establish ARID1A as a key tumor suppressor in LUAD whose inactivation drives aggressive disease through chromatin remodeling dysfunction and oncogenic signaling activation.

title: Impaired PIEZO1 function drives uterine hypercontractility in adenomyosis-associated dysmenorrhea
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that impaired PIEZO1 mechanosensitive channel function drives adenomyosis-associated dysmenorrhea through disruption of nitric oxide (NO) signaling in the myometrium. In adenomyotic tissue, reduced PIEZO1 expression—caused by promoter hypermethylation—downregulates endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) and subsequently decreases NO production, which normally promotes smooth muscle relaxation and suppresses contractility. Simultaneously, adenomyosis elevates expression of oxytocin receptor (OTR), vasopressin receptor (VP1αR), and the mechanosensitive channel PIEZO2, creating a pathological shift toward uterine hypercontractility and irregular contractions that manifest as severe dysmenorrhea. The mechanistic link involves tissue fibrosis and increased myometrial stiffness in adenomyotic lesions, which preferentially activates stiffness-sensitive PIEZO2 and OTR while suppressing PIEZO1 function, establishing a mechanotransduction-based feedback loop. Notably, treatment with the histone deacetylase inhibitor valproic acid (VPA) can reactivate PIEZO1 expression, suggesting potential therapeutic intervention for adenomyosis-related pain.

title: IL20RB promotes proliferation and migration in clear cell renal cell carcinoma and is associated with immune infiltration
summary:
# Summary

IL20RB is markedly elevated in clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) compared to normal tissue and functions as a key oncogenic driver through its role as a cytokine receptor subunit. IL20RB binds IL20RA or IL22RA1 to form heterodimeric receptors that respond to IL19, IL20, and IL24, and experimental suppression of IL20RB via siRNA significantly diminishes both ccRCC cell proliferation and migration. High IL20RB expression correlates strongly with aggressive tumor characteristics including advanced pathologic T, N, and M stages, poor differentiation, and disease progression, with enhanced expression linked to poor disease-specific and overall survival in ccRCC patients. The oncogenic effects of IL20RB appear mechanistically linked to immune modulation, as elevated IL20RB expression associates with infiltration of multiple immune cell populations including regulatory T cells, natural killer cells, and T helper cells, alongside altered chemokine and immunomodulator expression. While IL20RB was originally identified in inflammatory conditions including endometriosis, psoriasis, and inflammatory bowel disease, subsequent research reveals its crucial role across multiple cancer types, with elevated expression in late-stage, high-grade tumors and in patients with fatal outcomes. These findings establish IL20RB as a potential prognostic biomarker and therapeutic target in ccRCC and suggest broader implications for IL20RB-targeted interventions in cancer treatment.

title: Integrative In Silico Multi-Omics Profiling of circRNA-Mediated ceRNA Networks Reveals Prognostic Biomarkers and Repurposed Therapeutic Candidates in Gastric Cancer
summary: This multi-omics study reveals that circular RNAs (circRNAs) regulate gastric cancer progression through competitive endogenous RNA (ceRNA) networks involving microRNAs and messenger RNAs. CircNRIP1 promotes tumor progression by sequestering miR-149-5p to activate AKT1 signaling, while circRNA_100269 suppresses tumorigenesis through miR-630 interaction, demonstrating opposing roles in disease pathogenesis. Differentially expressed genes in these networks regulate cell cycle progression, PI3K-Akt signaling, and extracellular matrix organization, with key hub molecules including IGF2BP3, MMP14, TGM2, and COL4A1 showing significant associations with patient survival and elevated expression in gastric cancer tissues. Network analysis identified hub circRNAs (hsa_circ_0001886, hsa_circ_0104566, hsa_circ_0103504) and microRNAs (hsa-miR-375, hsa-miR-133b, hsa-miR-145) as central regulators, with implications extending to endometriosis and other malignancies. Computational drug screening identified five compounds—Withaferin A, Tozasertib, Pevonedistat, Fedratinib, and Celastrol—as potential repurposed therapeutics with strong transcriptional reversal effects, while MMP14 and TGM2 emerged as predictive biomarkers for fedratinib response based on receiver operating characteristic analysis.

title: Circadian Biology and Gynecological Diseases: Genetic Mechanisms and Potential Therapeutic Targets Underlying the Causal Link Between Insomnia and Ovarian Cysts
summary: This study demonstrates that insomnia and circadian misalignment significantly increase the risk of ovarian cysts and gynecological cancers through dysregulation of core circadian genes and pathways. Insomnia shows a strong unidirectional genetic association with increased ovarian cyst risk, while circadian disruption broadly elevates ovarian, endometrial, and cervical cancer risk by impairing multiple cellular processes including cell cycle progression, DNA repair, and apoptosis. The circadian clock machinery (CLOCK, BMAL1, PER1-3, CRY1-2) normally regulates metabolism, immune function, hormone secretion, and cell proliferation; aberrant expression of PER and CRY genes reduces apoptotic capacity and promotes unchecked proliferation, while dysregulation of CLOCK and BMAL1 alters oncogenic signaling cascades involving p53, MYC, and NF-κB. Integration of shared dysregulated genes between insomnia and ovarian cysts identified three core druggable targets (GFRA1, SORCS2, RASGRF2) that participate in neuronal signaling, immune activation, and oxidative stress pathways. The study proposes circadian-aligned therapeutic strategies including chronotherapy with melatonin and sleep optimization as preventive approaches for these gynecological conditions.

title: Long non-coding RNA LINC00607 epigenetically regulates endothelial TSPAN18 to promote hypoxia-induced thromboinflammation
summary: This study demonstrates that the long non-coding RNA LINC00607 serves as a critical epigenetic regulator of endothelial dysfunction and thromboinflammation under hypoxic conditions. Hypoxia upregulates LINC00607 in a HIF-1α-dependent manner, and LINC00607 in turn is both necessary and sufficient for the induction of TSPAN18, a tetraspan membrane protein enriched in endothelial cells. Mechanistically, LINC00607 associates with the chromatin remodeler BRG1 to facilitate BRG1-mediated activation of the TSPAN18 enhancer, enhancing chromatin accessibility and transcriptional engagement at this locus. The LINC00607–TSPAN18 axis functionally promotes store-operated calcium entry and endothelial-monocyte adhesion, key mechanisms driving the thromboinflammatory response in hypoxia. These findings reveal an epigenetic regulatory network linking hypoxia to endothelial activation and suggest that targeting the LINC00607–TSPAN18 axis could represent a novel therapeutic strategy for hypoxia-associated thrombotic complications such as venous thromboembolism.

title: Unraveling the genetic links between stature and disease in East Asians: A multi-biobank genetic correlation and risk prediction study
summary: This multi-biobank study reveals significant genetic correlations between body height and endometriosis risk in East Asian populations. Taller genetically-determined stature increases endometriosis risk, partly mediated through earlier menarche and increased body weight, while familial short stature confers protective effects against the disease. The analysis identified 619 genome-wide significant genes associated with height variation, including growth-related genes (IGF1R, ADAMTS17, FNDC3B) and novel loci (GIGYF2, NSD2, THBS2, PIEZO1), with five additional loci specifically linked to familial short stature (DIS3L2, NCAPG, ATF7, CS, IGF1). Height polygenic risk scores successfully predicted endometriosis risk across biobanks, suggesting shared genetic architecture between stature regulation and endometriosis susceptibility. These findings highlight how growth hormone and insulin-like growth factor pathways, which regulate final stature through growth plate development and pubertal timing, may influence endometriosis pathogenesis through reproductive and metabolic mechanisms.

title: Integrating Bioinformatics and Experimental Validation Identify CCNA2 as a Novel Prognostic Biomarker and Tumor Promoter via the PI3K/AKT Pathway in Lung Adenocarcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies CCNA2 (Cyclin A2) as a novel prognostic biomarker and oncogenic driver in lung adenocarcinoma through integrated bioinformatics and experimental validation. CCNA2 is highly elevated in lung adenocarcinoma tissues and associated with poor patient outcomes, particularly in smokers and ALK-mutant patients, with knockdown experiments confirming its role in promoting cell proliferation, invasion, and migration. Mechanistically, CCNA2 functions through the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway and regulates core cellular processes including cell cycle progression, DNA replication, and mitotic division. Additionally, CCNA2 expression correlates with immune checkpoint molecules (PD-L1, CTLA-4, TIM-3, LAG3, TIGIT) and shows divergent associations with immune cell infiltration, positively correlating with CD8+ T cells, neutrophils, and macrophages while inversely correlating with CD4+ T cells and B cells. The findings suggest CCNA2 as a potential therapeutic target, with curcumin identified as a compound capable of inhibiting CCNA2 expression through PI3K/AKT pathway modulation.

title: Discovering the Molecular Secrets of Traditional Chinese Medicine: An Interpretation of Herbal Therapeutics Using Systems Biology
summary:
# Summary

This review demonstrates how systems biology approaches are revolutionizing the understanding and validation of Traditional Chinese Medicine by integrating genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics to elucidate molecular mechanisms. Systems biology technologies enable comprehensive analysis of gene regulatory networks, cellular signaling, and biochemical processes, while TCM's multi-target, multi-pathway therapeutic actions are well-suited to network pharmacology investigations that identify drug targets and predict efficacy. Key applications include transcriptomic analysis of disease-related gene expression patterns and TCM syndromes, proteomic profiling of protein interactions and modifications, and metabolomic assessment of endogenous metabolites to ensure quality control and safety. Genomic sequencing of medicinal plants such as *Paeonia lactiflora*, *Epimedium brevicornu*, and *Psoralea corylifolia* has identified hundreds of genes and established chloroplast DNA barcodes for authentication. These integrated systems biology methodologies provide a modern scientific framework for understanding TCM's holistic disease prevention mechanisms and optimizing herbal therapeutic development through evidence-based characterization of active ingredient biosynthesis and plant breeding applications.

title: Exploring hub genes related to adipocytokines in keloids: a combined analysis integrating single-cell, Mendelian randomization and bulk transcriptome data with experimental verification
summary:
# Summary

This integrated multi-omics study identified PIK3R3 and ANGPTL5 as hub genes in keloid pathogenesis through analysis of single-cell RNA-seq, bulk transcriptomics, and Mendelian randomization. Mendelian randomization analysis established PIK3R3 as a causal risk factor for keloids, with the gene involved in the TEL pathway and predominantly expressed in fibroblasts, while ANGPTL5 was linked to adipocyte differentiation. Pro-inflammatory adipocytokines including leptin, IL-6, and TNF-α activate keloid fibroblasts to promote excessive collagen accumulation and fibrosis, with resistin further enhancing fibrosis through the TGF-β/Smad pathway while reduced adiponectin impairs anti-fibrotic responses. RT-qPCR validation confirmed PIK3R3 upregulation in keloid tissue, and drug prediction analysis identified harmine as a potential therapeutic candidate targeting PIK3R3. These findings reveal that adipocytokine dysregulation and chronic inflammation mediated by genetic factors and metabolic imbalance are central mechanisms underlying keloid formation, offering new insights for targeted therapeutic development.

title: A Comparative Transcriptome and WGCNA of Tomato Reveals Hub Genes and a Hormone-Mediated Defense Network Against Ralstonia solanacearum
summary:
# Summary

This comparative transcriptome study identified a hormone-mediated defense network that confers resistance to bacterial wilt caused by *Ralstonia solanacearum* in tomato. The resistant cultivar ZM3 showed fewer differentially expressed genes overall but activated a coordinated defense response involving key hub genes including transcription factors (SlWRKY75, ERF1), kinases (MAPK9, RLK), and the ethylene biosynthesis gene ACO. The transcription factor SlWRKY75 emerged as a critical regulator that enhances resistance by simultaneously activating antioxidant defenses, maintaining reactive oxygen species homeostasis, and modulating both jasmonic acid and salicylic acid signaling pathways. Resistant tomato integrates multiple layers of defense encompassing plant hormone signal transduction, cell wall reinforcement, phenylpropanoid biosynthesis, and plant-pathogen interaction responses. In contrast, the susceptible cultivar ZM86 showed disproportionate enrichment in carbohydrate metabolism and stress-response pathways, suggesting a dysregulated response that fails to contain the pathogen. These findings reveal that effective *Ralstonia* resistance depends on coordinated transcriptional regulation of structural defenses and hormone-mediated immune signaling rather than constitutively elevated defense gene expression.

title: Transcriptional Modulation of Infertility-Associated Genes Following Chlamydia trachomatis Infection in Human Fallopian Tube Mesenchymal Cells: In Silico Study
summary: Chlamydia trachomatis infection triggers an exacerbated inflammatory response in fallopian tube mesenchymal cells that leads to pelvic inflammatory disease, tubal occlusion, and tubal factor infertility through tissue remodeling and fibrosis. In silico analysis revealed that CT infection induces time-dependent transcriptional reprogramming of infertility-associated genes, with AKAP12 dysregulated at 24 hours post-infection and fifteen additional genes including BTG4, INHBB, PATL2, and SYNE1 dysregulated at 48 hours. CT infection simultaneously upregulates genes involved in cell proliferation and inflammatory processes while downregulating genes associated with cell cycle regulation, mitochondrial function, and tissue repair, including ANAPC4, BMP1, BNC2, and TRIP13. These transcriptional alterations are driven by inflammatory signaling, oxidative stress, and tissue remodeling processes that compromise normal fallopian tube function. The involvement of dysregulated genes in embryonic development, meiosis, and oocyte maturation suggests that CT-induced inflammation disrupts multiple molecular pathways essential for female fertility. These findings provide mechanistic insight into how chronic chlamydial infection impairs reproductive capacity by altering the transcriptional networks that govern tubal integrity and reproductive cell function.

title: Genetic Variations in the M6A Modification Pathway as Potential Predictors of Imatinib Secondary Resistance in Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumors
summary:
# Summary

Imatinib mesylate is the first-line treatment for gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs), which are driven by KIT and PDGFRA mutations; however, long-term therapy frequently leads to secondary resistance through mechanisms involving the m6A RNA modification pathway, autophagy, and DNA damage repair. Genetic variants in m6A pathway genes (METTL3, YTHDC2, ALKBH5, and ZC3H13) show opposing associations with progression-free survival, with specific variants such as METTL3 rs1263793 A>G conferring longer survival while YTHDC2 rs1833678 T>C and multiple other variants associate with shortened survival. The molecular basis of resistance involves METTL3-mediated enhancement of MRP1 translation and imatinib-induced ATG5 stabilization that promotes protective autophagy in tumor cells. A cumulative genetic risk score constructed from m6A pathway variants significantly predicts progression-free survival, demonstrating that stratification into intermediate- and high-risk groups can identify patients likely to develop secondary resistance. These findings suggest that genetic profiling of m6A modification and related pathways could serve as a predictive biomarker to personalize imatinib therapy and inform treatment intensification strategies for GIST patients.

title: Divergent Associations of the VEGF (–2578C/A) Polymorphism with Imaging-Based Severity and Symptom Profile of Adenomyosis in Infertile Women: An Exploratory Analysis
summary: This exploratory study investigates the VEGF −2578C/A polymorphism's association with adenomyosis severity and clinical symptoms in infertile women. Adenomyosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium, involves dysregulated angiogenesis, chronic inflammation, aberrant proliferation, and immune dysfunction. The VEGF −2578C/A A allele and AA+CA genotypes were significantly more frequent in women with adenomyosis and strongly associated with increased ultrasound-based disease severity, demonstrating a dose-dependent relationship. Notably, despite this molecular-imaging correlation, carriers of the AA+CA genotypes paradoxically exhibited lower odds of clinically relevant dysmenorrhea, suggesting a divergence between imaging-defined adenomyosis severity and symptom profile. The −2578C/A polymorphism functions in the VEGF promoter region to influence transcription and angiogenic signaling, with the A allele potentially associated with altered VEGF expression and endometrial vascular remodeling. Other VEGF polymorphisms (−936C/T and −634G/C) showed no significant associations with adenomyosis presence or severity, though specific haplotypes incorporating the −2578A allele emerged as independent risk factors for the disease.

title: The genetics of cancer heterogeneity and mesothelioma
summary:
# Summary

Mesothelioma is characterized by recurrent mutations in key tumor suppressor genes and epigenetic regulators, particularly BAP1, NF2, TP53, and SETD2, which collectively drive malignant transformation through alterations in cell cycle regulation, DNA repair, Hippo signaling, RNA processing, and histone regulation. While asbestos exposure remains the primary environmental risk factor for pleural mesothelioma, endometriosis and therapeutic radiation have also been identified as causative factors, with ALK gene rearrangements emerging as a notable genetic feature in peritoneal mesothelioma cases, particularly in younger women. Histologic subtypes show distinct genetic and imaging profiles: epithelioid mesotheliomas display higher apparent diffusion coefficient values and lower BAP1 mutation frequency (~50%), whereas sarcomatoid and biphasic variants exhibit higher standardized uptake values correlating with worse survival, elevated CDKN2A/MTAP alterations, and increased TERT mutations. Clinical diagnosis has advanced through immunohistochemical and molecular testing of BAP1, MTAP, and merlin (NF2), enabling more precise tumor classification and prognostication based on genetic heterogeneity.

title: The Homeobox Genes: Classification, Regulation, Biological Functions, and Diseases
summary:
# Summary

Homeobox genes constitute a large family of master regulatory transcription factors that control development, cell differentiation, and tissue homeostasis through their conserved homeodomain, which enables sequence-specific DNA binding and precise gene expression regulation. The HOX gene clusters (A–D) play critical roles in establishing cellular identity, body patterning, and anterior-posterior axis development during embryogenesis, while remaining active in adult tissues including mesenchymal stem cells and fibroblasts to maintain tissue physiology and integrity. Dysregulation of homeobox gene expression through pathogenic variants, aberrant DNA methylation, or epigenetic alterations contributes to diverse human pathologies spanning congenital malformations, metabolic disorders, neurodegenerative diseases, and malignancies. Aberrant homeobox gene expression promotes tumor invasion, metastasis, and poor prognosis across multiple cancer types, with dysregulation influencing oncogenic signaling pathways and implicated in lung cancer progression through epigenetic mechanisms. Beyond development, homeobox genes modulate fundamental biological processes including proliferation, lineage specification, differentiation, hematopoiesis, and programmed cell death, with their activity regulated by long noncoding RNAs, microRNAs, and DNA methylation patterns.

title: Glycosylation in premature ovarian insufficiency: from genetic etiology to precision diagnostics
summary:
# Summary

Aberrant protein glycosylation has emerged as a crucial pathogenic mechanism in premature ovarian insufficiency (POI), a condition characterized by loss of ovarian function before age 40 that causes amenorrhea, infertility, and long-term health complications including osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease. Mutations in glycosylation-related genes, particularly those involved in N-glycosylation (PMM2, ALG6, ALG8) and O-glycosylation (GALNT family, CST3), disrupt multiple aspects of ovarian physiology including folliculogenesis, gonadotropin signaling, extracellular matrix dynamics, and oocyte-granulosa cell interactions. Glycosylation defects impair critical cellular processes such as protein folding, hormone-receptor interactions, cell adhesion, and signal transduction, with specific examples including altered FSH glycoforms (FSH21/18 promoting versus FSH24 reducing follicle survival) and disrupted integrin-mediated adhesion. Beyond POI, abnormal glycosylation contributes to other reproductive disorders including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome, while in ovarian cancer, MGAT3-mediated glycosylation of CD82 suppresses cancer cell migration. These findings establish glycosylation dysregulation as a unifying molecular mechanism across multiple reproductive pathologies and identify glycosylation-related genes as targets for precision diagnostics and therapeutics in POI and related conditions.

title: Expression and function of lncRNA ANRIL in a mouse model of acute myocardial infarction combined with type 2 diabetes mellitus
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the long noncoding RNA ANRIL (antisense noncoding RNA in the INK4 locus) is significantly elevated in both peripheral blood and myocardial tissues of mice with combined type 2 diabetes mellitus and acute myocardial infarction (T2DM-MI), and plays a pathogenic role in this disease model. ANRIL overexpression exacerbates cardiac dysfunction by increasing left ventricular dimensions, reducing ejection fraction and fractional shortening, enlarging infarct size, and promoting myocardial fibrosis and cardiomyocyte apoptosis—effects mediated through upregulation of transforming growth factor-beta 1 (TGF-β1) and collagen I and III. Conversely, ANRIL knockdown reverses these pathological outcomes by suppressing the TGF-β1 signaling pathway and reducing fibrosis and apoptosis, thereby improving cardiac function. These findings suggest that ANRIL may be a key mediator of cardiac injury in the context of T2DM-MI through promotion of fibrotic remodeling and myocardial cell death, positioning it as a potential therapeutic target for preventing progression to heart failure in diabetic patients experiencing myocardial infarction.

title: The recent progress and therapy in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease affecting the ovary, leads to endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers (EAOCs), particularly the endometrioid and clear cell subtypes (type I cancers), through multiple dysregulated molecular pathways. The malignant transformation from endometriosis to EAOC involves chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and dysfunction in key signaling cascades including the ERBB-PI3K-AKT pathway, along with frequent mutations in KRAS, BRAF, ERBB2, PTEN, ARID1A, and PIK3CA. Inflammasome complexes and their associated genes (NLRP3, AIM2, PYCARD, NAIP) are significantly dysregulated during this transformation and drive proinflammatory responses that contribute to tumorigenesis. The AIM2 component of the inflammasome complex interacts with pattern recognition receptors and activates proinflammatory cytokine production, with elevated AIM2 and Ki-67 levels found in EAOC tissues compared to endometriosis alone. High expression of inflammasome genes and toll-like receptor pathway components (TLR1, TLR7, TOLLIP, NFKBIA, TNF) correlates with poor survival outcomes in EAOC patients. These findings illuminate how endometriosis-associated chronic inflammation and immune dysregulation promote ovarian carcinogenesis and suggest potential therapeutic targets for preventing or treating EAOC progression.

title: Early modulation of autophagy-associated markers in murine adenomyosis and selective persistence in human stromal lesions
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that autophagy dysregulation plays a temporal and spatially distinct role in adenomyosis development. In early murine adenomyosis induced by tamoxifen, autophagy-associated markers including Akt1, Mapk1, Nfkb1, and apoptosis regulators (Bax, Bcl2, Bcl2l1) are transiently modulated at one month, with elevated p-MAPK/MAPK and BAX protein levels confirmed by Western blot. However, these early transcriptional changes resolve by three months despite established lesions, whereas in human adenomyotic stromal lesions, LC3B (an autophagosome marker) reduction persists selectively in the stromal compartment. Adenomyosis pathogenesis involves dysregulated autophagy within broader mechanisms including epithelial-mesenchymal transition, PI3K/AKT/mTOR and KRAS signaling activation, and hormonal dysregulation where estrogen inhibits and progesterone promotes autophagy. The selective persistence of stromal autophagy impairment in human disease suggests that stromal compartment-specific autophagy dysfunction may contribute to the chronic symptoms of adenomyosis, including abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, and infertility, distinguishing it from other autophagy-dysregulated pathologies like endometrial cancer and endometriosis.

title: Concordance of cortical morphological anomalies with genetic predisposition to multisite chronic pain in females
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that female multisite chronic pain (MCP) is associated with distinct cortical morphological alterations across multiple brain regions, establishing a genetic basis for pain susceptibility in women. Genetic predisposition to MCP correlates with increased cortical thickness in frontal and parietal regions (rostral middle frontal, caudal middle frontal, superior and inferior parietal cortices) but decreased thickness in temporal regions, and similarly shows positive associations with cortical surface area in prefrontal and parietal areas while showing negative associations in temporal regions. The gene SLC39A8, which encodes a zinc transporter critical for cellular function, emerged as a major genetic contributor to both MCP susceptibility and the observed cortical morphological changes, particularly affecting the rostral middle frontal cortex and parsorbitalis regions. Additional genes including FGF18, ECM1, MRPS21, TARS2, MLN, OLFM4, and NPM1 were identified as enriched in MCP pathology, with NPM1 playing key roles in cellular stress response, chromosome stability, cell proliferation, and pain perception modulation. The study indicates that MCP arises from central nervous system mechanisms involving neuroepithelial cell remodeling and central pain sensitization driven by chronic inflammatory responses, and that affected individuals experience significant complications including limited mobility, sleep disorders, and emotional difficulties, with females exhibiting intrinsically lower pain thresholds and reduced pain tolerance compared to males.

title: Epigenetic Compound Library Screen Identifies Ibrutinib as an Inhibitor of Ovarian Clear Cell Carcinoma Viability
summary: Ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) is an endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer subtype characterized by a distinct molecular profile dominated by high-frequency mutations in ARID1A, PIK3CA, and TERT promoter, with ARID1A mutations often co-occurring with PIK3CA alterations and implicating defective chromatin remodeling in disease pathogenesis. An epigenetic compound library screen identified ibrutinib, a Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitor, as a potent inhibitor of OCCC cell viability, with multiple OCCC cell lines (RMG-1, JHOC-5, OV207, OVISE, OVMANA, OVTOKO, and TOV-21G) demonstrating significantly greater sensitivity to ibrutinib compared to non-OCCC cells. The mechanism of ibrutinib's activity in OCCC involves inhibition of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR cell survival signaling pathway, though this inhibition was not uniform across all cell lines tested, suggesting variable pathway dependencies. Notably, ARID1A-mutant cell lines showed heightened sensitivity to ibrutinib, while the ARID1A wild-type cell line RMG-1 displayed a biphasic response pattern, indicating that ARID1A mutational status may influence drug response. This finding is particularly significant given that OCCC exhibits platinum-based chemotherapy resistance and presents a therapeutic opportunity for a BTK inhibitor in a cancer subtype with poor clinical prognosis and shorter progression times, especially in high-incidence Asian populations.

title: Meta‐Analysis of CYP1A1 MspI and Ile462Val Polymorphisms in Cancer Susceptibility Among Different Ethnic Populations
summary: The cytochrome P450 1A1 (CYP1A1) gene encodes enzymes critical for both metabolic activation and detoxification of carcinogens, as well as estrogen hydroxylation and drug metabolism. CYP1A1 polymorphisms, particularly the MspI and Ile462Val variants, are significantly associated with increased cancer risk across multiple ethnic populations, with ethnicity-specific patterns emerging: the MspI polymorphism showed stronger association in Brazilian populations while the Ile462Val variant demonstrated increased cancer risk in Asian groups. The Ile462Val polymorphism results in elevated enzyme activity, which when combined with alterations in CYP1A1 expression and function, can disrupt the balance between metabolic activation and detoxification, leading to DNA damage and enhanced carcinogenic susceptibility. This meta-analysis of twenty case-control studies validates that understanding CYP1A1 genetic variants is essential for determining cancer development and progression risk, as polymorphism-associated functional changes in estrogen metabolism and xenobiotic detoxification significantly contribute to population-level cancer susceptibility variation.

title: From mutation to treatment: The dual role of BRCA1 and BRCA2 in gynecological malignancy development and management a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

BRCA1 and BRCA2 are tumor suppressor genes essential for homologous recombination repair and maintenance of genomic stability, functioning through interactions with critical proteins including p53, RAD51, and PALB2. Mutations in these genes cause hereditary breast and ovarian cancers with lifetime risks of 60–80% for breast cancer and 20–40% for ovarian cancer, while specific mutation domains correlate with distinct cancer phenotypes, such as BRCA1 mutations driving triple-negative breast cancer and BRCA2 mutations associating with estrogen receptor-positive tumors. BRCA1 deletion or depletion triggers increased apoptosis, cell cycle checkpoint errors, and upregulation of oncogenes including Cyclin D1, c-Myc, and ErbB2, whereas BRCA2 loss impairs RAD51 recruitment and homologous recombination capacity. The identification of genotype-phenotype correlations, including ovarian cancer cluster regions in BRCA2 and the distinct functions of PALB2 interactions with BRCA proteins, has enabled precision medicine approaches. PARP inhibitors and immunotherapy have emerged as effective targeted treatments for BRCA-deficient tumors, exploiting the synthetic lethality created by impaired DNA repair mechanisms and genomic instability.

title: OncoRisk: a state-of-the-art web server for bridging the oncogenic databases and pan-cancer cohorts to the translational oncology
summary: OncoRisk is a comprehensive web server designed to integrate oncogenic databases with pan-cancer cohorts to advance translational oncology research. The platform demonstrates that driver mutations cause tumorigenesis by altering protein functions and dysregulating gene expression, with specific mutations like EGFR L858R strongly associated with non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and treatable with tyrosine kinase inhibitors such as gefitinib and erlotinib. The server reveals complex mutational landscapes, showing that individual mutations like EGFR L858R connect to multiple cancer types, including high-grade glioblastoma, and that drug responses depend on specific mutation contexts—for example, EGFR L747P confers poor erlotinib response despite involvement in the same pathway. OncoRisk also clarifies drug-target interactions by demonstrating that trametinib acts on MEK1 rather than directly on BRAF V600E, even though BRAF V600E mutations drive papillary thyroid carcinoma. By providing integrated evidence from CiVIC and other clinical databases alongside genomic data, the platform enables researchers to identify driver mutations, predict treatment responses, and understand cross-cancer mutational signatures for improved precision oncology applications.

title: Identifying Immunological Biomarkers for Major Depressive Disorder: Insights From Machine Learning, Single‐Nucleus Bioinformatics, and Experimental Validation
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies three key immunological biomarkers—DACH1, FZD7, and GULP1—associated with major depressive disorder (MDD) through integrated machine learning and single-nucleus RNA sequencing analysis. DACH1 and GULP1 are significantly upregulated in brain tissues of MDD patients and animal models, while FZD7 is downregulated, with all three genes showing correlations with immune cell infiltration. The 132 differentially expressed genes identified are enriched in immune-related functions and neuroimmune signaling pathways, including cAMP signaling and neuroactive ligand-receptor interactions, supporting the mechanistic link between MDD and immune system activation. Using LASSO regression, SVM-RFE, and random forest algorithms, the researchers validated these three genes as potential diagnostic markers and therapeutic targets for MDD. Single-nucleus analysis revealed altered immune cell-related expression patterns in MDD, highlighting immune infiltration as a key pathological feature alongside neuronal and glial dysfunction.

title: A Pilot Study on Multigenic Thrombophilic Risk in Recurrent Pregnancy Loss: Interactions Between MTHFR Polymorphisms and Classical Thrombophilia-Associated SNPs
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study demonstrates that multiple thrombophilic genetic polymorphisms, particularly in the MTHFR gene and classical thrombophilia-associated variants, significantly contribute to recurrent pregnancy loss through distinct but overlapping mechanisms. Homozygous MTHFR C677T and A1298C polymorphisms directly increase miscarriage frequency by impairing folate metabolism and elevating homocysteine levels, which subsequently promote endothelial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and platelet aggregation leading to placental insufficiency. The F5 Leiden and F2 G20210A mutations independently associate with pregnancy complications by creating a hypercoagulable state that compromises placental circulation and promotes thrombosis. Notably, the study reveals genetic clustering effects where combined heterozygous mutations substantially elevate miscarriage risk, such as the combination of heterozygous MTHFR C677T/A1298C with F5 Leiden heterozygosity significantly increasing the probability of four or more miscarriages. These findings support a cumulative, multigenic model of thrombophilic risk in recurrent pregnancy loss, where low-penetrance SNPs converge to produce clinically significant vascular compromise and placental dysfunction.

title: Distinct Molecular Pathways in Ovarian Endometrioid Adenocarcinoma with Concurrent Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Women with endometriosis, a benign ectopic growth of endometrial tissue, have increased risk of ovarian endometrioid adenocarcinoma (OEA), yet paradoxically demonstrate improved clinical prognosis compared to those without concurrent endometriosis. This study reveals that OEA arising from secretory endometrial epithelial cells displays a distinct molecular signature when concurrent endometriosis is present, characterized by 682 unique differentially expressed genes and divergent pathway activation patterns. The pathogenesis of endometriosis-associated OEA is driven by activation of NFκB and RAS signaling, elevated TGFβ signaling, and enhanced inflammatory responses including dysregulated leukocyte extravasation and acute phase response signaling. Notably, this subset shows downregulation of developmental pathways including bone morphogenetic protein, WNT, and NOTCH signaling, along with reduced expression of PAK2, which normally suppresses ovarian cancer cell migration. These findings indicate that the endometriotic tumor microenvironment uniquely shapes OEA biology through inflammatory and growth factor signaling, potentially explaining both the increased cancer susceptibility and improved outcomes in women with concurrent endometriosis.

title: Genomic sequencing of multicystic mesothelioma finds cohesin complex mutations associated with disease recurrence in patients referred for cytoreductive surgery and HIPEC
summary:
# Summary

Multicystic mesothelioma (MCM) is a rare mesothelial tumor of the peritoneum characterized by symptoms including severe abdominal pain, distention, and urinary disturbances, and is treated with cytoreductive surgery and hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy. Genomic sequencing identified distinct mutation profiles separating MCM from malignant peritoneal mesothelioma (MPeM), with MCM showing frequent mutations in cohesin complex genes—particularly SMC3 (45% of cases) and DNAAF5 (27% of cases)—rather than the BAP1, TP53, and NF2 mutations typical of MPeM. Notably, mutations in SMC3 or SMC1A components of the cohesin complex were significantly associated with disease recurrence, with all five recurrent MCM cases carrying mutations in one of these genes. Molecular modeling revealed that E1144 variants in SMC3 (K, V, G, Q substitutions) disrupt ATP hydrolysis by altering the coordination of the catalytic water molecule critical for cohesin function. These findings establish cohesin dysfunction as a previously unrecognized molecular driver of MCM pathogenesis and suggest that cohesin mutations represent a biomarker for identifying patients at high risk of recurrence following surgery.

title: Novel link between plasma bilirubin and anti-inflammatory miRNA profiles in follicular fluid of IVF patients
journal: American journal of physiology. Endocrinology and metabolism
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a novel association between elevated plasma bilirubin levels and a distinct anti-inflammatory microRNA profile in follicular fluid of IVF patients. Elevated bilirubin correlated with significantly higher expression of miR-146a-5p, miR-146b-5p, and miR-487b-3p, which collectively suppress key inflammatory mediators including IL-6, COX-2, TLR4, and components of the NF-κB signaling pathway (IRAK1, TRAF6). Notably, elevated bilirubin levels were associated with reduced oocyte fertility rates, and the anti-inflammatory miRNAs inversely correlated with oocyte fertilization success, suggesting that excessive suppression of inflammatory signaling may compromise reproductive outcomes. Bilirubin's multifaceted mechanisms—including antioxidant activity, cell signaling modulation, and activation of PPARα—appear to regulate these miRNA profiles and modulate the intrafollicular inflammatory microenvironment. These findings link bilirubin's established immunomodulatory and antioxidant properties to miRNA-mediated control of reproductive processes, with implications for understanding how oxidative and inflammatory states influence fertility in IVF patients.

title: Human genetics implicate thromboembolism in the pathogenesis of long COVID in individuals of European ancestry
summary:
# Summary

This study provides genetic evidence that venous thromboembolism pathways are causally implicated in long COVID pathogenesis in individuals of European ancestry. Using Mendelian randomization analysis, the researchers found that genetically predicted venous thromboembolism risk increases the odds of long COVID by 1.21-fold per unit increase in log-odds, with the strongest effects mediated through the F2R gene encoding protease-activated receptor 1 (PAR-1). Specifically, genetic variants increasing F2R expression in platelets—where PAR-1 mediates thrombin-induced platelet activation and proinflammatory responses—showed the most robust association with long COVID susceptibility. The findings suggest that thromboinflammation triggered by SARS-CoV-2 spike protein interaction with fibrin, combined with genetically determined variations in PAR-1 signaling, contributes to long COVID symptomatology including cognitive deficits. Notably, venous rather than arterial thrombotic pathways show specificity for long COVID, and the association appears independent of acute COVID-19 severity, suggesting distinct pathogenic mechanisms. These results support investigation of anticoagulant or PAR-1-targeted therapies for long COVID prevention or treatment.
